topics.title: Shipbuilding Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shipbuilding Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shipbuilding Material Definition (KTU Standard)**
> A *shipbuilding material* is any engineering material — predominantly **ferrous (steel)**, **non-ferrous (aluminum, copper alloys)**, **polymeric (FRP/GRP)**, **natural (timber)**, or **cementitious (ferro-cement)** — that possesses the requisite combination of **mechanical strength, ductility, toughness, corrosion resistance, weldability, and availability in plate/section form**, and which conforms to the rules of an **IACS member Classification Society** (Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, Bureau Veritas, etc.) for use in the hull, superstructure, machinery, or outfit of a sea-going vessel.

In KTU parlance, the term is therefore inseparable from the phrase **"Class-approved material"** — because no material may be incorporated into the load-bearing hull of a classed vessel unless its chemical composition, mechanical properties, and delivery condition are certified against an international standard (e.g., **ASTM A36 / A131**, **EN 10025**, **IACS S21/S22**).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Material is the Skeleton"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a ship as a human body floating on water.** The hull is the *skeleton* — it must carry weight (cargo), resist bending (waves), absorb shock (slamming), survive a hostile environment (saltwater corrosion), and be repairable mid-life. A *good shipbuilding material* is therefore one that is simultaneously **strong, light, tough, weldable, and durable** — exactly the qualities you'd want in the bones and tendons of an Olympic swimmer.

If we swapped bone (rigid, strong, but brittle) for cartilage alone, the swimmer would deform permanently. If we swapped it for muscle (flexible but weak), the swimmer would buckle. Steel is the *bone*; aluminum is *lightweight cartilage*; FRP is *carbon-fibre tendon*. The art of naval architecture lies in **assigning the right material to the right part** of the vessel.

## 1.3 Hierarchy of Required Material Properties

For KTU examination purposes, properties of shipbuilding materials are grouped into **four mandatory buckets**:

| Bucket | Property | Symbol / Unit | Why It Matters Onboard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Yield Stress | $\sigma_y$ [MPa] | Sets the elastic limit under hull girder bending |
| **Mechanical** | Ultimate Tensile Stress | $\sigma_u$ [MPa] | Reserves strength beyond yield |
| **Mechanical** | Elongation at fracture | $\varepsilon_f$ [%] | Measures ductility — vital for collision survivability |
| **Mechanical** | Charpy Impact Energy | KV [J] @ °C | Confirms toughness in cold northern waters |
| **Physical** | Density | $\rho$ [kg/m³] | Lower $\rho \Rightarrow$ lighter $\Rightarrow$ higher payload |
| **Physical** | Elastic Modulus | $E$ [GPa] | Governs hull deflection (stiffness) |
| **Chemical** | Corrosion rate in seawater | mm/yr | Determines maintenance cycle |
| **Technological** | Weldability (CE / CEV) | % | Critical for fabrication cost |
| **Technological** | Formability / cold-work | – | Allows plate bending and frame rolling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Keyword:** Any answer describing a shipbuilding material MUST contain the words **"Class-approved"** or **"IACS / Classification Society rules"** to earn full marks on a 14-mark question.

## 1.4 The "Big Four" Shipbuilding Materials — A Snapshot

| Material | Density $\rho$ | Yield Stress $\sigma_y$ | Specific Strength $\sigma_y / \rho$ | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mild Steel (MS)** | **7850 kg/m³** | 235 MPa | 29.9 kNm/kg | Hull plates of bulk carriers, tankers |
| **High Tensile Steel (HTS)** | 7850 kg/m³ | 315 – 390 MPa | 40 – 50 kNm/kg | Hatch covers, side-shell of large ships |
| **Aluminium Alloy (5083/5086)** | **2700 kg/m³** | 125 – 215 MPa | 46 – 80 kNm/kg | Superstructures, fast ferries, naval craft |
| **FRP / GRP** | 1500 – 1900 kg/m³ | 200 – 400 MPa | 105 – 267 kNm/kg | Hulls of small craft, workboats, mine-hunters |

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific Strength (also called *strength-to-weight ratio*)** is the single most important comparative index used in KTU theory questions. Notice that FRP outperforms steel by a factor of **3.5 to 9×** even though its raw strength is comparable — because it is *only ¼ the weight*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Shipbuilding Materials

Following KTU Module 1, the material universe is split into **five families**:

1. **Ferrous Metals** — Mild Steel, High Tensile Steel, Stainless Steel (limited to pipework/cargo).
2. **Non-Ferrous Metals** — Aluminium alloys, Copper–Nickel (Cu-Ni) for seawater piping, Brass, Bronze for propellers.
3. **Non-Metallic Inorganic** — Ferro-cement (cement + steel mesh), Glass.
4. **Polymeric / Composite** — Glass Reinforced Plastic (GRP), Carbon Fibre Reinforced Polymer (CFRP), Sandwich panels (PVC foam + skins).
5. **Natural Materials** — Timber (teak, mahogany, marine plywood).

## 2.2 Mechanical Property Requirements — The "Class Rule"

The Classification Societies fix **minimum** values for each structural role:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Governing Equations for Acceptance**
> $$\sigma_y \;\geq\; 235 \text{ MPa (for Grade A mild steel)}$$
> $$\sigma_u \;\geq\; 400 \text{ MPa (for Grade A mild steel)}$$
> $$\varepsilon_f \;\geq\; 22\%$$
> $$\text{Charpy KV} \;\geq\; 27 \text{ J at } 0\,^\circ\text{C (for ordinary shipbuilding)}$$

A *single point* failure on any of these four numbers **disqualifies** the plate from hull use — even if it is the cheapest in the market.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of "Why Steel Dominates"

- **Step 1 — Strength:** Steel offers $\sigma_y = 235$–390 MPa at relatively low cost.
- **Step 2 — Ductility:** Steel elongates by ≥ 22 % before fracture, allowing redistribution of stress in collision (the "**plastic hinge**" concept).
- **Step 3 — Weldability:** Carbon Equivalent $\text{CEV} \le 0.45\%$ permits arc-welding without pre-heating up to 25 mm thickness.
- **Step 4 — Anisotropy Reduction:** Hot-rolled plates are nearly *isotropic* in the plane, simplifying finite-element modelling.
- **Step 5 — Cost & Recyclability:** Steel scrap is magnetically separable and re-meltable at ~ 70 % the cost of virgin ore → **circular-economy friendly**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Table Convention:** The vertical pipe character `\vert` is used in place of `\mid` or plain `|` to keep the markdown renderer stable.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma \;=\; \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $\sigma$ = stress [Pa], $F$ = axial load [N], $A$ = area [m²] | Tensile / compressive stress in plate |
| 2 | $\sigma_b \;=\; \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | $M$ = bending moment [Nm], $Z$ = section modulus [m³] | Hull-girder bending check |
| 3 | $Z \;=\; \dfrac{B\,T^{2}}{6}$ (for rectangle) | $B$ = breadth [m], $T$ = thickness [m] | Section modulus of a stiffener web |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$ | Specific strength [kNm/kg] | Material selection index |
| 5 | $\text{CEV} \;=\; C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | Alloying percentages by mass | Weldability screening |
| 6 | $\sigma_{cr} \;=\; k\,\dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{12\,(1-\nu^{2})}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}$ | $k$ = buckling coefficient (≈ 4 for simply-supported long plate), $t$ = thickness, $b$ = breadth, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio | Plate-buckling check |
| 7 | $\lambda \;=\; \dfrac{a}{b}$ | $a$ = longer side, $b$ = shorter side | Plate aspect ratio |
| 8 | $W_{LL} \;=\; \rho g \nabla$ | $\nabla$ = displaced volume [m³], $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | Lightweight of ship (weight by buoyancy) |
| 9 | $C_b \;=\; \dfrac{\nabla}{L_b\,B\,T}$ | $L_b$ = length between perpendiculars | Block coefficient (used in plate thickness selection) |
| 10 | $t_{plate} \;=\; s\,k\sqrt{L}$ (CSR empirical) | $s$ = spacing factor, $k$ = material factor (1.0 for MS), $L$ = ship length [m] | Rule-based minimum plate thickness |

## 2.5 Specific Strength — Real Engineering Insight

A naval architect chooses **aluminium** for a fast ferry because:

$$\left(\frac{\sigma_y}{\rho}\right)_{\text{Al}} \;=\; \frac{215\;\text{MPa}}{2700\;\text{kg/m}^{3}} \;=\; 79.6 \;\frac{\text{kNm}}{\text{kg}}$$

$$\left(\frac{\sigma_y}{\rho}\right)_{\text{MS}} \;=\; \frac{235\;\text{MPa}}{7850\;\text{kg/m}^{3}} \;=\; 29.9 \;\frac{\text{kNm}}{\text{kg}}$$

That is **2.66× the specific strength** of mild steel. Translated into ferry economics, this means a **30 % lighter hull**, **smaller engines**, **lower fuel cost**, and **higher payload** — the reason every catamaran fast ferry above 25 knots uses aluminium.

## 2.6 FRP — Why It is Taking Over Small-Craft Market

- **No corrosion** in seawater — eliminates paint systems (saves ~ 8 % of build cost).
- **Single-shot moulding** — entire hull in one lay-up, fewer parts, fewer welds (weld defects are zero).
- **Radio-transparent** — preferred for naval mine-countermeasure vessels (mines triggered by magnetic signature).
- **Tooling for series production** — once the mould is made, hull # 2 costs 60 % of hull # 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation:** FRP loses strength above 80 °C and has poor through-thickness properties. Therefore **large ships (> 100 m)** are still dominated by steel — KTU expects this distinction in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Section Modulus of a Rectangular Plate

The section modulus $Z$ of a rectangular plate (breadth $b$, thickness $t$) about its **neutral axis** is required in nearly every KTU bending-stress problem.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 — Define cross-section:} \quad & \text{A rectangular plate of breadth } b \text{ and thickness } t. \\
& \text{Neutral axis passes through the centroid of the rectangle.} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2 — Locate centroid from one face:} \quad & \bar{y} \;=\; \frac{t}{2} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3 — Moment of area about the neutral axis:} \quad & I \;=\; \int_{-t/2}^{+t/2} y^{2}\,b\,dy \;=\; b\!\left[\frac{y^{3}}{3}\right]_{-t/2}^{+t/2} \;=\; b\!\left(\frac{t^{3}}{24}+\frac{t^{3}}{24}\right) \\[4pt]
& I \;=\; \frac{b\,t^{3}}{12} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4 — Maximum distance from neutral axis to extreme fibre:} \quad & c \;=\; \frac{t}{2} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5 — Section modulus (general definition):} \quad & Z \;=\; \frac{I}{c} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 6 — Substitute Step 3 and Step 4 into Step 5:} \quad & Z \;=\; \frac{b\,t^{3}\,/\,12}{t\,/\,2} \;=\; \frac{b\,t^{3}}{12}\cdot\frac{2}{t} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 7 — Final compact form:} \quad & \boxed{\,Z \;=\; \frac{b\,t^{2}}{6}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We substitute $I = b t^{3}/12$ and $c = t/2$ into $Z = I/c$. The factor $(1/12) \times (2/1) = 1/6$ emerges naturally. **For unit width (b = 1 m)** this reduces to $Z = t^{2}/6$ — the canonical formula used in CSR plate-bending checks.

## 3.2 Derivation — Critical Plate Buckling Stress (Bryan’s Formula, Simply-Supported Long Plate)

Used in KTU Module 1 to explain why a 10 mm plate between stiffeners spaced at 600 mm will *buckle* under hull-girder shear long before yielding.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 — A flat plate of thickness } t \text{ and breadth } b \text{ (long in the other direction).} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2 — Classical Euler buckling of a column gives } \sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{(L/r)^{2}}. \quad & \text{For a plate, replace } (L/r) \text{ by } (b/t)\sqrt{12(1-\nu^{2})}. \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3 — Substituting:} \quad & \sigma_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E}{12\,(1-\nu^{2})}\,\frac{t^{2}}{b^{2}} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4 — Empirical correction factor } k \text{ (boundary & loading coefficient):} \quad & \sigma_{cr} \;=\; k\,\frac{\pi^{2} E}{12\,(1-\nu^{2})}\,\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^{2} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5 — For a simply-supported plate under uniform compression: } k \approx 4. \quad & \text{(Per IACS S21 buckling curves)} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 6 — Plug-in numerical case (mild steel, } \nu = 0.3,\; E = 2.1\times10^{5}\text{ MPa):} \\[4pt]
\sigma_{cr} \;=\; 4 \cdot \frac{\pi^{2} (2.1\times10^{5})}{12\,(1-0.09)} \left(\frac{0.010}{0.600}\right)^{2} \\[4pt]
\sigma_{cr} \;=\; 4 \cdot \frac{\pi^{2} (2.1\times10^{5})}{10.92} \cdot 4.62\times10^{-4} \\[4pt]
\sigma_{cr} \;\approx\; 279\;\text{MPa} \;\;(\text{below } \sigma_y = 235 \text{ MPa ? No — compare to yield.}) \\[4pt]
\text{Note: Yielding of 235 MPa is reached first — so buckling is governed by yield, not Euler.} \quad & \text{For } b/t = 60 \text{ the plate is classed "thick" and yielding controls.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** When $b/t$ is small, yielding governs. When $b/t$ is large (say 100), the $\sigma_{cr}$ term falls below $\sigma_y$ and *elastic buckling* controls. The transition is the **slenderness limit** — a frequent KTU conceptual question.

## 3.3 Derivation — Strength-to-Weight Ratio Comparison Table

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mild Steel:} \quad & \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho} = \frac{235\times10^{6}\;\text{Pa}}{7850\;\text{kg/m}^{3}} = 29{,}936\;\text{Nm/kg} = 29.94\;\text{kNm/kg} \\[4pt]
\text{High Tensile Steel (HTS-32):} \quad & \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho} = \frac{315\times10^{6}}{7850} = 40{,}127\;\text{Nm/kg} = 40.13\;\text{kNm/kg} \\[4pt]
\text{Aluminium 5083-H116:} \quad & \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho} = \frac{215\times10^{6}}{2700} = 79{,}630\;\text{Nm/kg} = 79.63\;\text{kNm/kg} \\[4pt]
\text{FRP (woven E-glass/polyester):} \quad & \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho} = \frac{250\times10^{6}}{1800} = 138{,}889\;\text{Nm/kg} = 138.89\;\text{kNm/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Analytical matrix (ratio referenced to Mild Steel = 1.00):**

| Material | $\sigma_y / \rho$ [kNm/kg] | Multiplier over MS |
|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | 29.94 | **1.00×** |
| HTS-32 | 40.13 | **1.34×** |
| Aluminium 5083 | 79.63 | **2.66×** |
| FRP | 138.89 | **4.64×** |

> **Conversion Logic:** To get the multiplier, simply divide the candidate material's specific strength by that of mild steel. KTU students are expected to reproduce this table in 7-mark comparison sub-questions.

## 3.4 Derivation — Rule-based Plate Thickness (CSR-BC Empirical)

The IACS Common Structural Rules (CSR) prescribe hull-girder plate minimum thickness:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{min} \;=\; s\,\sqrt{L}\,k \quad \text{[mm]} \\[4pt]
\text{where} \quad & s = \text{section factor (stringer / plate),} \\
& L = \text{ship length in metres,} \\
& k = \text{material factor} \;=\; 1.00 \text{ for MS},\; 0.78 \text{ for HTS},\; 1.69 \text{ for Aluminium} \\[4pt]
\text{Numerical case:} \quad & L = 200 \text{ m, side-shell plate, } s = 3.5, \text{ MS} \\[4pt]
t_{min} \;=\; 3.5 \cdot \sqrt{200} \cdot 1.00 \;=\; 3.5 \cdot 14.142 \;=\; 49.5\;\text{mm} \\[4pt]
\text{If HTS-32 is substituted (k = 0.78):} \quad & t_{min} \;=\; 49.5 \times 0.78 \;=\; 38.6\;\text{mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Saving in steel mass per m² of plate:} \quad & \Delta m \;=\; \rho\,(49.5 - 38.6)\times10^{-3} \cdot 1\;\text{m}^{2} \;=\; 85.6\;\text{kg/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This shows **why HTS-32 saves ~22 % hull weight** in a 200 m bulk-carrier — exactly the type of numerical KTU Part-B (a) sub-question worth 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Material Selection Decision Tree for Shipbuilding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Vessel Design] --> B{Vessel Length}
    B -- Less than 25 m --> C{Craft Type}
    B -- 25 to 100 m --> D{Operating Speed}
    B -- More than 100 m --> E[Use Mild Steel or HTS]

    C -- Pleasure boat --> F1[FRP / GRP]
    C -- Fishing boat --> F2[Wood or FRP]
    C -- Patrol craft --> F3[Aluminium Alloy]

    D -- Less than 25 knots --> E1[Mild Steel Hull]
    D -- More than 25 knots --> E2[Aluminium or HTS]

    E --> E3{Structural Role}
    E3 -- Hatch coaming, sheer strake --> E4[High Tensile Steel]
    E3 -- Bottom plating --> E5[Mild Steel]

    F1 --> G1[Apply ISO 12215 Part 5]
    F2 --> G2[Apply Class Rules for Wood]
    F3 --> G3[Apply ASTM B928 for Marine Aluminium]
    E1 --> G4[Apply IACS CSR / S21]
    E2 --> G5[Apply IACS S21 and S22]
    E4 --> G4
    E5 --> G4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the chart:** Start at the top, follow the decision branches by ship length, then speed, then structural role. Each leaf node is a Class-rule document — a 14-mark answer that names **both** the material *and* the rule document is awarded full marks.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Material Property Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Material Family Selection] --> P2[Mechanical Properties]
    P1 --> P3[Physical Properties]
    P1 --> P4[Environmental Properties]
    P2 --> P5[Hull Girder Bending Check]
    P3 --> P6[Stability and Displacement]
    P4 --> P7[Maintenance and Lifecycle Cost]
    P5 --> P8[Class Approval and Welding Plan]
    P6 --> P8
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[Approved Material Issued to Yard]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The four property buckets feed into three engineering checks (bending, stability, lifecycle), which converge into the Class-approval gate. Only materials that pass **all three** checks are released to the shipyard.

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Application Zones Inside a Modern Ship

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Hull_Region
    H1[Bottom Plating] --> M1[Mild Steel 235 MPa]
    H2[Side Shell] --> M2[Mild Steel or HTS 315 MPa]
    H3[Deck Plating] --> M3[Mild Steel 235 MPa]
    H4[Bow and Stern Fabrications] --> M4[HTS for slamming resistance]
    end

    subgraph Superstructure
    S1[Wheelhouse] --> N1[Aluminium Alloy 5083]
    S2[Funnel Casing] --> N2[Mild Steel]
    S3[Decks above] --> N3[Aluminium for weight saving]
    end

    subgraph Outfit
    O1[Rudder and Stern Frame] --> K1[Cast Steel or Bronze]
    O2[Shaft Brackets] --> K2[Stainless or Cu-Ni]
    O3[Deck Fittings] --> K3[Stainless Steel 316]
    end

    subgraph Small_Craft
    T1[Patrol Boat] --> L1[FRP]
    T2[Yacht] --> L2[FRP or Wood]
    T3[Submarine Hull] --> L3[HY-80 or HY-100 Steel]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A 14-mark question often asks *"Where in a ship would you use aluminium and why?"* — the answer must specify the **zone** (superstructure), the **alloy** (5083-H116), and the **reason** (weight saving above waterline reduces KG). This diagram is your visual cheat-sheet.

## 4.4 Schematic — Stress-Strain Behaviour of MS vs HTS vs Aluminium

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Origin] --> A2[Linear Elastic Region]
    A2 -- MS --> B1[Yield Plateau at 235 MPa]
    B1 --> B2[Strain Hardening up to 400 MPa]
    B2 --> B3[Fracture at 25 percent elongation]

    A2 -- HTS --> C1[Linear up to 315 MPa]
    C1 --> C2[Yields sharply]
    C2 --> C3[Fracture at 22 percent elongation]

    A2 -- Aluminium --> D1[Linear up to 125 MPa]
    D1 --> D2[No distinct yield, 0.2 percent proof used]
    D2 --> D3[Fracture at 12 percent elongation]
```

**Interpretation:** Mild steel has the *longest plastic plateau* (best for collision energy absorption). Aluminium has *no yield plateau* — design must be based on the **0.2 % proof stress**, which is why aluminium scantlings are derived differently in the Class rules.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part-A Style Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper Style)**

### Question 1 [3 Marks]

> *"List any six desirable mechanical properties of a shipbuilding material and state the minimum Charpy impact energy required for ordinary shipbuilding steel as per IACS."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **High Yield Strength** ($\sigma_y \ge 235$ MPa) — to resist hull-girder bending.
2. **Adequate Ultimate Tensile Strength** ($\sigma_u \ge 400$ MPa) — reserves strength above yield.
3. **High Ductility** — elongation $\ge 22\%$ — to absorb collision energy.
4. **Toughness at low temperature** — Charpy KV $\ge$ **27 J at 0 °C** (ordinary); 34 J at –20 °C for high-strength grades.
5. **Good Weldability** — Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV) $\le 0.45\%$ without pre-heat.
6. **Corrosion Resistance in Seawater** — preferably low corrosion rate, ≤ 0.1 mm/yr unprotected in still seawater.

**Valuation Key (KTU pattern):** 0.5 marks per correct property × 6 = 3 marks. The Charpy value of **27 J** is the *single keyword* that separates 2 marks from 3 marks.

### Question 2 [3 Marks]

> *"Differentiate between Mild Steel (Grade A) and High Tensile Steel (Grade AH32) used in shipbuilding."*

**Model Answer (Tabular — 3 Marks, 1 mark per correct row):**

| Parameter | Mild Steel (Grade A) | High Tensile (AH32) |
|---|---|---|
| Yield Stress $\sigma_y$ | 235 MPa | **315 MPa** |
| Tensile Strength $\sigma_u$ | 400 – 520 MPa | 440 – 590 MPa |
| Specific Strength | 29.9 kNm/kg | **40.1 kNm/kg** |
| Weldability | Excellent (no pre-heat ≤ 25 mm) | Good (pre-heat for > 25 mm) |
| Application | Hull bottom, side-shell | Hatch coaming, sheer strake, bow |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write *only* the yield-stress difference and miss the application row. **Always include the *why* and the *where*** — KTU examiners award 1 mark for the engineering reason.

---

## 5.2 KTU Part-B Style Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Format)

> **KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style**
> *Answer one full question. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**(a)** *"Explain in detail the various grades of steel used in shipbuilding. Discuss the role of yield stress, tensile strength, and impact toughness in their selection."* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **General Strength (Ordinary) Steels — Grades A, B, D, E** (1 mark)
   - Grade A — $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa; Grade B — 235 MPa with improved deoxidation.
   - Grade D — 235 MPa; **normalised** for better toughness.
   - Grade E — 235 MPa; **TMCP** (Thermo-Mechanically Controlled Process) for finest grain, best low-temp toughness.

2. **Higher Tensile Steels — AH32, DH32, EH32, AH36 … EH40** (2 marks)
   - 32 denotes $\sigma_y = 315$ MPa; 36 denotes $\sigma_y = 355$ MPa; 40 denotes $\sigma_y = 390$ MPa.
   - Suffix **H** = Hull grade; second letter (**A/B/D/E**) = quality level (A ordinary, E best).

3. **Yield Stress** (1 mark)
   - Sets the **elastic limit** under still-water and wave-induced bending. CSR (Common Structural Rules) sets $\sigma_y$ as the *governing strength parameter* for scantling derivation.

4. **Tensile Strength** (1 mark)
   - Provides a *reserve* beyond yielding; ratio $\sigma_u/\sigma_y$ must lie between **1.05 and 1.90** for ductile behaviour (Charpy V-notch requirement).

5. **Impact Toughness** (2 marks)
   - Charpy V-notch test at **0 °C** for ordinary grades and **–20 °C / –40 °C** for higher grades.
   - Minimum acceptance: **27 J** (longitudinal) for grade A; **34 J** (long.) for grade E and AH32-EH32.
   - Ensures the steel is **not brittle** in cold seas (e.g., North Atlantic winter).

**(b)** *"What are the advantages, disadvantages, and typical applications of Fibre-Reinforced Plastic (FRP) as a shipbuilding material?"* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (in bullet form, 1 mark per well-developed bullet):**

- **Advantage 1 — Corrosion Immunity.** FRP does not rust. Maintenance cost reduces by ~ 30 % over a 25-year service life. *(1 mark)*
- **Advantage 2 — High Specific Strength.** $\sigma_y / \rho \approx 138$ kNm/kg — about 4.6 × mild steel. Allows lighter craft. *(1 mark)*
- **Advantage 3 — Single-Piece Moulding.** Hull, deck, and internal bulkheads can be laid up in one mould, reducing welded joints to *zero*. *(1 mark)*
- **Advantage 4 — Magnetic & Radar Transparency.** Naval mine-countermeasure vessels (MCMVs) use GRP to reduce magnetic signature. *(1 mark)*
- **Disadvantage 1 — Temperature Limitation.** Loses strength above 80 °C; not suitable for engine rooms or fire-zones without intumescent coating. *(1 mark)*
- **Disadvantage 2 — Poor Through-Thickness Strength.** Impact from grounding causes *delamination* — hidden damage is hard to detect. *(1 mark)*
- **Applications.** Hulls of fishing boats, pleasure yachts, life-boats, patrol craft up to 50 m, mine-hunters, fast rescue craft. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**(a)** *"Compare Aluminium alloys (5083-H116) with Mild Steel (Grade A) for ship superstructure applications. Compute the weight saving per square metre of plating."* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Comparison Axis | Mild Steel (Grade A) | Aluminium 5083-H116 |
|---|---|---|
| Density $\rho$ | 7850 kg/m³ | **2700 kg/m³** |
| Yield Stress $\sigma_y$ | 235 MPa | 215 MPa |
| Specific Strength | 29.9 kNm/kg | **79.6 kNm/kg** |
| Modulus $E$ | 210 GPa | 70 GPa (lower ⇒ larger deflection) |
| Corrosion | Needs paint (≈ 0.1 mm/yr) | Self-passivating oxide layer |
| Cost | ₹ 70/kg (approx.) | ₹ 280/kg (≈ 4×) |

**Weight Saving Calculation (3 marks of the 7):**

For an equivalent strength design, plate thickness scales inversely with yield stress:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{Al}} &= t_{\text{MS}} \cdot \frac{\sigma_{y,\text{MS}}}{\sigma_{y,\text{Al}}} = t_{\text{MS}} \cdot \frac{235}{215} = 1.093\,t_{\text{MS}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a 6 mm MS plate ($t_{\text{MS}} = 6$ mm) the equivalent Al plate is **6.56 mm**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of MS plate per m}^{2}: \quad & m_{\text{MS}} = \rho \cdot t = 7850 \times 0.006 = 47.10 \;\text{kg/m}^{2} \\[4pt]
\text{Mass of Al plate per m}^{2}: \quad & m_{\text{Al}} = 2700 \times 0.00656 = 17.71 \;\text{kg/m}^{2} \\[4pt]
\text{Weight Saving per m}^{2}: \quad & \Delta m = 47.10 - 17.71 = \mathbf{29.4\;kg/m^{2}} \;(\approx 62\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Aluminium superstructure saves ~ **62 %** weight per unit area at a 4× material-cost penalty — economically justified when the saved weight is converted to **payload, speed, or stability gain**.

**(b)** *"Discuss the criteria for material selection in ship construction, with reference to classification society rules."* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (1 mark per criterion):**

1. **Strength Criterion** — $\sigma_y$ must exceed maximum service stress (wave + still-water + dynamic) with a safety factor of 1.5–2.0 (per CSR-BC).
2. **Stiffness Criterion** — Plate deflection between stiffeners must be $\le b/150$ to avoid plate-buckling fatigue cracks.
3. **Weldability Criterion** — $\text{CEV} \le 0.45\%$ for MS; pre-heat required above.
4. **Corrosion Criterion** — Corrosion allowance of 0.5–3.0 mm added to net thickness; or use of Cu-Ni for piping.
5. **Cost Criterion** — Initial cost + lifecycle cost (LCC); FRP wins for small craft, MS for large.
6. **Availability Criterion** — Plate must be procurable in required thickness from class-approved mills (Bhilai, SAIL, JSW, POSCO for MS).
7. **Environmental Criterion** — Recyclability (steel is 100 % recyclable; FRP is not), embodied carbon, end-of-life dismantling.

**Closing line for full marks:** *"The final material selection is the optimum point in the multi-dimensional trade-off space bounded by the above criteria, validated by an IACS member Classification Society."*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT list the criteria without a numerical justification.** A 7-mark question expects at least **one formula** (e.g., $\sigma_y/\rho$ or $t = sk\sqrt{L}$) — pure prose loses 2 marks.
> - **Do NOT forget to name the Class rule** (CSR-BC, S21, S22, UR S33). Examiners award 1 mark just for the correct rule citation.
> - **In comparison answers, always include a table** — it is the single fastest way to earn the "presentation mark" in KTU 14-mark scripts.
> - **Avoid "FRP is better than steel" statements.** FRP is better only in specific contexts — your answer must specify the vessel size, speed, and application zone.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid-Fire Revision — Shipbuilding Materials**

- **The single most-used shipbuilding material in the world is Mild Steel (Grade A)** — $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³.
- **High Tensile Steels** (AH32 to EH40) save ~ **22 %** hull weight over MS but cost ~ 25 % more per kg.
- **Aluminium 5083-H116** has the highest **specific strength** among metals used in ships (~ 2.66 × MS) — preferred for superstructures and fast craft.
- **FRP/GRP** offers the *highest* specific strength overall (~ 4.6 × MS) and is corrosion-free — used for hulls up to 50 m.
- **Section modulus of a rectangular plate:** $Z = b t^{2} / 6$ — must be memorised verbatim.
- **Plate buckling (Bryan):** $\sigma_{cr} = k\,\dfrac{\pi^{2}E}{12(1-\nu^{2})}\,(t/b)^{2}$ — buckling governs when $b/t > 60$ for MS.
- **Minimum Charpy impact energy** = **27 J at 0 °C** for ordinary grades; **34 J at –20 °C** for HTS grades.
- **CEV formula:** $\text{CEV} = \text{C} + \dfrac{\text{Mn}}{6} + \dfrac{\text{Cr+Mo+V}}{5} + \dfrac{\text{Ni+Cu}}{15} \le 0.45\%$ for unrestricted welding.
- **CSR-BC plate thickness formula:** $t = s\,k\,\sqrt{L}$ [mm] — increases with ship length; decreases with HTS grade factor.
- **Class approval is non-negotiable** — every KTU 14-mark answer must reference an **IACS rule** (CSR, S21, S22, UR S33).
- **Specific Strength ranking (best → worst):** FRP > Aluminium 5083 > HTS > MS.
- **Density ranking (lightest → heaviest):** FRP (~1800) < Aluminium (2700) < MS (7850) < Cu-Ni (8900).
- **Common pitfall to avoid in KTU valuation:** Never claim aluminium is "stronger" than steel — it is *stronger per kilogram*, not in absolute terms.
- **Wood and ferro-cement** are still taught for traditional and developing-nation shipyards — but rarely used in modern commercial hulls > 30 m.
- **Magnetic signature reduction** → GRP hulls are used for naval mine-counter-measure vessels — high-yield KTU keyword.
- **Single keyword for full marks in 14-mark questions:** *"Class-approved / IACS-compliant material."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wood
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wood

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wood** is a naturally occurring, anisotropic, hygroscopic, and cellular composite material primarily composed of cellulose (≈ **40–50%**), hemicellulose (≈ **20–30%**), and lignin (≈ **20–30%**), extracted from the stems and branches of trees (xylem tissue). In the context of **shipbuilding and marine structures**, wood is historically one of the oldest construction materials, used for hulls, masts, decking, keels, and interior joinery of vessels ranging from small boats to mid-sized traditional craft.

From the standpoint of the **KTU OENST614 – Basic Ship Structures** syllabus, wood is studied as a *traditional ship structural material* alongside steel and other modern materials, with emphasis on its **mechanical behavior, structural grading, moisture interaction, and durability** in marine environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Wood is examined primarily for its **physical, mechanical, and moisture-related properties**, its **structural behavior under load (bending, compression, shear)**, **seasoning & preservation techniques**, and its **defect classification** relevant to ship structural design.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Living Skeleton of a Tree**
> Imagine a tree as a giant bundle of microscopic drinking straws glued together with natural resin. Each "straw" is a hollow cellulose fiber (running along the trunk), and the glue is **lignin**. When the tree is cut into a plank, those aligned straws become the **grain** you see on the surface. **Load applied along the grain** (e.g., pulling) is extremely strong — like trying to snap a bundle of straws lengthwise. **Load applied across the grain** (e.g., pushing sideways) is weak — the straws simply flatten and collapse.

This intuition is critical in ships because:
- A **mast** (loaded along the grain) is very strong in tension/compression along its length.
- A **deck plank** (loaded across the grain) must be sized much thicker because transverse bending stress is governed by the weaker cross-grain modulus.

---

### 1.3 Why Wood Matters in Ship Structures (Even in the Steel Era)

| Application | Reason Wood is Chosen |
|---|---|
| Traditional wooden boats / fishing vessels | Easy to fabricate, repairable with hand tools |
| Masts and spars (sailing ships) | High strength-to-weight ratio along the grain |
| Ship's decking (over steel hulls) | Insulation, anti-skid, reduces condensation |
| Interior joinery, furniture, hatches | Aesthetic finish, easy machinability |
| Plug & mold making for fiberglass hulls | Cheap, easy to carve to complex curves |
| Boatbuilding (small craft, lifeboats historically) | Buoyant (density ~ 400–900 kg/m³ vs water 1025 kg/m³) |

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Wood Property Constants (must memorize for KTU exams):**
> - **Density of seasoned wood:** typically **400 – 900 kg/m³** (average ≈ **600 kg/m³**)
> - **Density of water (sea water):** **1025 kg/m³**
> - **Modulus of Elasticity (E) along grain:** ≈ **8 – 15 GPa** (softwood), **10 – 18 GPa** (hardwood)
> - **Modulus of Elasticity (E) across grain:** ≈ **0.5 – 1.5 GPa** (≈ 10× weaker)
> - **Fiber saturation point (FSP):** ≈ **25 – 30%** moisture content
> - **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)** in marine atmosphere: ≈ **12 – 18%**
> - **Coefficient of thermal expansion:** negligible for ship structural purposes (≈ **3–6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**)
> - **Standard moisture content (basis for test data):** **12%**

---

### 1.5 Classification of Wood (Broad Overview)

Wood used in marine/naval applications is classified along several orthogonal axes — these frequently appear as 2- or 3-mark questions in KTU exams.

**A. By Botanical Origin**

| Class | Examples | Density | Marine Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Softwood (Gymnosperms)** | Teak (actually hardwood), Pine, Deodar, Fir, Cedar | Low–Medium | Masts, spars, planking |
| **Hardwood (Angiosperms)** | Teak, Oak, Sal, Mahogany, Iroko | Medium–High | Hulls, decking, structural members |

> [!NOTE]
> **Botanical misconception:** "Hardwood ≠ Hard" and "Softwood ≠ Soft." *Balsa* is technically a hardwood but is the softest commercially used wood. The classification is based on **seed structure** (angiosperm vs gymnosperm), not actual hardness.

**B. By Moisture Condition**
1. **Green wood** (moisture content > 30%)
2. **Seasoned / Air-dried wood** (MC ≈ 12–18%)
3. **Kiln-dried wood** (MC ≈ 6–12%)

**C. By Structural Use in Ships**
- **Structural timber** — keel, frames, stem, sternpost
- **Planking timber** — hull & deck planking
- **Joinery timber** — interior fittings
- **Spar timber** — masts, booms, gaffs

---

### 1.6 Growth Anatomy of Wood (Foundation for Property Understanding)

A tree trunk (cross-section) shows:
- **Pith** (innermost core)
- **Heartwood** — inner dead wood, dark, durable, resistant to decay (preferred for shipbuilding)
- **Sapwood** — outer living wood, lighter, moist, prone to decay
- **Cambium layer** — growth layer (becomes wood + bark)
- **Bark** — protective outer layer
- **Annual growth rings** — each ring = 1 year; *springwood (earlywood)* is less dense than *summerwood (latewood)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** "Heartwood is preferred over sapwood for marine use because it contains extractives (tannins, resins, oils) that make it naturally more resistant to decay, fungi, and marine borers."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy — Macro, Micro, and Sub-Micro Scales

Wood is a **three-level composite**:

1. **Macro level** — Annual rings, grain direction (longitudinal / radial / tangential)
2. **Micro level** — Hollow cells called *tracheids* (softwoods) or *vessels & fibres* (hardwoods), aligned along the trunk axis
3. **Sub-micro level** — Cell walls built of **cellulose microfibrils** embedded in a **lignin–hemicellulose matrix**

This three-level architecture is the direct reason for wood's **anisotropy** (different properties in three perpendicular directions):

| Direction | Definition | Typical Modulus of Elasticity (E) |
|---|---|---|
| **Longitudinal (L)** | Parallel to grain / trunk axis | $E_L \approx 10\,000 - 15\,000 \text{ MPa}$ |
| **Radial (R)** | Perpendicular to rings, towards bark | $E_R \approx E_L / 15 - 1/20$ |
| **Tangential (T)** | Perpendicular to rings, along circumference | $E_T \approx E_L / 20 - 1/30$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Consequence:** Wood is approximately **10–30× stiffer along the grain** than across the grain. Therefore, *ship structural members (masts, keelsons, deck beams) must always be oriented with the grain parallel to the principal load direction.*

---

### 2.2 Moisture in Wood — The Single Most Important Property

Wood is a **hygroscopic material** — it absorbs and releases moisture from the surrounding air until equilibrium is reached.

**Forms of water in wood:**
1. **Free water** — held in cell cavities (lumens). Present above the **Fiber Saturation Point (FSP)**.
2. **Bound water** — held within cell walls by hydrogen bonding. Present below FSP.
3. **Chemical water** — part of the molecular structure (negligible amount, removed only by chemical decomposition).

**FSP ≈ 25–30% (average 28%)** — the critical threshold above which wood behaves like a green material (no dimensional change, no strength change with MC) and below which shrinkage, strength gain, and stiffness gain occur.

**Key relationships (memorize for KTU):**

$$\boxed{MC\,(\%) = \frac{W_{wet} - W_{oven\,dry}}{W_{oven\,dry}} \times 100}$$

where:
- $W_{wet}$ = mass of wood at given MC
- $W_{oven\,dry}$ = mass after oven-drying at **103 ± 2 °C** until constant mass

**Shrinkage from green to oven-dry condition:**

$$\boxed{S\,(\%) = \frac{D_{green} - D_{dry}}{D_{green}} \times 100}$$

Typical values for hardwoods:

| Direction | Total Shrinkage (green → oven-dry) |
|---|---|
| Longitudinal (L) | 0.1 – 0.3 % (negligible for design) |
| Radial (R) | 3 – 6 % |
| Tangential (T) | 6 – 12 % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why tangential > radial?** Tangential cells shrink perpendicular to their long axis (the dominant direction of the cellulose microfibrils), whereas radial cells have restraints from ray cells and growth ring curvature. This is why *flat-sawn boards cup* and *quarter-sawn boards are more stable*.

---

### 2.3 Mechanical Properties of Wood

Wood's structural capacity is described by five principal strength values, each measured per **IS 1708 / ASTM D143** standard tests:

| Property | Symbol | Test | Typical Range (Seasoned) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bending Strength (Modulus of Rupture)** | $\sigma_b$ / MOR | 3-point or 4-point bend | 50 – 150 MPa |
| **Modulus of Elasticity in bending** | $E_b$ | Slope of load–deflection curve | 8 000 – 18 000 MPa |
| **Compressive strength parallel to grain** | $\sigma_{c,\parallel}$ | Axial compression | 30 – 80 MPa |
| **Compressive strength perpendicular to grain** | $\sigma_{c,\perp}$ | Bearing test | 4 – 12 MPa |
| **Shear strength parallel to grain** | $\tau_{\parallel}$ | Block shear | 5 – 15 MPa |
| **Tensile strength parallel to grain** | $\sigma_{t,\parallel}$ | Axial tension | 60 – 180 MPa |
| **Hardness (Janka)** | $H$ | Indentation | 2 – 10 kN |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse *modulus of rupture* (a stress value, units MPa) with *modulus of elasticity* (also a stress, but from slope, units MPa). MOR is the **maximum bending stress at failure**; MOE is the **initial linear slope of the stress–strain curve**. Do not interchange.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are exam-critical — memorize them as a set.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $MC = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$ | Moisture content | % |
| 2 | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Density (often given at 12% MC) | kg/m³ |
| 3 | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{wood}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Specific gravity | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Bending stress (simple beam) | Pa |
| 5 | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{W L^3}{48 E I}$ | Mid-span deflection (simply supported, UDL) | m |
| 6 | $\sigma_{c,\parallel} = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Compressive stress parallel to grain | Pa |
| 7 | $\sigma_{c,\perp} = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Bearing stress perpendicular to grain | Pa |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{V Q}{I b}$ | Transverse shear stress in beam (rare in KTU) | Pa |
| 9 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$ | Euler buckling load (mast, column) | N |
| 10 | $F_{allow} = \dfrac{F_{ult}}{FOS}$ | Allowable design stress | Pa |
| 11 | $\Delta L = \alpha \, L \, \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion (rare for wood) | m |
| 12 | $S\,(\%) = \dfrac{D_{green} - D_{dry}}{D_{green}} \times 100$ | Shrinkage % | % |
| 13 | $\sigma_{adj} = \sigma_{test} \times C_M \times C_D$ | Adjustment for moisture & duration (ASD) | Pa |
| 14 | $I = \dfrac{b h^3}{12}$ | Rectangular section moment of inertia | m⁴ |
| 15 | $Z = \dfrac{b h^2}{6}$ | Rectangular section modulus | m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Reference (no fabrication):** Mechanical properties of wood are conventionally tested at **12% moisture content** and reported as such in all timber design codes (IS 883, IS 1708, ASTM D245, Eurocode 5). When moisture differs, *strength adjustment factors* (e.g., $C_M$) are applied.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of Wood Property Knowledge

- **Ship design:** Determines scantlings (sizes) of masts, keelsons, deck beams.
- **Naval architecture:** Predicts buoyancy (since buoyancy depends on wood density vs water density).
- **Repair & maintenance:** Determines which species to use as replacement planks to match original durability.
- **Durability design:** Drives selection of preservation treatment (CCA, creosote, CCA-oil for marine piles).

---

### 2.6 Seasoning of Wood (Why & How)

> [!NOTE]
> **Seasoning** = controlled drying of timber from green state to EMC suitable for use.

**Why season wood?**
1. Reduces weight (easier handling)
2. Increases strength & stiffness (~50% gain from green → dry)
3. Improves dimensional stability (reduces shrinkage after installation)
4. Increases natural resistance to decay & insects
5. Improves nail-holding, glue-bonding, paint adhesion

**Methods of Seasoning:**

| Method | Medium | Time | Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Air seasoning** | Natural air | 2–24 months | Good for ship timbers |
| **Kiln seasoning** | Hot air / steam in chamber | 2–7 days | Highest, most uniform |
| **Solar seasoning** | Greenhouse solar heat | 2–4 weeks | Moderate |
| **Chemical seasoning** | Salt solutions | Slow | Specialized |

**Defects from improper seasoning:**
- **Warping** (bow, cup, twist, crook)
- **Checking** (surface cracks)
- **Splitting** (end cracks)
- **Honeycombing** (internal collapse due to too-fast surface drying)
- **Case-hardening** (hard outer shell, soft core, with internal stresses)

---

### 2.7 Preservation of Wood (Marine Use)

**Marine hazards:** Fungi (rot), marine borers (*Teredo navalis* — shipworm, *Limnoria*, *Sphaeroma*), bacterial decay.

**Preservation methods:**

1. **Natural durability** — using heartwood of resistant species (Teak, Sal, Deodar)
2. **Surface applications** — paints, varnishes, lacquers (limited penetration, only surface)
3. **Pressure impregnation** — forcing preservatives (Creosote, CCA, ACC, Borates) into cell walls under pressure in autoclaves
4. **Non-pressure methods** — soaking, dipping, brush, hot-cold bath
5. **Chemical modification** — acetylation, furfurylation (modern, eco-friendly)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine-grade timber must be both seasoned AND preservative-treated.** Untreated wood in seawater lasts only 6 months to 3 years depending on borer activity.

---

### 2.8 Defects in Timber (KTU Favourite Topic)

Defects are categorized as:

**A. Defects due to Natural Forces (growth defects)**
- Knots (live, dead, encased, decayed)
- Shakes (cup shake, heart shake, star shake, ring shake, thunder shake)
- Twisted fibres / cross-grain
- Wind cracks, rind galls
- Burls, cankers

**B. Defects due to Seasoning / Conversion**
- Warping (bow, cup, twist, crook)
- Checks, splits
- Honeycombing
- Case-hardening

**C. Defects due to Fungi / Insects**
- Dry rot, wet rot, blue stain
- Marine borer attack (shipworm, gribble)
- Termite galleries

**D. Defects due to Conversion (sawing)**
- Wane (bark present on edge)
- Sloping grain due to improper sawing
- Diagonal grain

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Moisture Content Calculation with Step-by-Step Logic

**Statement:** *A wooden plank for ship decking has a wet mass of 4.250 kg. After oven drying at 103 °C, the constant dry mass is 3.500 kg. Find the moisture content.*

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

$$MC\,(\%) = \frac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute given values.**

$$MC = \frac{4.250 - 3.500}{3.500} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator (mass of water).**

$$W_{water} = 4.250 - 3.500 = 0.750 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute ratio.**

$$MC = \frac{0.750}{3.500} \times 100 = 0.21428 \times 100$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{MC = 21.43\,\%}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.**
Since MC (21.43%) **< FSP (28%)**, bound water is being lost from the cell walls. The wood is in the *hygroscopic range* and will continue to shrink/strengthen as it loses moisture. For ship use, this wood needs further seasoning to reach EMC ≈ 15% to be dimensionally stable in service.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Shrinkage Calculation

**Statement:** *A green timber section has a tangential dimension of 250 mm. After seasoning, the same dimension measures 232 mm. Compute tangential shrinkage percentage.*

**Step 1 — Governing equation.**

$$S_T\,(\%) = \frac{D_{green} - D_{dry}}{D_{green}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$S_T = \frac{250 - 232}{250} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator.**

$$\Delta D = 250 - 232 = 18 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute ratio.**

$$S_T = \frac{18}{250} \times 100 = 7.2\,\%$$

**Step 5 — Result.**

$$\boxed{S_T = 7.2\,\%}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**
Tangential shrinkage of 7.2% is **typical for hardwoods (range 6–12%)**. This is a *high* value — the plank will shrink considerably during drying, and design must accommodate the movement (e.g., use *tongue-and-groove* decking with gap allowance, or use *quarter-sawn* stock).

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Bending Strength of a Wooden Ship Deck Beam

**Statement:** *A simply-supported wooden deck beam of a ship has a rectangular cross-section of breadth b = 150 mm and depth h = 300 mm. The span L = 4.0 m. A uniformly distributed load (deck cargo + self weight) is w = 12 kN/m. Find (a) maximum bending moment, (b) maximum bending stress, (c) check against allowable stress of MOR/3 = 30 MPa for the timber species.*

**Step 1 — Compute maximum bending moment for a simply supported beam under UDL.**

$$M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{8}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$M_{max} = \frac{12 \times (4.0)^2}{8} = \frac{12 \times 16}{8} = \frac{192}{8}$$

$$M_{max} = 24 \text{ kN·m} = 24 \times 10^3 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute section modulus Z.**

For a rectangular section:

$$Z = \frac{b h^2}{6}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (with b, h in metres).**

$$Z = \frac{0.150 \times (0.300)^2}{6} = \frac{0.150 \times 0.090}{6} = \frac{0.0135}{6}$$

$$Z = 2.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Compute maximum bending stress.**

$$\sigma_{b,max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z}$$

$$\sigma_{b,max} = \frac{24 \times 10^3}{2.25 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{b,max} = 10.67 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 10.67 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with allowable.**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{MOR}{FOS} = \frac{90}{3} = 30 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{b,max} = 10.67 \text{ MPa} \quad < \quad \sigma_{allow} = 30 \text{ MPa} \quad \therefore \text{ SAFE}$$

**Step 7 — Factor of safety actually achieved.**

$$FOS_{actual} = \frac{MOR}{\sigma_{b,max}} = \frac{90}{10.67} \approx 8.44$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation.**
The beam is significantly over-designed (FOS ≈ 8.4 vs target 3). For ship scantling regulations (e.g., classification societies), designers may reduce the section size. But for *first-cycle safety and dynamic loading* (waves slamming the deck), the conservative size is justified.

---

### 3.4 Derivation — Euler Buckling of a Wooden Mast

**Statement:** *A ship mast is approximated as a vertical wooden column, circular cross-section, diameter d = 200 mm, length L = 12 m, fixed at the base (heel of mast) and free at the top. Modulus of elasticity E = 12 GPa. Compute the Euler critical buckling load.*

**Step 1 — Identify effective length factor.**
For *fixed–free* column: $K = 2.0$

$$L_e = K L = 2.0 \times 12 = 24 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Moment of inertia of circular section.**

$$I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute (d in metres).**

$$I = \frac{\pi \times (0.200)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{64}$$

$$I = \frac{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}}{64} = 7.85 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 4 — Euler critical load.**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute.**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 12 \times 10^9 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-5}}{(24)^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 12 \times 10^9 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-5}}{576}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify numerator.**

$$9.8696 \times 12 \times 10^9 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-5} = 9.296 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m}^2$$

**Step 7 — Divide by $L_e^2$.**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{9.296 \times 10^6}{576}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 16\,139 \text{ N} \approx 16.14 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 8 — Slenderness check.**

$$r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \frac{d}{4} = \frac{200}{4} = 50 \text{ mm}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r} = \frac{24\,000}{50} = 480$$

**Step 9 — Interpretation.**
Slenderness ratio 480 is **extremely slender** — the mast is well within the *long-column* regime where Euler theory applies. However, the actual compressive strength parallel to grain of wood (≈ 50 MPa × π/4 × 200² = 1.57 MN) is far above the buckling load. **So buckling is the governing failure mode for a slender mast, not crushing.** This justifies *heel supports, stays, and shrouds* — they reduce the effective buckling length.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of All Derived Equations (Quick Recap Table)

| Phenomena | Key Equation | Typical Numerical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture content | $MC = (W_w - W_d)/W_d \times 100$ | 12–18 % at EMC |
| Shrinkage | $S = (D_g - D_d)/D_g \times 100$ | 0.2 % L, 4 % R, 8 % T |
| Bending stress | $\sigma_b = M/Z$ | 10–50 MPa |
| Bending deflection | $\delta = wL^3/(48EI)$ | mm to cm scale |
| Euler buckling | $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI/L_e^2$ | 10–100 kN for masts |
| Density ratio | $\rho_{wood}/\rho_{water}$ | 0.4–0.9 (buoyancy < 1, wood floats) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Diagrams:** Wood's physical structure (rings, cells) cannot be accurately rendered in Mermaid, so the diagrams below show **block-level functional architecture** of wood's structure, classification, and property relationships — exactly as recommended for non-graphical topics.

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Wood as a Three-Level Composite Material

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[WOOD STRUCTURE]
    rootB[MACRO SCALE: Visible to eye]
    rootC[MICRO SCALE: Optical microscope]
    rootD[SUB MICRO SCALE: Electron microscope]

    rootA --> rootB
    rootA --> rootC
    rootA --> rootD

    rootB --> nodeB1[Pith - innermost core]
    rootB --> nodeB2[Heartwood - dead, dark, durable]
    rootB --> nodeB3[Sapwood - living, light, moist]
    rootB --> nodeB4[Cambium layer - growth]
    rootB --> nodeB5[Bark - outer protection]
    rootB --> nodeB6[Annual growth rings]

    rootC --> nodeC1[Tracheids - softwood cells]
    rootC --> nodeC2[Vessels - hardwood water transport]
    rootC --> nodeC3[Fibres - hardwood structural]
    rootC --> nodeC4[Ray cells - radial transport]

    rootD --> nodeD1[Cellulose microfibrils]
    rootD --> nodeD2[Hemmicellulose matrix]
    rootD --> nodeD3[Lignin binder]
    rootD --> nodeD4[Extractives - tannins, resins]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Classification of Wood (Multi-Axis View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classify[WOOD CLASSIFICATION]

    classify --> axisA[By Botanical Origin]
    classify --> axisB[By Moisture Condition]
    classify --> axisC[By Marine Use]
    classify --> axisD[By Durability Class]

    axisA --> softwood[SOFTWOOD - Gymnosperms]
    axisA --> hardwood[HARDWOOD - Angiosperms]
    softwood --> pineA[Pine, Deodar, Fir, Cedar]
    hardwood --> teakA[Teak, Sal, Oak, Mahogany]

    axisB --> greenW[Green wood - MC over 30 percent]
    axisB --> airW[Air dried - MC 12 to 18 percent]
    axisB --> kilnW[Kiln dried - MC 6 to 12 percent]

    axisC --> structT[Structural - keel, frames]
    axisC --> plankT[Planking - hull and deck]
    axisC --> sparT[Spar - masts and booms]
    axisC --> joinT[Joinery - interior fittings]

    axisD --> dur1[Class 1 - Highly Durable - Teak]
    axisD --> dur2[Class 2 - Durable - Sal, Deodar]
    axisD --> dur3[Class 3 - Moderately Durable]
    axisD --> dur4[Class 4 - Non Durable - needs treatment]
    axisD --> dur5[Class 5 - Perishable - never use untreated]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Mechanical Property Test Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mechProp[MECHANICAL PROPERTIES OF WOOD]
    mechProp --> t1[BENDING TEST]
    mechProp --> t2[COMPRESSION TEST]
    mechProp --> t3[TENSION TEST]
    mechProp --> t4[SHEAR TEST]
    mechProp --> t5[HARDNESS TEST]

    t1 --> r1a[MOR - Modulus of Rupture]
    t1 --> r1b[MOE - Modulus of Elasticity]

    t2 --> r2a[Parallel to grain sigma c parallel]
    t2 --> r2b[Perpendicular to grain sigma c perp]

    t3 --> r3a[Parallel to grain sigma t parallel]
    t3 --> r3b[Perpendicular to grain - rare]

    t4 --> r4a[Shear parallel to grain tau]
    t4 --> r4b[Shear perpendicular - negligible]

    t5 --> r5a[Janka hardness]
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Ship Application vs Wood Property Requirement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    app[SHIP COMPONENT] --> req[REQUIRED PROPERTY]
    req --> species[SUITABLE TIMBER SPECIES]

    app1[HULL PLANK] --> req1[Decay resistance + bending strength]
    req1 --> sp1[Teak, Sal, Iroko]

    app2[MAST] --> req2[High stiffness along grain + low density]
    req2 --> sp2[Spruce, Pine, Oregon Pine - Douglas Fir]

    app3[DECK PLANK] --> req3[Abrasion resistance + slip]
    req3 --> sp3[Teak, Oak, Burma Teak]

    app4[KEEL] --> req4[High density + bending + impact]
    req4 --> sp4[Sal, Oak, Teak]

    app5[INTERIOR JOINERY] --> req5[Aesthetic finish + machinability]
    req5 --> sp5[Mahogany, Teak, Walnut]

    app6[SHIP TIMBER REPAIRS] --> req6[Matched species, seasoned, treated]
    req6 --> sp6[Original timber species preserved]
```
