
## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Design Decision Flow for Alloy Selection in a Ship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNew[Start Component Design] --> q1{Is Welding Required?}
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{Is it a Hull or Watertight Part?}
    q1 -->|No| q3{Is it a High Strength Fitting?}

    q2 -->|Yes| a1[Select AA 5083 H116 or H321]
    q2 -->|No| a2[Select AA 5083 or 5052]

    q3 -->|Yes| a3[Select AA 7075 T6 - Mechanical Fastening]
    q3 -->|No| q4{Is it an Extruded Profile?}

    q4 -->|Yes| a4[Select AA 6061 T6 or 6082 T6]
    q4 -->|No| a5[Select AA 5052 or 5086 Sheet]

    a1 --> endNode[Verify Classification Society Rules DNV LR ABS]
    a2 --> endNode
    a3 --> endNode
    a4 --> endNode
    a5 --> endNode
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Galvanic Corrosion Risk & Mitigation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    alumNode[AA 5083 Aluminium Plate] -->|Contact| steelNode[Steel Support]
    alumNode -->|Contact| monelNode[Monel Bolt]
    steelNode -->|Cathodic - Protected| riskNode[Galvanic Risk]
    monelNode -->|Cathodic - Noble| riskNode
    alumNode -->|Anodic - Sacrificed| riskNode
    riskNode --> mit1[Insulating Washers]
    riskNode --> mit2[Anti Corrosion Paint]
    riskNode --> mit3[Zinc Anodes]
    riskNode --> mit4[Use Similar Metals]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the four-digit aluminium alloy series that are commonly used in marine ship structures. State the principal alloying element of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Series | Principal Alloying Element | Marine Use |
|--------|----------------------------|------------|
| **5xxx** | Magnesium (Mg) | Hull plates (5083, 5086, 5454, 5456) |
| **6xxx** | Magnesium + Silicon (Mg + Si) | Extruded superstructures (6061, 6082) |
| **7xxx** | Zinc (Zn) | High-strength fittings (7075) |
| **3xxx** | Manganese (Mn) | Sheet panels and cladding |

*(1 mark each — any 4; full 3 marks for correct identification + principal element.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]
**Why is AA 5083-H116 preferred over AA 2024-T3 for welded marine hull construction?**

**Model Answer:**
- AA **5083-H116** contains Mg as the principal alloying element, giving excellent **corrosion resistance in seawater** and superior **weldability** using 5xxx filler wires.
- AA **2024-T3** contains Cu, which makes it susceptible to **intergranular corrosion and stress-corrosion cracking** in marine environments.
- Furthermore, 2024 is **not recommended for welded construction** — the heat-affected zone (HAZ) loses its T3 temper and strength.

*(2 marks for AA 5083 advantages, 1 mark for AA 2024 disadvantages.)*

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

#### (a) Explain the IADS four-digit designation system for aluminium alloys with marine examples. Discuss the role of alloying elements in 5xxx and 6xxx series. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Four-Digit Designation Logic (2 marks):**
   - The 1st digit indicates the **principal alloying element series** (1xxx = pure Al, 2xxx = Cu, … 7xxx = Zn).
   - The 2nd digit indicates **modifications** to original alloy (0 = original).
   - The 3rd & 4th digits indicate the **specific alloy** within the series (just sequential numbering).

2. **5xxx Series — Mg as principal alloying element (2.5 marks):**
   - **Mg content (3–5%)** provides solid-solution strengthening.
   - Alloys are **non-heat-treatable**; strength achieved by cold work.
   - **Marine example: AA 5083** (~4.5% Mg, 0.7% Mn, 0.15% Cr).
   - **Tempers: H116 / H321** are used for welded hulls to prevent sensitization (intergranular corrosion).
   - Excellent weldability, corrosion resistance, and cryogenic toughness.

3. **6xxx Series — Mg + Si as principal alloying elements (2.5 marks):**
   - Forms **Mg₂Si** precipitate → enables **heat treatment** (T4, T6 tempers).
   - **Marine example: AA 6061-T6 / 6082-T6** — used for extruded profiles (superstructure frames, masts).
   - High strength after T6 aging + good extrudability + reasonable corrosion resistance.
   - Less weldable than 5xxx; used in mechanically fastened structures.

---

#### (b) A ship hull side plate is currently 10 mm thick mild steel (ρ = 7850 kg/m³, σ_u = 410 MPa). It is to be replaced by AA 5083-H116 aluminium alloy (ρ = 2700 kg/m³, σ_u = 305 MPa). If the new aluminium plate must provide **equal ultimate tensile load capacity** per unit width, determine: **(i)** the **required aluminium thickness**; **(ii)** the **percentage weight saving per unit area**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**

$$\sigma_{u,steel} = 410\ MPa, \quad t_{steel} = 10\ mm, \quad \rho_{steel} = 7850\ kg/m^{3}$$

$$\sigma_{u,Al} = 305\ MPa, \quad \rho_{Al} = 2700\ kg/m^{3}$$

**(i) Required aluminium thickness (3 marks):**

For equal ultimate tensile load per unit width $F_u = \sigma_u \cdot t$:

$$\sigma_{u,steel} \cdot t_{steel} = \sigma_{u,Al} \cdot t_{Al}$$

$$t_{Al} = \frac{\sigma_{u,steel} \cdot t_{steel}}{\sigma_{u,Al}}$$

$$t_{Al} = \frac{410 \times 10}{305} = \frac{4100}{305} \approx \mathbf{13.44\ mm}$$

**(ii) Percentage weight saving per unit area (4 marks):**

$$m_{steel} = \rho_{steel} \cdot t_{steel} = 7850 \times 0.010 = 78.50\ kg/m^{2}$$

$$m_{Al} = \rho_{Al} \cdot t_{Al} = 2700 \times 0.01344 = 36.29\ kg/m^{2}$$

$$\%W_{saved} = \frac{78.50 - 36.29}{78.50} \times 100$$

$$\%W_{saved} = \frac{42.21}{78.50} \times 100 \approx \mathbf{53.77\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert mm → m when calculating mass per unit area. Also, ensure the strength criterion is correctly identified — *strength equivalence* (not stiffness equivalence) was used here. Don't confuse with deflection-based designs.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]

#### (a) Discuss the heat treatment and temper designation system for 6xxx series aluminium alloys. Explain why **T6 temper** is preferred for marine superstructure extrusions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Heat Treatment Stages for 6xxx (3 marks):**
   - **Solution Heat Treatment:** Heat to ~530 °C, hold → Mg and Si dissolve into solid solution.
   - **Quenching:** Rapid cool (water) → supersaturated solid solution (SSSS).
   - **Natural Aging (T4):** Mg₂Si precipitates slowly at room temperature → moderate strength.
   - **Artificial Aging (T6):** Reheat to ~175–200 °C for several hours → fine, uniform Mg₂Si precipitates → **maximum strength**.

2. **T6 Temper Advantages in Marine Superstructures (4 marks):**
   - **Highest strength** (~310 MPa UTS for 6082-T6).
   - Excellent **dimensional stability** — critical for extruded profiles with tight tolerances.
   - **Good corrosion resistance** (no Cu content).
   - Combined with **good extrudability** of 6xxx alloys → enables complex hollow sections for superstructure frames.
   - Superior to 5xxx for **load-bearing superstructure** elements under compressive / bending loads.

---

#### (b) Compare mild steel and AA 5083-H116 for a ship hull application with respect to **(i)** density, **(ii)** Young's modulus, **(iii)** corrosion resistance, **(iv)** weldability, and **(v)** relative cost. State **two classification society rules** that govern aluminium hull construction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Property | Mild Steel | AA 5083-H116 |
|----------|------------|---------------|
| (i) Density | **7850 kg/m³** | **2700 kg/m³** (≈ 1/3 of steel) |
| (ii) Young's Modulus | **210 GPa** | **70 GPa** (≈ 1/3 of steel) |
| (iii) Corrosion Resistance | Low — needs coatings/cathodic protection | **High** — self-passivating Al₂O₃ layer |
| (iv) Weldability | Excellent (SMAW, GMAW) | Good (GMAW/MIG with 5356 filler) |
| (v) Relative Cost | Low | **~3–4× higher** per kg |

*(5 properties × 1 mark each = 5 marks)*

**Classification Society Rules (any two — 2 marks):**

1. **DNV GL Rules for Ships (Pt.3 Ch.4)** — specifies design stresses, minimum plate thickness, and weld details for aluminium hulls.
2. **Lloyd's Register (LR) Rules for Special Service Craft** — Chapter on aluminium alloy construction.
3. **ABS Rules for High-Speed Craft** — Section on aluminium superstructure and hull design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Marks are awarded only when students mention **classification society names + specific rule/chapter**, not generic "society rules exist."

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Density of Aluminium Alloy ≈ 2700 kg/m³** (vs. steel 7850 kg/m³) — weight saving is the primary advantage.
- ✅ **E_Al ≈ 70 GPa = (1/3) × E_steel** — aluminium deflects more; design for stiffness (not just strength).
- ✅ **AA 5083-H116/H321** — workhorse alloy for welded marine hulls.
- ✅ **AA 6082-T6 / 6061-T6** — preferred for extruded superstructures.
- ✅ **AA 7075-T6** — highest strength but **not weldable**; mechanical fastening only.
- ✅ **5xxx alloys** are non-heat-treatable (cold-work strengthened); **6xxx** are heat-treatable (T6 aging).
- ✅ **Galvanic corrosion** is a major risk — aluminium is anodic; isolate from steel/monel using insulating washers and sacrificial anodes.
- ✅ **Thermal expansion coefficient of Al (≈ 23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C)** is about **2× steel** — expansion joints critical in long superstructures.
- ✅ **Specific strength** of 5083 is **~2.2×** that of mild steel → justifies thinner plates.
- ✅ **Cryogenic use:** AA 5083 retains toughness down to −196 °C (LNG carrier tanks).
- ✅ **Classification societies:** DNV, LR, ABS, BV — all publish specific aluminium hull design rules.
- ✅ Always state **alloy + temper** together (e.g., **5083-H116**, never just "5083") in KTU answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proof Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Proof Stress ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition **Proof Stress (Rp0.2)** is defined as the amount of stress required to produce a *small specified amount of inelastic (permanent) strain* in a material — conventionally taken as **0.2 % of the original gauge length** — when the load is removed. Mathematically, it is the stress corresponding to the intersection of the stress–strain curve with a line parallel to the initial elastic (Hookean) portion, offset along the strain axis by **ε = 0.002**. In ship structures, the classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, BV, ABS) use the **0.2 % proof stress of mild-steel hull plate as the reference material strength**, because the loading condition in a seagoing vessel involves combined static + dynamic stresses where the material must remain essentially elastic. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):** > *“Proof stress is the stress at which a material exhibits a specified permanent (non-recoverable) strain — most commonly 0.2 % — measured from the origin along a line parallel to the initial linear portion of the stress–strain curve.”* ### 1.2 Physical Constants Used in Ship-Steel Calculations | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | |---|---|---| | Young's Modulus for shipbuilding steel | E | **2.06 × 10⁵ N/mm² (≈ 206 GPa)** | | Poisson's Ratio | ν | **0.30** | | Specified offset strain | ε₀ | **0.002 (i.e. 0.2 %)** | | Density of mild steel | ρ | **7.85 t/m³** | | Shear Modulus | G | **E / [2(1+ν)] ≈ 79.0 GPa** | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The “Rubber Band + Paper Clip” Model > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine stretching a rubber band. If you release it below a certain force, it springs back to its original length — that is the *elastic limit*. Now glue a tiny paper clip to the rubber band and pull until the clip just shifts by 0.2 % of the original length and stays shifted when you let go. That pulling force per unit cross-sectional area is your **proof stress**. Engineers use this 0.2 % “shift” instead of trying to find the exact elastic-limit point (which is fuzzy for ductile steels) because it gives a **reproducible, contractually-binding number** for ship-plate acceptance. This is why proof stress is *the* number that classification societies print on mill certificates — every plate of grade **Grade A / AH / DH / EH** ship steel must satisfy a **minimum Rp0.2 = 235 N/mm²** (for normal-strength) or **Rp0.2 = 315 / 355 / 390 N/mm²** for higher-tensile grades. ### 1.4 Why Proof Stress Instead of Yield Stress? | Feature | Yield Stress (σy) | Proof Stress (Rp0.2) | |---|---|---| | Definition | Stress at first detectable plastic flow | Stress at 0.2 % permanent strain | | Visible on curve? | Yes — sharp “knee” (for mild steel) | Always — by construction | | Applicable to alloys? | Only if yield point is well-defined | Universal — works for all ductile metals | | Used by KTU/IACS rules? | Yes, when available | **Yes — primary design parameter** | > [!TIP] > Many high-strength shipbuilding steels (e.g. **EH36, FH36**) do *not* exhibit a sharp yield point. Hence, the **proof-stress method** is the only practical way to specify and verify their strength. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Construction 1. **Conduct a tensile test** on a standardised specimen (per **IS 1608 / ISO 6892-1**) and record the load–elongation curve. 2. **Convert to engineering stress–strain** using: $$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} \qquad \varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$ 3. **Identify the linear-elastic slope** in the initial straight portion — this slope is **E (Young's Modulus)**. 4. **Draw the Offset Line** as a line parallel to the elastic line, but shifted rightwards by exactly **ε = 0.002**. 5. **Find the intersection** of this offset line with the stress–strain curve. The stress value at this intersection is the **0.2 % Proof Stress, Rp0.2**. ### 2.2 Why the “Why” and “How” Matter - **Why 0.2 %?** It is small enough to be inside the “essentially elastic” zone (residual strain is barely measurable), yet large enough to be reliably detectable on a standard tensile-test chart — a regulatory compromise. - **How does it relate to design?** Permissible stresses in ship scantling formulae are computed as: $$\sigma_{\text{perm}} = \frac{R_{p0.2}}{\gamma_m \cdot \gamma_n}$$ where γm = material partial safety factor (typically **1.02–1.10** for steel) and γn = consequence-of-failure factor depending on hull-girder location. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | Engineering stress | | 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Engineering strain | | 3 | $\sigma = E \, \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (elastic region) | | 4 | $\sigma_{\text{offset}} = E(\varepsilon - 0.002)$ | Equation of the 0.2 % offset line | | 5 | $R_{p0.2} = E(\varepsilon_{int} - 0.002)$ | Proof stress at intersection point | | 6 | $\sigma_{\text{perm}} = \dfrac{R_{p0.2}}{\text{SF}}$ | Permissible / allowable stress | | 7 | $E = 2.06 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$ | Modulus for shipbuilding steel | | 8 | $\nu = 0.30$ | Poisson's ratio | | 9 | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Shear modulus ≈ 79 GPa | | 10 | $R_m = \dfrac{F_{\max}}{A_0}$ | Ultimate tensile strength (for reference) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse **Rp0.2** (proof stress) with **Rm** (ultimate tensile strength). For Grade A steel, typical values are **Rp0.2 = 235 N/mm²** and **Rm = 400–520 N/mm²** — different points on the same curve. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Ship Classification Rules (IRS / Lloyd's / DNV / BV / ABS):** All scantling formulae for plates, stiffeners, and primary hull members are anchored to **Rp0.2 of the grade of steel specified for the region**. - **Welding Qualification:** Welded joint strength is verified by ensuring the *weld metal* proof stress ≥ *parent metal* proof stress. - **Corrosion Allowance:** Effective plate thickness is reduced for corrosion; design is still based on original Rp0.2. - **Crashworthiness & Slamming:** Bow and bottom plates are required to use higher Rp0.2 grades (e.g. **EH36 with 355 N/mm²**) for added resistance against slamming loads. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the 0.2 % Offset-Line Equation Let the elastic (Hookean) portion of the stress–strain curve pass through the origin with slope **E**: $$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon \quad \text{(line L}_1\text{)}$$ To construct the **offset line**, we shift this line **horizontally to the right by ε₀ = 0.002**: $$\sigma_{\text{offset}} = E \, (\varepsilon - \varepsilon_0)$$ Substituting **ε₀ = 0.002**: $$\sigma_{\text{offset}} = E(\varepsilon - 0.002) \quad \text{(line L}_2\text{)}$$ The **proof stress Rp0.2** is the value of σ where the actual stress–strain curve intersects this offset line: $$R_{p0.2} = E(\varepsilon_{int} - 0.002)$$ where εint is the strain at the intersection point. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** During a tensile test on a Grade A shipbuilding steel specimen of diameter **d = 20 mm** and gauge length **L₀ = 50 mm**, the offset line (parallel to the elastic portion with E = 2.06 × 10⁵ N/mm²) intersects the stress–strain curve at a point where the total strain is **εint = 0.0035**. Compute: (a) The **0.2 % Proof Stress (Rp0.2)**. (b) The **corresponding load (F)** at this proof-stress point. (c) The **permanent elongation** if the specimen is unloaded from this point. --- **Solution — Step (a):** Apply the offset-line equation. $$\begin{aligned} R_{p0.2} &= E \, (\varepsilon_{int} - 0.002) \\ &= (2.06 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}) \times (0.0035 - 0.0020) \\ &= (2.06 \times 10^{5}) \times (0.0015) \\ &= 309.0 \;\text{N/mm}^{2} \end{aligned}$$ > **Answer (a): Rp0.2 = 309 N/mm²** --- **Solution — Step (b):** Compute the cross-sectional area and the load. $$\begin{aligned} A_0 &= \frac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (20)^{2} = 314.159 \;\text{mm}^{2} \\ F &= R_{p0.2} \times A_0 = 309.0 \times 314.159 \\ &= 97\,075 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 97.08 \;\text{kN} \end{aligned}$$ > **Answer (b): F ≈ 97.08 kN** --- **Solution — Step (c):** Compute the elastic (recoverable) strain, then subtract from total strain to obtain permanent strain. $$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon_{\text{elastic}} &= \frac{R_{p0.2}}{E} = \frac{309.0}{2.06 \times 10^{5}} = 0.0015 \\ \varepsilon_{\text{permanent}} &= \varepsilon_{int} - \varepsilon_{\text{elastic}} \\ &= 0.0035 - 0.0015 = 0.0020 \; (= 0.20\,\%) \\ \Delta L_{\text{perm}} &= \varepsilon_{\text{perm}} \times L_0 = 0.0020 \times 50 \\ &= 0.10 \;\text{mm} \end{aligned}$$ > **Answer (c): Permanent elongation = 0.10 mm** ### 3.3 Derivation of Permissible Stress for Hull-Plate Design From classification-society rules, the permissible bending stress for a hull-girder plate is: $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_{\text{perm}} &= \frac{R_{p0.2}}{\gamma_m \cdot \gamma_n} \\ \text{Typical values: } \gamma_m &= 1.02, \quad \gamma_n = 1.05 \\ \sigma_{\text{perm}} &= \frac{R_{p0.2}}{1.02 \times 1.05} = \frac{R_{p0.2}}{1.071} \end{aligned}$$ For Grade A steel (**Rp0.2 = 235 N/mm²**): $$\sigma_{\text{perm}} = \frac{235}{1.071} \approx 219.4 \;\text{N/mm}^{2}$$ > This is the *maximum bending stress* a designer is allowed to assign to the plate under combined still-water + wave bending moment. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Determination of Proof Stress The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **step-by-step procedure** used in a tensile-testing laboratory to determine Rp0.2 for a shipbuilding-steel plate acceptance test. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Receive mill-certified plate sample"] --> B["Machine standard tensile specimen
per IS 1608 / ISO 6892-1"] B --> C["Mount on UTM
attach extensometer"] C --> D["Apply uniaxial tensile load
record F versus Delta L"] D --> E["Convert to engineering
sigma vs epsilon curve"] E --> F["Identify linear elastic slope E
in initial straight portion"] F --> G["Construct offset line
parallel to elastic line
shift right by epsilon equals 0.002"] G --> H{"Offset line intersects
stress-strain curve?"} H -- No --> I["Re-draw offset line
or re-check data"] H -- Yes --> J["Read stress at intersection
this is Rp0.2"] J --> K["Compare with minimum
specified by class rule"] K --> L{"Rp0.2 greater than
or equal to specified?"} L -- Yes --> M["Plate ACCEPTED
issue mill certificate"] L -- No --> N["Plate REJECTED
return to supplier"] I --> G ``` ### 4.2 Schematic — Stress–Strain Curve with 0.2 % Offset The following Mermaid block-diagram conceptually represents the relationship between the elastic line, the offset line, the intersection point, and the proof-stress value on the classical stress–strain plot. ```mermaid flowchart LR O["Origin
sigma 0, epsilon 0"] -->|Elastic slope E| EL["Elastic Line
sigma equals E times epsilon"] EL -->|shifted right by 0.002| OL["Offset Line
sigma equals E times (epsilon minus 0.002)"] OL --> IP["Intersection Point P
defines Rp0.2"] IP --> PSTR["Rp0.2 value
read on sigma-axis"] IP --> PSTN["epsilon at P
on epsilon-axis"] SC["Stress-Strain Curve
full test data"] --> IP YP["Yield Region
for mild steel knee"] --> SC ULT["Ultimate Region
sigma reaches Rm"] --> SC ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The **Offset Line** is *parallel* to the **Elastic Line** but displaced to the right. Where this parallel line cuts the actual stress–strain curve, drop a vertical line down to the strain axis to read the **ε at P**, and a horizontal line across to the stress axis to read the **Rp0.2** value. ### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Why Proof Stress Matters in Ship Design ```mermaid flowchart TD PS["Rp0.2 of Steel Grade"] --> SP["Specification of Plates
Stiffeners Girders"] PS --> AL["Computation of Allowable Stress
sigma perm equals Rp0.2 divided by SF"] PS --> WQ["Welding Procedure Qualification
weld metal Rp0.2 greater than parent"] PS --> CA["Corrosion Allowance Check
effective thickness design"] PS --> SL["Slamming and Bottom Impact
higher grades for bow region"] AL --> DS["Design Scantlings
thickness spacing dimensions"] DS --> OK["Classification Approval
and Freeboard Assignment"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper-1]** *Define proof stress. Why is the 0.2 % offset value used universally in shipbuilding-steel specifications?* **Model Answer (10 lines, ~3-mark depth):** > Proof stress is the stress at which a material exhibits a small specified amount of permanent (non-recoverable) strain, conventionally taken as **0.2 % of the original gauge length**. It is obtained from the intersection of the stress–strain curve with a line parallel to the initial elastic portion, shifted right by ε = 0.002. The 0.2 % value is used because (i) it is small enough to lie within the essentially elastic regime of the material, (ii) it is large enough to be measured reliably with a standard extensometer, and (iii) it gives a **reproducible, contractually-binding strength figure** for steels that do not show a sharp yield point — particularly the high-tensile grades EH36, DH36, FH36 used in ice-strengthened and container ships. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]** *State the standard values of Young's modulus, Poisson's ratio and 0.2 % proof stress for Grade A shipbuilding steel.* **Model Answer (6 lines, ~3-mark depth):** > For Grade A normal-strength shipbuilding steel as per IRS / IACS UR W11: > - Young's Modulus **E = 2.06 × 10⁵ N/mm² (206 GPa)**. > - Poisson's Ratio **ν = 0.30**, giving Shear Modulus G = E / [2(1+ν)] ≈ **79 GPa**. > - Minimum 0.2 % Proof Stress **Rp0.2 = 235 N/mm²**. > - Ultimate Tensile Strength **Rm = 400 – 520 N/mm²**. > - Minimum elongation at fracture **= 22 %** on a 5.65√A₀ gauge length. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > Choose **ONE** of the following two questions. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. --- #### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper-3, Module-1 Choice-A]` **(a)** *With the help of a neat sketch of the stress–strain curve, explain the graphical method of determining the 0.2 % proof stress of a ductile material.* **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** 1. Draw the engineering stress–strain curve obtained from the tensile test, with **σ (N/mm²)** on the Y-axis and **ε** (dimensionless or %) on the X-axis. **(1 mark)** 2. Identify the initial linear portion (Hookean region). Measure its slope; this slope is the **Young's Modulus E = 2.06 × 10⁵ N/mm²** for ship steel. **(1 mark)** 3. Mark a point on the strain axis at **ε = 0.002** (i.e. 0.2 %). Draw a vertical line through this point until it meets the elastic line extended; call this meeting point **M**. **(1 mark)** 4. Through M, draw a line **parallel to the elastic line** — this is the **offset line** of equation σ = E (ε − 0.002). **(1 mark)** 5. The offset line will intersect the actual stress–strain curve at a point **P**; in mild steel this occurs just *after* the yield-point knee. **(1 mark)** 6. Drop a horizontal line from P to the stress axis; the stress value read is the **0.2 % Proof Stress Rp0.2**. For Grade A shipbuilding steel this should be ≥ **235 N/mm²**. **(1 mark)** 7. Conclude by stating that this Rp0.2 is the *design reference* for all hull scantling calculations. **(1 mark)** --- **(b)** *During a tensile test on a 16 mm diameter specimen of Grade A steel (gauge length 50 mm), the elastic line slope is found to be 2.06 × 10⁵ N/mm². The offset line intersects the stress–strain curve at total strain ε = 0.0032. Determine: (i) the 0.2 % proof stress, (ii) the corresponding load, and (iii) the permanent elongation on unloading.* **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **(i) Proof Stress:** $$\begin{aligned} R_{p0.2} &= E(\varepsilon_{int} - 0.002) \\ &= (2.06 \times 10^{5})(0.0032 - 0.0020) \\ &= (2.06 \times 10^{5})(0.0012) \\ &= 247.2 \;\text{N/mm}^{2} \end{aligned}$$ **(2 marks)** — *Result: Rp0.2 = 247.2 N/mm²* **(ii) Load:** $$\begin{aligned} A_0 &= \frac{\pi}{4}(16)^2 = 201.062 \;\text{mm}^{2} \\ F &= R_{p0.2} \times A_0 = 247.2 \times 201.062 \\ &= 49\,703 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 49.70 \;\text{kN} \end{aligned}$$ **(2 marks)** — *Result: F ≈ 49.70 kN* **(iii) Permanent Elongation:** $$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon_{\text{elastic}} &= \frac{247.2}{2.06 \times 10^{5}} = 0.0012 \\ \varepsilon_{\text{perm}} &= 0.0032 - 0.0012 = 0.0020 \;(=0.20\,\%) \\ \Delta L_{\text{perm}} &= 0.0020 \times 50 = 0.10 \;\text{mm} \end{aligned}$$ **(3 marks)** — *Result: ΔLperm = 0.10 mm* (and a clear statement that this **must** equal 0.20 % × 50 = 0.10 mm by definition of the offset method — used as a self-check). --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper-3, Module-1 Choice-B]` **(a)** *Explain the difference between yield stress and 0.2 % proof stress. In what situations would a designer prefer to use proof stress rather than yield stress?* **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** 1. **Yield Stress (σy):** stress at which the material begins to deform plastically; on the stress–strain curve, mild steel shows a sharp **upper / lower yield point**, while aluminium and many alloys show a *gradual* transition. **(1 mark)** 2. **Proof Stress (Rp0.2):** stress at which the material exhibits a *specified* (0.2 %) permanent strain, found by the **offset method** — universally applicable to all ductile metals. **(1 mark)** 3. **Why offset instead of yield?** The offset method is **objective** (a number, not a slope change), **reproducible** across labs, and works for materials **without a defined yield point**. **(1 mark)** 4. For **mild steel** (Grade A, B, D, E) the proof stress at 0.2 % offset is **slightly higher** than the lower yield point, but used as a conservative design anchor. **(1 mark)** 5. For **high-tensile steels (AH, DH, EH, FH)** no sharp yield exists — proof stress is the **only** defined strength parameter. **(1 mark)** 6. **IACS Unified Rules** mandate Rp0.2 as the contractual material strength for ship scantling formulae. **(1 mark)** 7. Concluding sentence: designers prefer proof stress because classification-society rules, welding-procedure specs, and mill certificates are all expressed in Rp0.2, giving a **single, unambiguous design value**. **(1 mark)** --- **(b)** *A 12 mm thick × 80 mm wide mild-steel plate is to be used as a lower-deck plate of a bulk carrier. The minimum specified proof stress is 235 N/mm². Using a partial safety factor γm = 1.02 and a consequence factor γn = 1.05, determine: (i) the permissible bending stress, (ii) the permissible bending moment if the effective span is 2.5 m, and (iii) the section modulus and minimum thickness required to resist this moment with a maximum allowable bending stress equal to σperm.* **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **(i) Permissible Bending Stress:** $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_{\text{perm}} &= \frac{R_{p0.2}}{\gamma_m \cdot \gamma_n} = \frac{235}{1.02 \times 1.05} \\ &= \frac{235}{1.071} \approx 219.42 \;\text{N/mm}^{2} \end{aligned}$$ **(2 marks)** — *σperm ≈ 219.4 N/mm²* **(ii) Permissible Bending Moment (per unit width) — using Section Modulus per unit width:** Section modulus per unit width: $$\begin{aligned} Z &= \frac{t^2}{6} = \frac{(12)^2}{6} = 24 \;\text{mm}^{3}/\text{mm} \\ M_{\text{per m}} &= \sigma_{\text{perm}} \times Z \times 1000 \\ &= 219.42 \times 24 \times 1000 \\ &= 5.27 \times 10^{6} \;\text{N·mm per m width} \\ &= 5.27 \;\text{kN·m per m width} \end{aligned}$$ For effective span L = 2.5 m (assume simply supported UDL): $$\begin{aligned} M_{\text{max, UDL}} &= \frac{w L^2}{8} \\ w_{\text{allow}} &= \frac{8 M}{L^2} = \frac{8 \times 5.27}{(2.5)^2} \\ &= 6.74 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \end{aligned}$$ **(2 marks)** — *Result: wallow ≈ 6.74 kN/m²* **(iii) Required Thickness for given design load q = 5.0 kN/m² (assumption):** $$\begin{aligned} M_{\text{req}} &= \frac{q L^2}{8} = \frac{5.0 \times (2.5)^2}{8} = 3.906 \;\text{kN·m per m} \\ &= 3.906 \times 10^{6} \;\text{N·mm per m} \\ Z_{\text{req}} &= \frac{M_{\text{req}}}{\sigma_{\text{perm}}} = \frac{3.906 \times 10^{6}}{219.42} \\ &= 17\,801 \;\text{mm}^{3}/\text{mm} \\ t_{\text{req}} &= \sqrt{6 \times Z_{\text{req}}} = \sqrt{6 \times 17\,801} \\ &= \sqrt{106\,806} \approx 326.8 \;\text{mm} \;(\text{per m width, unrealistic scale}) \end{aligned}$$ > **Examiner Note (3 marks):** Re-state the result clearly: required section modulus is **17.8 × 10³ mm³/mm**, which for a 1-metre strip translates to a very thick plate — illustrating why in practice the deck is **stiffened** (corrugation or longitudinals + transverses) so that the effective span L is reduced and a realistic plate thickness suffices. Highlight that *proof stress sets the ceiling* for σperm, which then dictates the minimum plate thickness. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students lose marks in Proof-Stress questions:** > > 1. **Confusing proof stress with ultimate tensile strength.** Rp0.2 ≈ 235 N/mm² and Rm ≈ 400–520 N/mm² for Grade A. Writing "235 = ultimate" will cost 1 mark instantly. > 2. **Forgetting the factor 0.002 in the offset-line equation.** The expression is **E(ε − 0.002)**, *not* E·ε. > 3. **Mixing up Young's modulus units.** E must be in **N/mm²** (or MPa) for these calculations; converting to GPa (× 10³) by mistake will produce a stress 1000× too large. > 4. **Not stating the units** of load (N vs kN) and area (mm² vs m²). KTU examiners explicitly deduct **½ mark** for missing units. > 5. **Skipping the sketch.** For a 7-mark sub-part asking "explain graphically", a missing neat sketch is an automatic **2-mark loss**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Definition:** Proof stress = stress at 0.2 % permanent strain; located graphically via an *offset line parallel to the elastic line*. - ✅ **Standard formula:** $R_{p0.2} = E(\varepsilon_{int} - 0.002)$. - ✅ **Constants:** E = 2.06 × 10⁵ N/mm², ν = 0.30, ε₀ = 0.002. - ✅ **Typical values for shipbuilding steel grades:** - Grade A → Rp0.2 = **235 N/mm²** - Grade AH/DH/EH → Rp0.2 = **315 / 355 / 390 N/mm²** - ✅ **Design use:** σperm = Rp0.2 / (γm · γn) ≈ Rp0.2 / 1.07. - ✅ **Why 0.2 %:** Reproducible, objective, applicable to all ductile metals including those without a sharp yield point. - ✅ **Permanent elongation on unloading = 0.002 × L₀** by definition — always use this as a self-check in numerical problems. - ✅ **Distinguish:** Rp0.2 ≠ Rm ≠ σy; each represents a different point on the stress–strain curve. - ✅ **KTU must-mention keywords:** *offset method*, *IACS Unified Requirements*, *0.2 % non-proportional elongation*, *engineering stress–strain*, *gauge length L₀*. - ✅ **Units discipline:** Keep σ in N/mm², E in N/mm², ε dimensionless — never mix.

topics.title: GRP and FRP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** GRP and FRP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 GRP — Glass Reinforced Plastic

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** **GRP (Glass Reinforced Plastic)** is a composite material formed by embedding glass fibres as the reinforcement phase within a thermosetting or thermoplastic polymer matrix (typically polyester, vinyl ester, or epoxy resin). The glass fibres carry the primary structural loads, while the resin matrix binds them together, transfers load between fibres, and protects them from environmental degradation.

In shipbuilding terminology, GRP is the most common form of FRP used in hull construction. The glass fibres may be arranged as:
- **Chopped strand mat (CSM)** — randomly oriented short fibres
- **Woven roving (WR)** — bidirectional cloth
- **Unidirectional roving** — aligned continuous fibres
- **Continuous filament mat (CFM)**

## 1.2 FRP — Fibre Reinforced Plastic / Polymer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** **FRP (Fibre Reinforced Plastic / Fibre Reinforced Polymer)** is the broad umbrella term for composite materials in which high-strength fibres (glass, carbon, aramid, basalt, or natural fibres) are embedded in a polymer matrix. GRP is therefore a *sub-category* of FRP where the reinforcement is specifically glass fibre.

**Other FRP families used in marine applications:**
| Family | Reinforcement Fibre | Typical Marine Use |
|---|---|---|
| **GRP** | E-glass, S-glass | Hulls, decks, superstructures |
| **CFRP** | Carbon fibre | High-performance racing yachts, masts |
| **AFRP** | Aramid (Kevlar) | Ballistic protection, mine-hunter hulls |
| **BFRP** | Basalt fibre | Fire-resistant structures |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine a bundle of dry spaghetti (the fibres) dipped in a thick layer of superglue (the resin). On its own, the spaghetti bends and snaps easily, and the glue alone is brittle. But combine them and you get a material that is **strong, light, and stiff** — exactly like a boat hull. The fibres act like the "skeleton" providing tensile strength, while the resin acts like the "muscle and skin" holding everything in position and resisting compression/shear.

This synergy between two weak constituents producing a strong composite is called the **rule of mixtures** and is the central principle of fibre-reinforced composites.

## 1.4 Why GRP/FRP Matters in Modern Shipbuilding

- **Weight reduction:** GRP hulls are **30–50% lighter** than equivalent steel hulls, increasing payload and fuel efficiency.
- **Corrosion resistance:** No rusting in seawater — a major advantage over mild steel.
- **Formability:** Complex double-curvature hull surfaces can be moulded in a single operation.
- **Low magnetic signature:** Critical for **mine countermeasure vessels (MCMV)**.
- **Repairability:** Damaged panels can be patched in-situ using lay-up techniques.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of *Basic Ship Structures* requires the student to compare GRP/FRP with conventional shipbuilding materials (steel and aluminium) and identify their structural roles, fabrication routes, and limitations in marine service.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constituents of a GRP/FRP Laminate

A marine GRP/FRP laminate is built from **four functional ingredient groups**:

### 2.1.1 Reinforcement (Fibre Phase)
The **load-bearing skeleton** of the composite. Provides tensile strength, stiffness, and impact resistance.

| Fibre Type | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Young's Modulus (GPa) | Density (g/cm³) | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| E-glass | **2400–3500** | **70–75** | **2.55** | Low |
| S-glass | **4500–4800** | **85–90** | **2.49** | Medium |
| Carbon (HS) | **3500–7000** | **230–700** | **1.75–1.95** | High |
| Aramid (Kevlar 49) | **2700–3600** | **70–130** | **1.44** | High |
| Basalt | **2800–3200** | **80–95** | **2.65** | Medium |

### 2.1.2 Matrix (Resin Phase)
The **binder and load-transfer medium**. Determines chemical resistance, temperature tolerance, and processing behaviour.

| Resin Type | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Elongation at Break (%) | Marine Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Orthophthalic polyester** | 50–60 | 2–3 | Low (non-structural) |
| **Isophthalic polyester** | 65–80 | 3–4 | Good (general marine) |
| **Vinyl ester** | 80–95 | 4–6 | Very good (hulls, tanks) |
| **Epoxy** | 70–130 | 3–6 | Excellent (high-performance) |
| **Phenolic** | 35–50 | 1–2 | Excellent fire/smoke performance |

### 2.1.3 Fillers & Additives
- **Fillers** (calcium carbonate, silica, alumina trihydrate): reduce cost, control shrinkage, improve fire performance.
- **Additives:** UV stabilisers, pigments, fire retardants, thixotropic agents (prevent resin drain on vertical moulds).

### 2.1.4 Core Material (for sandwich construction)
Used between two GRP skins to increase bending stiffness without adding weight.
- **PVC foam** (most common in marine)
- **Balsa wood** (end-grain)
- **SAN / PET foam**
- **Honeycomb** (Nomex, aluminium)

## 2.2 Rule of Mixtures — The Core Composite Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why":** Because the fibres and matrix act *in parallel* under load (each strain-compatible), their individual contributions add up to give the laminate property. This is the simplest predictive model.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Fibre volume fraction | $V_f = \dfrac{v_f}{v_f + v_m}$ | Ratio of fibre volume to total volume |
| Resin mass fraction | $W_m = \dfrac{\rho_m \, v_m}{\rho_f v_f + \rho_m v_m}$ | Mass-based resin content |
| Longitudinal modulus (Voigt) | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Modulus along fibre direction |
| Transverse modulus (Reuss) | $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Modulus perpendicular to fibres |
| Density of composite | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Rule of mixtures for density |
| Laminate density | $\rho_{lam} = \dfrac{\Sigma \, w_i}{\Sigma \, (w_i / \rho_i)}$ | Mass-weighted density of ply stack |
| Specific strength | $\sigma_t / \rho_c$ | Strength-to-weight ratio |
| Glass-to-resin ratio (by weight) | typically **2:1 to 3:1** for marine laminates | Rule-of-thumb for hand lay-up |

> **Note on table syntax:** All absolute-value bars are written using `\vert` (rendered as $\vert x \vert$) to keep the markdown table parser safe.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

In ship design, the **specific strength** ($\sigma_t / \rho$) and **specific stiffness** ($E / \rho$) are the *figures of merit* because weight is critical for a floating structure. GRP's specific stiffness is comparable to steel, while its specific strength often **exceeds mild steel**, which is why high-speed planing craft and racing yachts almost universally use composites.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The Royal Navy's *Hunt*-class and *Sandown*-class mine hunters are built almost entirely from GRP to reduce magnetic and acoustic signatures — a direct maritime application of composite technology.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Modulus

**Assumption:** Fibres and matrix experience the **same strain** (iso-strain, parallel loading). This is valid for a unidirectional laminate loaded along the fibre direction.

**Step 1 — Force equilibrium:**
The total load on the composite equals the sum of the loads carried by the fibre and matrix phases.

$$
P_c = P_f + P_m
$$

**Step 2 — Express each load using stress × area:**
Let $A_c$, $A_f$, $A_m$ be the cross-sectional areas of composite, fibre, and matrix respectively.

$$
\sigma_c \, A_c = \sigma_f \, A_f + \sigma_m \, A_m
$$

**Step 3 — Convert stresses to strains using Hooke's law** ($\sigma = E \, \varepsilon$):

$$
E_c \, \varepsilon_c \, A_c = E_f \, \varepsilon_f \, A_f + E_m \, \varepsilon_m \, A_m
$$

**Step 4 — Apply iso-strain assumption** ($\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$):

$$
E_c \, \varepsilon \, A_c = \varepsilon \, (E_f \, A_f + E_m \, A_m)
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\varepsilon$ and divide by $A_c$:**

$$
E_c = E_f \dfrac{A_f}{A_c} + E_m \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}
$$

**Step 6 — Replace area ratios with volume fractions** (since fibres are continuous and parallel):

$$
\boxed{\,E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m\,}
$$

This is the **Voigt rule** (or upper-bound rule) for the longitudinal Young's modulus.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Inverse Rule of Mixtures (Transverse Direction)

**Assumption:** Fibres and matrix experience the **same stress** (iso-stress, series loading). This holds in the direction perpendicular to the fibres.

**Step 1 — Compatibility of strains:**

$$
\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m
$$

**Step 2 — Express each strain as stress / modulus:**

$$
\dfrac{\sigma_c}{E_c} = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{E_f} = \dfrac{\sigma_m}{E_m} = \varepsilon
$$

**Step 3 — Force equilibrium (parallel model in transverse direction):**

$$
\sigma_c \, A_c = \sigma_f \, A_f + \sigma_m \, A_m
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon$ and $\sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon$:**

$$
\sigma_c \, A_c = \varepsilon \, (E_f \, A_f + E_m \, A_m)
$$

**Step 5 — Divide both sides by $A_c$:**

$$
\sigma_c = \varepsilon \, \left( E_f \, \dfrac{A_f}{A_c} + E_m \, \dfrac{A_m}{A_c} \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Replace $\sigma_c$ with $E_c \varepsilon$ and divide by $\varepsilon$:**

$$
E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m \quad \text{(same form, but this is NOT the transverse modulus)}
$$

**Correction — for the transverse case the iso-stress condition gives:**

$$
\boxed{\,\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}\,}
$$

This is the **Reuss rule** (or lower-bound rule). It correctly predicts the *much lower* transverse stiffness, which is why quasi-isotropic or woven reinforcements are used for marine laminates that must resist multiaxial loads.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A marine GRP laminate has $V_f = 0.40$ (40% E-glass fibres by volume) in a polyester matrix. Given $E_f = 72$ GPa and $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, calculate the longitudinal and transverse moduli.

**Step 1 — Compute $V_m$:**

$$
V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60
$$

**Step 2 — Longitudinal modulus (Voigt):**

$$
E_L = (72 \times 0.40) + (3.5 \times 0.60)
$$

$$
E_L = 28.8 + 2.1 = 30.9 \; \text{GPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Transverse modulus (Reuss):**

$$
\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{0.40}{72} + \dfrac{0.60}{3.5}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{E_T} = 0.00556 + 0.17143 = 0.17699 \; \text{GPa}^{-1}
$$

$$
E_T = \dfrac{1}{0.17699} \approx 5.65 \; \text{GPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on anisotropy:**

$$
\text{Anisotropy ratio} = \dfrac{E_L}{E_T} = \dfrac{30.9}{5.65} \approx 5.47
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The composite is **5.5× stiffer along the fibres** than across them. This is why marine GRP laminates use **woven roving, chopped strand mat, or multi-axial fabrics** — to approach quasi-isotropic behaviour.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — GRP vs. Steel vs. Aluminium

| Property | Mild Steel | Aluminium 5083 | GRP (typical marine) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density $\rho$ (g/cm³) | 7.85 | 2.66 | 1.50–1.80 |
| Young's Modulus $E$ (GPa) | 200–210 | 70 | 10–25 (laminate) |
| Tensile Strength $\sigma_t$ (MPa) | 400–500 | 270–310 | 150–400 |
| Specific Stiffness $E/\rho$ | 26.7 | 26.3 | 7–14 |
| Specific Strength $\sigma_t / \rho$ | 57–64 | 101–117 | 83–267 |
| Corrosion in seawater | Severe | Moderate (galvanic) | Negligible |
| Repair complexity | Welding (skilled) | Welding (skilled) | Lay-up (semi-skilled) |
| Initial cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Fire performance | Good | Good | Poor (needs fire retardants) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — GRP/FRP Composite System

The following block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a GRP/FRP marine composite — how the four ingredient families interact to form a finished structural laminate.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    raw1[Raw Materials Group 1]:::raw --> fiberProc[Fibre Processing]
    raw2[Raw Materials Group 2]:::raw --> resinProc[Resin Formulation]
    raw3[Raw Materials Group 3]:::raw --> fillerMix[Filler and Additive Mixing]
    raw4[Raw Materials Group 4]:::raw --> corePrep[Core Preparation]

    fiberProc -->|rovings and mats| layup[Layup Stage]
    resinProc -->|catalysed resin| layup
    fillerMix -->|filled resin system| layup
    corePrep -->|foam or balsa| layup

    layup --> cure[Curing and Consolidation]
    cure --> lam[Finished GRP FRP Laminate]

    lam --> app1[Hull Shell Plating]
    lam --> app2[Deck and Superstructure]
    lam --> app3[Internal Bulkheads]
    lam --> app4[Masts Funnels Pipes]

    classDef raw fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#1E3A8A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef app fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Manufacturing Process Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Define Part Geometry and Size] --> B{Vessel Length}
    B -->|Below 10 m| C[Hand Layup or Sprayup]
    B -->|10 to 30 m| D[Vacuum Infusion or RTM]
    B -->|Above 30 m| E[Prepreg or Filament Winding]

    C --> F[Low Tooling Cost]
    D --> G[Medium Tooling Cost]
    E --> H[High Tooling Cost]

    F --> I[Lower Fibre Volume 25 to 35 percent]
    G --> J[Higher Fibre Volume 45 to 55 percent]
    H --> K[Highest Fibre Volume 55 to 65 percent]

    I --> L[Selected for Low Volume and Repairability]
    J --> M[Selected for Production Series and Quality]
    K --> N[Selected for High Performance Naval and Racing]
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Typical Marine GRP Laminate

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["EXTERNAL GELCOAT LAYER 0.4 to 0.6 mm"]
        A1[UV resistant resin with pigment]
    end

    subgraph L2["FIRST REINFORCEMENT LAYER CSM 450 g per sqm"]
        A2[Chopped Strand Mat for print resistance]
    end

    subgraph L3["STRUCTURAL CORE Woven Roving 800 g per sqm"]
        A3[WR 800 in 0 90 orientation]
    end

    subgraph L4["OPTIONAL CORE Balsa or PVC 10 to 25 mm"]
        A4[Sandwich core for stiffness]
    end

    subgraph L5["INTERNAL REINFORCEMENT LAYER WR 600 g per sqm"]
        A5[WR 600 inner skin]
    end

    subgraph L6["INTERNAL GELCOAT OR FLOWCOAT"]
        A6[Resin rich surface for osmosis barrier]
    end

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** A typical small-craft hull laminate is a **5-to-7-ply** stack, with total thickness of **8–20 mm**. The outer **gelcoat** gives the smooth, UV-resistant finish; CSM prevents fibre print-through; woven roving carries the structural loads; the **sandwich core** dramatically increases panel stiffness ($D \propto h^3$) without adding weight.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model 1]`
**Differentiate between GRP and FRP with two specific examples of fibres used in each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **GRP (Glass Reinforced Plastic):** A composite in which the reinforcement phase is *specifically glass fibre* (E-glass, S-glass, or C-glass). The matrix is typically polyester, vinyl ester, or epoxy. Used in small fishing vessels, pleasure craft, mine-countermeasure hulls.
> - **FRP (Fibre Reinforced Plastic):** A *broader category* of composites reinforced with any type of fibre — glass, carbon, aramid, basalt, or natural fibres. Used in racing yachts (carbon), ballistic panels (aramid), and fire-resistant structures (basalt).
> - **Key distinction:** Every GRP is an FRP, but not every FRP is a GRP. FRP is the family; GRP is one member.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model 2]`
**State the role of (a) the matrix and (b) the reinforcement in a fibre-reinforced composite.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - (a) **Matrix:** Holds the fibres together in the desired geometry, transfers applied loads into the fibres through shear, protects the fibres from moisture and chemical attack, and determines the surface finish and chemical resistance of the laminate.
> - (b) **Reinforcement:** Carries the bulk of the tensile and flexural loads, provides stiffness and strength, and dictates the directional mechanical properties of the laminate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set A]`

**(a)** With neat schematic sketches, describe the **constituent materials** used in marine GRP laminates. Compare **polyester, vinyl ester, and epoxy resins** for hull construction in terms of mechanical, chemical, and processing properties. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **Rule of Mixtures** for the longitudinal Young's modulus of a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite. State the assumption on which the derivation is based. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Constituent Materials & Resin Comparison (7 marks)**

A GRP laminate consists of **four ingredient families** as detailed below.

**1. Reinforcement (fibres):** E-glass rovings, chopped strand mat (CSM), woven roving (WR), continuous filament mat (CFM), and surface tissues.

**2. Matrix (resin):** Thermosetting polymer, typically polyester, vinyl ester, or epoxy.

**3. Fillers & additives:** Calcium carbonate, ATH, pigments, UV absorbers, thixotropes.

**4. Core materials (for sandwich panels):** PVC foam, end-grain balsa, SAN foam, honeycomb.

**Resin Comparison Table:**

| Property | Polyester (Ortho) | Polyester (Iso) | Vinyl Ester | Epoxy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile strength (MPa) | 50–60 | 65–80 | 80–95 | 70–130 |
| Elongation (%) | 2–3 | 3–4 | 4–6 | 3–6 |
| Water resistance | Poor | Good | Very good | Excellent |
| Chemical resistance | Low | Moderate | High | Very high |
| Cost | Lowest | Low | Medium | Highest |
| Marine hull use | Non-structural parts | General small craft | **Primary hull resin** | High-performance vessels |

> **Mark distribution:** Identification of 4 ingredient groups = 3 marks; resin comparison table & discussion = 4 marks.

**(b) — Derivation of the Rule of Mixtures (7 marks)**

The full derivation is shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The expected answer outline:

- **Assumption:** Fibres and matrix are perfectly bonded and deform together (iso-strain condition). Load is applied parallel to the fibre direction.
- **Force equilibrium:** $P_c = P_f + P_m$
- **Substitute Hooke's law and iso-strain:** $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$
- **Final boxed result:** $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$

A complete working should include all five steps (force equilibrium → area substitution → Hooke's law → strain compatibility → volume-fraction conversion).

> **Mark distribution:** Correct statement of assumption = 2 marks; force equilibrium and stress substitution = 2 marks; final derivation and boxed answer = 3 marks.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set B]`

**(a)** Explain the **hand lay-up**, **vacuum infusion**, and **filament winding** processes for GRP/FRP boat construction. Compare their achievable fibre volume fraction, tooling cost, and part size. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A marine GRP panel uses 35% E-glass fibres by volume in a polyester matrix. Given $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, calculate: (i) the **longitudinal modulus**, (ii) the **transverse modulus**, and (iii) the **density** of the composite. Comment on the anisotropy. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Manufacturing Processes (7 marks)**

**Hand Lay-up:**
- Fibres are placed by hand into an open mould; resin is brushed or rolled in.
- Pros: Low tooling cost, simple, suitable for large parts and one-off hulls.
- Cons: Labour intensive, air voids, fibre volume limited to **25–35%**, inconsistent quality.

**Vacuum Infusion (VARTM/SCRIMP):**
- Dry reinforcement is laid in the mould, sealed under a vacuum bag, and resin is drawn through by suction.
- Pros: Higher fibre content (**45–55%**), low void content, repeatable quality, better fibre wet-out, lower styrene emissions.
- Cons: Higher tooling cost, more complex consumables (bag, peel-ply, flow media).

**Filament Winding:**
- Continuous rovings are pulled through a resin bath and wound onto a rotating mandrel under controlled tension and pattern.
- Pros: Very high fibre content (**55–65%**), excellent strength-to-weight ratio, suited to axisymmetric parts (pipes, masts, pressure vessels).
- Cons: Limited to convex shapes; expensive mandrels; not suited to complex ship hulls.

| Process | $V_f$ Range | Tooling Cost | Part Size Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hand lay-up | 0.25–0.35 | Low | Very large |
| Vacuum infusion | 0.45–0.55 | Medium | Large (up to 30 m) |
| Filament winding | 0.55–0.65 | High | Small–medium axisymmetric parts |

> **Mark distribution:** Description of 3 processes = 4 marks; comparative table = 3 marks.

**(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

Given: $V_f = 0.35$, $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³.

**Step 1:** $V_m = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65$

**(i) Longitudinal modulus (Voigt):**

$$
E_L = (72 \times 0.35) + (3.5 \times 0.65) = 25.20 + 2.275 = 27.48 \; \text{GPa}
$$

**(ii) Transverse modulus (Reuss):**

$$
\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{0.35}{72} + \dfrac{0.65}{3.5} = 0.004861 + 0.185714 = 0.190575 \; \text{GPa}^{-1}
$$

$$
E_T = \dfrac{1}{0.190575} = 5.25 \; \text{GPa}
$$

**(iii) Density of the composite:**

$$
\rho_c = (2.55 \times 0.35) + (1.20 \times 0.65) = 0.8925 + 0.7800 = 1.6725 \; \text{g/cm}^3
$$

**Anisotropy comment:**

$$
\dfrac{E_L}{E_T} = \dfrac{27.48}{5.25} \approx 5.23
$$

> **Comment:** The panel is more than **5× stiffer along the fibre direction** than transverse to it. For a real boat hull, the laminate must be built up with multiple orientations (e.g., $\pm 45°$ plies, CSM, WR) to give a **quasi-isotropic** response.

> **Mark distribution:** Setting up $V_m$ = 1 mark; $E_L$ = 2 marks; $E_T$ = 2 marks; $\rho_c$ = 1 mark; anisotropy comment = 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in GRP/FRP Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing GRP and FRP:** Many students write as if they are unrelated. *Correct:* GRP is a *sub-class* of FRP. (Lose 1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the iso-strain assumption** when deriving the rule of mixtures — examiners expect this stated explicitly. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 3. **Wrong formula for transverse modulus** — using the *parallel* form ($E_T = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$) instead of the *series* form gives a wildly wrong answer. (Lose 2 marks)
> 4. **Ignoring units in numerical problems** — $E_f$ in GPa must be used consistently with $E_m$ in GPa. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **No mention of quasi-isotropy** in practical applications — a marine laminate is almost never unidirectional, so mentioning woven roving or $\pm 45°$ plies shows engineering maturity. (Lose 1 mark)
> 6. **Skipping the volume-fraction complement:** always write $V_m = 1 - V_f$ before substituting. (Lose 1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — GRP and FRP**

- **GRP ⊂ FRP:** Glass Reinforced Plastic is a *specific* sub-class of Fibre Reinforced Plastic.
- **Two-phase composite:** Fibre (load-bearer) + Matrix (binder & protector). Properties are *better* than either alone.
- **Common marine resins:** Isophthalic polyester (general), vinyl ester (hulls, osmosis resistance), epoxy (high-performance). Phenolic for fire resistance.
- **Common marine fibres:** E-glass (most used, cheap), S-glass (higher strength), carbon (racing/masts), aramid (ballistic), basalt (fire).
- **Rule of mixtures (longitudinal, Voigt):** $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — iso-strain assumption.
- **Inverse rule of mixtures (transverse, Reuss):** $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ — iso-stress assumption.
- **Density rule:** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ (parallel model works for density).
- **Marine laminate glass-to-resin ratio by weight** is typically **2:1 to 3:1** (i.e., $V_f \approx 0.25$–$0.40$).
- **Sandwich construction** uses a low-density core (PVC foam, balsa) to dramatically increase panel flexural rigidity ($D \propto h^3$).
- **Three dominant marine processes:** Hand lay-up (low $V_f$, low cost), vacuum infusion (medium-high $V_f$, better quality), filament winding (high $V_f$, axisymmetric parts only).
- **Key advantages in shipbuilding:** Light weight, corrosion-free, low magnetic/thermal signature, easy repair, formability of complex shapes.
- **Key disadvantages:** Lower stiffness than steel, poor fire performance (needs retardants), susceptible to UV degradation, higher initial cost, complex failure modes (delamination, fibre pull-out, matrix cracking).
- **Quasi-isotropy:** A marine laminate uses **multiple ply orientations** to overcome the 5× anisotropy of a unidirectional composite.
- **Always quote $V_m = 1 - V_f$** as a first step in numerical problems.
- **Always state the assumption** (iso-strain or iso-stress) before applying a rule of mixtures.
- **Typical GRP laminate stack:** gelcoat → CSM → WR → core → WR → CSM → flowcoat.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification Societies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification Societies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 1 — CLASSIFICATION SOCIETIES

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification Society (Definition as per IMO & IACS Framework):**
> A **Classification Society** is a non-governmental, independent technical organization that establishes and applies technical standards (Rules) for the design, construction, and survey of ships and other marine structures, with the objective of **ensuring structural integrity, seaworthiness, and operational safety** of the vessel throughout its operational life.

The classification society acts as a **third-party quality assurance body** that verifies a ship meets the required **Class Rules** and assigns a **Class Notation** reflecting the vessel's compliance level. These societies are governed internationally under the umbrella of the **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)**, founded in **1968** in Hamburg, Germany.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology to Remember:**
> - **Class Rules** → Proprietary technical standards published by each society
> - **Class Notation** → A symbolic code describing the vessel's design, service, and survey status
> - **Class Certificate** → Issued upon satisfactory completion of classification surveys
> - **Statutory Work** → Mandatory work under Flag State / IMO Conventions (delegated to Class)
> - **Classification Work** → Voluntary work related to hull and machinery integrity

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are buying a used **car** from a stranger. You want to be sure that the engine, brakes, frame, and chassis are all in good condition. You would likely take it to a **trusted independent mechanic** for a detailed inspection before paying. The mechanic issues a report telling you what is safe, what needs repair, and what is unsafe.

A **Classification Society does exactly this for ships** — but on a much larger, more rigorous, and lifelong scale:

| Aspect | Car Analogy | Ship Classification Reality |
|---|---|---|
| Independent Inspector | Local garage mechanic | Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, etc. |
| Inspection Report | Vehicle fitness certificate | Classification Certificate |
| Standards Manual | Car service checklist | Class Rules (e.g., DNV Rules) |
| Periodic Checks | Annual car servicing | Annual, Intermediate, Renewal Surveys |
| National Authority | RTO (Regional Transport Office) | Flag State Administration (e.g., Panama, Liberia) |

> [!NOTE]
> **In simple words:** A classification society is the **"trusted family doctor"** of a ship — it watches over the vessel **from cradle (keel-laying) to grave (scrapping)**, ensuring the ship is structurally and mechanically fit for the sea at all times.

---

## 1.3 Why Classification Societies Exist — The Real Engineering Need

Ships operate in one of the most hostile environments known to engineering:

- **Corrosive seawater** attacking steel continuously
- **Dynamic wave loads** causing cyclic fatigue
- **Cargo-induced stresses** (especially tankers and bulk carriers)
- **Extreme weather** (storms, ice, hurricanes)
- **Long service life** of **20–30 years** without major dry-dock intervals

No shipowner can self-certify the safety of a vessel because:
1. **Conflict of interest** exists between profit and safety
2. **International trade** requires a **universally accepted safety stamp**
3. **Insurance underwriters** (P&I Clubs, H&M insurers) demand independent verification

Hence, classification societies provide **technical neutrality, global acceptance, and engineering credibility**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Metrics to Remember:**
> - **IACS Members** control classification of approximately **>90% of world tonnage** (by gross tonnage)
> - A modern classification society employs **>1,000 exclusive surveyors worldwide**
> - Class Rules can exceed **500+ volumes** in their complete library

---

## 1.4 Historical Evolution (Brief Timeline)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{1760} &\rightarrow \text{Lloyd's Register founded (UK) — world's first classification society} \\
\text{1828} &\rightarrow \text{Bureau Veritas (France) established} \\
\text{1860} &\rightarrow \text{Registro Italiano Navale (RINA) — Italy} \\
\text{1862} &\rightarrow \text{American Bureau of Shipping (ABS) — USA} \\
\text{1864} &\rightarrow \text{Nippon Kaiji Kyokai (ClassNK) — Japan} \\
\text{1864} &\rightarrow \text{Det Norske Veritas (DNV) — Norway} \\
\text{1968} &\rightarrow \text{IACS founded in Hamburg, Germany} \\
\text{1994} &\rightarrow \text{ISO 9001 quality certification of IACS QSCS system}
\end{aligned}$$

The classification concept originated in **18th-century London coffee houses** where shipowners and merchants (notably at **Edward Lloyd's Coffee House**) gathered to share information about ship condition and seaworthiness. This informal data-sharing evolved into the world's first formal ship register — **Lloyd's Register** — still operational today.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Classification Society

A classification society operates on a **four-pillar functional model**:

### Pillar 1 — Rule Development
- Each society develops its own proprietary **Class Rules** based on:
  - **Empirical engineering data** (years of failure analysis)
  - **R&D programs** (e.g., DNV's research arm — DNV GL, now DNV)
  - **Feedback from casualties and incidents**
  - **Harmonization with IACS Unified Requirements (URs)**
- Class Rules cover: **Hull, Machinery, Electrical, Automation, Materials, Welding, NDT, Stability, Fire Protection, Pollution Prevention**

### Pillar 2 — Design Approval
- Approval of **design drawings and technical documentation** before construction begins
- Includes: **lines plan, general arrangement, midship section, structural drawings, machinery arrangements, electrical schematics**
- The society issues a **Design Approval Letter** once drawings conform to Rules

### Pillar 3 — Construction Survey
- **Continuous on-site surveillance** at the shipyard during building
- Surveyors inspect: **steel quality, welding procedures, NDT reports, machinery installation, alignment, sea trials**
- Society issues a **Class Certificate** upon successful delivery

### Pillar 4 — In-Service Surveys
- **Lifelong classification surveys** to maintain the Class
- Survey intervals defined by IACS (every year, 2.5 years, 5 years)
- Critical for **insurance validity** and **port state control compliance**

---

## 2.2 IACS — The International Association of Classification Societies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IACS Mission Statement:**
> *"To promote the highest possible standards in maritime safety, environmental protection, and quality assurance through the development and implementation of Unified Requirements, Quality System Certification, and the harmonization of class rules."*

### Structure of IACS

| Body | Function |
|---|---|
| **IACS Council** | Highest decision-making body, comprising CEOs of all member societies |
| **IACS General Committee** | Coordinates technical work and policy |
| **IACS Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)** | Audits member societies for ISO 9001 compliance |
| **IACS Panels & Working Groups** | Develop Unified Requirements (URs) and Unified Interpretations (UIs) |
| **IACS Permanent Secretariat** | Located in **London, UK** |

### Key IACS Documents

- **Unified Requirements (URs)** → Mandatory technical rules applicable to all members
- **Unified Interpretations (UIs)** → Common interpretations of IMO Conventions
- **Procedural Requirements (PRs)** → Standardize survey procedures
- **Recommendation Notes (RNs)** → Advisory guidelines

> [!NOTE]
> **Currently IACS has 12 full members and 1 associate member**, covering virtually the entire world fleet by tonnage.

---

## 2.4 The Shipbuilding Process — Where Classification Intervenes

A typical ship is built through the following sequence, with classification society involvement at **each critical stage**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1} &\rightarrow \text{Owner specifies requirements (tender)} \\
\text{Stage 2} &\rightarrow \text{Yard prepares design and submits to Class for approval} \\
\text{Stage 3} &\rightarrow \text{Class reviews design → Design Approval} \\
\text{Stage 4} &\rightarrow \text{Steel cutting → Keel laying} \\
\text{Stage 5} &\rightarrow \text{Hull construction under Class survey} \\
\text{Stage 6} &\rightarrow \text{Launching} \\
\text{Stage 7} &\rightarrow \text{Outfitting, machinery installation} \\
\text{Stage 8} &\rightarrow \text{Sea trials} \\
\text{Stage 9} &\rightarrow \text{Class Certificate issued} \\
\text{Stage 10} &\rightarrow \text{Operational life → Periodic Class surveys}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 2.5 Class Notation — The "Health Card" of a Ship

A **Class Notation** is an alphanumeric code that describes a vessel's **design, service area, structural features, and survey status**.

### Anatomy of a Class Notation

$$\text{[Class Symbol] [Construction Symbol] [Service Notations] [Additional Notations] [Survey Status]}$$

### Example (Lloyd's Register Notation)

$$\text{100 A 1} \cdot \text{ ESP } \cdot \text{ Bulk Carrier ESP } \cdot \text{ CSR BC-A } \cdot \text{ TMS } \cdot \text{ IWS } \cdot \text{ Unrestricted Navigation}$$

| Component | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **100 A 1** | Lloyd's Register symbol; 100 = 100% hull compliance, A = anchors/life-saving, 1 = well-found and equipped |
| **ESP** | Enhanced Survey Programme (for bulk carriers/tankers) |
| **CSR BC-A** | Common Structural Rules for Bulk Carriers, BC-A category |
| **TMS** | Triton Management System or similar notation |
| **IWS** | In-Water Survey |
| **Unrestricted Navigation** | Allowed to operate worldwide |

---

## 2.6 Classification Surveys — The Lifelong Health Check

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IACS-mandated survey schedule for a ship with 5-year class cycle:**

| Survey | Timing | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **Annual Survey** | Every year | General external inspection, safety equipment, machinery running checks |
| **Intermediate Survey** | Between 2nd & 3rd Annual | More detailed internal inspection, thickness measurements |
| **Special Survey (Class Renewal)** | Every 5 years | Comprehensive — equivalent to rebuilding survey; dry-dock mandatory |
| **Continuous Survey** | Continuous rolling basis | All major items surveyed on a 5-year cycle without a major event |
| **Docking Survey** | At least twice in 5 years | Underwater hull inspection in dry dock |
| **Propeller Shaft Survey** | Every 5 years | Shaft withdrawal, bearing inspection |
| **Boiler Survey** | Periodic | Internal inspection of boilers |
| **Unscheduled / Occasional Survey** | As required | After damage, grounding, major modification |

> [!NOTE]
> **A vessel is said to be "in class" if it holds a valid Classification Certificate and all surveys are up to date. Loss of class can result in:**
> - Insurance becoming void
> - Vessel being detained by Port State Control
> - Cargo charterers refusing to load

---

## 2.7 Statutory vs Classification Work

This is a critical KTU examination distinction:

| Aspect | Classification Work | Statutory Work |
|---|---|---|
| **Governing Body** | Class Society (LR, DNV, ABS) | Flag State (e.g., Panama Maritime Authority) |
| **Voluntary/Mandatory** | Voluntary (but commercially essential) | Mandatory under international conventions |
| **Scope** | Hull, machinery, technical integrity | SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC, Load Lines, Tonnage |
| **Issued By** | Class Society directly | Flag State, often via "Delegation" to Class |
| **Examples** | Hull scantlings, shaft alignment | Load Line Certificate, Safety Construction Certificate |

> [!WARNING]
> **Many classification societies also act as "Recognized Organizations (ROs)"** under formal delegation from Flag States, performing statutory surveys on behalf of the flag. This is called **"delegated statutory work"**.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since Classification Societies is a **conceptual, regulatory topic**, the "formulas" are essentially **standard abbreviations, codes, and structural ratios** that students must memorize:

| Concept | Standard / Code | Key Value |
|---|---|---|
| IACS Survey Cycle | URs Z1 to Z23 | **5 years** for Special Survey |
| Class Renewal Interval | IACS UR Z1 | **Every 5 years (maximum)** |
| ESP (Enhanced Survey Programme) | IACS UR Z10 (tankers), Z11 (bulk carriers) | Mandatory from age **5 years** |
| Thickness Measurement | IACS UR Z7 | Substantial corrosion = **75–100% of original** |
| Annual Survey Window | IACS UR Z1 | ±3 months from anniversary date |
| Dry-dock Interval | IACS UR Z1 | Max **30 months** (underwater inspection in lieu allowed twice in 5 years) |
| Class Society Performance | IACS QSCS | ISO 9001:2015 minimum standard |
| IMO Mandatory Codes | SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW, MLC, Load Lines | Enforced through Flag State |
| CSR Application Threshold | IACS Common Structural Rules | Bulk Carriers ≥ **90 m LOA**, Oil Tankers ≥ **150 m LOA** |
| Class Notation Symbol for "100% Hull" | Varies by society | LR: **100A1**, DNV: **✠1A1**, ABS: **✠A1** |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

For a **Marine / Naval Architect / Ocean Engineer** (which is the B.Tech stream under OENST614), understanding classification societies is essential because:

1. **Design Career** — Every new ship design must be checked against Class Rules from day one
2. **Shipyard Career** — Welding procedures, steel grades, NDT must all be Class-approved
3. **Surveyor Career** — Many KTU graduates join classification societies as **Junior Surveyors / Naval Architects**
4. **Failure Investigation** — Class casualty reports are foundational data for marine research
5. **Insurance & P&I** — Class status directly affects insurance premiums and claims
6. **Legal Liability** — In maritime disputes, Class records are admissible as evidence
