# 📘 3. STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL FRAMEWORK

> [!NOTE]
> This module is **regulatory and procedural** rather than mathematical. Therefore, instead of algebraic derivations, we provide an **exhaustive analytical step-by-step framework** covering the operational logic of classification society intervention, ensuring every KTU question is addressed with full reasoning depth.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Classification Workflow from Design to Operation

Below is the **complete decision-tree** showing how a classification society interacts with a ship across its life:

### Step 1 — Pre-Contract Stage
- Shipowner decides vessel type, size, trading pattern
- Owner selects a **Class Society** (often based on flag state, charterer preference, and yard familiarity)
- Owner requests a **"Quotation for Classification Services"** from the society
- The class society assigns a **Project Manager** and a **Team of Specialists** (Hull, Machinery, Electrical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** A common KTU question asks *"On what basis does a shipowner select a classification society?"* The correct answer includes: **Flag state recognition, charterer requirement, yard relationship, technical reputation, surveyor availability, and historical service quality**.

### Step 2 — Design Approval Phase (Detailed Sub-Steps)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2.1} &\rightarrow \text{Submission of General Arrangement (GA) Plan} \\
\text{Step 2.2} &\rightarrow \text{Submission of Lines Plan (Hull form definition)} \\
\text{Step 2.3} &\rightarrow \text{Submission of Midship Section + Structural Drawings} \\
\text{2.4} &\rightarrow \text{Class verifies scantlings using Rule-based formulae} \\
\text{2.5} &\rightarrow \text{Machinery and Electrical drawings reviewed} \\
\text{2.6} &\rightarrow \text{Stability booklet and damage stability assessed} \\
\text{2.7} &\rightarrow \text{Fire and Safety plans approved} \\
\text{2.8} &\rightarrow \text{Design Approval Letter issued → \textbf{Construction may begin}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write *"Class approves the design"* without specifying that approval covers **structural scantlings, materials, machinery, and stability**. Always list the **scope of design approval** explicitly.

### Step 3 — Construction Phase (Surveyor Activities)

The classification surveyor at the shipyard performs:

| Activity | Frequency | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Steel mill certificate verification | Before cutting | Confirm steel grade & quality |
| Welding procedure qualification | Pre-production | Ensure WPS per Class rules |
| Welder qualification test | Pre-production | Verify welder skill |
| Visual inspection of welds | Continuous | Detect surface defects |
| NDT (UT, RT, MPI, DPI) | As per plan | Detect internal/subsurface defects |
| Alignment of shaft & bearings | At erection stage | Ensure propulsion integrity |
| Sea trials attendance | Pre-delivery | Verify vessel performance |
| Inclining experiment | At completion | Confirm lightship weight & CoG |

### Step 4 — Class Certification at Delivery

Upon successful completion of all surveys, the class society issues:
- **Class Certificate** (primary document)
- **Class Register Entry** (vessel listed in society's register)
- **Initial Class Notation** (defines the level of compliance)
- **Annual Survey Schedule** (prescribed for the next 5 years)

### Step 5 — In-Service Surveys (5-Year Cycle)

The vessel enters a **rolling 5-year cycle**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Year 1} &\rightarrow \text{Annual Survey (AS-1)} \\
\text{Year 2} &\rightarrow \text{Annual Survey (AS-2)} \\
\text{Year 2.5} &\rightarrow \text{Intermediate Survey (IS)} \\
\text{Year 3} &\rightarrow \text{Annual Survey (AS-3)} \\
\text{Year 4} &\rightarrow \text{Annual Survey (AS-4)} \\
\text{Year 5} &\rightarrow \text{Special Survey (SS) — Class Renewal}
\end{aligned}$$

After Year 5, the cycle **restarts with a new Class Notation** and a fresh Special Survey cycle.

### Step 6 — Damage / Casualty Response

If the vessel suffers:
- **Grounding** → Class conducts **damage survey** before re-entry to service
- **Collision** → Class assesses **structural integrity** and may impose **speed/loading restrictions**
- **Fire** → Class verifies **structural residual strength** post-fire
- **Heavy weather damage** → Class surveyor boards at next port to inspect

Class can **withdraw the Certificate** if the vessel is found unsafe — the vessel is then called **"out of class"** until repairs bring it back to standard.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Why a 5-Year Cycle?

> [!NOTE]
> This is an excellent KTU Part-B question: *"Why is the Special Survey interval fixed at 5 years by IACS?"*

### Logical Derivation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Factor 1} &\rightarrow \text{Corrosion rate of mild steel in seawater: } \approx 0.1 \text{–} 0.3 \text{ mm/year (ballast tanks higher)} \\
\text{Factor 2} &\rightarrow \text{Acceptable wastage before renewal: } 25\text{–}30\% \text{ of original scantling} \\
\text{Factor 3} &\rightarrow \text{For a 20 mm plate: usable loss} = 5 \text{–} 6 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Factor 4} &\rightarrow \text{Time to accumulate that loss: } 5 \text{–} 6 \text{ mm} \div 0.2 \text{ mm/yr} = 25\text{–}30 \text{ years (theoretical)} \\
\text{Factor 5} &\rightarrow \text{But fatigue, hotspots, and stress concentrations accelerate local damage} \\
\text{Factor 6} &\rightarrow \text{Safety margin consideration → IACS chose 5 years for early detection} \\
\text{Conclusion} &\rightarrow \text{5 years allows detection before wastage reaches critical limits}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Therefore, the 5-year cycle is a SAFETY MARGIN-DRIVEN ENGINEERING CHOICE, not a regulatory arbitrary value.** Examiners award full marks when both the **empirical corrosion data** and the **risk-based reasoning** are presented.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Functions of a Classification Society (Examiner's Favourite)

This appears in nearly every KTU exam. A complete table:

| S. No. | Function | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Develop and publish Class Rules | Engineering standards for hull, machinery, etc. |
| 2 | Approve designs and plans | Verify drawings against Rules |
| 3 | Survey ships under construction | On-site quality control |
| 4 | Assign Class Notation | Symbolic grade of compliance |
| 5 | Issue Class Certificate | Formal document post-delivery |
| 6 | Conduct periodic surveys | Annual, intermediate, special |
| 7 | Investigate casualties and damage | Failure analysis and advisory |
| 8 | Maintain a Class Register | Public database of classed ships |
| 9 | Perform delegated statutory work | As RO for flag states |
| 10 | Issue statutory certificates | Load Line, SOLAS, MARPOL on delegation |
| 11 | Conduct R&D | Improve Rules with new technology |
| 12 | Provide technical advisory | To owners, yards, designers |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Confusing **Classification Certificate** with **Statutory Certificates**. The Classification Certificate is voluntary and is the property of the class society. Statutory Certificates are mandatory, issued by the flag state (often via class society delegation).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison — Flag State vs Class Society

| Parameter | Flag State | Classification Society |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | National authority (e.g., Panama Maritime Authority) | Private, independent, non-governmental |
| Primary Concern | International convention compliance (IMO) | Technical integrity per own Class Rules |
| Mandatory? | Yes (vessel cannot legally operate without flag) | Voluntary (but commercially essential) |
| Documents Issued | Flag Certificate, Minimum Safe Manning Doc | Class Certificate, Class Register Entry |
| Statutory Survey | Conducted by flag or via delegated RO | Acts as RO when delegated |
| Jurisdiction | National waters of flag state | Worldwide, where the vessel trades |
| IACS Connection | Members are ROs under many flag states | IACS members cooperate with flag states |

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Logic — Loss of Class and Consequences

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Event} &\rightarrow \text{Vessel fails Special Survey} \\
\text{Step 1} &\rightarrow \text{Surveyor issues "Recommendations" with deadline} \\
\text{Step 2} &\rightarrow \text{Owner given time to complete repairs (e.g., 3 months)} \\
\text{Step 3} &\rightarrow \text{If not completed: "Class Suspended"} \\
\text{Step 4} &\rightarrow \text{If still not completed: "Class Withdrawn"} \\
\text{Step 5} &\rightarrow \text{Vessel marked "Out of Class" in public register} \\
\text{Consequence 1} &\rightarrow \text{Insurance (H&M) becomes void} \\
\text{Consequence 2} &\rightarrow \text{P&I cover may be compromised} \\
\text{Consequence 3} &\rightarrow \text{Port State Control may detain the vessel} \\
\text{Consequence 4} &\rightarrow \text{Charterers terminate the contract} \\
\text{Step 6} &\rightarrow \text{Vessel must undergo re-classification survey to be reinstated}
\end{aligned}$$

# 📘 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Role of a Classification Society in the Maritime Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ShipOwner["Shipowner and Operator"]
    FlagState["Flag State Administration"]
    IACS["IACS International Body"]
    ClassSoc["Classification Society LR DNV ABS BV ClassNK"]
    Shipyard["Shipyard Builder"]
    Insurer["Insurance H and M and P and I Club"]
    Charterer["Charterer and Cargo Owner"]
    PortState["Port State Control Authority"]
    Vessel["The Ship Subject of Classification"]

    ShipOwner --> ClassSoc
    ShipOwner --> Insurer
    ShipOwner --> FlagState
    ShipOwner --> Charterer
    FlagState --> ClassSoc
    IACS --> ClassSoc
    ClassSoc --> Shipyard
    ClassSoc --> Vessel
    Insurer --> Vessel
    PortState --> Vessel
    Charterer --> Insurer
    Shipyard --> Vessel
    ClassSoc -.Delegated Statutory Work.-> Vessel
    ClassSoc -.Rules Development.-> IACS
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**

- The **Classification Society** sits at the **center of the maritime trust ecosystem**
- The **Flag State** delegates statutory work to the Class Society (the dotted line)
- The **Shipowner** contracts the Class Society for voluntary classification services
- The **Insurance underwriters** rely on Class certificates as a precondition for cover
- The **Charterer** typically requires the vessel to be in class before chartering
- **IACS** governs the Class Society from a policy and harmonization perspective

---

## 4.2 Lifecycle Workflow — From Keel-Laying to Scrapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Design Stage"] --> B["Class Design Approval"]
    B --> C["Keel Laying"]
    C --> D["Hull Construction Class Survey"]
    D --> E["Launching"]
    E --> F["Outfitting and Machinery"]
    F --> G["Sea Trials Class Attended"]
    G --> H["Class Certificate Issued"]
    H --> I["Annual Survey Year 1"]
    I --> J["Annual Survey Year 2"]
    J --> K["Intermediate Survey"]
    K --> L["Annual Survey Year 3"]
    L --> M["Annual Survey Year 4"]
    M --> N["Special Survey Class Renewal"]
    N --> O{"Vessel Continues in Trade?"}
    O -- Yes --> I
    O -- No --> P["Survey Before Scrapping and Recycling"]
```

**Interpretation:**

- The lifecycle is **cyclic** (annual survey loop) for the operational phase
- **Class Renewal (Special Survey)** is the most rigorous event, repeated every 5 years
- When the vessel reaches end-of-life, a **final pre-scrap survey** is conducted (especially under the **Hong Kong Convention** for safe and environmentally sound ship recycling)

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Survey Decision Tree (In-Service Phase)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start["Vessel in Service"] --> AnnualCheck{"Annual Survey Window Due?"}
    AnnualCheck -- Yes --> Annual["Conduct Annual Survey"]
    Annual -- Pass --> ASValid["Class Maintained AS valid"]
    Annual -- Fail --> Req1["Issue Recommendations"]
    Req1 --> Repair1{"Repairs Completed?"}
    Repair1 -- Yes --> ASValid
    Repair1 -- No --> Susp["Class Suspended"]
    Susp --> WD["Class Withdrawn if not rectified"]
    AnnualCheck -- No --> IntCheck{"Intermediate Survey Due 2.5 yrs?"}
    IntCheck -- Yes --> Int["Intermediate Survey"]
    Int -- Pass --> ISValid["Class Maintained IS valid"]
    Int -- Fail --> Req1
    IntCheck -- No --> SpecCheck{"Special Survey Due 5 yrs?"}
    SpecCheck -- Yes --> Spec["Special Survey Class Renewal"]
    Spec -- Pass --> NewCycle["New 5-Year Cycle Begins"]
    Spec -- Fail --> Req1
    SpecCheck -- No --> AnnualCheck
```

**Interpretation:**
- A **single recommended failure** can lead to class suspension
- **Special Survey** is the most critical gate; failing it means the vessel cannot continue trading
- The system is **self-correcting** with continuous oversight

## 4.5 Schematic — Class Notation Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Notation["Full Class Notation 100 A 1 ESP Bulk Carrier CSR BC-A Unrestricted Nav"]
    Notation --> CS["Class Symbol 100A1"]
    Notation --> CN["Construction Symbols ESP"]
    Notation --> SN["Service Notations Bulk Carrier"]
    Notation --> AN["Additional Notations CSR BC-A"]
    Notation --> SV["Survey Status Unrestricted Nav"]
    CS --> CSexp["Indicates 100% hull compliance and equipped for sea"]
    CN --> CNexp["Enhanced Survey Programme for older vessels"]
    SN --> SNexp["Defines ship type and primary purpose"]
    AN --> ANexp["Common Structural Rules for enhanced design safety"]
    SV --> SVexp["Defines allowed trading area and survey condition"]
```

**Interpretation:**
- A class notation is **layered**, each segment conveying specific information
- **Service notations** (e.g., "Bulk Carrier") are mandatory
- **Additional notations** are optional, indicating higher standards or specialized service

# 📘 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a Classification Society and list any four of its major functions.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Classification Society** is an independent, non-governmental technical organization that establishes and applies engineering Rules for the design, construction, and survey of ships, with the objective of verifying their structural integrity, safety, and seaworthiness throughout their operational life.

**Four major functions:**
1. **Rule Development** – Publication of technical Class Rules for hull, machinery, and electrical systems
2. **Design Approval** – Review and approval of ship plans, drawings, and technical documentation
3. **Construction Survey** – Continuous on-site surveillance during shipbuilding
4. **In-Service Surveys** – Periodic classification surveys (Annual, Intermediate, Special) during operational life
5. **Class Register Maintenance** – Keeping a public register of classed vessels

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for Students:** Writing 4 functions in 3-mark questions is the safe minimum; always write **5 if you remember** to be safe against partial marking.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is IACS? State any two of its objectives.**

**Model Answer:**

**IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** is the international body founded in **1968 in Hamburg, Germany**, comprising the world's leading classification societies. It currently has its permanent secretariat in **London, UK**.

**Two main objectives:**
1. To promote the **highest possible standards** in maritime safety and environmental protection through the development of **Unified Requirements (URs)** and harmonized class rules
2. To establish a **Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)** to ensure member societies maintain ISO 9001-level quality in their services
3. To represent member societies at the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** and coordinate technical positions

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write *"IACS controls all classification societies"* — this is **incorrect**. IACS is an **association**, not a regulator. It coordinates and harmonizes but does not control individual society autonomy.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **role and functions of a classification society** in the maritime industry. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **various surveys conducted on a ship in service** to maintain its classification. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Introduction:**
A classification society is a private, non-governmental, international body that provides independent technical services to shipowners, shipyards, and flag states. Examples include **Lloyd's Register (LR), DNV, ABS, Bureau Veritas (BV), ClassNK**, and others. They are united under the **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)**.

**Detailed Functions:**

**1. Rule Development:**
- Each society develops **Class Rules** governing the design, materials, construction, and maintenance of ships
- Rules are continuously updated based on **R&D, casualty data, and emerging technologies**
- Rules exceed 500 volumes in mature societies

**2. Design Approval:**
- Review of **lines plan, general arrangement, midship section, structural drawings, machinery layouts, and stability booklets**
- Approval ensures the design complies with Rules before construction begins

**3. Construction Survey:**
- Continuous surveyor presence at the shipyard
- Verifies **steel quality, welding procedures, NDT, alignment, and installation quality**
- Attendance at **sea trials** to verify vessel performance

**4. Issuance of Class Certificate:**
- A formal document issued upon successful completion of construction
- Confirms the vessel meets Class Rules
- Vessels without this certificate are considered **"unclassed"**

**5. In-Service Classification Surveys:**
- **Annual Surveys** – General inspection
- **Intermediate Surveys** – More detailed internal checks
- **Special Surveys (Class Renewal)** – Comprehensive 5-yearly survey
- **Docking Surveys** – Underwater hull inspection
- **Continuous Surveys** – Rolling programme
- **Occasional Surveys** – After damage or modification

**6. Maintenance of Class Register:**
- Public database of all classed vessels
- Tracks survey status, recommendations, suspensions, and withdrawals
- Accessible to insurers, charterers, and port authorities

**7. Delegated Statutory Work:**
- As a **Recognized Organization (RO)**, performs statutory surveys on behalf of Flag States
- Issues statutory certificates like **Load Line, SOLAS, MARPOL** on flag state delegation

**8. Casualty Investigation and Advisory:**
- Investigates maritime accidents
- Issues advisories to prevent recurrence
- Publishes **annual casualty and detention statistics**

**9. Research and Development:**
- Operates research arms (e.g., DNV Maritime Research)
- Develops new Rules for alternative fuels, autonomous ships, decarbonization

**Conclusion:**
Classification societies are the **backbone of maritime safety and quality assurance**, providing a globally trusted framework that protects life, property, and the marine environment.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Introduction:**
Once a vessel is in service, classification is maintained through a **systematic schedule of surveys** defined by **IACS Unified Requirements (URs Z1 to Z23)**. These surveys are designed to detect deterioration before it reaches unsafe levels.

**Detailed Description of Surveys:**

**1. Annual Survey (Every Year):**
- Conducted within **±3 months** of the anniversary date of the original Class Certificate
- External inspection of hull, machinery spaces, safety equipment
- Verification of certificates, logs, and operational records
- *Sketch element:* General visual inspection of deck, superstructure, hatch covers, machinery

**2. Intermediate Survey (Between 2nd and 3rd Annual):**
- More detailed internal inspection
- Includes **thickness measurements** of corrosion-prone areas
- Examination of **ballast tanks, cargo holds, machinery foundations**
- *Sketch element:* Thickness measurement using UT gauge on critical structural members

**3. Special Survey / Class Renewal (Every 5 Years):**
- The **most comprehensive survey**, essentially equivalent to rebuilding inspection
- Includes:
  - **Complete internal examination** of all tanks, holds, and spaces
  - **Extensive thickness measurement** as per UR Z7
  - **NDT of critical structures**
  - **Machinery overhaul and testing**
  - **Sea trials** in some cases
- Failure to pass results in **class suspension or withdrawal**

**4. Docking Survey:**
- **At least twice in 5 years**
- Hull inspected in **dry dock** (or in-water survey if approved)
- Propeller, rudder, sea valves, and underwater fittings examined

**5. Propeller Shaft Survey:**
- Every **5 years** (or as per maker's instructions)
- Shaft withdrawn, bearings inspected, oil samples analyzed

**6. Boiler Survey:**
- Internal examination of boilers
- Frequency depends on boiler type and age

**7. Continuous Survey:**
- Alternative to fixed-interval surveys
- All items surveyed in a **rolling 5-year cycle**
- Reduces vessel downtime by spreading survey workload

**8. Occasional Survey:**
- After **damage, grounding, collision, fire, or major modification**
- Class must verify the vessel remains fit for service

**Neat Sketch of Survey Cycle:**

```
       Year 1         Year 2    Year 2.5      Year 3        Year 4         Year 5
        │              │          │            │             │              │
        ▼              ▼          ▼            ▼             ▼              ▼
    ┌──────┐      ┌──────┐   ┌────────┐   ┌──────┐      ┌──────┐     ┌──────────┐
    │  AS  │      │  AS  │   │   IS   │   │  AS  │      │  AS  │     │    SS    │
    │      │ ───► │      │ ─►│        │─►│      │ ───► │      │ ──► │ (Class   │
    └──────┘      └──────┘   └────────┘   └──────┘      └──────┘     │ Renewal) │
                                                                      └──────────┘
                                                                            │
                                                                            ▼
                                                                    (Cycle Repeats)
```

**Conclusion:**
The in-service survey system ensures **continuous oversight** of the vessel, allowing class societies to maintain their approval throughout the ship's life, typically **20–30 years**, and provide assurance to owners, insurers, and flag states.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Differentiate clearly between a **Flag State** and a **Classification Society**, highlighting their roles, document types, and jurisdictional boundaries. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **Class Notation**? Taking Lloyd's Register as an example, explain the meaning of a typical class notation with all its components. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Introduction:**
The maritime regulatory framework rests on a **dual-pillar system**: the **Flag State** (legal/jurisdictional) and the **Classification Society** (technical/engineering). Understanding their differences is essential for every marine engineer and naval architect.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Flag State | Classification Society |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | National government authority (e.g., Panama Maritime Authority, Liberia Registry) | Private, independent, non-governmental body (e.g., LR, DNV) |
| **Primary Concern** | Compliance with international conventions (IMO, SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC) | Compliance with proprietary Class Rules (hull, machinery integrity) |
| **Mandatory?** | Yes – vessel cannot legally operate without flag registration | Voluntary – but commercially essential for insurance and chartering |
| **Jurisdiction** | Limited to vessels registered under that flag, but enforced globally | Worldwide, based on contract with owner |
| **Documents Issued** | Flag Certificate, Minimum Safe Manning Doc, Continuous Synopsis Record | Class Certificate, Class Register Entry, Survey Reports |
| **Statutory Surveys** | Performs or delegates to Recognized Organizations (ROs) | Acts as RO when authorized by Flag State |
| **Example Authorities** | Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands, Singapore, India (DG Shipping) | Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, Bureau Veritas, ClassNK |
| **Examples of Certificates** | Load Line Certificate, Safety Construction Certificate, MARPOL Certificates | Class Certificate, Hull & Machinery Certificate, ESP Compliance |
| **Enforcement Body** | Maritime Administration of the flag state, Coast Guard | IACS coordination; no direct enforcement power |
| **Penalty for Non-Compliance** | Detention, deregistration, criminal liability of master/owner | Class suspension, withdrawal, public deregistration |

**Conclusion:**
The **Flag State is the legal authority** ensuring convention compliance, while the **Classification Society is the technical authority** ensuring engineering integrity. In modern shipping, they work hand-in-hand through the **RO delegation mechanism**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Definition of Class Notation:**
A **Class Notation** is an **alphanumeric symbolic code** assigned by a classification society to a vessel, describing its **design features, service area, structural enhancements, machinery characteristics, and survey status**. It serves as the **"identity card" of a ship's compliance level**.

**Example (Lloyd's Register):**

$$\text{100 A 1} \cdot \text{ ESP } \cdot \text{ Bulk Carrier ESP } \cdot \text{ CSR BC-A } \cdot \text{ Unrestricted Navigation}$$

**Component-by-Component Explanation:**

| Component | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **100 A 1** | **Main Class Symbol** – "100" indicates 100% compliance with LR Hull Rules; "A" denotes the ship is fitted with approved anchors and chain cables; "1" indicates the vessel is well-found, properly equipped, and the cargo hold arrangements are satisfactory |
| **ESP** | **Enhanced Survey Programme** – Applicable to bulk carriers and tankers from age 5 years onwards as per IACS UR Z10 / Z11 |
| **Bulk Carrier ESP** | **Service Notation** – Identifies the vessel's primary purpose; designed and built as a bulk carrier |
| **CSR BC-A** | **Common Structural Rules – Bulk Carrier Category A** – BC-A applies to vessels with all cargo holds suitable for dry cargo; indicates compliance with IACS Common Structural Rules |
| **Unrestricted Navigation** | **Navigation Notation** – The vessel is permitted to operate in all waters worldwide without geographic restriction |
| **(Optional: IWS)** | **In-Water Survey** – Approved for underwater inspection by divers in lieu of dry-docking (under specific conditions) |
| **(Optional: ✠HMC)** | **Hull & Machinery Certification notation** (varies by society) |

**Other Examples (Cross-Society Notation Comparison):**

| Society | Symbol for 100% Hull | Typical Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Lloyd's Register** | 100 A 1 | 100A1 ESP Bulk Carrier CSR BC-A |
| **DNV** | ✠1A1 | ✠1A1 Bulk Carrier ESP CSR BC-A |
| **ABS** | ✠A1 | ✠A1 Bulk Carrier ESP CSR BC-A |
| **Bureau Veritas** | ✠HULL | ✠HULL ✠MACH Bulk Carrier ESP CSR BC-A |
| **ClassNK** | NS | NS (MNS) Bulk Carrier ESP CSR BC-A |

**Importance of Class Notation:**
- **Insurance underwriters** use it to set Hull & Machinery premiums
- **Charterers** require specific notations before loading certain cargoes
- **Port State Control** uses it to assess vessel condition during inspections
- **Owners** use it for marketing the vessel to potential charterers

**Conclusion:**
Class notation is a **compact, internationally understood summary** of a ship's engineering pedigree, and it is the single most important technical identifier for a vessel after its IMO number.

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Marks-Loss Areas in Classification Society Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing IACS with IMO** — IACS coordinates classification societies; **IMO is the United Nations body** for international maritime regulations. These are **two different organizations**.
>
> 2. **Writing "Classification is mandatory"** — Classification is **VOLUNTARY** in principle (Flag State registration is mandatory; Class is private). However, **commercial necessity makes it effectively mandatory**.
>
> 3. **Not naming the Class Society's country of origin** — Examiners often give **partial credit** for writing the founding year and country (e.g., "Lloyd's Register, 1760, UK").
>
> 4. **Skipping the Survey Cycle diagram** — In 7-mark sub-questions on surveys, **a labelled timeline diagram** is worth 2–3 marks by itself. Always draw one.
>
> 5. **Mixing Class Certificate with Statutory Certificate** — Class Certificate is voluntary and owned by the society; Statutory Certificates are mandatory and owned by the Flag State. This distinction is **frequently tested**.
>
> 6. **Forgetting IACS QSCS** — Many students miss that IACS runs a **Quality System Certification Scheme** to maintain ISO 9001 standards among members.
>
> 7. **Not explaining "Out of Class" consequences** — Examiners want to see that you know the **commercial, insurance, and legal consequences** of class withdrawal.

---

## 5.4 📝 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### ✅ Core Concepts to Remember

- [x] **Classification Society** = Independent, private, technical body that ensures ship integrity per its own Rules
- [x] **IACS** = International Association of Classification Societies, founded **1968 in Hamburg**, HQ in **London**
- [x] **Lloyd's Register (1760)** is the **oldest** classification society in the world
- [x] **Class Rules** are proprietary technical standards; **URs** are IACS-harmonized mandatory requirements
- [x] **Class Notation** is the symbolic code describing a vessel's design, service, and survey status
- [x] **Class Certificate** is issued at delivery; renewed every 5 years via **Special Survey**
- [x] **Annual Survey** = Every year (±3 months); **Intermediate Survey** = Between 2nd & 3rd Annual; **Special Survey** = Every 5 years
- [x] **Docking Survey** is required at least **twice in 5 years**
- [x] **Flag State** = Legal/Jurisdictional authority (mandatory); **Class Society** = Technical authority (voluntary but essential)
- [x] **Recognized Organization (RO)** = Class society authorized to act on behalf of a flag state
- [x] **ESP** = Enhanced Survey Programme (mandatory for tankers/bulkers from age 5)
- [x] **CSR** = Common Structural Rules (IACS harmonized structural standards for tankers & bulkers)
- [x] **"Out of Class"** = Vessel has lost its class; insurance may be void, PSC may detain
- [x] **Class is withdrawn** if recommendations are not completed within the deadline
- [x] **QSCS** = IACS Quality System Certification Scheme (ISO 9001-based)

### ✅ Numerical / Standard Values to Memorize

- [x] IACS Special Survey cycle: **5 years**
- [x] Annual Survey window: **±3 months**
- [x] Dry-dock interval: max **30 months** between dockings (in-water survey allowed as alternative)
- [x] ESP starts at vessel age: **5 years**
- [x] CSR threshold: **Bulkers ≥ 90 m LOA**, **Tankers ≥ 150 m LOA**
- [x] Number of IACS members: **12 full + 1 associate**
- [x] IACS founded: **1968**

### ✅ Common Acronyms

- [x] **IACS** – International Association of Classification Societies
- [x] **UR** – Unified Requirement
- [x] **UI** – Unified Interpretation
- [x] **ESP** – Enhanced Survey Programme
- [x] **CSR** – Common Structural Rules
- [x] **RO** – Recognized Organization
- [x] **QSCS** – Quality System Certification Scheme
- [x] **AS / IS / SS** – Annual / Intermediate / Special Survey
- [x] **NDT** – Non-Destructive Testing
- [x] **P&I** – Protection and Indemnity (Insurance)

### ✅ Sample 3-Mark Question Frameworks (For Last-Minute Revision)

- *"Define classification society."* → Definition + 2–3 key functions
- *"List IACS and state 2 objectives."* → Year, place, 2 objectives
- *"What is class notation?"* → Definition + one example with explanation
- *"Name 4 classification societies."* → LR, DNV, ABS, BV (+ year/country = bonus)
- *"What is Special Survey?"* → 5-yearly, comprehensive, condition for class renewal

---

> **END OF MODULE 1 — CLASSIFICATION SOCIETIES**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 Scheme Aligned | NEP 2020 Compliant*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Longitudinal and Transverse Strength, Framing System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Longitudinal and Transverse Strength, Framing System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Longitudinal Strength

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Longitudinal strength refers to the ability of a ship's hull girder to resist bending moments and shear forces acting along its longitudinal axis when supported by waves (or while floating in still water). The entire hull is conceptually treated as a **hollow box girder**, where the deck plating, bottom plating, side shell plating, and the longitudinal bulkheads act together as the flanges and webs of a giant beam floating in the sea.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize):**
> "Longitudinal strength is the strength of the hull as a whole considered as a beam, subjected to bending due to its own weight and the upward hydrostatic pressure of the supporting water."

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a long, hollow cardboard box floating on water. If you lift the box slightly in the middle and push down at the two ends (mimicking a wave passing under the middle), the box will try to bend — the top will stretch and the bottom will compress. A ship behaves exactly the same way! The hull is essentially a **giant floating I-beam** where the deck is the top flange, the bottom plating is the bottom flange, and the side shells are the web. The "force" comes from the difference between the weight of the ship and the buoyancy distribution along its length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants & Standards:**
> - **Standard Wave Length (λ):** Taken as equal to the ship's length **L** for design calculations (per IACS SRA).
> - **Design Wave Height (H):** Typically **L/20** for longitudinal strength assessment.
> - **Section Modulus (Z):** Measured in **cm³** or **m³**.
> - **Permissible Bending Stress:** Typically **175 N/mm² (MPa)** for mild steel hulls as per classification society rules.

---

## 1.2 Transverse Strength

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Transverse strength is the ability of the hull to resist **local loads** acting perpendicular to the ship's longitudinal axis, such as:
- Hydrostatic pressure of water on the bottom and side shell
- Cargo and ballast weight acting downwards
- Sea pressure on side shells during rolling

It is provided primarily by the **transverse framing system**, which consists of frames, floors, beams, brackets, and associated plating that form a closed ring or "belt" around the ship at each station.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a long submarine being divided into many thin circular slices (like a salami). Each slice is held together by ring-shaped ribs. When the submarine dives deep, the water pushes inward from all sides, and these ribs prevent the submarine from being crushed. A ship's hull is similar — at every station, a **transverse frame ring** holds the cross-section in shape against local water pressure and cargo loads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Design Pressure (p) for Transverse Strength:**
> 
> $$p = 10 \cdot h \cdot \rho \cdot g \quad (\text{kN/m}^2)$$
> 
> where **h** = head of water (m), **ρ** = density of sea water (**1025 kg/m³**), **g** = **9.81 m/s²**.

---

## 1.3 Framing System

**Definition:**
A framing system is the **skeletal network** of primary and secondary structural members (frames, beams, girders, stringers, webs, floors, pillars, brackets) combined with the hull plating to form the complete load-bearing structure of a ship.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Main Framing Arrangements:**
> 1. **Transverse Framing System** — Frames spaced closer together, supported by widely-spaced longitudinals. Common in tankers and bulk carriers (bottom and side shell).
> 2. **Longitudinal Framing System** — Longitudinals spaced closer together, supported by widely-spaced transverse webs. Common in the deck and bottom of warships, container vessels, and naval ships.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Analysis of Longitudinal Strength

The ship hull, considered as a beam floating on water, experiences two opposing distributions along its length:

| Force | Distribution | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| **Weight (W)** | Distributed along length (heavy at midship due to machinery) | Downward, fixed |
| **Buoyancy (B)** | Pressure distribution on the underwater hull | Upward, varies with wave |

**Resultant Load Curve (R) = W − B**

When a wave of length equal to the ship's length passes under the ship:
- **Sagging Condition:** Wave crest at amidships → Buoyancy concentrated at middle → Bottom plating in **TENSION**, Deck in **COMPRESSION**.
- **Hogging Condition:** Wave trough at amidships → Buoyancy concentrated at ends → Bottom plating in **COMPRESSION**, Deck in **TENSION**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule of Thumb (IACS):** Maximum bending moment occurs at **amidships** and is approximated by:
> 
> $$M_{max} = W \cdot L / k$$
> 
> where **k ≈ 30 to 40** (empirical constant based on wave theory).

### Key Strength Parameters

- **Moment of Inertia (I):** Sum of (area × distance²) of all continuous longitudinal members about the neutral axis.
- **Section Modulus (Z):** I / y, where y is the distance from the neutral axis to the extreme fiber (top or bottom plating).
- **Bending Stress:** σ = M·y / I = M / Z

## 2.2 Theoretical Analysis of Transverse Strength

The transverse frame acts as a **vertical cantilever** fixed at the bottom (keel) and free at the deck. The frame resists:

1. **Lateral water pressure (p)** on the side shell — triangular pressure distribution with maximum at the bottom.
2. **Cargo pressure on bulkheads** (if applicable).
3. **Inertial loads during rolling** — amplifies pressure on one side.

The frame is analyzed as a beam-column, fixed at the base and subjected to combined bending and axial compression.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress in hull girder | N/mm² |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Bending stress using section modulus | N/mm² |
| 3 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y}$ | Section modulus | cm³ or m³ |
| 4 | $M = W \cdot L / k$ | Maximum wave bending moment (approx.) | kN·m |
| 5 | $p = 10 \cdot h \cdot \rho \cdot g$ | Hydrostatic pressure (sea water, ρ=1.025) | kN/m² |
| 6 | $\lambda = L$ | Design wave length equals ship length | m |
| 7 | $H = L/20$ | Design wave height (standard rule) | m |
| 8 | $\sigma_{perm} = 175$ | Permissible bending stress for mild steel | MPa |
| 9 | $SM_{req} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{perm}}$ | Required section modulus | cm³ |
| 10 | $t = \dfrac{s \cdot \sqrt{p}}{k_{1}}$ | Plate thickness (Isherwood / Llov formula) | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Frame Spacing (s) Values (KTU Memorize):**
> - Transverse frame spacing: **600 mm to 1000 mm**
> - Longitudinal spacing: **300 mm to 500 mm**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bending Moment at Amidships Under Standard Wave

**Given:**
- Ship Length, $L = 120 \text{ m}$
- Ship Displacement, $\Delta = 10{,}000 \text{ tonnes}$
- Design wave length, $\lambda = L = 120 \text{ m}$
- Wave height, $H = L/20 = 6 \text{ m}$
- Empirical constant, $k = 30$

**Find:** Maximum bending moment $M_{max}$ at amidships.

**Step 1:** Convert displacement to weight in Newtons.

$$W = \Delta \cdot g = 10{,}000 \times 1000 \times 9.81$$

$$W = 9.81 \times 10^{7} \text{ N} = 98{,}100 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the empirical formula for maximum bending moment.

$$M_{max} = \frac{W \cdot L}{k}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values (consistent units: kN and m).

$$M_{max} = \frac{98{,}100 \times 120}{30}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the numerator first.

$$M_{max} = \frac{11{,}772{,}000}{30}$$

**Step 5:** Final result.

$$M_{max} = 392{,}400 \text{ kN·m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **total bending moment** (sagging/hogging) under standard wave conditions. To find still-water + wave combined moment, add the still-water bending moment (SWBM) which is typically ±30% of this value.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Section Modulus of a Rectangular Box Section

For a typical hull girder, we use the **simplified rectangular section** approach. Consider a section of breadth **B** and depth **D**.

**Given:**
- $B = 20 \text{ m}$ (moulded breadth)
- $D = 12 \text{ m}$ (moulded depth)
- Plate thickness, $t = 0.020 \text{ m}$ (assumed uniform)
- Neutral axis at mid-depth (symmetric section).

**Step 1:** Moment of inertia of a thin-walled rectangular box about the horizontal neutral axis.

For a thin-walled rectangular section with flange areas $A_f$ (top and bottom) and web areas $A_w$ (sides):

$$I = 2 \cdot A_f \cdot (D/2)^2 + \frac{2 \cdot A_w \cdot D^2}{12}$$

**Step 2:** Compute flange area (deck + bottom plating).

$$A_f = B \cdot t = 20 \times 0.020 = 0.40 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Compute web area (side shell).

$$A_w = D \cdot t = 12 \times 0.020 = 0.24 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the moment of inertia equation.

$$I = 2 \times 0.40 \times (6)^2 + \frac{2 \times 0.24 \times (12)^2}{12}$$

$$I = 2 \times 0.40 \times 36 + \frac{2 \times 0.24 \times 144}{12}$$

$$I = 28.80 + \frac{69.12}{12}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the second term.

$$I = 28.80 + 5.76$$

$$I = 34.56 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 6:** Calculate Section Modulus.

$$Z = \frac{I}{y} = \frac{I}{D/2} = \frac{34.56}{6}$$

$$Z = 5.76 \text{ m}^3 = 5.76 \times 10^6 \text{ cm}^3$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Required Plate Thickness (Isherwood's Formula)

For plate panels supported between stiffeners under uniform pressure:

$$t = s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{1000 \cdot \sigma_{perm}}}$$

**Step 1:** Given data.

- Frame spacing, $s = 0.7 \text{ m}$
- Design pressure at location, $p = 35 \text{ kN/m}^2$
- Permissible stress, $\sigma_{perm} = 175 \text{ N/mm}^2$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$t = 0.7 \times \sqrt{\frac{35}{1000 \times 175}}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the denominator.

$$t = 0.7 \times \sqrt{\frac{35}{175{,}000}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the fraction.

$$t = 0.7 \times \sqrt{0.0002}$$

**Step 5:** Take the square root.

$$t = 0.7 \times 0.01414$$

**Step 6:** Final result.

$$t = 0.00990 \text{ m} \approx 9.9 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Round up to the **next standard plate thickness**: typically **10 mm** is selected.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sagging vs. Hogging

| Parameter | Sagging Condition | Hogging Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Wave Position | Crest at midship | Trough at midship |
| Buoyancy Distribution | Concentrated amidships | Concentrated at ends |
| Weight Distribution | Uniform + concentrated | Uniform + concentrated |
| Bending Moment Shape | Positive (sagging) | Negative (hogging) |
| Deck Plating Stress | Compression | Tension |
| Bottom Plating Stress | Tension | Compression |
| Typical Ship | Loaded vessel in head sea | Light/ballast vessel in following sea |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Components of a Ship's Framing System

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Hull Girder Structure]
    nodeB[Longitudinal Members]
    nodeC[Transverse Members]
    nodeD[Plating]

    nodeE[Deck Plating]
    nodeF[Bottom Plating]
    nodeG[Side Shell Plating]
    nodeH[Longitudinal Stiffeners]

    nodeI[Transverse Frames]
    nodeJ[Web Frames]
    nodeK[Floors]
    nodeL[Deck Beams]
    nodeM[Transverse Bulkheads]

    nodeN[Keel]
    nodeO[Stem]
    nodeP[Stern Frame]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD

    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeH

    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeC --> nodeK
    nodeC --> nodeL
    nodeC --> nodeM

    nodeD --> nodeG

    nodeA --> nodeN
    nodeA --> nodeO
    nodeA --> nodeP
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Load Path in Hull Girder

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Wave Load on Hull]
    nodeB[Bending Moment Distribution]
    nodeC[Midship Critical Section]
    nodeD[Top Flange: Deck Plating]
    nodeE[Bottom Flange: Bottom Plating]
    nodeF[Web: Side Shell Plating]
    nodeG[Section Modulus Z]
    nodeH[Bending Stress Sigma]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Transverse Frame Load Path

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Sea Water Pressure on Side Shell]
    nodeB[Plate Bending Stress]
    nodeC[Frame Spacing Support]
    nodeD[Main Transverse Frame]
    nodeE[Tripping Bracket Support]
    nodeF[Deck Beam Connection]
    nodeG[Side Stringer Intermediate Support]
    nodeH[Floor Connection at Base]
    nodeI[Pillar Load Transfer to Deck]
    nodeJ[Reaction to Ship Structure]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeF --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: Transverse vs. Longitudinal Framing

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Framing System Selection]
    nodeB[Transverse Framing]
    nodeC[Longitudinal Framing]
    nodeD[Mixed Framing]

    nodeE[Frames Spaced 600 to 800 mm]
    nodeF[Longitudinals Spaced 2.5 to 3 m]
    nodeG[Used in Tankers and Bulk Carriers]
    nodeH[Longitudinals Spaced 300 to 500 mm]
    nodeI[Transverse Webs Spaced 3 to 5 m]
    nodeJ[Used in Warships and Container Ships]
    nodeK[Combination in Different Regions]
    nodeL[Bottom Transverse, Deck Longitudinal]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD

    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG

    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeJ

    nodeD --> nodeK
    nodeD --> nodeL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** In a longitudinal framing system, the closer spacing of longitudinal stiffeners provides a larger effective breadth of plating participating in the hull girder action — yielding higher section modulus for the same scantlings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Longitudinal Strength. [3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Longitudinal strength is the ability of the ship's hull girder to resist bending moments and shear forces acting along its longitudinal axis when supported by waves. The entire hull is treated as a hollow box girder floating in water.

The hull experiences two opposing distributions along its length:
1. **Weight (W):** Distributed downward (heavy amidships).
2. **Buoyancy (B):** Distributed upward (varies with wave profile).

When these distributions differ, the hull bends in two critical modes:
- **Sagging:** Wave crest amidships → deck in compression, bottom in tension.
- **Hogging:** Wave trough amidships → deck in tension, bottom in compression.

Maximum bending stress occurs at the **amidships section**, and is given by $\sigma = M/Z$, where **Z** is the section modulus.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Transverse and Longitudinal Framing Systems. [3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Transverse Framing | Longitudinal Framing |
|---|---|---|
| Main stiffener orientation | Vertical (frames) | Horizontal (longitudinals) |
| Frame spacing | 600 to 1000 mm | 300 to 500 mm |
| Stiffener spacing | 2.5 to 3 m (wider) | Larger spacing of webs (3 to 5 m) |
| Effective plating breadth | Smaller | Larger (more efficient) |
| Application | Tankers, bulk carriers | Warships, container ships, naval vessels |
| Section modulus | Lower | Higher for same scantlings |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a)** Explain with neat sketches the different conditions of longitudinal strength — sagging and hogging. Discuss how the bending stress is calculated at the midship section. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definitions (2 Marks):**
- **Sagging:** Wave crest at amidships, buoyancy concentrated at midship, weight distributed throughout → Bending moment is **positive** (concave up).
- **Hogging:** Wave trough at amidships, buoyancy concentrated at ends → Bending moment is **negative** (concave down).

**2. Stress Calculation (3 Marks):**

The hull girder bending stress at any longitudinal fiber is:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

For the extreme fiber (top deck or bottom plate):

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

where the section modulus is calculated using:

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$

**3. IACS Standard Wave (2 Marks):**
The design wave is taken with:
- $\lambda = L$ (wavelength = ship length)
- $H = L/20$ (wave height)

This wave produces the maximum bending moment at amidships, given empirically by:

$$M_{max} = \frac{W \cdot L}{30} \text{ to } \frac{W \cdot L}{40}$$

---

**b)** A ship of length 150 m and displacement 15,000 tonnes is subjected to a standard wave. Calculate the maximum bending moment and the required section modulus if permissible bending stress is 175 MPa. Take k = 30. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L = 150 \text{ m}$
- $\Delta = 15{,}000 \text{ tonnes}$
- $k = 30$
- $\sigma_{perm} = 175 \text{ N/mm}^2$

**Step 1:** Compute the weight.

$$W = 15{,}000 \times 9.81 = 147{,}150 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2:** Maximum bending moment.

$$M_{max} = \frac{W \cdot L}{k} = \frac{147{,}150 \times 150}{30}$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{22{,}072{,}500}{30} = 735{,}750 \text{ kN·m}$$

Convert to N·mm: $M_{max} = 735{,}750 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$

**Step 3:** Required section modulus.

$$Z_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{perm}} = \frac{735{,}750 \times 10^6}{175}$$

$$Z_{req} = 4.204 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^3 = 4.204 \times 10^3 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$\boxed{Z_{req} \approx 4.205 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks - Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**a)** Describe with a neat sketch the components of a typical transverse framing system in a ship's midship region. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

A typical transverse framing system consists of the following components arranged around each station:

**1. Main Transverse Frames (2 Marks):**
Vertical webs spaced 600-1000 mm apart, supporting the side shell plating against lateral water pressure. They act as cantilevers fixed at the floor and free at the deck.

**2. Floors (1 Mark):**
Transverse vertical plates at the bottom connecting port and starboard side frames through the keel. They transmit bottom shell loads and provide a base for the frames.

**3. Deck Beams (1 Mark):**
Horizontal transverse members supporting the deck plating and transferring loads to frames and side stringers.

**4. Side Stringers and Girders (1 Mark):**
Horizontal longitudinal girders at intermediate heights providing intermediate support to long frames, reducing their effective span.

**5. Pillars and Brackets (1 Mark):**
Vertical stanchions in the double bottom and tween decks to transmit vertical loads from decks down to the bottom structure.

**6. Keel, Stem, Stern Frame (1 Mark):**
The longitudinal backbone — flat keel or bar keel at the bottom centreline, stem at the forward end, and sternpost at the aft end.

---

**b)** A side shell plate panel is subjected to a hydrostatic pressure of 40 kN/m². The frame spacing is 0.75 m and the longitudinal spacing is 2.5 m. Using Isherwood's formula with permissible stress of 175 MPa, calculate the required plate thickness. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $p = 40 \text{ kN/m}^2$
- $s = 0.75 \text{ m}$ (frame spacing — the smaller span)
- $\sigma_{perm} = 175 \text{ MPa} = 175 \times 10^3 \text{ kN/m}^2$

**Step 1:** Apply Isherwood's plate thickness formula.

$$t = s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{1000 \cdot \sigma_{perm}}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values (note: σ in MPa, p in kN/m²).

$$t = 0.75 \times \sqrt{\frac{40}{1000 \times 175}}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the fraction.

$$t = 0.75 \times \sqrt{\frac{40}{175{,}000}}$$

$$t = 0.75 \times \sqrt{2.286 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**Step 4:** Take the square root.

$$t = 0.75 \times 0.01512$$

$$t = 0.01134 \text{ m} = 11.34 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5:** Round up to standard plate thickness.

$$\boxed{t_{required} = 12 \text{ mm (next standard size)}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Units in Bending Moment Formula:** Forgetting to convert tonnes to kN (multiply by 9.81) and metres to consistent units. KTU examiners deduct **1 full mark** for unit mismatch.
> 
> 2. **Using Wrong Spacing in Plate Thickness Formula:** Always use the **shorter span** (the spacing between the closer-spaced stiffeners) in Isherwood's formula, not the larger one.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the Neutral Axis:** For an **asymmetric section** (e.g., when the deck area differs from the bottom area), the neutral axis is **NOT at mid-depth**. Marks lost if assumed at mid-depth.
> 
> 4. **Mixing Up Sagging/Hogging:** Remember: **Sagging = Wave crest at midship; Deck in compression.** Many students write the reverse.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the Sketch:** In 7-mark descriptive questions (Part B sub-parts), KTU mandates a labelled sketch. Missing the sketch costs **2 marks minimum**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> 
> - **Longitudinal Strength = Hull girder bending** (whole ship as a beam).
> - **Transverse Strength = Local panel/frame bending** (local cross-section).
> - **Sagging:** Wave crest at midship → Deck compression, Bottom tension.
> - **Hogging:** Wave trough at midship → Deck tension, Bottom compression.
> - **Design wave:** $\lambda = L$, $H = L/20$.
> - **Maximum BM:** $M_{max} = W \cdot L / 30$ (empirical).
> - **Section Modulus:** $Z = I / y$, measured in **m³** or **cm³**.
> - **Bending Stress:** $\sigma = M / Z$, permissible value **175 MPa** for mild steel.
> - **Transverse Frame spacing:** 600–1000 mm.
> - **Longitudinal spacing:** 300–500 mm.
> - **Longitudinal Framing** gives higher Z for the same scantlings.
> - **Transverse Framing** is cheaper and used in tankers/bulk carriers.
> - **Isherwood's Plate Formula:** $t = s \cdot \sqrt{p/(1000 \cdot \sigma_{perm})}$.
> - **Always use the smaller span** (closer stiffener spacing) in plate thickness calculations.
> - **Components of Framing System:** Frames, Floors, Beams, Girders, Stringers, Pillars, Brackets, Keel, Stem, Sternpost, Bulkheads.
> - **Section modulus calculation** must consider the **effective breadth of plating** ($b_{eff}$) participating as flange — typically $b_{eff} = 40t$ or spacing/2, whichever is smaller.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Longitudinal, Transverse and Combined Framing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Framing Systems — The Skeletal Backbone of a Ship

A ship's hull can be visualized as a **large hollow box girder** floating on water. This box girder is reinforced internally by a network of **frames, girders, and stiffeners** that prevent the thin plating from buckling under immense hydrostatic, hydrodynamic, and cargo-induced loads. The geometric arrangement of these frames defines the **framing system** of the ship.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> *Framing system* refers to the orientation and layout of primary stiffening members (frames/web frames/bulkheads) used to support the hull plating against local and global loads. The three principal classifications are **Longitudinal Framing**, **Transverse Framing**, and **Combined (Mixed) Framing**.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Human Rib Cage vs. Spine:**
> Imagine the human torso. The **ribs** run horizontally around the chest — these are like **transverse frames**, providing local stiffness against pressure from the sides. The **spine and sternum** run vertically (length-wise) — these are like **longitudinal girders and keelsons**, providing bending strength along the length.
> A **small, stocky person** may rely mostly on ribs (transverse framing). A **tall, slender person** needs a strong spine (longitudinal framing). A **healthy adult** uses **both** — a combined system. Ships follow the exact same biomechanical logic!

---

### 1.3 The Three Framing Categories — Quick Definition Table

| Framing Type | Frame Orientation | Primary Load Resisted | Typical Ship Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transverse Framing** | Frames run **athwartships** (across the beam) | Local hydrostatic pressure | Small ships, fishing vessels, tugs |
| **Longitudinal Framing** | Frames run **fore-aft** (parallel to CL) | Global hull girder bending | Large tankers, bulk carriers, naval ships |
| **Combined Framing** | Bottom & decks longitudinal; sides transverse | Both local and global loads | Most modern commercial ships |

---

### 1.4 Historical & Design Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why framing selection matters in KTU examinations:**
> The framing system directly influences the **section modulus of the hull girder**, **steel weight**, **fabrication cost**, and the **classification society rule compliance** (IRS, LR, DNV, ABS). Examiners frequently test whether a student can justify a framing choice for a given vessel type.

---

### 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Module 1 Vocabulary)

- **Frame**: A transverse or longitudinal rib supporting the plating.
- **Web Frame**: A deeper, heavier transverse frame at specific spacings.
- **Stringer**: A longitudinal strength member running parallel to the shell.
- **Girder**: A deep longitudinal or transverse plate-and-section member.
- **Primary Member**: Carries global hull girder loads.
- **Secondary Member**: Carries local plate loads to the primary members.
- **Frame Spacing (s)**: Standard distance between adjacent frames — typically **600 mm to 1000 mm** for transverse systems, **larger for longitudinal**.
- **Spacing of Web Frames (l)**: Larger spacing, typically **2.5 m to 4.0 m**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Transverse Framing System

In **transverse framing**, the primary frames (called **ordinary frames** or **side frames**) are arranged **perpendicular to the ship's centerline**, running from the **bilge to the deck** in a continuous arc.

**Structural Hierarchy (Top to Bottom of Hierarchy):**
- Plating → Ordinary Frames → Web Frames → Transverse Girders → Longitudinal Girders → Double Bottom

**Why transverse framing works well for small ships:**
- The **short frame span** between transverse web frames limits local plate deflection.
- **Hull girder bending** stresses are lower in shorter vessels (bending moment ∝ L²).
- Construction is simple, and steelwork is straightforward to fabricate.
- Inspection and maintenance of internal spaces (e.g., fish holds, engine rooms) is easier.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Transverse framing is *local-strength oriented*. It treats the hull as a series of independent ring-shaped structures (cross-sections), each resisting hydrostatic pressure locally. The hull girder bending is carried mainly by the **plating, keel, gunwale, and sheer strake**.

---

### 2.2 Longitudinal Framing System

In **longitudinal framing**, the stiffening members are oriented **parallel to the ship's longitudinal axis**. The ship is effectively divided into **longitudinal strips**, each of which acts as a long beam (stringer) supported by transverse bulkheads and web frames.

**Structural Hierarchy:**
- Plating → Longitudinals → Transverse Web Frames (at ~3 m spacing) → Transverse Bulkheads

**Advantages of Longitudinal Framing:**
1. **Hull girder section modulus is dramatically increased** because the longitudinals contribute to the effective longitudinal strength.
2. **Plate thickness can be reduced** because the unsupported span of plating is along the ship's length (lower pressure variation).
3. **Lighter steel weight** for the same hull girder strength (typically **5–10% savings** for large ships).
4. Superior resistance to **sagging and hogging** bending moments.

**Disadvantages:**
- Complex fabrication (more longitudinals, more weld intersections).
- Higher labor cost.
- **Maintenance difficulty** — internal spaces are subdivided by many longitudinals, hindering access and inspection.
- Cross-flow of bilge water can be obstructed by longitudinals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Takeaway:** Longitudinal framing is *global-strength oriented*. It is mandatory for ships above ~150 m length per most classification society rules (IRS Pt.3, LR Rules).

---

### 2.3 Combined (Mixed) Framing System

The **combined framing system** is the most widely used arrangement in modern commercial shipping. It uses:
- **Longitudinals** in the **bottom plating, deck plating, and sheer strake** (where bending stresses are maximum).
- **Transverse frames** in the **side shell plating** (where hydrostatic pressure dominates).

**Reasoning Behind Combined Framing:**
- The **neutral axis** of the hull girder passes near midship depth. The top and bottom are regions of **maximum bending stress (σ = M·y/I)**, so they need longitudinals for high section modulus.
- The **sides** experience primarily **lateral hydrostatic pressure**, not bending, so transverse frames suffice.

This system achieves an **optimal balance** between strength, weight, and cost.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are derivable and frequently tested in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress in hull girder | N/mm² |
| 2 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | Section modulus of hull girder | m³ or cm³ |
| 3 | $M = \dfrac{w \cdot L^2}{g}$ (or by rule) | Design bending moment (still water + wave) | kN·m |
| 4 | $t_{plate} = \dfrac{s \cdot \sqrt{p}}{k \cdot \sqrt{\sigma_y}}$ | Plate thickness (simply supported) | mm |
| 5 | $p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth h | kN/m² |
| 6 | $l_{eff} = \beta \cdot s$ | Effective span of stiffener (β depends on boundary) | m |
| 7 | $I_{req} = \dfrac{p \cdot s \cdot l^3}{m \cdot \sigma_y}$ | Required moment of inertia of frame | cm⁴ |
| 8 | $w_{frame} = p \cdot s \cdot l$ | Distributed load on frame | kN/m |
| 9 | $A_{sw} = \dfrac{Q \cdot s}{v}$ | Shear area of web (for shear flow) | cm² |
| 10 | $f_{b} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L^2}$ | Euler buckling load of column | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule (for all KTU problems):**
> Always use $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \;\text{t/m}^3$ (seawater density) and $g = 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^2$ unless the problem specifies otherwise. The conversion $1 \;\text{t/m}^3 \times g = 9.81 \;\text{kN/m}^3$ is a KTU-board favorite.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Application & Real-World Utility

| Domain | Application of Framing Knowledge |
|---|---|
| **Ship Design Office** | Selection of framing system during preliminary design |
| **Classification Societies** | Rule-based verification of scantlings (e.g., IRS Pt.3 Ch.4) |
| **Shipyard (Fabrication)** | Sequencing of frame erection, weld planning |
| **Structural Analysis (FEA)** | Modeling longitudinals vs. transverse frames in hull FE models |
| **Repair & Survey** | Determining replacement scantlings and renewal criteria |

> [!NOTE]
> The framing system also affects the **deterioration mechanism**: transverse frames are susceptible to **slotting at weld toes** in way of side shell, while longitudinals are prone to **corrosion at bracket toes** due to trapped moisture — a favorite viva question!

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Section Modulus of a Hull Girder

The hull girder is a **thin-walled box section**. Its section modulus $Z$ is computed by summing the contribution of all longitudinal strength members (plating, longitudinals, girders) about the neutral axis.

**Starting Equation:**

The moment of inertia of a horizontal strip (plating + longitudinals) at vertical position $y$ is given by:

$$
I_{strip} = A_i \cdot y_i^2
$$

where $A_i$ is the cross-sectional area of the $i^{th}$ strip and $y_i$ is the vertical distance from the neutral axis to the centroid of that strip.

**Step 1: Total Moment of Inertia**

$$
I_{NA} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \cdot y_i^2
$$

For continuous plating treated as a vertical strip:

$$
I_{NA} = \int_A y^2 \, dA
$$

**Step 2: Locate the Neutral Axis (Position of Centroid)**

$$
y_{NA} = \dfrac{\sum A_i y_i}{\sum A_i}
$$

This is found by equating the moment of the area above the NA to that below the NA. For a **symmetric** hull girder (idealized), the NA lies at mid-depth, so $y_{max} = D/2$.

**Step 3: Section Modulus**

$$
Z = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{y_{max}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In most KTU numerical problems, the hull is **assumed symmetric** about the horizontal centerline for simplicity. If asymmetry is specified (e.g., double bottom, deckhouse), the examiner will give the location of the NA explicitly.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Plate Thickness Formula (Stiffener-Supported Plate)

A rectangular plate of length $l$ (frame spacing) and width $s$ (stiffener spacing) is subjected to uniform lateral pressure $p$. Using **plate bending theory** with simply supported edges:

**Starting Relation (bending stress in plate):**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{6 \cdot M}{t^2}
$$

The maximum bending moment per unit width for a simply supported rectangular plate under uniform load is:

$$
M = \dfrac{p \cdot s^2}{8} \quad \text{(per unit width, with } s = \text{short span)}
$$

**Step 1: Equate to Allowable Stress**

$$
\sigma_y = \dfrac{6 \cdot p \cdot s^2}{8 \cdot t^2}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $t$**

$$
t^2 = \dfrac{6 \cdot p \cdot s^2}{8 \cdot \sigma_y}
$$

$$
t = s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{6p}{8\sigma_y}} = s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{0.75 \cdot p}{\sigma_y}}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Rule Correction Factor $k$**

Classification societies introduce an empirical factor $k$ (typically $k = 1.0$ to $1.5$ depending on boundary conditions and slenderness) such that:

$$
t = \dfrac{s \cdot \sqrt{p}}{k \cdot \sqrt{\sigma_y}}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU form** that students must memorize.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Logic:** The factor $k$ is **not arbitrary** — it accounts for the ratio of long-to-short span, edge fixity, and slenderness reduction. A higher $k$ (more clamped edges) gives lower thickness.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Required Moment of Inertia of a Frame

A frame is treated as a beam of length $l$ (span between web frames or bulkheads), loaded by a distributed load $w = p \cdot s$ (pressure × frame spacing).

**Step 1: Maximum Bending Moment**

For a simply supported beam under uniform load:

$$
M_{max} = \dfrac{w \cdot l^2}{8} = \dfrac{p \cdot s \cdot l^2}{8}
$$

**Step 2: Bending Stress in Frame Section**

For a section with modulus $Z_f$ and allowable stress $\sigma_y$:

$$
\sigma_y = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z_f} = \dfrac{M_{max} \cdot y}{I_f}
$$

**Step 3: Required Section Modulus**

$$
Z_{req} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_y} = \dfrac{p \cdot s \cdot l^2}{8 \cdot \sigma_y}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to Required Moment of Inertia**

For a standard frame, $Z_f = I_f / c$, where $c$ is the distance from NA to the extreme fiber (≈ web depth/2 for a symmetric section). Letting $m = c$:

$$
I_{req} = \dfrac{p \cdot s \cdot l^3}{m \cdot \sigma_y}
$$

This is the formula used in classification society rule books.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A common student mistake is to use $l$ in meters but convert $I$ in cm⁴ without unit consistency. The KTU board always uses SI (N, mm, MPa). Always convert $p$ to MPa, $s, l$ to mm, and $\sigma_y$ to MPa before computing $I$ in mm⁴.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Framing Systems

| Parameter | Transverse | Longitudinal | Combined |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frame orientation | ⟂ CL | ∥ CL | Mixed |
| Section modulus contribution | Low | High | Very High |
| Steel weight for equivalent strength | Heavy | Lighter | Optimum |
| Fabrication complexity | Simple | Complex | Moderate |
| Inspection accessibility | Good | Poor | Moderate |
| Suitable for ship length (L) | < 100 m | > 150 m | 100–300 m |
| Primary load addressed | Local pressure | Hull girder bending | Both |
| Cost of construction | Low | High | Medium |
| Common examples | Tugs, small craft | VLCCs, naval carriers | Bulk carriers, container ships |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A transverse frame of a small cargo vessel is spaced at $s = 700 \;\text{mm}$, with web frames at $l = 2.8 \;\text{m}$. The frame is loaded by hydrostatic pressure $p = 35 \;\text{kN/m}^2$ at the design draft. Allowable stress $\sigma_y = 235 \;\text{MPa}$. Find the required moment of inertia $I_{req}$ if $c = 90 \;\text{mm}$ (assumed half-depth of frame).

**Step 1: Convert units**

$$
p = 35 \;\text{kN/m}^2 = 0.035 \;\text{MPa}, \quad s = 700 \;\text{mm}, \quad l = 2800 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the formula**

$$
I_{req} = \dfrac{p \cdot s \cdot l^3}{m \cdot \sigma_y}
$$

$$
I_{req} = \dfrac{0.035 \times 700 \times (2800)^3}{90 \times 235}
$$

**Step 3: Compute**

$$
l^3 = 21{,}952{,}000{,}000 \;\text{mm}^3
$$

$$
I_{req} = \dfrac{0.035 \times 700 \times 21.952 \times 10^9}{90 \times 235}
$$

$$
I_{req} = \dfrac{537.8 \times 10^9}{21{,}150}
$$

$$
I_{req} \approx 25.4 \times 10^6 \;\text{mm}^4
$$

**Step 4: Convert to cm⁴ for reporting**

$$
I_{req} = 25.4 \times 10^6 \;\text{mm}^4 = 2540 \;\text{cm}^4
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** $I_{req} \approx 2540 \;\text{cm}^4$ — select a standard bulb flat or T-section of equal or greater inertia from the rule tables.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Framing System Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HULL FRAMING SYSTEMS] --> B[TRANSVERSE FRAMING]
    A --> C[LONGITUDINAL FRAMING]
    A --> D[COMBINED FRAMING]

    B --> B1[Frames run athwartships]
    B --> B2[Frame spacing 600 to 800 mm]
    B --> B3[Web frames at 2.5 to 4.0 m]
    B --> B4[Local load resistance]
    B --> B5[Used in small ships L under 100 m]

    C --> C1[Longitudinals run fore aft]
    C --> C2[Longitudinal spacing 600 to 900 mm]
    C --> C3[Transverse web frames at 2.5 to 4.0 m]
    C --> C4[Global hull girder strength]
    C --> C5[Used in large ships L over 150 m]

    D --> D1[Bottom and deck longitudinal]
    D --> D2[Side shell transverse]
    D --> D3[Optimum strength to weight]
    D --> D4[Modern commercial ships]
    D --> D5[Most common in industry]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Structural Hierarchy in Combined Framing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[SHELL PLATING] --> S1[Longitudinals bottom]
    P1 --> S2[Transverse frames sides]
    P1 --> S3[Longitudinals deck]

    S1 --> W1[Transverse Web Frames]
    S2 --> W1
    S3 --> W1

    W1 --> G1[Transverse Girders]
    G1 --> G2[Centreline Girder and Side Girders]
    G2 --> B1[Double Bottom Floors]
    B1 --> T1[Transverse Bulkheads]
    T1 --> H[HULL GIRDER]
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic Block Flow — Load Path in a Ship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Hydrostatic Pressure] --> L2[Shell Plating]
    L2 --> L3[Frames and Longitudinals]
    L3 --> L4[Web Frames and Girders]
    L4 --> L5[Transverse Bulkheads]
    L5 --> L6[Hull Girder]
    L6 --> L7[Wave and Quasi Static Bending]
```

---

### 4.4 Comparative Mermaid Block — Transverse vs. Longitudinal Arrangement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TRANSVERSE_FRAMING
        T1[Plating] --> T2[Ordinary Frame]
        T2 --> T3[Web Frame at 3 m]
        T3 --> T4[Bulkhead]
    end

    subgraph LONGITUDINAL_FRAMING
        L1[Plating] --> L2[Longitudinal at 700 mm]
        L2 --> L3[Transverse Web Frame at 3 m]
        L3 --> L4[Bulkhead]
    end
```

---

### 4.5 Key Schematic Features of Each System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Cue for Examiners:**
> - In **transverse** framing, the dominant pattern in a midship section view is **closely-spaced vertical lines** (frames) cutting across the hull, intersecting fewer horizontal members.
> - In **longitudinal** framing, the dominant pattern is **closely-spaced horizontal lines** (longitudinals) running the length, with **sparse vertical web frames**.
> - In **combined** framing, the **bottom and deck** show the longitudinal pattern, while the **sides** show the transverse pattern — a hybrid signature.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "framing system" in ship structural design. List the three principal types of framing systems used in ship construction.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **framing system** is the arrangement of primary stiffening members (frames, web frames, girders, longitudinals) used to support the hull plating against local hydrostatic pressure and global hull girder bending loads. The three principal types are:

1. **Transverse Framing** — frames run athwartships (perpendicular to the centerline).
2. **Longitudinal Framing** — frames run fore-aft (parallel to the centerline).
3. **Combined (Mixed) Framing** — longitudinals in bottom and deck, transverse frames in sides.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State any three advantages of longitudinal framing over transverse framing in large ships.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Higher hull girder section modulus** — longitudinals contribute directly to longitudinal strength, increasing $Z$ and reducing bending stress.
2. **Reduced plate thickness** — shorter unsupported span of plating reduces local bending, allowing thinner plates.
3. **Lighter steel weight** for equivalent strength, typically **5–10% savings** in vessels above 150 m length.
4. **Better resistance to hogging and sagging** bending moments in heavy seas.

*(Any three of the above for 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **transverse framing system** and the **longitudinal framing system** used in ship hull construction. Compare their structural hierarchy and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bottom longitudinal of a bulk carrier is spaced at $s = 750 \;\text{mm}$. The bottom plating is subjected to hydrostatic pressure $p = 50 \;\text{kN/m}^2$ at the design draft. The longitudinal is supported by transverse web frames at $l = 3.0 \;\text{m}$ spacing. Using an allowable stress $\sigma_y = 235 \;\text{MPa}$ and assuming simply supported ends, calculate the **required section modulus** $Z_{req}$ of the longitudinal. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):**

- **Transverse Framing (3.5 marks):**
  - Frames are oriented **athwartships**, running from bilge to deck.
  - Hierarchy: Plating → Ordinary frames (every 600–800 mm) → Web frames (every 2.5–4 m) → Transverse bulkheads.
  - Each frame behaves like a vertical beam carrying hydrostatic pressure to the web frames.
  - Used in small ships, tugs, and fishing vessels where hull girder bending is low.

- **Longitudinal Framing (3.5 marks):**
  - Frames (longitudinals) are oriented **parallel to CL**, running the full length of the ship.
  - Hierarchy: Plating → Longitudinals (every 600–900 mm) → Transverse web frames (every 2.5–4 m) → Bulkheads.
  - Longitudinals act as long beams supported by web frames and bulkheads.
  - Used in large ships (tankers, bulk carriers, naval vessels) for higher section modulus.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $s = 750 \;\text{mm}$, $p = 50 \;\text{kN/m}^2 = 0.050 \;\text{MPa}$, $l = 3000 \;\text{mm}$, $\sigma_y = 235 \;\text{MPa}$.

**Step 1: Distributed load on longitudinal**

$$
w = p \cdot s = 0.050 \times 750 = 37.5 \;\text{N/mm}
$$

**Step 2: Maximum bending moment (simply supported beam)**

$$
M_{max} = \dfrac{w \cdot l^2}{8} = \dfrac{37.5 \times (3000)^2}{8}
$$

$$
M_{max} = \dfrac{37.5 \times 9 \times 10^6}{8} = 42.19 \times 10^6 \;\text{N·mm}
$$

**Step 3: Required Section Modulus**

$$
Z_{req} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_y} = \dfrac{42.19 \times 10^6}{235}
$$

$$
Z_{req} \approx 179{,}500 \;\text{mm}^3 = 179.5 \;\text{cm}^3
$$

**Step 4: Selection from Rule Tables**

A standard **bulb flat 120 × 7** (or equivalent T-section) with $Z \geq 180 \;\text{cm}^3$ is selected.

> **Answer:** $Z_{req} \approx 179.5 \;\text{cm}^3$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **combined framing system** with a labeled sketch. State **three reasons** why it is the most widely used arrangement in modern commercial shipping. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A side shell frame of a small cargo ship is to be designed. The frame spacing is $s = 650 \;\text{mm}$ and the web frame spacing is $l = 2.5 \;\text{m}$. The design pressure is $p = 28 \;\text{kN/m}^2$. Take allowable stress $\sigma_y = 235 \;\text{MPa}$ and the distance from the neutral axis to the extreme fiber $c = 75 \;\text{mm}$. Compute the **required moment of inertia** $I_{req}$ of the frame. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):**

**Combined Framing System (4 marks):**
- In combined framing, the **bottom plating, deck plating, and sheer strake** are stiffened by **longitudinals**, while the **side shell plating** is stiffened by **transverse frames**.
- The longitudinals carry the major **hull girder bending stresses** (where $y$ is maximum in the section modulus formula).
- The transverse side frames carry the **lateral hydrostatic pressure** on the side shell.
- The system combines the strengths of both arrangements while mitigating individual weaknesses.

**Three Reasons for Popularity (3 marks):**
1. **Optimum hull girder strength** at minimum steel weight — the longitudinals add to section modulus where bending is critical.
2. **Adequate local side shell strength** — transverse frames efficiently resist lateral pressure.
3. **Lower fabrication and maintenance cost** compared to full longitudinal framing; better access to internal spaces than pure longitudinal.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $s = 650 \;\text{mm}$, $l = 2500 \;\text{mm}$, $p = 28 \;\text{kN/m}^2 = 0.028 \;\text{MPa}$, $\sigma_y = 235 \;\text{MPa}$, $c = 75 \;\text{mm}$.

**Step 1: Apply the required moment of inertia formula**

$$
