I_{req} = \dfrac{p \cdot s \cdot l^3}{c \cdot \sigma_y}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$
I_{req} = \dfrac{0.028 \times 650 \times (2500)^3}{75 \times 235}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $l^3$**

$$
l^3 = 15.625 \times 10^9 \;\text{mm}^3
$$

**Step 4: Numerator**

$$
0.028 \times 650 \times 15.625 \times 10^9 = 284.4 \times 10^9
$$

**Step 5: Denominator**

$$
75 \times 235 = 17{,}625
$$

**Step 6: Final Result**

$$
I_{req} = \dfrac{284.4 \times 10^9}{17{,}625} = 16.14 \times 10^6 \;\text{mm}^4
$$

**Step 7: Convert to cm⁴**

$$
I_{req} = 1614 \;\text{cm}^4
$$

**Step 8: Select a standard T-section or bulb flat with $I \geq 1614 \;\text{cm}^4$ from rule tables.**

> **Answer:** $I_{req} \approx 1614 \;\text{cm}^4$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing $p$ in kN/m² with $s$ and $l$ in mm without converting $p$ to MPa. The KTU board uses SI throughout — always convert $p$ to MPa before substituting into formulas.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $s$ factor in frame design (–1 mark):** Students often write $I_{req} = p l^3 / (c \sigma_y)$ instead of $I_{req} = p \cdot s \cdot l^3 / (c \sigma_y)$. The $s$ (frame spacing) is the tributary width of load — it is **mandatory**.
>
> 3. **Wrong frame orientation in sketches (–1 mark):** Transverse frames should be drawn as **vertical arcs across the section**; longitudinals should be **horizontal lines along the length**. Confusing these in the diagram loses full sketch marks.
>
> 4. **Assuming symmetry incorrectly (–2 marks):** The hull girder is **not perfectly symmetric** due to the double bottom and deckhouse. If the NA location is not given, assume $y_{max}$ = distance from NA to **deck or bottom**, whichever is larger, after computing NA.
>
> 5. **Ignoring effective span factor $\beta$ (–1 mark):** For continuous (not simply supported) frames, $l_{eff} = \beta \cdot l$ where $\beta = 0.7$ to $0.8$. KTU problems usually specify boundary conditions; read carefully.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ✅ **Framing system** = arrangement of primary stiffeners supporting the hull plating.
- ✅ **Three types:** Transverse, Longitudinal, Combined.
- ✅ **Transverse** → local pressure, small ships, simple construction.
- ✅ **Longitudinal** → global bending, large ships, high section modulus, lower steel weight.
- ✅ **Combined** → longitudinals in bottom/deck, transverse in sides; **most common in modern ships**.
- ✅ **Frame spacing (s):** typically 600–1000 mm.
- ✅ **Web frame spacing (l):** typically 2.5–4.0 m.
- ✅ **Section modulus** $Z = I/y_{max}$ — must be maximized for hull girder strength.
- ✅ **Plate thickness:** $t = \dfrac{s \cdot \sqrt{p}}{k \cdot \sqrt{\sigma_y}}$ (simply supported canonical form).
- ✅ **Frame required inertia:** $I_{req} = \dfrac{p \cdot s \cdot l^3}{c \cdot \sigma_y}$ (memorize with units: $p$ in MPa, $s$ and $l$ in mm, $I$ in mm⁴).
- ✅ **Seawater density:** $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \;\text{t/m}^3$ — used in $p = \rho g h$.
- ✅ **Allowable stress for mild steel (AH36/DH36):** $\sigma_y = 235 \;\text{MPa}$ (typical KTU problem value).
- ✅ **Convert $Z$ in mm³ to cm³ by ÷ 1000**; convert $I$ in mm⁴ to cm⁴ by ÷ 10⁴ for standard section tables.
- ✅ **Combined framing** is the industry default because it balances **strength, weight, cost, and inspectability**.
- ✅ **Longitudinal framing mandatory** for ships > 150 m per most classification society rules.
- ✅ **Sketch is essential** — always include labeled cross-section and profile views of the framing arrangement.

---

**End of Module 1 — Framing Systems Notes**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) for OENST614 — Basic Ship Structures*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Structural Components - Primary and Secondary Stiffeners
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basic Structural Components - Primary and Secondary Stiffeners ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In ship structural engineering, **stiffeners** are rolled or fabricated steel sections attached to plating (shell, deck, bulkhead, or platform plating) to enhance its load-bearing capacity, control local buckling, and maintain the intended geometric shape under operational and environmental loads. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition – Stiffener Hierarchy** > Stiffeners are classified into two principal categories based on their **section modulus, load-carrying function, structural continuity, and contribution to global hull-girder strength**: > > 1. **Primary Stiffeners** (also called *Main Strength Members*, *Longitudinals*, or *Heavy Stiffeners*) > 2. **Secondary Stiffeners** (also called *Ordinary Frames*, *Secondary Frames*, or *Light Stiffeners*) ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a thin, flat **cardboard sheet** (representing ship plating). Hold it between your two hands and gently push the center of the sheet — it will bend and buckle almost immediately because the sheet alone is too slender. Now, glue small wooden sticks **vertically across the sheet**, spaced regularly. Push the center again. The sheet becomes *noticeably stiffer* because each stick acts as a "helper bone" sharing the load with the sheet. The sticks are like **secondary stiffeners**. Now, place a much larger, heavier wooden beam running the full length of the cardboard across the middle of the sheet, and connect the smaller sticks to it. The structure becomes *exceptionally rigid* — almost like a mini-bridge. That heavy beam is the **primary stiffener**. The load path is therefore: $$\text{Load on Plating} \longrightarrow \text{Secondary Stiffeners} \longrightarrow \text{Primary Stiffeners} \longrightarrow \text{Global Hull Structure}$$ > [!NOTE] > The classification is **not based on size alone**, but on the **function** the member performs within the load-transfer hierarchy. A stiffener that ultimately transfers its load to the ship's main hull girder is a *primary* stiffener; one that only transfers load to a larger neighboring member is a *secondary* stiffener. ### 1.3 Standard Shipbuilding Metrics - **Stiffener Spacing (Pitch)**: Typical values range from **400 mm to 800 mm** depending on plate thickness and local pressure. - **Stiffener Span**: Distance between supports — bracketed ends for secondary, web frame/girder for primary. - **Material**: **Mild Steel (Grade A, B, D, EH36)** or **High-Tensile Steel** as per IACS / Class Society rules. - **Section Modulus Ratio (Z_primary : Z_secondary)**: Typically **2:1 to 4:1** in standard merchant ship practice. ### 1.4 Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 OENST614) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 1 – Must-Know Topics for KTU University Exam:** > - Differentiation between primary and secondary stiffeners. > - Role of each in hull-girder strength and local plate stiffening. > - Typical cross-sectional profiles (angles, T-bars, bulb plates, flat bars). > - Load path mechanism and force transfer hierarchy. > - Influence on buckling, deflection, and fatigue life. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Primary Stiffeners – Detailed Analysis Primary stiffeners (also termed **main strength members** or **heavy longitudinals / web frames**) are the **principal load-bearing members** of the ship's structural skeleton. They are characterized by: | Property | Description | |---|---| | **Section Size** | Larger cross-section (deeper web, thicker flange) | | **Continuity** | Continuous over several transverse supporting members | | **Contribution to Hull Girder** | **Yes** — integral part of longitudinal strength | | **Scallop Cutouts** | Provided in their webs to allow secondary stiffeners to pass through | | **Spacing (Pitch)** | Wider — typically **700 mm to 900 mm** in modern ships | | **Common Profiles** | T-sections, bulb plates, angles, I-sections, built-up plate girders | | **Examples** | Bottom longitudinals, side shell longitudinals, deck longitudinals, stringer plates, vertical primary members in double bottoms (floors, girders) | | **End Connection** | Welded continuously to plating and supporting transverse web frame | > [!NOTE] > **Why are scallops (lightening holes / bracket cutouts) provided?** > Scallops allow the **secondary stiffeners to pass uninterrupted through the web of the primary stiffener** without requiring termination. This preserves the structural continuity of the secondary member and maintains its load path. Class societies (DNV, LR, ABS) regulate scallop dimensions to avoid excessive stress concentration at cutout edges. ### 2.2 Secondary Stiffeners – Detailed Analysis Secondary stiffeners (ordinary frames, deck beams, transverse frames in side shell) are **lightweight, local plate-stiffening members**. Their principal functions are: - Reduce the effective **slenderness of plating** between primary supports. - Transfer **local pressure loads** (e.g., hydrostatic pressure, cargo pressure, sea pressure) to the primary members. - Prevent **local plate buckling** under combined in-plane and transverse loads. - Maintain the **shape and form** of the hull under hydrostatic and hydrodynamic loading. | Property | Description | |---|---| | **Section Size** | Smaller cross-section (light angles, flat bars, T-bars) | | **Continuity** | **Discontinuous** — ends are bracketed to primary stiffeners | | **Contribution to Hull Girder** | **Negligible** — does not participate in global bending | | **Scallop Cutouts** | **None** — they are terminated and bracketed | | **Spacing (Pitch)** | Closer — typically **500 mm to 700 mm** | | **Common Profiles** | Angle bars (L-sections), T-bars, flat bars | | **Examples** | Ordinary frames, deck beams, side frames, bulkhead stiffeners | | **End Connection** | Bracketed (with end brackets / collar plates) to primary stiffener webs | ### 2.3 The Three-Span Plate Stiffening Model The plating between stiffeners behaves as a **two-way plate** supported on its four edges: - Two opposite edges supported by **secondary stiffeners** (smaller span = *short span*). - The other two edges supported by **primary stiffeners** (larger span = *long span*). This produces the **classical three-span loading model**: $$L_1 \; (\text{secondary span}) \; < \; L_2 \; (\text{primary span})$$ The secondary stiffener reduces $L_1$, and the primary stiffener reduces $L_2$. The combined system behaves far more efficiently than a single-tier stiffening scheme. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following formulas are **frequently tested** in KTU University Examinations for OENST614. Memorize the **form, the variables, and the typical units** given in the table. | Formula | Expression | Application | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Plate slenderness ratio | $\beta = \dfrac{b}{t} \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}}$ | Buckling check of unstiffened/stiffened plating | dimensionless | | Critical buckling stress (simply supported, long plate) | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$ | Plate panel between stiffeners | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | | Effective breadth of plating (attached to stiffener) | $b_{eff} = \dfrac{s}{\psi + (1-\psi)(A_p/A_s)} \le s$ | Section modulus of stiffener with attached plate | mm | | Section modulus of stiffener + attached plate | $Z = \dfrac{I_{stiffener} + I_{plate,eff}}{\text{distance to NA}}$ | Required vs. actual section modulus check | $\text{cm}^3$ | | Minimum required section modulus (Class rules) | $Z_{min} = C_1 \cdot L^2 \cdot s \cdot p$ | Quick sizing of secondary stiffeners | $\text{cm}^3$ | | Hull girder section modulus (longitudinals) | $Z_{deck} = \dfrac{I_{hull}}{\overline{y}_{deck}}$ | Primary stiffener contribution to bending | $\text{cm}^3$ | | Euler buckling load for stiffener between supports | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{(K L)^2}$ | Column-type failure of long primary stiffener | N | | Bending stress in stiffener | $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum fibre stress check | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | | Shear stress in stiffener web | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{A_{web}}$ | Web shear check | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | | Web crippling / local buckling stress | $\sigma_{crip} = 0.9 k_f \sigma_y$ | Thin web under concentrated load | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | > **Legend of Standard Variables** > - $b$ = plate breadth (mm) between stiffeners > - $t$ = plate thickness (mm) > - $\sigma_y$ = yield stress of steel ($\text{N/mm}^2$) > - $E$ = Young's modulus = **$2.05 \times 10^5 \; \text{N/mm}^2$** for ship steel > - $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio = **0.3** > $k$ = buckling coefficient (depends on boundary conditions and aspect ratio) > - $L$ = stiffener span (mm or m depending on rule) > - $s$ = stiffener spacing / pitch (mm) > - $p$ = design pressure ($\text{kN/m}^2$) > - $C_1$ = class rule coefficient (depends on loading scenario) > - $\psi$ = stress ratio at plate edges (longitudinal stress distribution) ### 2.5 Engineering Utility in Real Ship Design | Application | Function of Stiffener System | |---|---| | **Bottom Shell (Singing Tank / Bilge Region)** | Primary longitudinals resist **slamming loads** in slamming conditions; secondary frames distribute local hydrostatic pressure. | | **Side Shell** | Primary longitudinals contribute to **hull girder still-water + wave bending** moment of inertia; secondary frames resist **sea pressure + green-water loads**. | | **Weather Deck** | Primary deck longitudinals + secondary deck beams resist **slamming + wheel load** and provide **torsional rigidity**. | | **Cargo Holds (Bulk Carrier)** | Primary transverse web frames resist **cargo-induced lateral pressure**; secondary side frames provide **local plate stiffening** for the hold. | | **Bulkheads** | Primary vertical stiffeners (girders) divide bulkhead span; secondary horizontal stiffeners reduce plate panel size. | | **Double Bottom** | Primary floors + longitudinal girders + longitudinals form a **grillage system** carrying cargo and sea pressure. | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Why Section Modulus Determines Primary vs. Secondary Status The **section modulus** $Z$ of a stiffener (with attached plating) is the geometric property that quantifies its ability to resist bending. It is derived as follows: **Step 1: Define the second moment of area (moment of inertia) of the composite section.** $$I = I_{stiffener} + I_{plate,eff} + A_{stiffener} \cdot e^2 + A_{plate,eff} \cdot (y_{NA} - y_{plate})^2$$ **Step 2: Identify the neutral axis of the composite section.** The neutral axis is located at distance $y_{NA}$ from a reference (typically the plate mid-surface), found by taking moments of area: $$y_{NA} = \dfrac{A_{stiffener} \cdot \bar{y}_{stiffener} + A_{plate,eff} \cdot \bar{y}_{plate}}{A_{stiffener} + A_{plate,eff}}$$ **Step 3: Define the section modulus at the extreme fibre (typically at the stiffener tip).** $$Z = \dfrac{I}{c}$$ where $c$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the extreme fibre of interest. **Step 4: Calculate the maximum bending stress under design bending moment $M$.** $$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$$ **Step 5: Apply the strength criterion.** For the design to be safe: $$\sigma_{max} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\text{FoS}}$$ where FoS is the **Factor of Safety** (typically **1.0 to 1.1** when working with ultimate strength in LRFD). **Step 6: Compare the resultant $Z$ with the class rule minimum $Z_{min}$.** If $Z_{actual} \geq Z_{min}$ — the stiffener is **adequate**. If $Z_{actual} < Z_{min}$ — the stiffener is **undersized**; either a larger section or a reduced spacing must be adopted. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this derivation matters in KTU exams:** > Class societies prescribe a **minimum required section modulus** $Z_{min}$ as a function of the local design pressure, stiffener span, and stiffener pitch. **Primary stiffeners carry a higher $Z_{min}$** because they are assumed to back-up the secondary members and transfer larger cumulative loads. ### 3.2 Derivation: Critical Buckling Stress of a Stiffened Plate Panel The plate panel between two adjacent stiffeners is treated as an **orthotropic plate** or, in a simpler approach, as an **isotropic plate with simple edge supports** subjected to in-plane compressive loading. **Step 1: Set up the differential equation for plate buckling.** For a thin elastic plate under uniform in-plane compression $N_x$ per unit length: $$D \nabla^4 w + N_x \dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} = 0$$ where $D$ is the **flexural rigidity of the plate**: $$D = \dfrac{E t^3}{12 (1 - \nu^2)}$$ **Step 2: Assume a double-sinusoidal deflection mode (Navier solution).** For simply supported edges at $x=0, a$ and $y=0, b$: $$w(x,y) = \sum_{m=1}^{\infty} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_{mn} \sin\left(\dfrac{m\pi x}{a}\right) \sin\left(\dfrac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$ **Step 3: Substitute the assumed mode into the governing equation.** This yields the **critical buckling load per unit length**: $$N_{x,cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 D}{b^2} \left( \dfrac{m b}{a} + \dfrac{a}{m b} \right)^2$$ **Step 4: Introduce the buckling coefficient $k$.** $$N_{x,cr} = \dfrac{k \pi^2 D}{b^2}$$ where the buckling coefficient is: $$k = \left( \dfrac{m b}{a} + \dfrac{a}{m b} \right)^2$$ **Step 5: Minimize $N_{x,cr}$ with respect to integer $m$ to find the lowest mode.** For long plates ($a \gg b$), the optimal $m = 1$, giving $k \approx 4.0$ for a simply supported long plate. **Step 6: Convert $N_{x,cr}$ to critical stress.** $$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{N_{x,cr}}{t} = \dfrac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$$ **Step 7: Apply the slenderness condition for stiffener design.** The plate must be sufficiently stiff so that: $$\sigma_{actual} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_{cr}}{\gamma_M}$$ This is the fundamental equation that governs **why stiffeners are required** in the first place. Without stiffeners, $b$ would be very large, $\sigma_{cr}$ would collapse, and the plate would buckle. > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** By inserting stiffeners, we **reduce the unsupported breadth** $b$ of the plate. The critical stress scales with $(t/b)^2$ — halving $b$ **quadruples** the buckling strength. ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing Primary vs. Secondary Orientation | Design Criterion | Primary Stiffener Choice | Secondary Stiffener Choice | |---|---|---| | **Global hull-girder bending** | Oriented **longitudinally** (parallel to ship centerline) | N/A — does not contribute | | **Local hydrostatic / cargo pressure** | Transverse (web frames) when spans are large | Orthogonal to primary, spaced at $500-700$ mm | | **Buckling control** | Spaced at $700-900$ mm to limit long-span panel slenderness | Spaced at $500-700$ mm to limit short-span panel slenderness | | **End connection** | **Continuous** through transverse support (with scallop) | **Terminated and bracketed** to primary | | **Section modulus rule** | $Z_{min}^{primary} = \alpha_1 \cdot L_p^2 \cdot s_p \cdot p$ | $Z_{min}^{secondary} = \alpha_2 \cdot L_s^2 \cdot s_s \cdot p$ | | **Typical orientation in side shell** | Longitudinal (lower 0.6L above baseline) | Transverse (frames) | | **Failure mode to prevent** | Tripping, lateral-torsional buckling, column buckling | Local plate panel yielding + inter-frame buckling | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key relationship between $Z_{min}$ for primary and secondary:** > Because primary stiffeners span a **longer distance** $L$ and support **a tributary area** that includes the secondary members, the cubic-to-quadratic dependence of $Z_{min}$ on $L$ makes $Z_{min}^{primary}$ substantially larger than $Z_{min}^{secondary}$. ### 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A side shell panel of a container ship has a design pressure of $p = 35 \; \text{kN/m}^2$. The secondary frames are spaced at $s_s = 600 \; \text{mm}$ and span $L_s = 3.0 \; \text{m}$ between primary web frames. The primary web frames are spaced at $L_p = 4.0 \; \text{m}$ and span vertically between decks. Steel of grade $\sigma_y = 315 \; \text{N/mm}^2$. Compute the **minimum required section modulus** for the secondary frames using the simplified class rule $Z_{min} = C_1 \cdot L^2 \cdot s \cdot p$, with $C_1 = 0.0045$ in consistent units ($\text{m, kN/m}^2$). **Solution:** Given: $L_s = 3.0 \; \text{m}$, $s_s = 0.6 \; \text{m}$, $p = 35 \; \text{kN/m}^2$, $C_1 = 0.0045$ Compute: $$Z_{min} = C_1 \cdot L_s^2 \cdot s_s \cdot p$$ $$Z_{min} = 0.0045 \times (3.0)^2 \times 0.6 \times 35$$ $$Z_{min} = 0.0045 \times 9.0 \times 0.6 \times 35$$ $$Z_{min} = 0.0045 \times 9.0 \times 21.0$$ $$Z_{min} = 0.0045 \times 189.0$$ $$Z_{min} = 0.8505 \; \text{cm}^3 \text{ per cm of plating breadth (class rule units)}$$ In standard SI rule units ($L$ in m, $s$ in m, $p$ in kN/m²), the result is then multiplied by the class rule unit conversion: $$Z_{min} \approx 85 \; \text{cm}^3 \text{ per metre of plating}$$ > **Engineer's Note:** A standard **L 100 × 100 × 10** angle bar stiffener (rolled-steel equal-leg angle) has a section modulus of approximately $Z = 18.0 \; \text{cm}^3$ **alone**, but when combined with $b_{eff}$ of effective plating (typically $0.2 L = 600 \; \text{mm}$ on each side of the stiffener), the **composite section modulus** rises to approximately $Z_{composite} \approx 95 - 110 \; \text{cm}^3$, which exceeds the minimum requirement. This illustrates the principle that **attached plating contributes substantially to the composite section modulus** — a key reason why $b_{eff}$ is included in modern class rules. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stiffener Classification and Load Path ```mermaid graph TD A[External Load
Hydrostatic / Cargo / Sea Pressure] --> B[Ship Plating] B --> C[Secondary Stiffeners
Ordinary Frames / Deck Beams] C --> D[Primary Stiffeners
Longitudinals / Web Frames] D --> E[Transverse Bulkheads / Floors] E --> F[Hull Girder
Global Bending Strength] F --> G[Keel / Hull Foundation] H[Section Modulus Requirement] --> D H --> C I[Span Reduction] --> J[Reduce Plate Slenderness b/t] J --> K[Increase Buckling Resistance] C -.->|Smaller Section| I D -.->|Larger Section| I ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cross-Sectional Profiles Used as Stiffeners ```mermaid graph LR P[Stiffener Section Profiles] --> P1[Flat Bar] P --> P2[Angle Bar L-Section] P --> P3[T-Bar] P --> P4[Bulb Plate] P --> P5[I-Section Rolled] P --> P6[Built-up Plate Girder] P1 --> Q1[Often used as secondary
in light scantlings] P2 --> Q2[Most common for
secondary frames] P3 --> Q3[Used for longitudinals
and primary frames] P4 --> Q4[Common for bottom
and side longitudinals] P5 --> Q5[Used for heavy
primary girders] P6 --> Q6[Used for web frames
and stringers] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical Plate Stiffening Model ```mermaid graph TD S1[Ship Hull Plating] --> S2[Level 1: Primary Stiffener
Longitudinal direction] S2 --> S3[Level 2: Secondary Stiffener
Transverse direction] S3 --> S4[Level 3: Plate Panel
Local slenderness b x s] S2 -.->|Scallop cutouts| S5[Allows secondary to
pass through uninterrupted] S3 -.->|Bracketed ends| S6[Bracket connection
to primary web] style S1 fill:#e6f2ff style S2 fill:#b3d9ff style S3 fill:#80bfff style S4 fill:#4da6ff style S5 fill:#ffe6cc style S6 fill:#ffe6cc ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Typical Mid-Ship Section Indicating Primary and Secondary Members ```mermaid graph TD subgraph MIDSHIP_SECTION[Typical Mid-Ship Transverse Section] A1[Deck Plate + Deck Longitudinals PRIMARY] --> A2[Deck Beam SECONDARY] A2 --> A3[Side Shell Longitudinals PRIMARY] A3 --> A4[Side Frames SECONDARY] A4 --> A5[Bottom Longitudinals PRIMARY] A5 --> A6[Bottom Floors PRIMARY] A6 --> A7[Inner Bottom Plate + Longitudinals PRIMARY] A2 -.->|Bracketed| A3 A4 -.->|Bracketed| A5 end B1[Longitudinal Strength PRIMARY contribution] --> A1 B1 --> A3 B1 --> A5 B1 --> A7 B2[Local Plate Stiffening SECONDARY] --> A2 B2 --> A4 ``` ### 4.5 ASCII Schematic: Scallop Cutout at a Primary Stiffener Web ``` Secondary stiffener (continuous) ─────────────────────────────► ┌─────────────┐ │ ╲ ╱ │ ◄── Scallop cutout (lightening hole) │ ╲ ╱ │ allows secondary stiffener to pass │ ╳ │ │ ╱ ╲ │ │ ╱ ╲ │ Plate ─────┴─────┴──────┴────── │ │ │ Primary stiffener web │ (continuous, full depth) │ │ ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the schematic:** The diagonal lines (`╲`, `╱`, `╳`) represent the **scallop** — a curved cutout in the web of the primary stiffener. The secondary stiffener passes through this cutout without being interrupted. Without the scallop, the secondary stiffener would have to be **terminated and re-attached**, which creates a stress concentration. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, OENST614]** *Distinguish clearly between primary and secondary stiffeners used in ship structures, with two examples of each.* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Primary Stiffener | Secondary Stiffener | |---|---|---| | **Function** | Carries cumulative load from secondary stiffeners and contributes to hull-girder longitudinal strength | Transfers local plate pressure load to the primary stiffener | | **Size / Section Modulus** | Larger (deeper web, thicker flange) | Smaller (lighter section) | | **Continuity** | Continuous, passes through transverse supports via **scallop cutouts** | Discontinuous, ends **bracketed** to primary stiffener | | **Spacing (Pitch)** | Wider (700 – 900 mm) | Closer (500 – 700 mm) | | **Contribution to Global Strength** | Yes (e.g., side longitudinals → hull bending modulus) | Negligible | **Examples:** - *Primary*: Bottom longitudinals, side shell longitudinals, deck longitudinals, web frames, stringer plates, vertical primary members in double bottom. - *Secondary*: Ordinary side frames, deck beams, transverse bulkhead stiffeners, secondary floors in double bottom. *(Marks distribution: 2 marks for clear distinction, 1 mark for examples.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OENST614]** *Why are scallop cutouts (lightening holes) provided in the webs of primary stiffeners, and what precaution must be taken in their design?* **Model Answer:** **Reason for scallop cutouts:** Primary stiffeners run continuously across the ship, and **secondary stiffeners must be allowed to pass through their webs uninterrupted** so that the secondary member's load path is preserved. Without scallops, the secondary stiffener would have to terminate at the primary, breaking its continuity and creating a stress concentration. The scallop also reduces the weight of the primary member. **Precautions in design:** 1. **Size regulation:** Scallop dimensions must conform to **class society rules** (DNV, LR, ABS, BV) — typically limited to a fraction of web depth (e.g., ≤ 0.25 × web depth). 2. **Shape:** Scallops are usually **circular or elliptical (free-cut)**, not square-cornered, to avoid sharp stress raisers. 3. **Edge distance:** Adequate material must remain between the scallop edge and the web/flange junction to prevent **net section failure**. 4. **Fatigue consideration:** In areas of high cyclic stress, scallops are **avoided** or replaced by **collars / ring-stiffened openings** to prevent fatigue crack initiation. *(Marks distribution: 2 marks for the reason, 1 mark for the precaution.)* --- ### PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A – Choice 1 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, OENST614 Module 1]** **(a)** Explain the role of primary and secondary stiffeners in maintaining the structural integrity of a ship's hull. Illustrate with a neat sketch of a typical mid-ship section showing both types. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A bottom shell panel of a bulk carrier has a design pressure of $p = 42 \; \text{kN/m}^2$. The secondary bottom frames are spaced at $s_s = 650 \; \text{mm}$ and span $L_s = 3.5 \; \text{m}$ between primary transverse web frames. The bottom longitudinals are spaced at $s_p = 850 \; \text{mm}$. Using the simplified class rule $Z_{min} = C_1 L^2 s p$ with $C_1 = 0.0048$ (SI units: m, kN/m²), determine: **(i)** the minimum required section modulus of the secondary frame, and **(ii)** the section modulus required for the primary longitudinal if its span is $L_p = 4.5 \; \text{m}$. Comment on the relative magnitudes. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### Part (a) – Roles of Stiffeners (7 marks) **Role of primary stiffeners:** 1. Carry the cumulative load transferred by **all secondary stiffeners** in their tributary area. 2. **Contribute to the hull-girder moment of inertia** $I$ and section modulus $Z$ — i.e., they participate in the **longitudinal bending strength** of the ship. 3. Reduce the **long span** of plating between transverse supports, controlling **column-type buckling** of plating under in-plane hull-girder compression. 4. Examples: bottom longitudinals, side shell longitudinals, deck longitudinals, vertical primary members in double bottoms. **Role of secondary stiffeners:** 1. Reduce the **short span** of plating between primary stiffeners, controlling **local plate buckling** under transverse (out-of-plane) pressure. 2. Transfer local loads (sea pressure, cargo pressure, tank pressure) to the primary members. 3. Maintain geometric shape of plating under hydrostatic and hydrodynamic loads. 4. Examples: ordinary side frames, deck beams, transverse bulkhead stiffeners. **Mid-ship section sketch (textual representation):** ``` ┌─────────────────────────────┐ ← Deck plate │ Deck Longitudinals (P) │ + deck beam (S) ═════════╪═════════════════════════════╪══════ │ │ │ Side Shell Longitudinals (P) │ + Side Frames (S) │ ═════════╪═════════════════════════════╪══════ │ Bottom Longitudinals (P) │ │ + Bottom Floors (P) │ └─────────────────────────────┘ P = Primary S = Secondary ``` *(Sketch: 2 marks; roles: 5 marks — distributed as 2 for primary, 2 for secondary, 1 for the comparison.)* --- #### Part (b) – Section Modulus Computation (7 marks) **Given:** - Secondary: $L_s = 3.5 \; \text{m}$, $s_s = 0.65 \; \text{m}$, $p = 42 \; \text{kN/m}^2$, $C_1 = 0.0048$ - Primary: $L_p = 4.5 \; \text{m}$, $s_p = 0.85 \; \text{m}$, same $p = 42 \; \text{kN/m}^2$, same $C_1 = 0.0048$ **(i) Minimum section modulus of secondary frame:** $$Z_{min,s} = C_1 \cdot L_s^2 \cdot s_s \cdot p$$ $$Z_{min,s} = 0.0048 \times (3.5)^2 \times 0.65 \times 42$$ $$Z_{min,s} = 0.0048 \times 12.25 \times 0.65 \times 42$$ $$Z_{min,s} = 0.0048 \times 12.25 \times 27.3$$ $$Z_{min,s} = 0.0048 \times 334.425$$ $$Z_{min,s} = 1.6052 \;\; (\text{rule unit})$$ Applying the standard class rule unit conversion for steel in shipbuilding (the factor 100 converts to the conventional $\text{cm}^3/\text{m}$ unit): $$Z_{min,s} \approx 160.5 \; \text{cm}^3/\text{m}$$ **Equivalent in absolute units** (assuming the stiffener carries a 1 m breadth of effective plate): $Z_{min,s} \approx 160 \; \text{cm}^3$. **(ii) Minimum section modulus of primary longitudinal:** $$Z_{min,p} = C_1 \cdot L_p^2 \cdot s_p \cdot p$$ $$Z_{min,p} = 0.0048 \times (4.5)^2 \times 0.85 \times 42$$ $$Z_{min,p} = 0.0048 \times 20.25 \times 0.85 \times 42$$ $$Z_{min,p} = 0.0048 \times 20.25 \times 35.7$$ $$Z_{min,p} = 0.0048 \times 722.925$$ $$Z_{min,p} = 3.470$$ $$Z_{min,p} \approx 347 \; \text{cm}^3/\text{m}$$ **Ratio:** $$\dfrac{Z_{min,p}}{Z_{min,s}} = \dfrac{347}{160.5} \approx 2.16$$ **Comment:** The primary longitudinal requires **roughly 2.16 times** the section modulus of the secondary frame. This is because the primary stiffener spans a **larger unsupported length** $L_p$ and supports a **larger tributary breadth** $s_p$. Since the formula scales as $L^2 \cdot s$, the cubic-like dependence on the span amplifies the section modulus requirement for primary members. *(Marks distribution: 2 for setup, 2 for secondary calculation, 2 for primary calculation, 1 for the comment.)* --- #### **QUESTION B – Choice 2 (14 Marks) [Alternative]** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, OENST614 Module 1]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **three-span plate stiffening model** used in ship structural design. Show how the introduction of primary and secondary stiffeners reduces the effective spans of plating. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A square plate panel of side $a = 800 \; \text{mm}$ and thickness $t = 12 \; \text{mm}$ is simply supported on all four edges and subjected to uniform in-plane compression. Steel properties: $E = 2.05 \times 10^5 \; \text{N/mm}^2$, $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_y = 315 \; \text{N/mm}^2$. Take the buckling coefficient for a simply supported square plate under uniform compression as $k = 4.0$. Compute: **(i)** the critical buckling stress, and **(ii)** the slenderness ratio $\beta = \dfrac{b}{t} \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}}$. Comment on whether the panel is **slender** or **stocky**. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### Part (a) – Three-Span Plate Stiffening Model (7 marks) **Concept:** In ship plating, when both primary and secondary stiffeners are present, the plate panel is divided into smaller sub-panels. The classical "three-span model" treats the plating as a continuous plate supported on stiffeners that act as line supports. **Illustration (textual schematic):** ``` Primary Stiffener (P1) → ┌────┬────┬────┬────┐ │ │ │ │ │ Secondary stiffener (S) → │ 1 │ 2 │ 3 │ 4 │ 5 ... (n sub-panels) │ │ │ │ │ Primary Stiffener (P2) → └────┴────┴────┴────┘ ←── s ──→ ←────── L_p ──────→ ``` - $L_p$ = primary span (distance between two primary stiffeners) - $L_s$ = secondary span (distance between two secondary stiffeners) - $s$ = plate breadth between secondary stiffeners (a sub-panel of length $L_s$ × breadth $s$) **Reduction in effective spans:** - **Without stiffeners:** the plate spans the full distance between major supports (e.g., bulkheads) — $L_{total}$ is huge → $\sigma_{cr}$ is very low. - **With primary stiffeners only:** the span reduces to $L_p$ → $\sigma_{cr}$ improves by factor $\left( L_{total}/L_p \right)^2$. - **With primary + secondary stiffeners:** the span further reduces to $L_s$ and breadth to $s$ → $\sigma_{cr}$ improves dramatically. **Plate behaviour:** Each sub-panel behaves as a **two-way plate** (behaving in both $L_s$ and $s$ directions). The smaller span is typically the secondary stiffener pitch $s$, so the plate spans primarily in the $L_s$ direction between secondary stiffeners and in the $s$ direction between the two adjacent secondary stiffeners. *(Marks distribution: 2 for sketch, 3 for explanation, 2 for span reduction discussion.)* --- #### Part (b) – Buckling Stress and Slenderness (7 marks) **Given:** $a = b = 800 \; \text{mm}$, $t = 12 \; \text{mm}$, $E = 2.05 \times 10^5 \; \text{N/mm}^2$, $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_y = 315 \; \text{N/mm}^2$, $k = 4.0$ **(i) Critical buckling stress:** $$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \pi^2 E}{12 (1 - \nu^2)} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$$ Compute $(1 - \nu^2) = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91$. Compute the geometric ratio: $$\dfrac{t}{b} = \dfrac{12}{800} = 0.015$$ $$\left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2 = 0.000225$$ Compute the numerator: $$k \pi^2 E = 4.0 \times (9.8696) \times 2.05 \times 10^5$$ $$k \pi^2 E = 4.0 \times 9.8696 \times 2.05 \times 10^5$$ $$k \pi^2 E = 4.0 \times 2.0233 \times 10^6$$ $$k \pi^2 E = 8.0931 \times 10^6 \; \text{N/mm}^2$$ Compute the denominator: $$12 (1 - \nu^2) = 12 \times 0.91 = 10.92$$ Compute $\sigma_{cr}$: $$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{8.0931 \times 10^6}{10.92} \times 0.000225$$ $$\sigma_{cr} = 7.4113 \times 10^5 \times 0.000225$$ $$\sigma_{cr} = 166.76 \; \text{N/mm}^2$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_{cr} \approx 166.8 \; \text{N/mm}^2}$$ **(ii) Slenderness ratio:** $$\beta = \dfrac{b}{t} \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}}$$ $$\dfrac{b}{t} = \dfrac{800}{12} = 66.67$$ $$\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E} = \dfrac{315}{2.05 \times 10^5} = 1.5366 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}} = \sqrt{1.5366 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0392$$ $$\beta = 66.67 \times 0.0392 = 2.614$$ $$\boxed{\beta \approx 2.61}$$ **Comment (slender or stocky):** - The plate is **moderately slender** because $\beta > 2.0$. - Since $\sigma_{cr} = 166.8 \; \text{N/mm}^2 < \sigma_y = 315 \; \text{N/mm}^2$, the panel will **buckle elastically before yielding**. This is typical of unstiffened ship plating in slender form. - For shipbuilding practice, a plate is considered **slender** if $\beta > 2.0$ and **stocky** if $\beta < 1.0$. Our plate lies in the **moderately slender range**, justifying the **need for stiffeners** to subdivide the panel and prevent elastic buckling. *(Marks distribution: 2 for substitution, 2 for $\sigma_{cr}$ calculation, 1 for $\beta$ calculation, 2 for the comment.)* --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing "primary" with "longitudinal"** — Primary stiffeners are defined by **function** (load hierarchy), not direction. A transverse web frame is a *primary* stiffener because it backs up the secondary side frames. > 2. **Skipping units** — The class rule $Z_{min}$ requires consistent units. Mixing mm with m in $L^2 s p$ produces a wrong answer. KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for unit inconsistency. > 3. **Forgetting $b_{eff}$ in composite section modulus** — The attached plating contributes massively to the section modulus. Students who compute $Z$ of the *bare* stiffener and compare with $Z_{min}$ will conclude "undersized" incorrectly. > 4. **Ignoring Poisson's ratio** in the buckling formula — Substituting $\nu = 0$ instead of $\nu = 0.3$ overestimates $\sigma_{cr}$ by 9.9%. This is a classic trap. > 5. **Treating scallops as "just lightening holes"** — They serve a structural function (continuity of secondary stiffeners). Examiners expect the answer to mention both functions. > 6. **Writing "(1 − 0.3²) = 0.7" instead of "0.91"** — This is the most common arithmetic slip in the buckling derivation. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Quick Facts for Last-Minute Revision:** > > ✅ Stiffeners are classified as **primary** or **secondary** based on **function** in the load hierarchy, not on size or direction alone. > > ✅ **Primary stiffeners** are continuous, larger in section, and contribute to **hull-girder longitudinal strength** (bending moment of inertia $I$ and section modulus $Z$). > > ✅ **Secondary stiffeners** are bracketed, smaller in section, and serve **local plate-stiffening** only. > > ✅ Load path: **Plating → Secondary → Primary → Transverse bulkheads/floors → Hull girder**. > > ✅ **Scallop cutouts** in primary webs allow secondary stiffeners to pass uninterrupted; they are not merely lightening holes. > > ✅ Typical profiles: **L-angles, T-bars, bulb plates, I-sections, built-up plate girders**. > > ✅ Typical spacing: **secondary 500–700 mm**; **primary 700–900 mm**. > > ✅ Critical buckling stress: $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left( \dfrac{t}{b} \right)^2$ — scales as **inverse square of panel breadth**. > > ✅ Ship steel standard: $E = 2.05 \times 10^5 \; \text{N/mm}^2$, $\nu = 0.3$. > > ✅ Class rule minimum section modulus: $Z_{min} = C_1 L^2 s p$ — scales as $L^2$. > > ✅ Composite section modulus (stiffener + effective plating) is **always** greater than the bare stiffener $Z$ — typically 3× to 8× higher. > > ✅ Plates are classified as **slender** if $\beta > 2.0$, **stocky** if $\beta < 1.0$ (slenderness parameter: $\beta = \dfrac{b}{t}\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}}$). > > ✅ **Primary stiffeners span a longer unsupported length** AND support a larger tributary area → hence $Z_{min}^{primary} \gg Z_{min}^{secondary}$ in practical design.

topics.title: Bottom Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bottom Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bottom Construction** is the complete assembly of structural members that form the lowest portion of a ship's hull. It comprises the **keel**, **bottom shell plating**, **floors (transverse members)**, **girders (longitudinal members)**, and, in modern vessels, the **double bottom**. Its primary function is to resist **hydrostatic pressure**, **slamming loads**, **grounding forces**, and **hogging/sagging bending moments**, while providing watertight integrity, cargo/ballast capacity, and structural continuity between the port and starboard sides of the vessel.

The bottom is the most heavily loaded region of a ship's hull because it must withstand:
- Static pressure from the displaced water column
- Dynamic loads from wave slamming
- Local loads from dry-docking (on the keel blocks)
- Global hull girder bending stresses

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine the bottom of a ship as the **"chassis of a car"** — it is the foundational frame that bears all the weight and stress from above while being supported only at specific points (the keel blocks when in dry dock). The keel is like the **central spine**, the floors are the **ribs** that hold the shape, and the girders are the **stringers** that run lengthwise to distribute loads. Adding a **double bottom** is like installing a **reinforced second floor in a basement** — it provides extra safety, creates compartmentalized tanks (for ballast or fuel), and acts as a backup watertight barrier in case the outer hull is breached.

## 1.3 Main Components of Bottom Construction (High-Yield List)

| Component | Orientation | Primary Role |
|-----------|------------|--------------|
| Keel | Longitudinal (centerline) | Backbone / hull girder strength |
| Bottom Shell Plating | Horizontal | Watertight skin, resists pressure |
| Garboard Strake | Outer first strake | Connects bottom plating to keel |
| Floors | Transverse (vertical plates) | Support bottom plating, transfer load |
| Centerline Girder | Longitudinal | Replaces/strengthens keel |
| Side Girders | Longitudinal | Reduce unsupported span of bottom |
| Inner Bottom Plating | Horizontal (top of double bottom) | Second skin, tank top |
| Margin Plate | Connects inner bottom to side shell | Transitions between bottom and side |
| Bilge | Curved transition | Reduces stress concentration |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever the question asks "List the components of bottom construction," always mention the **inner bottom** and **double bottom tanks** because the KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasizes the double bottom system for modern merchant ships.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Logic — Step-by-Step Loading Path

The bottom construction works on a **hierarchical load path** that any KTU board examiner loves to test:

1. **Sea pressure** acts on the outer bottom shell plating.
2. The plating is supported by **floors** (transverse) at regular spacing.
3. The floors transfer their reaction to **girders** (longitudinal), which act as continuous beams.
4. The girders, in turn, deliver the load to the **shell plating of the side** and to bulkheads.
5. The **keel** acts as the main longitudinal strength member, carrying the global bending moment (hogging/sagging) along with the deck and sheer strake.

## 2.2 Types of Keel (Detailed)

- **Bar Keel:** A solid flat-bar section welded/riveted to the centreline of the bottom plating. Common in small to medium vessels.
- **Flat Plate Keel:** A wide, thick plate running along the centreline; forms the **centreline girder** when a double bottom is fitted.
- **Duct Keel:** A hollow keel that doubles as a pipe tunnel (for piping, cables) within the double bottom.
- **Through Plate Keel:** The bottom plating is continuous, with the keel being a thicker centre strip — typical in modern welded ships.

## 2.3 Types of Floors

- **Solid Floors:** Single plate with flanges — used in the machinery space and forward of the collision bulkhead.
- **Bracket Floors:** Plate with triangular brackets on the upper edge — used in cargo holds to allow passage.
- **Open Floors (Perforated):** Plate with lightening holes — used in double-bottom tanks to reduce weight and allow access/ventilation.
- **Web Floors:** Simple plate floors that act as deep girders.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are commonly expected in KTU ESE numericals for OENST614. Memorize the variables, NOT just the formulas.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Unit Convention |
|---|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{d} + t_{cor}$ | **Bottom plating thickness** (s = spacing factor, k = stress coefficient, d = ship depth) | mm, d in m |
| 2 | $t = 0.03L + 5$ (approx.) | Quick rule-of-thumb bottom thickness for ships | mm, L in m |
| 3 | $Z = \dfrac{b \cdot d^{2}}{6}$ | **Section modulus** of a rectangular floor/girder | cm³ |
| 4 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Bending stress in floor/girder (must be < allowable) | N/mm² |
| 5 | $I = \dfrac{b \cdot d^{3}}{12}$ | Moment of inertia of rectangular section | cm⁴ |
| 6 | $p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | **Hydrostatic pressure** on bottom plating | N/m² or kPa |
| 7 | $W = 0.5 \cdot p \cdot s \cdot b^{2}$ | Load per unit length on a plate panel (clamped edges) | N/m |
| 8 | $M_{max} = \dfrac{W \cdot s}{10}$ | Maximum bending moment in a plate panel | N·m |
| 9 | $d_{floor} = (0.05 \text{ to } 0.08) \cdot D$ | Empirical depth of solid floor | mm, D in m |
| 10 | $\text{Floor Spacing} = (0.5 \text{ to } 0.6) \cdot D$ | Typical transverse floor pitch | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** In KTU answer scripts, always carry **units explicitly**. A formula written without units is considered incomplete and loses at least 1 mark in a 7-mark derivation question.

## 2.5 Why the Double Bottom?

- **Safety:** Acts as a redundant watertight barrier after collision/grounding.
- **Strength:** Increases the hull girder's moment of inertia drastically (e.g., by **4 to 5 times**).
- **Capacity:** Provides segregated tanks for **ballast, fresh water, fuel oil, and cargo**.
- **Inspection/Maintenance:** Allows access to the outer bottom for surveys without dry-docking.
- **Permitted Drafts:** Allows IACS double-bottom tank standards (e.g., MARPOL double hull rules for tankers).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In shipyards (e.g., Cochin Shipyard, Hyundai Heavy Industries, Mazagon Dock), the bottom construction sequence is the first structural activity after keel laying. Modern modular construction uses **Mega-blocks** where the entire bottom is pre-fabricated, lifted by gantry cranes, and joined to the bow and stern sections. The same principles govern submarine pressure hulls and offshore platform pontoons.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Section Modulus of a Rectangular Floor Plate

A floor is modeled as a **rectangular plate** standing on edge (web) with two horizontal flanges (face plates). To find the section modulus, we need its moment of inertia.

**Given:**
- Web depth: $d$ (in cm)
- Web thickness: $t_{w}$ (in cm)
- Face plate (flange) breadth: $b$ (in cm)
- Face plate thickness: $t_{f}$ (in cm)

**Step 1: Moment of inertia of the web (rectangle) about the neutral axis (N.A.)**

$$
I_{web} = \frac{t_{w} \cdot d^{3}}{12}
$$

*Text Row:* The web acts as a vertical rectangle. Since the neutral axis passes through the centroid (at mid-depth), the standard formula $I = b h^{3} / 12$ applies directly, with breadth = $t_{w}$ and height = $d$.

**Step 2: Moment of inertia of the face plate about the neutral axis of the floor**

By the parallel axis theorem:

$$
I_{flange} = \left[ \frac{b \cdot t_{f}^{3}}{12} \right] + b \cdot t_{f} \cdot \left( \frac{d}{2} + \frac{t_{f}}{2} \right)^{2}
$$

*Text Row:* The flange centroid is at a distance $(d/2 + t_f/2)$ from the floor N.A. The first term is the self moment of inertia of the flange about its own centroid; the second is the parallel axis shift.

**Step 3: Total moment of inertia**

$$
I_{total} = I_{web} + 2 \cdot I_{flange}
$$

*Text Row:* There are two flanges (top and bottom), so we multiply $I_{flange}$ by 2 and add $I_{web}$.

**Step 4: Section modulus**

$$
Z = \frac{I_{total}}{(d/2) + t_{f}}
$$

*Text Row:* Section modulus = moment of inertia ÷ distance from N.A. to the outermost fibre. The outermost fibre is at the top of the upper flange.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bottom Plating Thickness from Plate Theory

A bottom plate panel is supported by floors on both sides (transverse edges) and by longitudinal stiffeners (girders) on the other edges. Modeling the plate as a simply-supported rectangular plate under uniform pressure $p$:

**Step 1: Plate panel dimensions**
- Short span: $s$ (between floors, typically 0.5–0.6 D in m)
- Long span: $b$ (between girders, typically 2–3 m)

**Step 2: Equivalent line load per unit width**

$$
W = p \cdot s \quad [\text{N/m}]
$$

*Text Row:* Multiply the pressure by the supported span to convert to a line load on the plate strip.

**Step 3: Maximum bending moment (clamped plate approximation)**

For a long narrow plate ($b \gg s$), the moment in the short-span direction dominates:

$$
M_{max} = \frac{W \cdot s}{10} \quad [\text{N·m per m width}]
$$

*Text Row:* This is the classical plate-bending coefficient (0.1) for a long plate clamped on two long edges and free on the short edges.

**Step 4: Required section modulus per metre width**

$$
Z_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}}
$$

**Step 5: Solve for thickness $t$**

For a 1 m wide strip, $Z = t^{2} / 6$ (per metre width). Substituting:

$$
\frac{t^{2}}{6} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \sqrt{\frac{6 \cdot M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}}}
$$

**Step 6: Express in KTU-style empirical form**

Combining the substitution of $M_{max} = p s^{2} / 10$ into the above gives the **KTU standard rule**:

$$
t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{d} + t_{cor}
$$

where $k$ absorbs the stress and pressure terms and $d$ is the ship depth. (Classification society rules — Lloyd's, BV, DNV — use this format.)

## 3.3 Worked Numerical (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A bulk carrier has a **moulded depth** $D = 12$ m, **floor spacing** $s = 0.6$ m, **allowable stress** $\sigma_{allow} = 180$ MPa, and **sea pressure factor** $p = 0.012$ MPa/m. Compute the bottom plate thickness using the empirical rule $t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{d}$, with $k = 0.025$ for this region, and a **corrosion allowance** $t_{cor} = 2$ mm.

**Solution:**

$$
t = 0.6 \times 0.025 \times \sqrt{12} + 2
$$

$$
\sqrt{12} = 3.464
$$

$$
t = 0.015 \times 3.464 + 2 = 0.05196 + 2
$$

$$
\boxed{t \approx 2.052 \text{ mm (add standard rounding to 4–6 mm in practice)}}
$$

*Text Row:* In real practice, the calculated thickness is rounded **up** to the next standard plate gauge (typically 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12 mm). The KTU board typically awards full marks if the student shows the calculation and then states "rounded up to the next standard plate thickness."

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting the corrosion allowance, or using $D$ (moulded depth) instead of $\sqrt{d}$. Always verify the variable definition in the formula.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical Load Path in Bottom Construction

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Sea Pressure Load] --> B[Bottom Shell Plating]
    B --> C[Garboard Strake]
    B --> D[Transverse Floors]
    B --> E[Inner Bottom Plating - Double Hull]
    D --> F[Side Girders Longitudinal]
    D --> G[Centreline Keel Girder]
    E --> H[Margin Plate]
    E --> I[Double Bottom Tanks]
    G --> J[Hull Girder Bending Strength]
    F --> J
    H --> J
    I --> K[Ballast and Segregated Storage]
    J --> L[Global Ship Strength]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Components of Bottom Construction

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Bottom Construction System] --> B1[Keel Assembly]
    A0 --> B2[Bottom Shell Plating]
    A0 --> B3[Frames and Floors]
    A0 --> B4[Longitudinal Girders]
    A0 --> B5[Double Bottom Inner Skin]
    B1 --> C1[Bar Keel]
    B1 --> C2[Flat Plate Keel]
    B1 --> C3[Duct Keel]
    B3 --> C4[Solid Floor]
    B3 --> C5[Bracket Floor]
    B3 --> C6[Open Floor with Lightening Holes]
    B4 --> C7[Centreline Girder]
    B4 --> C8[Side Girder]
    B4 --> C9[Intermediate Girder]
    B5 --> C10[Inner Bottom Plate]
    B5 --> C11[Margin Plate]
    B5 --> C12[Bilge Bracket]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Cross-Sectional Layout (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Port Side Shell] --- M1[Port Margin Plate]
    M1 --- I1[Inner Bottom Plate - Port Tank Top]
    I1 --- CL[Centreline Keel - Middle Line]
    I1 --- I2[Inner Bottom Plate - Starboard Tank Top]
    I2 --- M2[Starboard Margin Plate]
    M2 --- P2[Starboard Side Shell]
    CL --- K1[Keel Plate]
    I1 --- S1[Side Girder Port]
    I2 --- S2[Side Girder Starboard]
    I1 --- T1[Transverse Floor Plates]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual interpretation:** The cross-section shows the **double bottom** as a horizontal "sandwich" — the outer bottom shell on the outside, the inner bottom plating on top, separated by floors and girders that form the tank space. The **centreline keel** runs vertically down the middle, the **margin plates** angle up on either side, and the **side girders** subdivide the tank into three longitudinal bays (port, centre, starboard).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define the term "Bottom Construction" in a ship. List any four major structural components forming it.**

**Model Answer:**
Bottom construction is the assembly of structural members at the lowest portion of a ship's hull that bears hydrostatic pressure, slamming, and grounding forces. It includes the **keel, bottom shell plating, transverse floors, longitudinal girders, and the inner bottom (in double bottom ships).** (Any four)

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for correct listing)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Distinguish between a solid floor and an open floor. State where each is used.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Solid Floor | Open Floor |
|---------|------------|------------|
| Construction | Single solid plate with flanges | Plate with lightening holes |
| Weight | Heavier | Lighter |
| Access | No passage | Allows access/ventilation |
| Application | Machinery space, forepeak, collision bulkhead | Cargo hold double-bottom tanks |

*(3 marks: 2 for table + 1 for application)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Model Paper 2]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **different types of keels** used in ship construction. Mention the advantages and disadvantages of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **double bottom construction** in modern merchant ships. Explain its structural and safety advantages. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The keel is the **backbone** of the ship, running along the centreline at the bottom. Types:

1. **Bar Keel:** A rectangular flat-bar section attached to the centreline of the bottom plating.
   - *Advantage:* Increases transverse stability and protection when grounding; easy to fabricate.
   - *Disadvantage:* Adds weight and drag; requires additional welding.

2. **Flat Plate Keel:** A wide, thick plate forming the centreline girder.
   - *Advantage:* Acts as a continuous strength member; simplifies fabrication; no extra appendage.
   - *Disadvantage:* Difficult to inspect internals if not a duct keel.

3. **Duct Keel:** A hollow keel forming a passage for pipes and cables.
   - *Advantage:* Houses piping (sea-water, ballast, sounding) safely; protects lines from damage.
   - *Disadvantage:* Complex fabrication, requires access manholes, careful sealing.

4. **Through-Plate Keel:** Bottom plating is continuous with a thicker central strip acting as the keel.
   - *Advantage:* Maximum structural continuity; preferred in modern welded ships.
   - *Disadvantage:* No additional grounding protection.

*(7 marks breakdown: 1 for definition, 3 for types with diagrams, 2 for advantages, 1 for disadvantages)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

The **double bottom** is an arrangement in which an **inner bottom plating** is fitted at a certain height above the outer bottom shell, creating a tank space between them. The height of the double bottom is typically **B/15 to B/20** (where B is the ship's breadth) or as per classification society rules.

**Structural Components:**
- Outer bottom shell plating
- Inner bottom plating (tank top)
- Centreline girder (keel)
- Side girders
- Transverse floors
- Margin plates
- Bilge brackets
- Manholes and access hatches
- Sounding pipes and vents

**Structural Advantages:**
- Increases the **moment of inertia** of the hull girder section by 4–5 times, drastically improving longitudinal strength.
- Provides **redundant watertight subdivision** — if the outer bottom is damaged, the inner bottom contains the flooding.
- Allows **even distribution** of concentrated loads (e.g., from heavy cargo on the tank top).

**Safety Advantages:**
- Prevents catastrophic flooding in case of bottom damage (SOLAS regulation).
- Required for **oil tankers** (MARPOL double-hull rule) and most bulk carriers above a certain size.
- Allows **segregated ballast** to replace ballast in cargo holds, reducing pollution risk.

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 for layout/sketch, 3 for components, 2 for advantages)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - 2023 Model Paper 1]`

**(a)** Explain with sketches the **different types of floors** used in ship bottom construction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier has a moulded depth of **14 m**, floor spacing of **0.7 m**, allowable stress of **180 MPa**, and a hydrostatic pressure factor of **0.013 MPa/m**. Using the empirical rule $t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{d} + t_{cor}$, with $k = 0.022$ and corrosion allowance $t_{cor} = 2$ mm, calculate the **required bottom plating thickness**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

Floors are **transverse vertical plates** fitted at regular intervals to support the bottom shell plating. Types:

1. **Solid Floors (Plain Plate Floors):** Single plates with face flanges.
   - Used in **machinery spaces, forward of collision bulkhead, and at transverse bulkheads**.
   - Provide maximum strength but reduce tank access.

2. **Bracket Floors:** Plate floor with **triangular brackets** on the upper edge in way of the tank top.
   - Used in **double bottom tanks** in cargo holds.
   - Allows pipe and cable passage through the bracket cut-outs.

3. **Open Floors (Perforated Floors):** Plate with **lightening holes** (manholes) along the centre.
   - Used in **double bottom tanks** to reduce weight and allow access between bays.
   - Most common in modern merchant ships.

4. **Web Floors:** Plain web plates without face flanges; efficient when used with double bottom.

*(7 marks: 1 intro + 4 types with sketches + 2 applications)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- Moulded depth $d = 14$ m
- Floor spacing $s = 0.7$ m
- Allowable stress $\sigma_{allow} = 180$ MPa
- Pressure factor = 0.013 MPa/m
- Empirical constant $k = 0.022$
- Corrosion allowance $t_{cor} = 2$ mm

**Formula:**

$$
t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{d} + t_{cor}
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
t = 0.7 \times 0.022 \times \sqrt{14} + 2
$$

**Step 1:** $\sqrt{14} = 3.7417$

**Step 2:** $0.7 \times 0.022 = 0.0154$

**Step 3:** $0.0154 \times 3.7417 = 0.05762$ m = 57.62 mm

Wait — units correction: $s \cdot k$ has units of mm/√m (from classification tables), so we interpret directly:

$$
t = 0.7 \times 0.022 \times 3.7417 + 2
$$

$$
t = 0.0576 + 2 = 2.0576 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Rounded up to next standard plate thickness (KTU convention: round to **3 mm or 4 mm**).

$$
\boxed{t \approx 4 \text{ mm (after rounding to standard plate gauge)}}
$$

*(7 marks breakdown: 1 for formula, 3 for substitution steps, 2 for final answer with units, 1 for rounding comment)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Bottom Construction Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** (m to mm, MPa to N/mm²). This alone costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing bar keel with flat plate keel** in sketches. Bar keel is an *external appendage*; flat plate keel is an *integral* centreline plate.
> 3. **Omitting the corrosion allowance** in thickness calculations.
> 4. **Not labelling diagrams** — KTU examiners specifically check for labelled components in sketches.
> 5. **Mixing up "inner bottom" with "tank top"** — they are the same physical plating, but "tank top" refers to the deck of the double-bottom tank.
> 6. **Wrong section modulus formula** — using $Z = bd$ instead of $Z = bd^2/6$ for rectangular sections is a frequent error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bottom Construction**

- **Bottom Construction** = Keel + Bottom Plating + Floors + Girders + Inner Bottom (if double).
- **Keel** = longitudinal centreline backbone; types: Bar, Flat Plate, Duct, Through-Plate.
- **Garboard strake** is the first strake of bottom plating adjacent to the keel — strongest and thickest.
- **Floors** are transverse; types: Solid (machinery), Bracket (cargo), Open/Perforated (tanks).
- **Girders** are longitudinal; types: Centreline (keel), Side, Intermediate. They reduce the **unsupported span** of the bottom plating.
- **Double bottom** = inner bottom + tank top + double bottom tanks. Mandatory in tankers, common in bulk carriers.
- **Margin plate** = the sloped/curved plate that connects the inner bottom to the side shell.
- **Bilge** = the curved transition region between bottom and side shell.
- **Plate thickness rule:** $t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{d} + t_{cor}$
- **Section modulus of rectangular floor:** $Z = bd^{2}/6$ (b = breadth, d = depth).
- **Hydrostatic pressure:** $p = \rho g h$
- **Floor spacing:** typically 0.5 D to 0.6 D.
- **Floor depth:** typically 0.05 D to 0.08 D.
- **Double bottom height:** typically B/15 to B/20 (where B = ship's breadth).
- **Key loads to remember:** Hydrostatic pressure, wave slamming, dry-dock (keel block) reaction, hogging/sagging, local cargo loads.
- **Double bottom advantages:** Increased I (longitudinal strength), redundancy, tank capacity, MARPOL compliance.
- **Always carry units** in numerical answers and **state the final value with the rounded-up standard gauge**.
- **Diagrams should be neat, labelled, and sectioned** (transverse section is the most common KTU sketch type for this module).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single and Double Bottom
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Single and Double Bottom # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Single Bottom — Formal Definition A **Single Bottom** is the most elementary form of ship bottom construction in which the **outer shell plating itself forms the watertight boundary of the hull**, supported internally by **transverse floors (solid or bracket)** at every frame station. There is **no inner bottom plating**, and the only horizontal boundary between the cargo/space and the sea is the **bottom shell plate**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** A single bottom essentially means a *single* layer of bottom shell plating doing the job of both strength and watertightness — the sea touches the hull directly under the cargo holds. **Construction Features of a Single Bottom:** - **Bottom shell plating** (garboard strake, bottom plating, etc.) - **Solid floors** or **bracket floors** at every frame - **Center keelson** (longitudinal stiffener) - **Side keelsons** (intermittent or continuous) - **Frames** (transverse or longitudinal) - No margin plate, no inner bottom ## 1.2 Double Bottom — Formal Definition A **Double Bottom** is a structural arrangement in which an additional layer of plating, called the **inner bottom plating (tank top)**, is fitted parallel to and above the **bottom shell plating**, thereby forming a **cellular void** between the two skins. This void is utilized for **ballast water, fresh water, fuel oil**, or simply left empty as a safety compartment. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Double bottom = (Outer Shell Plate) + (Inner Bottom Plate) + (Structural Framing) forming a closed cellular box girder along the length and breadth of the ship. **Mandatory Applications (MARPOL Annex I, Reg 13F):** - All **oil tankers of 5,000 DWT and above** must have a double bottom of minimum height **B/15 or 2 m, whichever is less** (for new buildings post-1993). - Bulk carriers carrying heavy cargoes also routinely adopt double bottom to enhance hull girder strength. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive Picture > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** > > Imagine a **single bottom** as a **single-walled steel cup**. The walls carry the load, and if they crack, the contents leak out directly. A **double bottom** is like a **double-walled thermos flask** — there are *two skins* with a gap in between. The gap can hold water (ballast), absorb impacts (like dropping the flask), and even if the outer skin cracks, the inner skin still holds the contents safely. > > In a ship: the **outer skin** is the shell plating, the **inner skin** is the inner bottom plating, and the **gap** is the double bottom tank used for ballast or storage. ## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics - **Double bottom height (h)**: Typically between **0.6 m to 1.8 m** for cargo ships; minimum **2 m or B/15** (B = molded breadth) per IACS/MARPOL for tankers. - **Floor spacing**: Equal to **frame spacing** (typically **0.5 m to 0.85 m**). - **Side girder spacing**: Roughly **1.5 m to 2.5 m** between centers. - **Material**: Mild steel (Grade A, B, D, E) for shells, AH/DH/EH for high-stress zones. - **Design pressure at bottom**: $p = \rho g h$ where $h$ is the draft. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Logic of a Single Bottom — Step-by-Step A single bottom is built on the principle of distributing **hydrostatic and cargo-induced loads** through a single plating system into transverse floor plates. **Step 1:** The **outer bottom shell plating** (typically 4 mm–25 mm thick depending on ship size) bears the sea pressure. **Step 2:** The pressure is transferred from the shell into **transverse floors** (plate stiffeners running athwartships at every frame). Floors act as the **primary transverse strength members** of the bottom. **Step 3:** A **center keelson** (longitudinal plate girder) is fitted at the centerline on top of the floors to provide **longitudinal strength** against the still-water and wave bending moments. **Step 4:** **Side keelsons** (intermittent) are added on either side of the centerline to give extra longitudinal stiffness and reduce the unsupported span of the bottom plating between floors. **Step 5:** **Frames** (transverse or longitudinal) run between the floors and shell to stiffen the shell plating. > [!NOTE] > **Why this design fails in modern ships:** A single bottom is structurally vulnerable because the bottom plating alone must resist both bending and accidental grounding loads. Modern classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) require **double bottoms for almost all cargo ships** to comply with **damage stability** and **environmental** rules. ## 2.2 Structural Logic of a Double Bottom — Step-by-Step A double bottom is essentially a **continuous box girder** integrated into the hull. Its components have specific load paths: **Step 1:** **Outer shell plating** (bottom skin) takes the hydrostatic pressure from the sea. **Step 2:** The load travels to the **floors (bottom transverse plates)** which are now sandwiched between the outer and inner bottom. The floors distribute the load **transversely** to the **side shell plating**. **Step 3:** **Longitudinal girders** (centerline girder + side girders) run fore-aft between the two skins to distribute load **longitudinally**, reducing the unsupported span of the bottom and inner bottom plating. **Step 4:** The **inner bottom plating** (tank top) carries the **weight of cargo/ballast above it** and transmits it to the floors and girders, eventually to the side shell. **Step 5:** The **margin plate** (a vertical or sloped plate at the inboard edge of the double bottom) connects the inner bottom to the **ship side shell plating**, providing a **watertight boundary** and stress transition zone. **Step 6:** The entire double bottom, in conjunction with the hull, acts as the **flange of the hull girder** to resist global bending. ## 2.3 Components of Double Bottom — Detailed Role | Component | Function | Orientation | |---|---|---| | **Bottom Shell Plating** | Outer watertight skin; resists sea pressure | Horizontal | | **Inner Bottom Plating (Tank Top)** | Inner watertight skin; forms top of double bottom | Horizontal | | **Solid Floor** | Transverse plate girder between outer & inner bottom | Transverse | | **Bracket Floor** | Lighter alternative to solid floor (open section) | Transverse | | **Centerline Girder (Docking Keel)** | Vertical plate at centerline; takes docking load + longitudinal strength | Longitudinal (vertical web) | | **Side Girders** | Longitudinal plate stiffeners (port & starboard) reducing inner bottom span | Longitudinal (vertical web) | | **Margin Plate** | Sloped/vertical plate connecting inner bottom to side shell | Transverse/inclined | | **Docking Bracket** | Reinforcement under docking points to take shore load | Local | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are standard IACS / Classification Society design formulas typically used in KTU 2024 Scheme examination problems. | # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Hydrostatic pressure at bottom** | $p = \rho g h$ | $\rho$ = seawater density (1025 kg/m³), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $h$ = draft (m) | | 2 | **Net plate thickness (Rule form)** | $t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{L} + t_{corr}$ | $s$ = stiffener spacing (m), $L$ = ship length (m), $k$ = coefficient, $t_{corr}$ = corrosion allowance (mm) | | 3 | **Required moment of inertia of stiffener** | $I = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | $M$ = bending moment, $c$ = distance to NA, $\sigma_{allow}$ = allowable stress | | 4 | **Required section modulus** | $Z = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | $M$ = bending moment, $\sigma_{allow}$ = allowable stress | | 5 | **Minimum double bottom height** | $h = \dfrac{B}{15}$ or **2 m** (whichever is less) — MARPOL | $B$ = molded breadth (m) | | 6 | **Side girder spacing** | $s_{sg} \approx \dfrac{1.5 B}{n}$ | $n$ = number of bays (typically 5–6) | | 7 | **Docking load per unit length** | $w_d = \dfrac{W_{dock}}{L_{dk}}$ | $W_{dock}$ = docking weight, $L_{dk}$ = docking block length | | 8 | **Effective breadth of plating (plate buckling)** | $b_{eff} = \dfrac{s}{\psi + \dfrac{1}{\psi}}$ | $s$ = spacing, $\psi$ = stress ratio | | 9 | **Plate critical buckling stress** | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \pi^2 E}{12 (1-\nu^2)} \left(\dfrac{t}{s}\right)^2$ | $k$ = buckling coefficient, $E$ = modulus, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio | | 10 | **Hull girder section modulus (bottom flange)** | $Z_{bot} = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{y_{bot}}$ | $I_{NA}$ = moment of inertia, $y_{bot}$ = distance to bottom from NA | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** The **double bottom is not just a structural feature — it is the lower flange of the hull girder**. By integrating it into the global strength calculation, designers achieve: > - Higher hull girder section modulus (Z) → reduced bending stresses > - Built-in tankage for ballast → improves stability > - Damage tolerance (single compartment flooding) > - Compliance with **MARPOL pollution prevention** regulations > - Reduced risk of cargo loss during grounding ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hull Girder Strength:** The double bottom forms the lower "flange" of the ship-as-box-girder, with the side shell as the "web" and the deck as the upper flange. Without it, $I_{NA}$ (second moment of area) drops significantly. - **Pollution Prevention:** MARPOL Annex I (Reg 13F) mandates double bottoms on all oil tankers built after 1993 to prevent oil spills in grounding casualties. - **Damage Stability:** SOLAS Chapter II-1 requires progressive flooding calculations; double bottom compartments are treated as separate floodable spaces. - **Cargo Operations:** The tank top serves as a clean, level working surface for cargo and machinery (especially in container ships, RO-RO, etc.). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Hydrostatic Pressure Acting on Bottom Plating **Problem:** Derive the pressure intensity acting on the outer bottom shell plating of a ship floating in seawater of density $\rho_{sw}$ at a draft $T$. ### Step-by-step Derivation The pressure at any depth $h$ below the waterline in a static fluid is given by the fundamental fluid statics equation: $$p = p_0 + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$ For a ship at the keel, the depth equals the full draft $T$. At the free surface, atmospheric pressure $p_0$ is balanced on both sides of the hull, so we consider only the **gauge pressure**: $$p_{gauge} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot T$$ Substituting the standard values for seawater: $$\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$ For a typical cargo ship with $T = 10 \text{ m}$: $$p = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 10 \text{ N/m}^2$$ $$p = 100,553 \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 100.55 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ > This is the **design pressure** the outer bottom plating must resist. The thickness and framing are then calculated from this pressure. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Plate Thickness from Bending Theory **Problem:** A bottom plate panel of span $s$ (between two floors) carries a uniformly distributed load $p$ (N/m²). Derive the required thickness using simple bending theory. ### Step-by-step Derivation **Step 1 — Treat the plate as a one-way slab** of unit width (1 m) spanning between two floors. **Step 2 — Compute the maximum bending moment** for a simply supported strip under UDL: $$M = \frac{p \cdot s^2}{8} \quad \text{(per unit width, N·m/m)}$$ **Step 3 — Section modulus of a rectangular strip** of width $b = 1$ m and thickness $t$: $$Z = \frac{b \cdot t^2}{6} = \frac{t^2}{6}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the bending stress relation:** $$\sigma = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{6 M}{t^2}$$ **Step 5 — Equate the bending stress to allowable stress** $\sigma_{allow}$ and solve for $t$: $$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{6 M}{t^2} \implies t^2 = \frac{6 M}{\sigma_{allow}}$$ $$t = \sqrt{\frac{6 M}{\sigma_{allow}}}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute $M = p s^2 / 8$:** $$t = \sqrt{\frac{6 \cdot \dfrac{p s^2}{8}}{\sigma_{allow}}}$$ $$t = s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{3 p}{4 \sigma_{allow}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Form:** > > $$t = s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{3 p}{4 \sigma_{allow}}}$$ > > This is the **classical ship plate thickness formula** used in KTU problems. Classification societies (Lloyd's, DNV, BV) tabulate the constant $k$ in the form $t = k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{L}$, where the wave-induced dynamic pressure is folded into the coefficient $k$ as a function of ship length and position in the hull. --- ## 3.3 Derivation — Moment of Inertia of the Double Bottom Section (Hull Girder Contribution) **Problem:** Calculate the moment of inertia contribution of the double bottom (acting as the lower flange of the hull girder) about the neutral axis. ### Step-by-step Derivation **Step 1 — Define the geometry:** - Inner bottom area $A_{IB}$ (at the top of double bottom) - Outer bottom area $A_{OB}$ (at the keel) - Centerline girder web area $A_{CG}$ (vertical web) - Side girder webs (port & starboard): $A_{SG, P}, A_{SG, S}$ - Margin plate area: $A_{MP}$ **Step 2 — Compute the area of each element:** For the inner bottom (assuming full breadth $B$ minus margins, thickness $t_{IB}$): $$A_{IB} = B \cdot t_{IB}$$ For the outer bottom (full breadth $B$, thickness $t_{OB}$): $$A_{OB} = B \cdot t_{OB}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the parallel axis contribution** of each flange to the global neutral axis (NA) located at $\bar{y}$ from the keel: $$I_{DB} = \underbrace{\left[\frac{B t_{IB}^3}{12} + A_{IB} (y_{IB} - \bar{y})^2 \right]}_{\text{Inner bottom contribution}} + \underbrace{\left[\frac{B t_{OB}^3}{12} + A_{OB} (\bar{y} - y_{OB})^2 \right]}_{\text{Outer bottom contribution}}$$ **Step 4 — Add the web (centerline girder) contribution:** $$I_{CG} = \frac{t_{CG} \cdot h_{DB}^3}{12}$$ **Step 5 — Total double bottom contribution:** $$I_{DB,total} = I_{IB} + I_{OB} + I_{CG} + 2 I_{SG} + I_{MP}$$ Where $I_{SG}$ is the moment of inertia of one side girder (× 2 for port + starboard). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice the **$A \cdot y^2$ term dominates** because the inner and outer bottoms are far from the neutral axis. This is why **doubling the breadth of the double bottom** (without changing thickness) increases hull girder strength by **4×** (since $I \propto y^2$ in extreme fiber positions). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of Single vs Double Bottom | Parameter | Single Bottom | Double Bottom | |---|---|---| | **Number of bottom skins** | 1 (outer shell only) | 2 (outer + inner) | | **Inner bottom plating** | Absent | Present | | **Ballast capacity** | Limited; uses wing tanks/double skin elsewhere | Excellent; dedicated DB tanks | | **Hull girder strength** | Lower $I_{NA}$, higher stresses | Higher $I_{NA}$, lower stresses | | **Damage tolerance** | Poor — single breach floods hold | High — outer breach floods DB only | | **MARPOL compliance** | Not for oil tankers | Mandatory for tankers | | **Construction cost** | Lower initial | Higher initial, lower lifecycle | | **Inspection access** | Direct access to shell (in drydock) | Requires internal access; harder inspection | | **Cargo working surface** | Exposed shell (dirty, uneven) | Clean inner bottom (tank top) | | **Weight** | Lighter | Heavier by ~5–10% of steel mass | | **Use case today** | Small craft, fishing boats, very small coasters | All commercial cargo ships, tankers, bulkers | | **Number of load paths** | 1 (shell → floor → keelson) | Multiple (shell ↔ floor ↔ inner bottom ↔ girders) | | **Resistance to grounding** | Low | Significantly higher (cellular cushion) | # 4.
