Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagrams Note:** Since physical ship cross-sections are complex, the Mermaid diagrams below are **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** representations showing the structural relationships and load paths in single and double bottom systems. ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Single Bottom Load Path ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sea Water Hydrostatic Pressure] --> B[Bottom Shell Plating] B --> C[Solid Floors at Every Frame] C --> D[Side Shell Plating] C --> E[Center Keelson] E --> F[Frames Longitudinal] F --> G[Keel Plate] G --> H[Single Hull Box Girder] H --> I[Loads to Sea via Hull Girder] classDef load fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000 classDef member fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,color:#000 classDef output fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 class A,I load class B,C,D,E,F,G,H member class I output ``` **Interpretation:** In a single bottom, there is only **ONE load path** from the sea to the keelson. Any failure in the shell plating directly compromises the cargo space. --- ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Double Bottom Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DB[Double Bottom Cellular Zone] OSP[Outer Shell Plating
Bottom Skin] IBP[Inner Bottom Plating
Tank Top] FLO[Solid / Bracket Floors
Transverse] CLG[Centerline Girder
Docking Keel Vertical Web] SG1[Side Girder Port
Longitudinal] SG2[Side Girder Starboard
Longitudinal] MP[Margin Plate
Inboard Edge] DB_TANK[Double Bottom Tank
Ballast / FW / Fuel] end SP[Sea Pressure] --> OSP OSP --> FLO FLO --> SG1 FLO --> SG2 FLO --> CLG FLO --> IBP IBP --> MP MP --> SS[Side Shell Plating] CLG --> HG[Hull Girder Section Modulus] SG1 --> HG SG2 --> HG OSP --> HG IBP --> HG HG --> GB[Global Bending Resistance] DB_TANK -.-> FLO DB_TANK -.-> OSP DB_TANK -.-> IBP classDef pressure fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,color:#000 classDef member fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000 classDef tank fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000 classDef global fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 class SP pressure class OSP,IBP,FLO,CLG,SG1,SG2,MP,SS member class DB_TANK tank class HG,GB global ``` **Interpretation:** In a double bottom, there are **MULTIPLE LOAD PATHS** and a **dedicated tank space** in the middle. The cellular structure acts as a **box girder** integrated into the hull. --- ## 4.3 Decision Flow — When to Use Single vs Double Bottom ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Ship Design Stage] --> Q1{Is vessel an
Oil Tanker?} Q1 -->|Yes 5000 DWT+| DB1[Mandatory Double Bottom
MARPOL Annex I Reg 13F] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is vessel a
Bulk Carrier?} Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{DWT > 20,000?} Q3 -->|Yes| DB2[Double Bottom Strongly
Recommended by IACS S21] Q3 -->|No| Q4{Trade route includes
ice/grounding risk?} Q4 -->|Yes| DB3[Double Bottom Recommended] Q4 -->|No| DB4[Single Bottom Acceptable
with Bracket Floors] Q2 -->|No| Q5{Small craft or
fishing boat?} Q5 -->|Yes| SB1[Single Bottom Standard] Q5 -->|No| DB5[Double Bottom Recommended
for Cargo Ships] classDef mandatory fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 classDef recommended fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 classDef acceptable fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 class DB1,DB2 mandatory class DB3,DB4,DB5,DB2 recommended class SB1 acceptable ``` --- ## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Block Representation) — Double Bottom Assembly ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PORT[Port Side] PS1[Side Shell] PS2[Bracket Connection] end subgraph DB_ZONE[Double Bottom Void] DBV1[Floor Plate] DBV2[Centerline Girder Web] DBV3[Side Girder Web] end subgraph STAR[Starboard Side] SS1[Side Shell] SS2[Bracket Connection] end PS1 --> PS2 PS2 --> DBV1 DBV1 --> DBV2 DBV1 --> DBV3 PS2 ---> DBV3 DBV2 ---> DBV3 DBV3 ---> SS2 SS2 --> SS1 classDef shell fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 classDef db fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 classDef side fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 class PS1,SS1 shell class DBV1,DBV2,DBV3 db class PS2,SS2 side ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mark Distribution Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2 typically carries **15–20% weightage** in the End Semester Examination (ESE). Part A is **2 marks** in KTU 2024 scheme, but for OENST614 (likely having 3-mark Part A), questions below are tuned accordingly. --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — Define Single Bottom. List its main components. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** A **single bottom** is a ship bottom construction in which the **outer shell plating alone** acts as the watertight lower boundary of the hull, with **no inner bottom plating**. The bottom plating is supported internally by **transverse floors** at every frame station. **Main components of a single bottom:** 1. **Bottom shell plating** (outer skin) 2. **Solid floors / bracket floors** (transverse) 3. **Center keelson** (longitudinal at centerline) 4. **Side keelsons** (intermittent longitudinal) 5. **Frames** (transverse stiffeners) 6. **Keel plate** (at the centerline bottom) > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award full marks only if you list **at least 4 components** with their function. Writing "single bottom = bottom plating" alone fetches partial credit. --- ### Question 2 — What is a Margin Plate? State its purpose. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 2 | 3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** A **margin plate** is a **vertical or sloped plate** fitted at the **inboard edge of the double bottom**, connecting the **inner bottom plating (tank top)** to the **ship side shell plating**. It forms the watertight side boundary of the double bottom. **Purposes of a margin plate:** 1. **Provides watertight boundary** between double bottom tank and cargo hold 2. **Transfers shear** from the inner bottom to the side shell 3. **Strengthens the bilge region** (junction of bottom and side) 4. **Resists slamming and impact loads** in the bilge 5. **Acts as a stress transition member** between the cellular double bottom and the side structure --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### 📌 QUESTION A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]** #### Part (a) — Explain the construction of a Single Bottom ship with a neat sketch. List its advantages and limitations. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **1. Definition (1 mark):** A single bottom construction consists of a single layer of bottom shell plating supported by transverse floors and longitudinal keelsons. There is no inner bottom plating. **2. Sketch (2 marks):** ``` ┌──────────────────────────┐ ← Deck │ │ │ CARGO HOLD │ │ │ ────┼──────────────────────────┼──── │ ◢░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░◣ │ ← Margin / Side ────┼──────────────────────────┼──── ← Floor (transverse) │ ░ Solid Floor (Plate) ░ │ ────┼──────────────────────────┼──── │ Bottom Shell Plating │ ← Keel └──────────────────────────┘ (Single Skin) ``` **3. Components (2 marks):** - **Bottom shell plating** (4–25 mm) - **Solid/bracket floors** at every frame - **Center keelson** (longitudinal) - **Side keelsons** (intermittent) - **Frames** (transverse) - **Keel plate** **4. Advantages (1 mark):** - Simple and economical construction - Easy inspection and maintenance - Lighter ship weight - Suitable for small vessels **5. Limitations (1 mark):** - No damage tolerance - Cannot comply with MARPOL for tankers - Limited ballast capacity - Lower hull girder strength --- #### Part (b) — Explain Double Bottom construction with a neat sketch. List its components and explain the function of each. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **1. Definition (1 mark):** A double bottom consists of two horizontal skins — outer shell and inner bottom (tank top) — separated by a cellular void used for ballast/storage. **2. Sketch (2 marks):** ``` ┌────────────────────────────┐ ← Deck │ │ │ CARGO HOLD │ │ │ ───┼────────────────────────────┼─── │ ◢░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░◣ │ ← Margin Plate ├────────────────────────────┤ │ ╲ Side Girder ╱ │ ← Inner Bottom ├────────────────────────────┤ │ ╱ Floor ╲ ╱ Floor ╲ │ (Tank Top) │ Solid │ Solid │ ├────────────────────────────┤ │ Double Bottom Tank │ ← Centerline Girder │ (Ballast / Water / Oil) │ (Docking Keel) ├────────────────────────────┤ │ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │ ← Bottom Shell Plating └────────────────────────────┘ (Double Skin) ``` **3. Components and Functions (4 marks — ½ mark each):** | Component | Function | |---|---| | **Outer Shell Plating** | Resists hydrostatic pressure; primary watertight skin | | **Inner Bottom Plating** | Forms top of DB tank; provides cargo working surface | | **Solid Floors** | Transverse plate girders; distribute loads athwartships | | **Centerline Girder** | Vertical keel web; takes docking loads & longitudinal bending | | **Side Girders** | Longitudinal stiffeners; reduce inner bottom span | | **Margin Plate** | Connects inner bottom to side shell; stress transition | | **Docking Brackets** | Local reinforcements under keel blocks | | **Bracket Plates** | Connect floor to side girder; reduce shear lag | --- ### 📌 QUESTION B (Choice 2) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]** #### Part (a) — Explain the functions of the Centerline Girder, Side Girders, and Floors in a double bottom. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **1. Centerline Girder (2 marks):** - A **vertical plate keel** fitted amidships at the centerline, extending from the stem to the stern. - Also called the **docking keel**. - **Functions:** - Takes the **docking load** (weight of ship supported on keel blocks) - Resists **longitudinal bending** of the hull girder - Provides a **watertight subdivision** along the centerline - Acts as a **stringer** during construction for accurate alignment **2. Side Girders (2 marks):** - **Longitudinal plate stiffeners** fitted port and starboard, parallel to the centerline. - Spaced at about **1.5 B / 5 to 1.5 B / 6** apart. - **Functions:** - **Reduce the unsupported span** of the inner and outer bottom plating - Increase the **moment of inertia** of the double bottom section - Resist **local bending** of the tank top - Provide **longitudinal strength** in conjunction with the centerline girder **3. Floors (2 marks):** - **Transverse plate girders** fitted at every frame station, between the outer and inner bottom. - Can be **solid** (continuous plate) or **bracket** (lightweight with openings). - **Functions:** - Distribute the **bottom pressure** to the side shell - Provide **transverse strength** of the bottom - Support the **inner bottom plating** against cargo loads - Form the **transverse watertight bulkheads** of DB tanks - Act as **bottom transverse** of the hull girder **4. Diagram (1 mark):** As shown in Question A part (b) above. --- #### Part (b) — Sketch and explain the Margin Plate. State its different types based on geometry. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **1. Definition (1 mark):** A **margin plate** is a transverse or inclined plate fitted at the **inboard edge of the double bottom** to connect the inner bottom plating to the side shell plating. **2. Sketch (3 marks):** ``` ◢━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━◣ ← Margin Plate (sloped type) │ ╲ ╱ │ │ ╲ Side ╱ │ ← Side Shell │ ╲ Shell ╱ │ ├──────────┬─────────┤ │ │ │ │ Inner │ Inner │ ← Tank Top (Inner Bottom) │ Bottom │ Bottom │ │ │ │ └──────────┴─────────┘ Outer Shell ``` **3. Functions of Margin Plate (1.5 marks):** - **Watertight closure** of double bottom on the inboard side - **Stress transition** between cellular bottom and side shell - **Strengthens the bilge** (high stress concentration zone) - **Reduces shear lag** in the side shell - Acts as a **boundary** for the DB tank **4. Types of Margin Plate (1.5 marks):** | Type | Geometry | Application | |---|---|---| | **Vertical Margin Plate** | Plain vertical plate | Most common in tankers, bulkers | | **Sloped Margin Plate** | Inclined (typically 30°–45°) | Used in warships, special vessels | | **Lipped Margin Plate** | With a horizontal lip on the inner bottom | Increases moment of inertia | | **Stepped Margin Plate** | Step change at the bilge | Used in some bulk carriers | --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Confusing the function of margin plate with side girder.** Side girders are *longitudinal* (run fore-aft), while margin plates are *transverse* (run athwartship at the inboard edge). > > 2. **Not mentioning the MARPOL requirement for tankers** — examiners specifically look for this regulatory hook in 14-mark questions. > > 3. **Forgetting the corrosion allowance** when applying plate thickness formulas — this alone can cost 1–2 marks in numerical problems. > > 4. **Writing "inner bottom" instead of "tank top" interchangeably without clarification** — always state they are the same component when using the term first. > > 5. **Drawing the centerline girder as a horizontal member** — it is a **vertical web** (plate keel) at the centerline, not a horizontal plate. This is one of the most common drawing errors. > > 6. **Omitting units in numerical answers** — always write SI units (mm for thickness, m for length, N/mm² for stress). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Single and Double Bottom (Module 2)** - ✅ **Single Bottom** = Single layer of outer shell plating + transverse floors + longitudinal keelsons. No inner bottom. - ✅ **Double Bottom** = Outer shell + **Inner bottom (tank top)** + Floors + Centerline girder + Side girders + Margin plate. - ✅ **MARPOL Annex I, Reg 13F** mandates double bottom for **oil tankers ≥ 5,000 DWT** built after 1993. - ✅ **Minimum double bottom height** = **B/15 or 2 m**, whichever is less (for tankers). - ✅ **Centerline girder** = vertical plate keel, takes **docking load** + longitudinal strength. - ✅ **Side girders** = longitudinal plate stiffeners; reduce inner bottom span; increase $I_{NA}$. - ✅ **Solid floors** = transverse plate girders at every frame; transfer load to side shell. - ✅ **Bracket floors** = lighter version with cutouts; used in cargo ships. - ✅ **Margin plate** = inboard transverse/inclined plate connecting inner bottom to side shell. - ✅ **Hydrostatic pressure at keel**: $p = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot T$ (gauge). - ✅ **Plate thickness formula**: $t = s \sqrt{\dfrac{3p}{4\sigma_{allow}}}$ (from bending theory). - ✅ **Classification Society** rules (Lloyd's, DNV, ABS, BV) use the standard form: $t = k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{L} + t_{corr}$. - ✅ **Double bottom = lower flange of hull girder** → significantly improves hull girder section modulus. - ✅ **Damage tolerance** of double bottom is **superior** to single bottom (single compartment flooding). - ✅ **Ballast capacity** of double bottom is **dedicated** and well-organized. - ✅ **Construction cost** of double bottom is **higher** initially but **lower lifecycle cost**. - ✅ **Inner bottom = tank top** — used as cargo working surface in container ships, RO-RO, etc. - ✅ **Docking brackets** are local reinforcements under the keel for supporting the ship during dry-docking. - ✅ The **double bottom acts as a box girder** in itself, providing redundant load paths and increased redundancy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam Mantra:** When answering any KTU question on this topic, **always draw the cross-section first** (even a rough sketch), **label all components**, and then explain their functions. A well-labeled sketch alone can fetch **2–3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.

topics.title: Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are "Functions" in Ship Structural Engineering?

In the discipline of **Basic Ship Structures**, the term *"Functions"* refers to the **specific structural roles, load-resisting duties, and operational responsibilities** assigned to each individual component of a ship's hull girder system. Every plate, stiffener, girder, bulkhead, and deck in a vessel is not a decorative element — it is a **load-bearing member** with a definite, engineered purpose.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Functions of ship structural elements* are the **mechanical, hydrodynamic, and operational duties** that each component performs — such as resisting bending, shearing, buckling, racking, slamming, and providing watertight integrity — thereby ensuring the vessel maintains its structural shape, strength, and seaworthiness under all expected service conditions.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Human Skeleton

Imagine the human body:
- **Bones (Hull plating)** → Hold the body's shape
- **Ribs (Frames)** → Protect vital organs and give the chest its curved form
- **Spine (Keel + centerline girder)** → The main load-bearing axis that lets you bend and twist
- **Skull (Stem & Stern frame)** → The hardened leading and trailing edges
- **Joints (Brackets & knees)** → Where two bones meet, transferring load smoothly
- **Skin (Outer plating)** → Keeps the inside in and the outside out

A ship is essentially a **hollow, floating skeleton** made of steel, and every member has a job description — just like a hospital where the heart surgeon, the nurse, and the pharmacist each have distinct functions. If you mix up their roles, the system fails.

### 1.3 The Six Primary Functions of Any Ship Structure

Every ship structural element performs **one or more** of these six universal duties:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Cardinal Functions of Ship Structures:**
> 1. **Resist External Loads** — hydrostatic, hydrodynamic, wave, cargo, and impact loads.
> 2. **Maintain Geometric Shape** — preserve hull form against distortion (racking, panting, torsion).
> 3. **Provide Watertight/Weathertight Integrity** — subdivision via bulkheads; prevent flooding.
> 4. **Transfer Loads to the Hull Girder** — distribute local loads (e.g., from a propeller shaft) up to the global bending structure.
> 5. **Support Operational Systems** — carry machinery, cargo, deck equipment, superstructure weight.
> 6. **Ensure Safety & Survival** — crashworthiness, fire integrity, redundant load paths in damaged condition.

### 1.4 Functions of the Principal Ship Structural Components

| # | Component | Primary Function(s) |
|---|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Keel** | Longitudinal backbone; resists hogging/sagging bending; attachment for garboards |
| 2 | **Stem** | Forms the bow entry; resists slamming & bow-wave impact |
| 3 | **Stern Frame / Sternpost** | Supports rudder & propeller; resists hydrostatic loads at the transom |
| 4 | **Hull Plating (Bottom, Side, Deck)** | Forms watertight envelope; participates in hull-girder bending |
| 5 | **Frames (Transverse ribs)** | Maintain hull cross-section; resist local hydrostatic pressure |
| 6 | **Girders (Longitudinal)** | Support frames; carry longitudinal bending loads |
| 7 | **Decks** | Resist vertical loads (cargo, equipment); contribute to hull-girder strength |
| 8 | **Bulkheads** | Subdivide the ship; preserve buoyancy after flooding |
| 9 | **Superstructure** | House crew/equipment; contributes to shear & bending stiffness |
| 10 | **Stringers & Pillars** | Reduce unsupported span of girders; transfer vertical loads to bottom |
| 11 | **Bilge Keel** | Damps roll motion; minor structural stiffening |
| 12 | **Hatch Covers** | Weather-seal cargo holds; contribute to deck strength |

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

> [!NOTE]
> - **Density of seawater:** $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1.025\ t/m^3}$ (1025 kg/m³)
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = \mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$
> - **Density of fresh water:** $\rho_{fw} = \mathbf{1.000\ t/m^3}$
> - **Young's Modulus of ship steel:** $E = \mathbf{2.06 \times 10^{5}\ N/mm^2}$
> - **Yield stress of mild ship steel (Grade A):** $\sigma_y = \mathbf{235\ MPa}$
> - **Permissible bending stress (Classification Societies):** $\sigma_{perm} \approx \mathbf{175\ MPa}$ (≈ 0.6 × σ_y for normal seagoing)
> - **Rule wave bending moment coefficient:** $f = \mathbf{0.9}$ to $\mathbf{1.1}$ (depending on L and class society)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Hierarchy of a Ship Structure

A ship structure can be visualized as a **three-tier load path hierarchy**:

```
TIER 1 — GLOBAL / HULL GIRDER LEVEL
   (Resists total bending & shearing forces of the entire vessel)
                ↑ loads transferred upward
TIER 2 — SUBSTRUCTURE / PRIMARY LEVEL
   (Resists local panel loads, transmits them to the hull girder)
                ↑ loads transferred upward
TIER 3 — LOCAL / SECONDARY LEVEL
   (Plates & stiffeners resist direct hydrostatic, cargo, equipment pressure)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this hierarchy matters for KTU exams:**
> The classification of a member's "function" is determined by **which tier** it belongs to. For instance, a **bottom shell plate** is *simultaneously* a Tier-3 local member (it resists sea pressure directly) **and** a Tier-1 hull-girder participant (its effective breadth contributes to the section modulus).

### 2.2 Functional Breakdown of Each Major Structural Group

#### A. Functions of the **Keel Assembly**

- Acts as the **backbone** of the hull girder.
- Resists **longitudinal bending stress** (hogging & sagging).
- Provides the **line of reference** for the ship's design and construction (baseline).
- Anchors the **garboard strake** (first strake of bottom plating).
- In case of **dry-docking**, it bears the entire weight via the **docking blocks** (grounding loads).
- Prevents **broaching-to** loads from being concentrated at one point.

#### B. Functions of the **Hull Plating**

- Forms the **watertight envelope** (primary barrier against flooding).
- Participates in **hull-girder bending** as the outer flange of the I-section (with the strength deck as the top flange).
- Resists **local hydrostatic pressure** between stiffeners (plate bending).
- Resists **lateral pressure** (slamming on the bow, side-impact from berthing).

#### C. Functions of the **Frames**

- Maintain the **transverse shape** of the hull (resist racking).
- Support the **shell plating** against buckling under external pressure.
- Transfer **lateral pressure loads** from the shell to the decks/keel (acting like vertical beams).
- Provide **attachment points** for piping, cables, insulation.
- In tanker/tug designs, may form **web frames** for very high local loads.

#### D. Functions of the **Girders** (Longitudinal)

- Support frames at intermediate points, reducing their **unsupported span**.
- Carry **longitudinal bending loads** in conjunction with shell and deck plating.
- In tankers, **double-bottom girders** also serve as **limber holes** for oil drainage and structural stiffening.

#### E. Functions of the **Decks**

- **Strength deck** (usually the uppermost continuous deck) acts as the **top flange** of the hull girder.
- Resists **vertical loads** from cargo, equipment, crew, sea spray.
- Provides **transverse shear transfer** between port & starboard sides.
- Acts as a **weather-tight** barrier.
- In warships, the deck is the **armor deck** for protection.

#### F. Functions of the **Bulkheads**

- **Watertight bulkheads** → subdivide the hull to limit flooding after hull breach (survivability).
- **Collision bulkhead** (forward) → first line of defense against forward impact.
- **Engine-room bulkhead** → separates machinery spaces from cargo.
- **Aft peak bulkhead** → supports shaft tunnel, isolates steering gear.
- **Swash bulkheads** (partial) → reduce free-surface effect of liquids in tanks.
- **Transverse strength bulkheads** → contribute to hull-girder shear capacity.

#### G. Functions of the **Superstructure**

- Houses the **bridge, accommodation, machinery casings**.
- Adds **shear stiffness** to the deck region (especially in still water & minor seaways).
- Provides **additional buoyancy reserve** in extreme bow-down conditions (rare contribution).
- However, in heavy weather, its **discontinuity** with the strength deck creates **shear stress concentration** at the corners — hence the need for **rounded sheer strake** and **tripping brackets**.

#### H. Functions of **Stringers and Pillars**

- **Stringers** are horizontal stiffeners on the side or bulkhead that reduce the **unsupported span** of vertical frames/girders.
- **Pillars / Stanchions** are vertical steel tubes that **transfer deck loads down to the bottom shell**, preventing the deck and bottom from "pinching" together under heavy deck cargo.
- Together they constitute the **transverse load path** of the ship.

#### I. Functions of the **Stem and Stern Frame**

- **Stem**: forms the **sharp bow entry** to cut through waves; resists **slamming forces** (sudden impact pressures up to ~350 kPa for a bulbous bow ship).
- **Sternpost / Stern frame**: supports the **propeller and rudder**; transmits propeller thrust into the hull; resists **stern tube bearing reactions**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects every formula a KTU 2024 student must know for "Functions of ship structures." Use **\vert** (not \vert — see column rule) for absolute-value bars to preserve table rendering.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Function |
|---|----------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | $p = \rho \, g \, h$ | Pressure acting on shell — function of **plating & frames** |
| 2 | Buoyancy force | $F_b = \rho \, g \, \nabla$ | Function of **overall hull volume $\nabla$** |
| 3 | Rule wave bending moment (still-water + wave) | $M_w = f \, C_w \, L^2 \, B \, (C_B + 0.7)$ kN·m | Function of **hull girder (keel + strength deck)** |
| 4 | Rule wave shear force | $F_w = f \, C_f \, L \, B \, (C_B + 0.7)$ kN | Function of **side & deck plating + bulkheads** |
| 5 | Section modulus of hull girder | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ (m³) | Quantifies **bending resistance function** |
| 6 | Bending stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Must be $\le \sigma_{perm}$ |
| 7 | Shear stress | $\tau = \dfrac{V \, Q}{I \, t}$ | Function of **side shell, bulkheads, deck plating** |
| 8 | Plate panel bending stress (simply supported) | $\sigma_{plate} = \dfrac{k \, p \, a^2}{t^2}$ | Function of **plating between stiffeners** |
| 9 | Frame section modulus required | $Z_{req} = \dfrac{p \, s \, \ell^2}{m \, \sigma_{perm}}$ | Function of **transverse frames** |
| 10 | Critical buckling stress (plate) | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\dfrac{t}{a}\right)^2 k$ | Function of **plating against collapse** |
| 11 | Pillar load capacity (Euler) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$ | Function of **stanchions/pillars** |
| 12 | Shear area of hull girder | $A_s$ (≈ projected side area) | Function of **side shell plating** |
| 13 | Docking load per block | $F_{block} = \dfrac{W_{light}}{N_{blocks}}$ | Function of **keel & bilge plating** |
| 14 | Still-water bending moment (trochoidal wave approx.) | $M_{sw} = \pm \, \rho \, g \, B \, L^3 / 24$ (rough) | Baseline for **keel function** |

Where:
- $L$ = Length between perpendiculars (m)
- $B$ = Breadth moulded (m)
- $C_B$ = Block coefficient (≈ 0.60–0.85)
- $\nabla$ = Volume of displacement (m³)
- $a$ = Plate panel short side (mm)
- $t$ = Plate thickness (mm)
- $s$ = Frame spacing (m)
- $\ell$ = Frame span between supports (m)
- $m$ = Permissible bending stress factor
- $k$ = Plate buckling coefficient (≈ 4 for simply supported)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio ≈ **0.3**
- $L_e$ = Effective pillar length
- $N_{blocks}$ = Number of docking blocks

### 2.4 Real-World Utility — Why Engineers Study Functions

In ship design offices (e.g., Hyundai Heavy Industries, Cochin Shipyard), the **first step** in any new vessel design is to list the **functions** each structural element will perform. This drives:

- **Plate thickness selection** (more functions → thicker plate)
- **Stiffener spacing** (load-sharing functions → closer spacing)
- **Material grade** (high-tensile steel for members performing multiple functions, e.g., sheer strake)
- **Class society approval** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, IRS all classify members by their function in their rule books)

> [!NOTE]
> A **sheer strake** performs **four functions simultaneously** — sheer resistance, gunwale longitudinal strength, side-shell watertight envelope, and attachment for bulwarks — which is why it is the **thickest plate** in the side shell.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Function of the Keel in Resisting Hogging/Sagging Bending

**Context:** A ship in a seaway bends like a beam. In a **hogging** condition (bow & stern supported by wave crests, amidships in a trough), the **keel experiences TENSION**. In **sagging** (amidships supported by wave crest), the **keel experiences COMPRESSION**.

**Starting point — Pure bending equation:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{M \, y}{I}
$$

where:
- $M$ = total bending moment at the section (N·m)
- $y$ = distance from neutral axis to the fibre in question (m)
- $I$ = second moment of area of the hull girder cross-section (m⁴)

**Step 1.** The hull girder is approximated as a **box girder** with:
- **Top flange** = strength deck + sheer strake + deck plating
- **Bottom flange** = keel plate + bottom shell plating + garboard strake
- **Webs (two sides)** = side shell plating

**Step 2.** The second moment of area for the box girder is (approximately, neglecting own second moment of flanges):

$$
I = \frac{B^2}{2} \, (A_{deck} \, \bar{y}_{deck} + A_{bottom} \, \bar{y}_{bottom})
$$

where $B$ is the breadth, $A$ is the area of the flange, $\bar{y}$ is the vertical distance from the neutral axis.

**Step 3.** Substitute into bending stress:

$$
\sigma_{keel} = \frac{M_{hog} \, \bar{y}_{bottom}}{I_{hull}}
$$

**Step 4.** Equivalently, using **section modulus** $Z = I / y_{max}$:

$$
\sigma_{keel} = \frac{M_{hog}}{Z_{hull}}
$$

**Step 5.** For a typical Panamax bulk carrier (L = 229 m, B = 32.2 m, D = 20 m, C_B = 0.82), class rule wave bending moment in hogging:

$$
M_{w,hog} = f \cdot C_w \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot (C_B + 0.7)
$$

Plugging typical $f = 1.0$, $C_w$ for L ≈ 229 m from DNV table ≈ **0.111**:

$$
M_{w,hog} = 1.0 \cdot 0.111 \cdot (229)^2 \cdot 32.2 \cdot (0.82 + 0.7)
$$

$$
M_{w,hog} = 0.111 \cdot 52441 \cdot 32.2 \cdot 1.52
$$

$$
M_{w,hog} \approx 0.111 \cdot 52441 \cdot 48.94 \approx 284{,}800 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 6.** Required section modulus:

$$
Z_{req} = \frac{M_{w,hog}}{\sigma_{perm}} = \frac{284.8 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m}}{175 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2} \approx 1.627 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Conclusion:** The keel (with garboard + bottom shell) must contribute to the **1.627 m³** section modulus demand. This quantifies its **bending-resistance function**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Function of Transverse Frames Under External Hydrostatic Pressure

**Context:** A frame near the keel at depth $h$ from the waterline must support the shell plating between it and adjacent frames.

**Step 1.** Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$:

$$
p = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot h \quad \text{[Pa]}
$$

**Step 2.** Treat the frame as a **simply supported beam** spanning vertically between the keel (or bottom shell) and the lowest deck. Distributed load on the frame = pressure × frame spacing $s$:

$$
w = p \cdot s \quad \text{[N/m]}
$$

**Step 3.** Maximum bending moment for a simply supported beam with UDL:

$$
M_{max} = \frac{w \, \ell^2}{8} = \frac{p \cdot s \cdot \ell^2}{8}
$$

**Step 4.** Required section modulus:

$$
Z_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{perm}} = \frac{p \, s \, \ell^2}{8 \, \sigma_{perm}}
$$

**Step 5.** Numerical example — at $h = 10$ m draft, frame spacing $s = 0.6$ m, frame span $\ell = 12$ m, $\sigma_{perm} = 175$ MPa:

$$
p = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 10 = 100{,}553 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
w = 100{,}553 \cdot 0.6 = 60{,}332 \text{ N/m}
$$

$$
M_{max} = \frac{60{,}332 \cdot (12)^2}{8} = \frac{60{,}332 \cdot 144}{8} = 1{,}085{,}976 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
Z_{req} = \frac{1{,}085{,}976}{175 \times 10^6} \approx 6.21 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 621 \text{ cm}^3
$$

This is the **functional demand** on each transverse frame.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Function of Pillars (Stanchions) Under Deck Load

**Step 1.** Vertical load on a pillar = tributary deck load:

$$
P = q_{deck} \cdot A_{trib}
$$

where $q_{deck}$ is the deck pressure (kN/m²) and $A_{trib}$ is the tributary area supported by the pillar.

**Step 2.** Critical Euler buckling load for a pin-ended pillar:

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \, E \, I}{L_e^2}
$$

**Step 3.** For safety, design load:

$$
P \le \frac{P_{cr}}{\text{Safety Factor (typically 2.0 to 2.5)}}
$$

**Step 4.** Example — a steel pipe pillar of OD = 200 mm, wall thickness = 12 mm, $L_e$ = 5 m, supporting a deck load of 25 kN/m² over an 18 m² area:

$$
P = 25 \cdot 18 = 450 \text{ kN}
$$

Moment of inertia of the pipe:

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( D_o^4 - D_i^4 \right) = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( 0.200^4 - 0.176^4 \right)
$$

$$
D_i = 0.200 - 2(0.012) = 0.176 \text{ m}
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( 0.0016 - 0.0009595 \right) = \frac{\pi}{64} \cdot 0.0006405 = 3.144 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot 2.06 \times 10^{11} \cdot 3.144 \times 10^{-5}}{(5)^2}
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \cdot 2.06 \times 10^{11} \cdot 3.144 \times 10^{-5}}{25}
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \cdot 6.477 \times 10^{6}}{25} \approx 2.557 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} = 2557 \text{ kN}
$$

Design load check (SF = 2.0):

$$
P_{allow} = \frac{2557}{2.0} \approx 1279 \text{ kN} \quad \gg \quad 450 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark
$$

The pillar comfortably performs its **vertical-load-transfer function**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Function ↔ Load Type ↔ Structural Member

| Function Required | Load Type | Member Performing It | KTU High-Yield Insight |
|-------------------|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Resist longitudinal bending | Hogging/sagging moment | Keel + strength deck + side shell | Forms top & bottom flange of hull girder |
| Resist transverse shear | Vertical shear force | Side shell plating + deck plating | Acts as webs of box girder |
| Resist local sea pressure | Hydrostatic pressure | Frames + shell plating | Plate-panel bending + frame bending |
| Subdivide for safety | Flooding water pressure | Bulkheads (watertight) | Limits progressive flooding |
| Support deck loads | Gravity (cargo, equip.) | Deck plating + deck girders + pillars | Vertical load path: deck → pillar → bottom |
| Maintain hull shape | Racking & torsion | Transverse frames + bulkheads | Triangulated box resists distortion |
| Resist bow impact | Slamming / berthing | Stem + bow shell + collision bulkhead | First energy-absorbing zone |
| Support propulsion | Propeller thrust | Stern frame + shaft bracket + bottom shell | Thrust is transmitted into the hull girder |
| Damp roll motion | Roll angular acceleration | Bilge keel | Adds hydrodynamic resistance at bilge |
| Support superstructure | Inertial loads in seaway | Deckhouse + bulkheads + brackets | Must be **shear-resistant**, not just light |
