## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Set A]`
**List the four principal functions of a ship's keel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four principal functions of the keel are:

1. **Acts as the backbone** of the hull girder and resists longitudinal bending stresses (hogging and sagging).
2. **Provides the construction baseline** of reference for the entire hull during design and erection.
3. **Transmits docking loads** uniformly to the keel blocks when the ship is supported in dry dock.
4. **Anchors the garboard strake** and ensures the watertight integrity of the bottom shell.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often list only "backbone" — that is worth only 1 mark. You must name **at least four** distinct functions to score full 3 marks.

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP Set B]`
**Differentiate between the functions of watertight bulkheads and swash bulkheads.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Watertight Bulkhead | Swash Bulkhead |
|--------|---------------------|-----------------|
| **Primary Function** | Subdivide the hull to limit progressive flooding | Reduce free-surface effect of liquids in partially filled tanks |
| **Height** | Full depth from bottom to deck | Partial height (does not reach deck) |
| **Construction** | Heavily stiffened; tested for tightness | Lightly stiffened; not tested for tightness |
| **Effect on Stability** | Damage stability | Intact stability |
| **Examples** | Collision bulkhead, engine-room bulkhead | Bulkhead inside a ballast tank |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper]`

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **functions of the various structural members** of a ship's hull girder system. Discuss how each member contributes to the **three-tier load path hierarchy** (global, primary, local). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier has length $L = 180$ m, breadth $B = 28$ m, block coefficient $C_B = 0.80$, draft $T = 14$ m. Using the rule wave bending moment formula, compute the **design wave bending moment** (assume $f = 1.0$ and $C_w = 0.115$). If the permissible bending stress is **175 MPa**, determine the **required section modulus** $Z_{req}$. Comment on the **functional implication** for the strength deck and keel. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A ship's hull is a complex assembly of plates, stiffeners, girders, decks, and bulkheads. Their functions fall into three tiers.

**Tier 3 — Local Members (2 marks):**
- **Shell plating** resists direct hydrostatic pressure.
- **Local stiffeners (frames)** support shell between transverse members.
- **Deck plating** resists direct cargo/equipment load.

**Tier 2 — Primary Members (2 marks):**
- **Transverse frames, web frames, longitudinal girders** transfer local loads to the hull girder.
- **Stringers** reduce frame spans; **pillars** transfer vertical deck loads to the bottom.

**Tier 1 — Global Members (1 mark):**
- **Hull girder** (top flange = strength deck; bottom flange = keel + garboard; webs = side shell) resists total bending moment $M_w$ and shear force $F_w$ over the entire ship length.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Every member's function is determined by which tier(s) it participates in. Members in multiple tiers (e.g., sheer strake, keel plate) are heavily designed.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1.** Given: $L = 180$ m, $B = 28$ m, $C_B = 0.80$, $T = 14$ m, $f = 1.0$, $C_w = 0.115$, $\sigma_{perm} = 175$ MPa.

**Step 2.** Rule wave bending moment:

$$
M_w = f \cdot C_w \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot (C_B + 0.7)
$$

$$
M_w = 1.0 \cdot 0.115 \cdot (180)^2 \cdot 28 \cdot (0.80 + 0.7)
$$

$$
M_w = 0.115 \cdot 32400 \cdot 28 \cdot 1.50
$$

$$
M_w = 0.115 \cdot 32400 \cdot 42 = 0.115 \cdot 1{,}360{,}800
$$

$$
M_w \approx 156{,}492 \text{ kN·m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 156.5 \text{ MN·m}
$$

**Step 3.** Required section modulus:

$$
Z_{req} = \frac{M_w}{\sigma_{perm}} = \frac{156.5 \times 10^6}{175 \times 10^6} \approx 0.894 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4.** Functional implication (2 marks):
- **Strength deck** must act as the **top flange** of the hull girder and provide sufficient effective breadth × thickness to contribute to ≥ 40–50% of $Z_{req}$.
- **Keel and bottom shell** must provide the **bottom flange**, contributing the remaining section modulus.
- Together they must satisfy the **bending-resistance function** under the worst hogging/sagging wave.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Forgetting to add **0.7** inside the bracket $(C_B + 0.7)$ — this is a **1-mark deduction**.
> 2. Confusing **MN·m vs kN·m** — write the units **everywhere**.
> 3. Not commenting on **functional implication** — a numerical answer alone is worth only **5 of 7 marks**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Paper]`

**Q. (a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the **functions of transverse frames, longitudinal girders, stringers, and pillars** in a typical merchant ship. Discuss how these members interact to maintain the hull's **shape under racking loads**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A transverse frame is to be designed at the location where the shell depth below waterline is $h = 8$ m. Frame spacing is $s = 0.6$ m, frame span $\ell = 10$ m (assumed simply supported), and permissible bending stress is $\sigma_{perm} = 165$ MPa. Seawater density = 1025 kg/m³. Compute the **required section modulus** of the frame and discuss the **functional requirement** of the shell plating between frames. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Transverse Frames (2 marks):**
- Maintain transverse hull shape.
- Resist racking loads when the ship rolls.
- Support shell plating against local buckling.
- Transfer side pressure to decks/keel.

**Longitudinal Girders (1.5 marks):**
- Reduce unsupported span of frames.
- Participate in hull-girder longitudinal bending.
- Provide attachment for machinery foundations in some cases.

**Stringers (1.5 marks):**
- Horizontal members reducing vertical frame span.
- Located at the **turn of the bilge** and on bulkheads.
- Improve buckling resistance of the side shell.

**Pillars (1.5 marks):**
- Transfer vertical deck loads to the bottom shell.
- Prevent deck and bottom from "closing in."
- Critical in **bulk carriers** under heavy ore cargo.

**Racking interaction (0.5 marks):**
Under racking (transverse horizontal force during rolling), the hull acts as a **horizontal cantilever box beam**, and these four members together triangulate the box and maintain shape.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1.** Hydrostatic pressure at $h = 8$ m:

$$
p = \rho \, g \, h = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 8 = 80{,}442 \text{ Pa} \approx 80.44 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2.** Distributed load on the frame:

$$
w = p \cdot s = 80{,}442 \cdot 0.6 = 48{,}265 \text{ N/m} \approx 48.27 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 3.** Maximum bending moment (simply supported, UDL):

$$
M_{max} = \frac{w \, \ell^2}{8} = \frac{48{,}265 \cdot (10)^2}{8} = \frac{48{,}265 \cdot 100}{8}
$$

$$
M_{max} = 603{,}313 \text{ N·m} \approx 603.3 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 4.** Required section modulus:

$$
Z_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{perm}} = \frac{603{,}313}{165 \times 10^6} \approx 3.657 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
Z_{req} \approx 365.7 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 5.** Functional requirement of shell plating (1 mark):
The shell plating between frames functions as a **plate panel** simply supported on four edges (two frames + two longitudinal stiffeners). It must:
- Resist **plate bending** under $p$.
- Resist **buckling** (clamped edges, $k \approx 4$) with thickness ≥ $\sqrt{\dfrac{k \, p \, a^2}{12(1-\nu^2) \, \sigma_{cr}}}$.
- Contribute its **effective breadth** to the hull-girder bending function.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Using $g = 9.8$ instead of **9.81** — minor deduction but acceptable; using **10** is not.
> 2. Forgetting to convert **MPa → Pa** in denominator — common 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Not describing the shell plating function** — full marks require commentary, not just numbers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Functions of Ship Structures**

- ✅ The **six cardinal functions** are: resist loads, maintain shape, watertight integrity, load transfer, support systems, ensure safety.
- ✅ Hull structure is a **three-tier hierarchy**: local (plates/stiffeners) → primary (frames/girders) → global (hull girder).
- ✅ The **keel** is the bottom flange of the hull girder; the **strength deck** is the top flange; **side shell** is the web.
- ✅ Rule wave bending moment: $\mathbf{M_w = f \, C_w \, L^2 \, B \, (C_B + 0.7)}$ — memorize this verbatim.
- ✅ Section modulus requirement: $\mathbf{Z_{req} = M / \sigma_{perm}}$.
- ✅ Hydrostatic pressure: $\mathbf{p = \rho \, g \, h}$ with $\mathbf{\rho_{sw} = 1025\ kg/m^3}$.
- ✅ Frame section modulus: $\mathbf{Z_{req} = p \, s \, \ell^2 / (8 \, \sigma_{perm})}$.
- ✅ Euler pillar load: $\mathbf{P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / L_e^2}$.
- ✅ Bulkheads serve **damage stability** (flooding limitation); swash bulkheads serve **intact stability** (free-surface effect).
- ✅ Pillars + stringers form the **vertical load path**; frames + girders form the **transverse and longitudinal load paths**.
- ✅ The **sheer strake** performs multiple functions — always the **thickest plate** in the side shell.
- ✅ Always quote **units** in every numerical answer (kN, kN·m, m³, MPa).
- ✅ Always include the **functional implication** in 14-mark answers, not just the numbers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inner Bottom Plating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inner Bottom Plating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inner Bottom Plating** is the continuous plating forming the **upper boundary (deck) of the double bottom** in a ship. It is the horizontal structural member positioned directly **above the bottom shell plating** and **below the engine seating, cargo holds, or tanks** of the vessel. It is supported by **inner bottom longitudinals**, **bottom longitudinals**, and **bottom transverses/girders**.

The inner bottom plating effectively divides the hull into two distinct zones:
1. **The double bottom space (DB tanks)** — used for ballast water, fuel oil, or fresh water storage.
2. **The cargo/operational space above** — used for cargo, machinery, or accommodation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The inner bottom is classified under the **bottom structure** group, alongside the bottom shell plating, margin plate, center keel, and bottom girders. The classification society rule references are typically from **IRS (Indian Register of Shipping)** or **IACS** (International Association of Classification Societies) unified requirements.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **modern coffee cup with a sealed lid** floating in a bathtub. The cup has:
- An **outer shell** (the bathtub-facing wall) → this is the **Bottom Shell Plating**.
- An **inner shell** (the lid) → this is the **Inner Bottom Plating**.
- The **hollow space between them** → this is the **Double Bottom Tank**.

The "lid" (inner bottom) protects the contents (cargo) from leakage if the outer shell gets punctured. It also adds **structural stiffness**, much like how a car's floor pan reinforces the chassis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Insight:** In a **bulk carrier**, if the bottom shell is breached by grounding, the inner bottom prevents catastrophic flooding of the cargo holds. The ballast water in the double bottom also acts as a **counterweight** to improve ship stability during voyages.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Standard inner bottom thickness range:** **8 mm to 25 mm** (varies with ship size and rule requirements).
- **Minimum design pressure** is governed by the **head of ballast water** in the double bottom tank plus a **safety margin** as per classification rules.
- **Connection spacing:** Inner bottom plating is typically stiffened by **longitudinals spaced 600 mm to 900 mm** apart (depending on plate thickness).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Hierarchy of the Double Bottom

The double bottom structure is built up in a layered, load-bearing hierarchy. From bottom to top, the components are arranged as follows:

1. **Bottom Shell Plating** (lowest, faces seawater).
2. **Bottom Longitudinals** (run longitudinally, stiffen the bottom shell).
3. **Bottom Transverses / Floors** (run transversely at every frame station).
4. **Side Girders** (run longitudinally on either side of the centerline).
5. **Center Keel (Center Girder)** (vertical plate girder along the centerline).
6. **Inner Bottom Longitudinals** (run longitudinally, stiffen the inner bottom).
7. **Inner Bottom Plating** (top of the double bottom).
8. **Margin Plate** (where inner bottom meets the side shell).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Why" this layered design?** Each component has a specific load path. The bottom shell resists **hydrostatic pressure (external water)**; the inner bottom resists **hydrostatic pressure (internal ballast) plus cargo/equipment loads above**. The girders act as **primary load collectors** transferring forces to the side shell.

### 2.2 Functional Requirements of Inner Bottom Plating

- **Strength:** Must withstand the **maximum head of ballast water** in the double bottom tank when fully flooded.
- **Tightness:** Must be **water/oil-tight** to prevent leakage into cargo holds or machinery spaces.
- **Fatigue resistance:** Must resist cyclic loading from **slamming** and **wave-induced vibrations** in the bottom region (the most fatigue-prone area of a ship).
- **Corrosion allowance:** Typically includes a **1 mm to 3 mm corrosion addition** per IACS S21 standards.
- **Drainage:** Must be sloped or fitted with **drainage holes** at low points to allow water removal during inspection.

### 2.3 Connection Details (Critical for KTU Board Exams)

The inner bottom plating is **not** a free-floating plate. It is welded to:

| Connection | Structural Member | Welding Type |
|------------|------------------|--------------|
| Sides | **Margin Plate** | Double continuous fillet weld |
| Centerline (if no center girder) | **Centerline bulkhead plate** (vertical) | Double continuous weld |
| Fore/Aft ends | **Bulkhead plating** of forward/aft peak tanks | Slot weld + fillet weld |
| Top | **Inner bottom longitudinals** | Continuous fillet welds |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the key design equations used for inner bottom plating per IACS / IRS rules.

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| Design pressure on inner bottom | $p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h + p_{0}$ | $\rho$ = density of ballast ($\approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$), $h$ = tank head, $p_0$ = design margin |
| Required plate thickness (IACS S21) | $t = \frac{s \cdot \sqrt{p}}{k}$ | $s$ = stiffener spacing, $k$ = material factor ($\approx 5.5$ for mild steel) |
| Slenderness ratio check | $\beta = \frac{s}{t} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{k}}$ | Must be $\le 1.0$ for adequate strength |
| Corrosion addition | $t_{total} = t_{calc} + t_{corr}$ | $t_{corr} = 1 \text{ mm to } 3 \text{ mm}$ typical |
| Minimum thickness (IRS) | $t_{min} = 0.04L + 5 \text{ (mm)}$ | $L$ = Rule length in metres; for bottom structure |
| Net thickness | $t_{net} = t_{gross} - t_{corr}$ | Used in all strength calculations |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Safety:** Forms the **second line of defense** against flooding after a grounding or collision.
- **Operational efficiency:** Provides **ballast capacity** for trim and stability adjustments during voyages.
- **Structural integrity:** Acts as a **horizontal diaphragm**, dramatically increasing the **torsional rigidity** of the hull girder (resistance to twisting under wave loads).
- **Regulatory compliance:** Mandatory under **SOLAS Reg. II-1** (Subdivision and Stability) and **MARPOL** for oil tankers (double hull requirement).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Required Inner Bottom Plate Thickness

This derivation follows the **IACS S21 / IRS unified requirement** procedure. Students should memorize this workflow for KTU 14-mark questions.

**Given:**
- Rule length of ship, $L = 150 \text{ m}$.
- Spacing of inner bottom longitudinals, $s = 750 \text{ mm} = 0.75 \text{ m}$.
- Maximum ballast head in double bottom, $h = 2.5 \text{ m}$.
- Seawater density with ballast, $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- Acceleration factor (slamming), $a_{sl} = 1.0$ (for inner bottom in non-slamming region).
- Material factor for mild steel, $k = 5.5$.
- Corrosion addition, $t_{corr} = 2 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1: Compute the design pressure.**

$$p_{static} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

Substituting values:

$$p_{static} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times 2.5 \text{ m}$$

Carrying out the multiplication:

$$p_{static} = 25139.625 \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 25.14 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Apply the dynamic design pressure correction.**

For the inner bottom, the slamming effect is reduced (unlike the bottom shell). The design pressure is:

$$p_{design} = p_{static} \times a_{sl} = 25.14 \times 1.0 = 25.14 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute the slenderness ratio (assumed plate thickness as start).**

Assume $t_{trial} = 12 \text{ mm} = 0.012 \text{ m}$.

$$\beta = \frac{s}{t} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{k}}$$

Substituting:

$$\beta = \frac{0.75}{0.012} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{25.14}{5.5}}$$

$$= 62.5 \cdot \sqrt{4.571}$$

$$= 62.5 \cdot 2.138$$

$$\beta = 133.6$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:** The slenderness ratio $\beta$ is **much greater than 1.0**, which means the assumed 12 mm plate is **inadequate**. The IACS S21 formula $t = \frac{s\sqrt{p}}{k}$ is only valid for **stiffened panels where the slenderness is controlled**. For the inner bottom plating as a **plating member**, we use a different approach.

**Step 3 (Corrected): Use the IACS S21 plating thickness formula.**

$$t_{req,net} = s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{k}} \times C_{f}$$

Where $C_f$ is a correction factor (typically 1.0 for standard inner bottom).

$$t_{req,net} = 0.75 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{25.14}{5.5}}$$

$$= 0.75 \cdot \sqrt{4.571}$$

$$= 0.75 \cdot 2.138$$

$$t_{req,net} = 1.603 \text{ m}$$

This result is **unrealistic** because the formula gives metres when applied naively. The correct interpretation:

The formula gives the thickness in **mm** when $p$ is in **kN/m²** and $s$ in **mm**. Let us redo with proper units.

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Conversion Correction:** $p = 25.14 \text{ kN/m}^2 = 0.02514 \text{ N/mm}^2$. $k = 5.5$ (with implicit units $\text{N}^{1/2} \cdot \text{mm}^{-1/2}$). Then:

$$t = s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{k}} \text{ (in mm)}$$

$$t = 750 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.02514}{5.5}}$$

$$= 750 \cdot \sqrt{0.004571}$$

$$= 750 \cdot 0.0676$$

$$t_{net} = 50.7 \text{ mm}$$

This is still very thick. In practice, the longitudinals **share the load**, so the effective span is reduced. The inner bottom longitudinals are spaced closer, and the effective bending is reduced.

**Step 4: Apply the actual design with effective span (between stiffeners).**

If the actual stiffener spacing is reduced to $s = 600 \text{ mm}$:

$$t_{net} = 600 \cdot 0.0676 = 40.6 \text{ mm}$$

Even this is excessive. This indicates that for such ballast heads, the **inner bottom must be stiffened** with a deeper section, and the **plate between stiffeners** is checked separately.

**Step 5: Final required thickness with corrosion addition.**

$$t_{gross} = t_{net} + t_{corr} = 40.6 + 2 = 42.6 \text{ mm}$$

This is uncommonly thick for a typical inner bottom, indicating our initial ballast head assumption (2.5 m) is on the high side. **For a typical merchant ship with $h = 1.5 \text{ m}$:**

$$p = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 = 15083.4 \text{ N/m}^2 = 0.01508 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$t_{net} = 600 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.01508}{5.5}} = 600 \cdot \sqrt{0.002742} = 600 \cdot 0.0524 = 31.4 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Takeaway:** A typical inner bottom plating thickness is in the range **10 mm to 20 mm**. The large values above indicate that the design head must be reduced, or the inner bottom is treated as a **stiffened panel with primary supporting members**.

### 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Design Step | Formula | Result | Engineering Interpretation |
|-------------|---------|--------|---------------------------|
| Static pressure | $p = \rho g h$ | 15.08 kN/m² (h=1.5 m) | Hydrostatic ballast load |
| Net thickness | $t_{net} = s \sqrt{p/k}$ | ~31 mm (conservative) | Theoretical minimum |
| Gross thickness | $t_{gross} = t_{net} + t_{corr}$ | ~33 mm | Adds 2 mm corrosion |
| Practical thickness | Rule minimum | 10–20 mm | Real ships use stiffened design |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Double Bottom

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the inner bottom plating integrates with the surrounding structural members. The labels are kept in **clean uppercase alphanumeric text** with no markdown formatting to ensure successful rendering.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[HULL GIRDER] --> B[BOTTOM STRUCTURE GROUP]
    B --> C[BOTTOM SHELL PLATING]
    B --> D[INNER BOTTOM PLATING]
    B --> E[MARGIN PLATE]
    B --> F[CENTER KEEL]
    B --> G[INNER BOTTOM LONGITUDINALS]
    B --> H[BOTTOM TRANSVERSE FLOORS]
    B --> I[SIDE GIRDERS]
    C --> J[HYDROSTATIC SEAWATER PRESSURE]
    D --> K[HYDROSTATIC BALLAST PRESSURE]
    D --> L[CARGO OR MACHINERY LOAD ABOVE]
    E --> M[CONNECT INNER BOTTOM TO SIDE SHELL]
    F --> N[VERTICAL STRENGTH MEMBER]
    G --> O[STIFFEN INNER BOTTOM PLATE]
    H --> P[TRANSVERSE STRENGTH MEMBER]
    I --> Q[REDUCE SPAN OF FLOORS]
    D --> R[SECONDARY DEFENSE LINE]
    D --> S[HORIZONTAL DIAPHRAGM EFFECT]
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Block Diagram (Midship Region)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents a **simplified cross-section** of the double bottom structure, showing the spatial relationship between key components. The diagram uses purely alphanumeric node identifiers and plain uppercase text labels.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SHIP_BOTTOM_REGION
        NODE1[BOTTOM_SHELL_PLATING]:::shell
        NODE2[BALLAST_TANK_SPACE]:::tank
        NODE3[INNER_BOTTOM_PLATING]:::plate
        NODE4[CARGO_HOLD_OR_MACHINERY_SPACE]:::top
        NODE5[MARGIN_PLATE_LEFT]:::margin
        NODE6[MARGIN_PLATE_RIGHT]:::margin
        NODE7[CENTER_KEEL_VERTICAL]:::center
        NODE8[BOTTOM_LONGITUDINAL]:::long
        NODE9[INNER_BOTTOM_LONGITUDINAL]:::long
        NODE10[TRANSVERSE_FLOOR]:::floor
    end

    NODE1 --> NODE2
    NODE2 --> NODE3
    NODE3 --> NODE4
    NODE3 --> NODE5
    NODE3 --> NODE6
    NODE3 --> NODE7
    NODE2 --> NODE8
    NODE3 --> NODE9
    NODE2 --> NODE10

    classDef shell fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#003566,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef tank fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#8b5a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef plate fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#3a0ca3,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef top fill:#caffbf,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef margin fill:#ffc8dd,stroke:#800f2f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef center fill:#f1c0e8,stroke:#5a189a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef long fill:#ffadad,stroke:#660708,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef floor fill:#fdffb6,stroke:#7d4f00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Schematic Explanation of the Cross-Section

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram (top to bottom):**
>
> 1. **CARGO_HOLD_OR_MACHINERY_SPACE** — operational zone above the inner bottom.
> 2. **INNER_BOTTOM_PLATING** — the key structural plate forming the upper boundary of the double bottom.
> 3. **BALLAST_TANK_SPACE** — hollow region between inner bottom and bottom shell.
> 4. **BOTTOM_SHELL_PLATING** — outer plate in contact with seawater.
> 5. **MARGIN_PLATE_LEFT** and **MARGIN_PLATE_RIGHT** — connect inner bottom to the side shell on port and starboard sides.
> 6. **CENTER_KEEL_VERTICAL** — runs along the centerline, providing vertical strength.
> 7. **BOTTOM_LONGITUDINAL** and **INNER_BOTTOM_LONGITUDINAL** — stiffen the plates.
> 8. **TRANSVERSE_FLOOR** — runs athwartships, supporting the bottom.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Define the term "Inner Bottom Plating" and state its primary functions in a ship's double bottom construction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The inner bottom plating is the **continuous plating that forms the upper boundary of the double bottom**, positioned between the bottom shell plating and the cargo hold / machinery space. Its primary functions are:
- Acts as the **top of the double bottom tank** (ballast, fuel, or fresh water).
- Provides a **second line of defense** against flooding.
- Increases the **torsional rigidity** of the hull girder.
- Forms a working platform for **machinery seating and cargo loading**.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **What is a "Margin Plate" and how is it related to the inner bottom plating?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The margin plate is the **outermost strake of the inner bottom plating**, fitted in line with the side shell at a distance equal to the **depth of the double bottom** from the baseline. It connects the inner bottom plating to the side shell and is typically **doubled in thickness** as it forms a critical structural junction subject to high shear stresses.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — 14 Marks**
> **(a)** Draw a neat **midship cross-section** of a typical merchant ship showing the double bottom structure. Label all key components including inner bottom plating, margin plate, center keel, side girders, and floors. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **functions of the inner bottom plating** in detail, with specific reference to its role in **safety, structural strength, and operational efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Midship Cross-Section Diagram (7 marks):**

A well-labeled sketch should include:
- **Outer shell (bottom shell plating)** with a thickness of $t_b$.
- **Double bottom tank space** (typically $h = 0.8 \text{ m to } 2.0 \text{ m}$ deep).
- **Inner bottom plating** with thickness $t_{ib}$.
- **Margin plates** on both sides, of thickness $1.4 t_{ib}$ to $1.6 t_{ib}$.
- **Center keel (center girder)** — vertical plate running along the centerline.
- **Side girders** — longitudinal plates on either side of the centerline.
- **Bottom transverses (floors)** — at every frame station.
- **Bottom longitudinals and inner bottom longitudinals** — for plate stiffening.
- **Limber holes** at the margin plate to allow tank drainage.
- **Air pipes, sounding pipes, and manholes** on the inner bottom.

Marking distribution: 3 marks for proper proportions, 2 marks for correct labels, 2 marks for engineering details (limber holes, stiffeners).

**(b) Functions of Inner Bottom Plating (7 marks):**

1. **Safety — Second Line of Defense:** In the event of a grounding or collision, if the bottom shell is breached, the inner bottom prevents immediate flooding of the cargo holds, giving the crew time to take corrective action. **(2 marks)**

2. **Structural Strength — Torsional Rigidity:** The inner bottom, together with the deck plating, forms a **closed box girder** (the hull girder). It dramatically increases the **torsional constant $J$** of the section, reducing twisting moments under oblique wave loading. **(2 marks)**

3. **Operational Functions:**
   - **Ballast storage:** Allows the ship to adjust trim and draft by flooding/emptying tanks.
   - **Fuel oil/fresh water storage:** Reduces the need for separate bunker tanks.
   - **Machinery seating:** Provides a stiff, flat platform for main engines and auxiliaries. **(2 marks)**

4. **Maintenance and Inspection:** Provides a working floor for inspection, cleaning, and repairs of the double bottom space. **(1 mark)**

> **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 14 Marks**
> **(a)** Discuss the **connection details of the inner bottom plating** with adjacent structural members, including the margin plate, center girder, and bulkheads. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A double bottom tank has a ballast head of **$h = 1.8 \text{ m}$**. The inner bottom longitudinals are spaced at **$s = 700 \text{ mm}$**. Using the IACS S21 formula, compute the **required plate thickness** for mild steel with a corrosion addition of **2 mm**. Take material factor $k = 5.5$ and seawater density $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Connection Details (7 marks):**

1. **Connection to Margin Plate:** The inner bottom strake adjacent to the side shell is the **margin plate**. It is connected by a **double continuous fillet weld** on both sides. The margin plate thickness is **$1.4 t$ to $1.6 t$** of the standard inner bottom, where $t$ is the inner bottom plate thickness. **(2 marks)**

2. **Connection to Center Girder:** The inner bottom plating is welded to the **flat bar or flange** of the center girder. A **double continuous fillet weld** is used, with a scallop (slot) provided to ensure full penetration. **(2 marks)**

3. **Connection to Bulkheads:** At the fore and aft ends, the inner bottom meets the **collision bulkhead** and **aft peak bulkhead**. The connection uses a **slot weld + fillet weld** combination to maintain watertight integrity. **(1.5 marks)**

4. **Connection to Stiffeners:** Inner bottom longitudinals are welded to the plate using **continuous fillet welds**, with intermittent welds permitted in non-critical zones. **(1.5 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute the design pressure.**

$$p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

$$p = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1.8$$

Carrying out the multiplication:

$$p = 18098.85 \text{ N/m}^2$$

Converting to N/mm²:

$$p = 0.01810 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Apply the IACS S21 thickness formula.**

$$t_{net} = s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{k}}$$

$$t_{net} = 700 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.01810}{5.5}}$$

$$= 700 \cdot \sqrt{0.003291}$$

$$= 700 \cdot 0.05736$$

$$t_{net} = 40.15 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Add corrosion allowance.**

$$t_{gross} = t_{net} + t_{corr}$$

$$t_{gross} = 40.15 + 2.0 = 42.15 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Adopt the next standard plate thickness.**

Standard shipbuilding plate thicknesses are in 1 mm increments. The required gross thickness is **42 mm** (or next higher standard size, 44 mm depending on yard practice).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Error:** The most common mistake is mixing SI units (N/m²) with mm. Always convert pressure to **N/mm²** before using the IACS S21 formula. **(−2 marks penalty)**
> 2. **Forgetting Corrosion Addition:** Students often forget to add $t_{corr}$. The final answer **must include** the corrosion allowance. **(−1 mark penalty)**
> 3. **Wrong Formula Application:** Some students use the **bottom shell** formula instead of the **inner bottom** formula. The inner bottom has **lower slamming factors**, so the design pressure is different. **(−2 marks penalty)**
> 4. **Skipping Units in Final Answer:** Always write the final thickness with units (e.g., **42 mm**), not just the number. **(−0.5 mark penalty)**
> 5. **No Sketch or Diagram:** For 7-mark sub-parts involving a diagram, a missing or unlabeled sketch loses **up to 2 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Inner Bottom Plating** forms the **upper boundary of the double bottom** tank.
- It is positioned between the **bottom shell** and the **cargo/machinery space**.
- The **margin plate** is the outermost strake of the inner bottom, **doubled in thickness**.
- The inner bottom is connected via **continuous fillet welds** to all surrounding members.
- The inner bottom **increases hull girder torsional rigidity** — a critical structural function.
- It acts as a **second line of defense** in case of bottom shell damage.
- **Design pressure** is governed by **maximum ballast head**, not slamming (unlike the bottom shell).
- The **IACS S21 / IRS formula** for thickness is $t = s \sqrt{p/k} + t_{corr}$.
- Standard inner bottom thickness range: **10 mm to 20 mm** for typical merchant ships.
- The inner bottom must be **watertight** to prevent leakage between ballast and cargo spaces.
- **Limber holes** in the margin plate allow water to flow to the drain suctions.
- For **oil tankers**, double bottom is mandatory under **MARPOL Reg. 19** (double hull).
- The **center girder** is the primary vertical strength member in the double bottom.
- **Side girders** reduce the unsupported span of the bottom floors.
- The inner bottom plating, together with the deck, forms a **closed box section** that resists **torsion**.
- **Corrosion addition** of **1–3 mm** must always be added to the net calculated thickness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEN614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Floors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In ship structural engineering, a **Floor** is defined as a vertical (or near-vertical) transverse plate girder fitted inside the double bottom of a ship, running across the ship's breadth (athwartships) and connecting the port and starboard bottom shell plating. Floors are an integral part of the ship's **bottom structure** and work in conjunction with the **bottom shell plating**, **inner bottom plating**, **margin plates**, and **girders** to form a rigid grillage capable of resisting hydrostatic pressure, cargo loads (in dry cargo vessels), ballast pressure (in tankers), and dynamic forces arising from wave slamming and ship motions.

Floors may be fabricated as:
- **Solid (Plate) Floors** — continuous plates with or without lightening holes.
- **Bracket Floors** — consisting of a center bracket and two side brackets connected to the bottom shell.
- **Open Floors** — comprising a face plate and a web plate with cut-outs.
- **Pipe Floors** — lightening holes replaced by pipes.

According to **Classification Society Rules** (e.g., Lloyd's Register, DNV, IRS, BV, ABS), floors are mandatory transverse strengthening members in single-bottom ships and form the backbone of the **double-bottom tank structure** in all modern ocean-going vessels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Floors are a core sub-topic under Module 2 (Ship Structural Framing). Students must master the **types of floors**, **scantling determination**, **design loads**, and **structural detailing** including connections to keel, margin plate, and bottom shell.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Skeleton of a Building

Imagine a multi-storey building where the **concrete slab floor of each storey** is supported by **beams running across the building's width**. These beams transfer the load of people, furniture, and the slab itself down to the columns and eventually to the foundation.

A ship floating in water faces an almost identical problem — except the "load" comes from **water pressure pushing up from below** and **cargo or ballast pushing down from above**. The **bottom shell plating of the ship acts like the floor slab**, and the **floors act like the transverse beams**, transferring these pressures to the **side shell plating** (the building's outer walls) and the **keel** (the foundation).

Just as a building cannot stand without strong beams between floors, a ship cannot survive the ocean's pressure without well-designed floors running across its bottom.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| **Floor spacing** (frame spacing in double bottom) | **2.0 m to 3.5 m** typically |
| **Minimum thickness of solid floor plate** | As per Rule t = f(s, L, B) |
| **Lightening hole diameter** | ≤ **0.6 × floor depth** (typical rule) |
| **Distance between lightening holes** | ≥ **1.5 × hole diameter** |
| **Floor depth (height)** | Equal to double bottom height (typically **0.8 m – 1.8 m**) |
| **Standard ship bottom pressure head** | **2.5 m to 4.0 m** of seawater (for solid floors in dry cargo) |

> [!NOTE]
> The exact floor scantlings depend on the **Rule Set** (Lloyd's, DNV, IRS) and the **ship type, length, and service area**. KTU examiners typically expect application of standard plate-formula or the simplified Rule formula.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Requirements of Floors

Floors must satisfy the following structural demands:

1. **Transverse Strength** — Maintain the cross-sectional shape of the ship against external water pressure.
2. **Longitudinal Strength Participation** — Together with the bottom plating, floors contribute a portion to the hull girder section modulus.
3. **Load Distribution** — Distribute localized loads from sea bottom (in case of grounding) and from cargo over a larger area.
4. **Support for Inner Bottom** — Act as supports for the inner bottom plating in dry cargo ships (when inner bottom is present).
5. **Boundary for Tanks** — In tankers and bulk carriers, floors act as the transverse boundaries of ballast and cargo tanks.

## 2.2 Types of Floors — Structured Logic

### A. By Construction
- **Solid Floors** — Made of a single rolled or fabricated plate, no cut-outs. Used where high strength is needed (e.g., engine room, forepeak, afterpeak).
- **Open Floors** — Face plate + web plate with circular or oval cut-outs (lightening holes) to reduce weight and allow passage for piping/inspection.
- **Bracket Floors** — Three brackets welded to the bottom plating; used in small vessels or as auxiliary members.
- **Pipe Floors** — Lightening holes replaced by fitted pipes (used historically and in some special vessels).

### B. By Location & Function
- **Watertight Floors** — Fully solid, used at the boundaries of compartments and tanks (e.g., collision bulkhead floor, afterpeak floor).
- **Non-watertight Floors** — Open or bracket-type, used in the central cargo/ballast region.
- **Docking Floors** — Extra-reinforced floors at the docking positions (under the bilge keel ends and side docking blocks).
- **Centerline Floors** — Special floors aligned on the centerline, often forming part of the duct keel.

## 2.3 Structural Action — Why Floors Work

Floors act as **beams fixed at both ends** (built-in at port and starboard bilges) and loaded by **bottom shell pressure**. The reaction from the side shell resists this load, creating **end fixity moments**. To analyze a floor:

- **Effective span (l)** = Distance between side shell supports ≈ Ship's **moulded breadth (B)** minus the bilge radius.
- **Effective depth (d)** = Double bottom height (h_db).
- **Effective width (b)** = Floor plate spacing (s_f) for bending, or the full breadth for overall strength.
- The floor behaves like a **fixed-fixed beam** under uniform pressure loading.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Floor Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes all critical formulas for floor scantling calculation. Memorize these for KTU board examinations.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Floor plate thickness (solid floor, IRS)** | $t = s_f \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{k_1}} + t_c$ | mm | $s_f$ = floor spacing (mm); $p$ = pressure (kN/m²); $k_1$ = coefficient |
| 2 | **Required section modulus (open floor)** | $Z = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_{\text{perm}}}$ | cm³ | $M$ = B.M. at critical section; $\sigma_{\text{perm}}$ = allowable stress |
| 3 | **Maximum B.M. (fixed-fixed beam, UDL)** | $M_{\max} = \dfrac{p \cdot l^2}{12}$ | kN·m | At supports |
| 4 | **Maximum S.F. (fixed-fixed beam)** | $V_{\max} = \dfrac{p \cdot l}{2}$ | kN | At supports |
| 5 | **Floor web height (typical)** | $d = h_{db}$ | mm | Equals double-bottom height |
| 6 | **Face plate area (open floor)** | $A_{fp} = \dfrac{V}{h_w \cdot \tau_{\text{perm}}}$ | mm² | For shear transfer |
| 7 | **Pressure head for solid floor** | $h = T + h_w$ | m | $T$ = draught, $h_w$ = wave allowance |
| 8 | **Floor as grillage (effective width)** | $b_{\text{eff}} = s_f$ | mm | For bending calc |
| 9 | **Hogging moment contribution** | $\sigma = \dfrac{M_{\text{hog}}}{Z_{\text{floor}}}$ | N/mm² | Stress in floor plate |
| 10 | **Web plate shear stress** | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{h_w \cdot t_w}$ | N/mm² | Must be ≤ $\tau_{\text{perm}}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe symbol restriction:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` inside markdown tables. The expressions above use display-math blocks; do not embed raw `|` characters within table cells.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bulk Carriers & Tankers**: Floors form double-bottom tanks for ballast, critical for vessel trim and stability.
- **Naval Warships**: Solid floors at machinery spaces provide resistance to shock loading from underwater explosions.
- **Submarines**: Floors contribute significantly to the pressure hull grillage.
- **Repair & Inspection**: Open floors allow access for surveyors to inspect the bottom plating — a major regulatory requirement.
- **Conversion Projects**: Floor scantlings determine whether a vessel can be lengthened or uprated in cargo capacity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bending Moment on a Solid Floor (Fixed-Fixed Beam)

Consider a solid floor modeled as a beam **fixed at both ends** (port and starboard bilge) of effective span $l$, loaded by a uniformly distributed load $w$ (kN/m) from the bottom shell pressure.

**Step 1: Identify Loading**
The hydrostatic + dynamic bottom pressure is $p$ (kN/m²). The floor supports a strip of width equal to the floor spacing $s_f$. Hence:

$$w = p \cdot s_f$$

**Step 2: Apply Standard Fixed-Fixed Beam Formulas**
For a fixed-fixed beam with UDL:

$$M_{\text{support}} = -\dfrac{w \cdot l^2}{12}$$

$$M_{\text{mid-span}} = +\dfrac{w \cdot l^2}{24}$$

**Step 3: Identify the Critical Section**
For a fixed-fixed beam, the **maximum bending moment** occurs at the supports (built-in ends), not at mid-span. The mid-span moment is half the support moment in magnitude.

$$M_{\max} = \dfrac{w \cdot l^2}{12} = \dfrac{p \cdot s_f \cdot l^2}{12}$$

**Step 4: Maximum Shear Force**
The maximum shear occurs at the supports:

$$V_{\max} = \dfrac{w \cdot l}{2} = \dfrac{p \cdot s_f \cdot l}{2}$$

**Step 5: Convert to Section Modulus Requirement**

$$Z_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{M_{\max}}{\sigma_{\text{perm}}}$$

Substituting:

$$Z_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{p \cdot s_f \cdot l^2}{12 \cdot \sigma_{\text{perm}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This $Z_{\text{req}}$ is provided by the combined action of the floor plate and the attached bottom shell plating acting as a flange (effective breadth $s_f$).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Floor Plate Thickness (IRS Rule Form)

The **Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)** and several Classification Societies provide a direct plate-thickness formula for solid floors.

**Step 1: Reference pressure**
The reference bottom pressure head is taken as:

$$h_{\text{ref}} = T + h_w + h_c$$

where:
- $T$ = design draught (m)
- $h_w$ = wave pressure allowance (typically **2.5 m** for vessels in unrestricted service)
- $h_c$ = correction for ship length / location

**Step 2: Compute effective pressure**

$$p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h_{\text{ref}}$$

For seawater, $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$p \approx 10.06 \cdot h_{\text{ref}} \;\; \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Apply Rule Formula**
The required floor plate thickness is:

$$t = s_f \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{k_1}} + t_c$$

where:
- $s_f$ = floor spacing in **mm**
- $p$ = pressure in **kN/m²**
- $k_1$ = coefficient (depends on material grade; for mild steel, $k_1 \approx 245$)
- $t_c$ = corrosion allowance (**0.5 mm to 1.5 mm** typically, **1.0 mm** in many Rules)

**Step 4: Numerical Worked Example**
Let $s_f = 2500$ mm, $h_{\text{ref}} = 12$ m, $k_1 = 245$, $t_c = 1.0$ mm.

$$p = 10.06 \times 12 = 120.72 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2$$

$$t = 2500 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{120.72}{245}} + 1.0$$

$$t = 2500 \cdot \sqrt{0.4927} + 1.0$$

$$t = 2500 \cdot 0.7019 + 1.0$$

$$t = 1754.8 + 1.0 \approx 1755.8 \;\; \text{mm}$$

This is clearly absurd — indicating that **a solid floor across the full breadth would be over-conservative**, which is precisely why most classification rules use **effective shear** or **net section** calculations considering lightening holes, or use the floor **as part of a grillage**.

**Step 5: Realistic Engineering Adjustment**
In practice, the solid floor is **not** designed to span the full breadth unsupported. It works together with:
- The **side shell** (acting as a flange at the ends)
- The **inner bottom** (acting as a compression flange)
- The **bottom shell** (acting as a tension flange)

Hence, the **floor web** carries primarily **shear**, and the floor plate itself is sized for **shear and local pressure**, not bending across the full span.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Section Modulus of an Open Floor

For an **open floor** (web + face plate), the section modulus about the neutral axis parallel to the bottom shell is:

$$Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$$

where the moment of inertia $I$ of the T-section is:

$$I = \dfrac{t_w \cdot d^3}{12} + A_{fp} \cdot \left(\dfrac{d}{2} + \dfrac{t_{fp}}{2} - \bar{y}\right)^2 + b_{fp} \cdot t_{fp} \cdot \left(\bar{y} - \dfrac{t_{fp}}{2}\right)^2$$

with

$$\bar{y} = \dfrac{A_{fp} \cdot (d + t_{fp})/2 + (t_w \cdot d^2/2)}{A_{fp} + t_w \cdot d}$$

- $t_w$ = web thickness
- $d$ = web depth
- $b_{fp}$, $t_{fp}$ = face plate breadth and thickness
- $A_{fp} = b_{fp} \cdot t_{fp}$

**Set the condition:**

$$\dfrac{M_{\max}}{Z} \leq \sigma_{\text{perm}}$$

Solving for the face plate area or web depth yields the required scantling.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Floor Types

| Criterion | Solid Floor | Open Floor | Bracket Floor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Weight** | Heaviest | Moderate | Lightest |
| **Strength** | Highest | Moderate-High | Lowest |
| **Cost** | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Inspection Access** | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| **Use Region** | Engine room, peaks | Cargo/ballast tanks | Small ships, aux. frames |
| **Watertight?** | Yes (if required) | Generally no | No |
| **Rule Compliance** | All Rules | Most Rules | Limited (small vessels) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Floor in Double Bottom Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bottom Shell Plating - Acts as Tension Flange] --> B[Floor - Transverse Plate Girder]
    C[Inner Bottom Plating - Acts as Compression Flange] --> B
    B --> D[Port Bilge Bracket]
    B --> E[Starboard Bilge Bracket]
    D --> F[Port Side Shell - Acts as Support]
    E --> G[Starboard Side Shell - Acts as Support]
    B --> H[Centerline Strut or Center Girder]
    H --> I[Keel Plate]
    B --> J[Lightening Holes - Weight Reduction]
    B --> K[Floor Face Plate - Local Stiffening]
    L[Hydrostatic Pressure - p] --> A
    M[Cargo or Ballast Load] --> C
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Load Path Through a Floor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Sea Water Pressure] -->|Acts upward| S1[Bottom Shell Plating]
    S1 -->|Distributes to| F1[Floor Plate and Web]
    F1 -->|Transfers shear to| B1[Bilge Bracket]
    B1 -->|Reacts at| SS1[Side Shell]
    P2[Cargo or Ballast Load] -->|Acts downward| S2[Inner Bottom Plating]
    S2 -->|Distributes to| F1
    F1 -->|Bending resisted by| FP1[Face Plate and Flanges]
    F1 -->|Contributes to| HG1[Hull Girder Longitudinal Strength]
```

## 4.3 Detailed Component Architecture of an Open Floor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OF[Open Floor Assembly] --> WP[Web Plate - Vertical Plate]
    OF --> FP[Face Plate - Horizontal Flange]
    OF --> LH[Lightening Holes - Staggered]
    OF --> WTPS[Weld to Bottom Shell - Fillet Weld]
    OF --> WTIB[Weld to Inner Bottom - Fillet Weld]
    OF --> WTBK[Bilge Bracket Connection]
    OF --> RE[Reinforcement Ring around Lightening Holes]
    LH --> RE
```

## 4.4 Hierarchy of Structural Action

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HULL[Hull Girder - Overall Longitudinal Bending] --> TG[Transverse Grillage]
    TG --> SH[Side Shell Support]
    TG --> FL[Floor Members]
    TG --> GD[Girders - Longitudinal]
    FL --> ST1[Shear Transfer]
    FL --> ST2[Bending Participation]
    FL --> ST3[Pressure Resistance]
    GD --> ST4[Longitudinal Pressure Distribution]
    SH --> ST5[Reaction Support]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above depict the **functional interaction** between floors and adjacent structural members. A physical section drawing would show the floor as a vertical plate (in solid floor) or web + face plate (in open floor) connecting the bottom shell to the inner bottom.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define a floor in a ship structure. List any four functions of a floor.**

**Model Answer:**
A **floor** is a vertical transverse plate girder fitted inside the bottom of a ship, running across the ship's breadth from port to starboard bilge, connecting the bottom shell plating and (where present) the inner bottom plating.

**Functions of a floor:**
1. Provides **transverse strength** to resist bottom shell pressure and maintain hull cross-sectional form.
2. Acts as a **support for the inner bottom plating** and bottom shell plating in a double-bottom ship.
3. Contributes to the **longitudinal hull girder section modulus** as part of the effective bottom structure.
4. Distributes **localized loads** (e.g., docking forces, grounding loads) over a wider bottom area.
5. Forms the **transverse boundaries** of ballast and cargo tanks in tankers and bulk carriers.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a solid floor and an open floor.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Solid Floor | Open Floor |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | Continuous plate, no cut-outs | Web + face plate with lightening holes |
| **Weight** | Heavier | Lighter |
| **Strength** | Higher bending and shear capacity | Adequate for non-critical regions |
| **Use** | Engine room, peaks, watertight boundaries | General cargo/ballast regions |
| **Inspection Access** | Limited | Excellent (passage through holes) |
| **Piping/Electrical** | Cannot pass through | Easy routing of services |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]

**A) (a)** Explain with neat sketches the **different types of floors** used in ship bottom structures. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The principal types of floors used in ship construction are:

**1. Solid (Plate) Floor**
A solid floor consists of a single rolled or fabricated plate fitted vertically between the bottom shell and the inner bottom. It may include small lightening holes near the neutral axis. Used where high strength and watertight integrity are required.

**2. Open Floor**
An open floor comprises a **vertical web plate** and a **horizontal face plate**, with circular or oval **lightening holes** cut in the web. The face plate is usually fitted at the top (against the inner bottom) to provide additional section modulus. Lightening holes reduce weight and permit inspection.

**3. Bracket Floor**
A bracket floor consists of a **center bracket** (on the centerline) and two **side brackets** at the bilges. No continuous plate is used; instead, triangular brackets welded to the bottom shell provide transverse stiffness. Used in small vessels and as auxiliary floors.

**4. Pipe Floor**
A pipe floor is a variation of the open floor where the lightening holes are replaced by **fitted pipes** welded into the cut-outs. The pipes maintain the structural continuity while reducing weight. Now largely obsolete but historically used.

**5. Special Floors**
- **Docking Floors** — Extra thickness at the docking position.
- **Watertight Floors** — Solid floors fitted at collision bulkhead, engine room bulkhead, etc.
- **Centerline Floors** — Floors on the centerline forming part of the duct keel in tankers.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **draw only one type** or **confuse bracket floors with side brackets of girders**. Always sketch all main types, label the web, face plate, lightening holes, and connection welds. Marks are awarded for clarity and completeness of sketch.

---

**A) (b)** A ship has a moulded breadth of **B = 24 m**, a double bottom height of **h_db = 1.2 m**, and a floor spacing of **s_f = 2.5 m**. The bottom shell pressure head is **h_ref = 12 m** of seawater. Determine the **support bending moment** on a solid floor treated as a fixed-fixed beam. Take ρ = 1.025 t/m³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the bottom shell pressure.**

$$p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h_{\text{ref}}$$

$$p = 1.025 \times 9.81 \times 12 = 120.66 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Effective span of the floor.**
For a fixed-fixed beam, the effective span is approximately equal to the **moulded breadth minus twice the bilge radius**. For simplicity (as is common in academic problems):

$$l \approx B = 24 \;\; \text{m}$$

**Step 3: Compute the UDL on the floor (per unit length).**
The floor carries a strip of width equal to the floor spacing:

$$w = p \cdot s_f = 120.66 \times 2.5 = 301.65 \;\; \text{kN/m}$$

**Step 4: Apply the fixed-fixed beam moment formula at the support.**

$$M_{\text{support}} = -\dfrac{w \cdot l^2}{12}$$

$$M_{\text{support}} = -\dfrac{301.65 \times 24^2}{12}$$

$$M_{\text{support}} = -\dfrac{301.65 \times 576}{12}$$

$$M_{\text{support}} = -\dfrac{173{,}750.4}{12}$$

$$\boxed{M_{\text{support}} = -14{,}479.2 \;\; \text{kN·m}}$$

**Step 5: Mid-span moment (for completeness).**

$$M_{\text{mid}} = +\dfrac{w \cdot l^2}{24} = +7{,}239.6 \;\; \text{kN·m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Students often take $l$ = ship's length instead of breadth — this is a **common error**.
> 2. The support moment is **negative** (hogging), while mid-span is **positive** (sagging) — sign convention matters.
> 3. The floor carries a strip of width $s_f$, not the full pressure — students forget to multiply $p$ by $s_f$.
> 4. Always state the assumption that the floor behaves as a **fixed-fixed beam** explicitly.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]

**B) (a)** With the aid of a labeled sketch, describe the **constructional details of an open floor**, including the role of face plates, web, and lightening holes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An **open floor** is a fabricated plate girder fitted in the double-bottom region of a ship. Its components are:

**1. Web Plate (Vertical)**
The web plate is the main vertical plate spanning from the bottom shell to the inner bottom. It is the primary load-carrying element, resisting **shear** from bottom pressure and providing the **depth** of the floor section.

**2. Face Plate (Horizontal Flange)**
A horizontal flat bar (or rolled section) welded along the top edge of the web, in contact with the inner bottom plating. The face plate:
- Increases the **section modulus** of the floor.
- Resists **bending stresses** induced by bottom pressure.
- Acts as a connection flange to the inner bottom plating.

**3. Lightening Holes**
Circular or oval cut-outs in the web plate. Their functions:
- Reduce the **weight** of the floor.
- Provide **access** for inspection, painting, and survey.
- Allow routing of **piping, cables, and ventilation ducts**.

**4. Reinforcement Rings**
Each lightening hole is surrounded by a **collar plate** or **reinforcement ring** to distribute stress concentrations around the cut-out. Typical rules require the ring thickness ≥ web thickness.

**5. Welded Connections**
- Fillet weld connecting the web to the bottom shell.
- Fillet weld connecting the face plate to the inner bottom.
- The web is typically **sniped** (cut back) at the bilge to fit the curvature.

**6. End Connections**
The ends of the floor are connected to **bilge brackets**, which in turn transmit the reaction to the side shell plating.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors:** (1) Drawing the face plate at the **bottom** instead of the top — face plate is conventionally fitted against the inner bottom. (2) Omitting the reinforcement rings around lightening holes. (3) Confusing the face plate of a floor with the face plate of a girder.

---

**B) (b)** A solid floor is required to resist a maximum bending moment of **M = 12,000 kN·m** at the support. The floor spacing is **s_f = 2.8 m** and the double-bottom height is **h_db = 1.4 m**. If the floor is treated as a fixed-fixed beam of effective span **l = 22 m**, compute the **equivalent bottom pressure head** that produces this moment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Use the support moment formula for a fixed-fixed beam.**

$$M_{\text{support}} = \dfrac{w \cdot l^2}{12}$$

where $w$ = UDL on the floor (kN/m).

**Step 2: Solve for w.**

$$w = \dfrac{12 \cdot M}{l^2} = \dfrac{12 \times 12{,}000}{22^2} = \dfrac{144{,}000}{484}$$

$$w = 297.52 \;\; \text{kN/m}$$

**Step 3: Compute the pressure from w.**

The UDL is related to the bottom pressure by:

$$w = p \cdot s_f$$

$$p = \dfrac{w}{s_f} = \dfrac{297.52}{2.8} = 106.26 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Convert to equivalent head of seawater.**

$$h_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{p}{\rho \cdot g} = \dfrac{106.26}{1.025 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{106.26}{10.055}$$

$$\boxed{h_{\text{ref}} \approx 10.57 \;\; \text{m}}$$

**Verification:**
$$p = 10.055 \times 10.57 \approx 106.3 \;\; \text{kN/m}^2 \;\; \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse the support moment with the mid-span moment. The support moment is **twice** the mid-span moment in magnitude. Also remember that the floor pressure head includes **draught + wave allowance**, not just static draught.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Floors (Module 2)**

- ☐ **Floor** = vertical transverse plate girder in the bottom structure.
- ☐ Floors span **athwartships** (port to starboard), supported by the **side shell** at the bilges.
- ☐ In **single-bottom ships**, floors extend from the bottom shell to the hold; in **double-bottom ships**, they extend between the **bottom shell** and the **inner bottom**.
- ☐ **Solid Floors** are used where maximum strength and watertightness are needed (engine room, peaks, bulkhead boundaries).
- ☐ **Open Floors** are the most common type, using lightening holes to reduce weight and provide access.
- ☐ **Bracket Floors** use triangular brackets instead of a continuous plate — limited to small/auxiliary vessels.
- ☐ The floor behaves as a **fixed-fixed beam** under bottom pressure; max BM at supports, max SF at supports.
- ☐ Standard formulas: $M = w l^2 / 12$ (support) and $V = w l / 2$ (shear) for UDL.
- ☐ Floor plate thickness (IRS Rule): $t = s_f \sqrt{p/k_1} + t_c$.
- ☐ Lightening hole diameter ≤ **0.6 × floor depth**; spacing ≥ **1.5 × diameter** (typical rule).
- ☐ Floor spacing in double bottoms: typically **2.0 – 3.5 m**, depending on ship size.
- ☐ Floors contribute to **hull girder longitudinal strength** as part of the effective bottom plating.
- ☐ Special floors: **docking floors**, **watertight floors**, **centerline floors (duct keel)**.
- ☐ In bulk carriers and tankers, floors form **double-bottom tanks** for ballast and cargo segregation.
- ☐ Bottom pressure head = **draught + wave allowance + correction**; standard wave allowance ≈ **2.5 m**.
- ☐ The face plate of an open floor is fitted at the **top** (against the inner bottom), not at the bottom.
- ☐ Lightening holes must be surrounded by **reinforcement rings** to reduce stress concentration.
- ☐ KTU exam tip: Always state the **assumptions** (fixed-fixed beam, UDL, effective breadth = s_f) explicitly.
- ☐ KTU exam tip: Sketch + label every floor type — clear diagrams fetch 2-3 extra marks.
- ☐ KTU exam tip: Watch sign convention — support moment is negative (hogging), mid-span positive (sagging).

topics.title: Transversely and Longitudinally Framed Double Bottom
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transversely and Longitudinally Framed Double Bottom

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Double Bottom**: A double bottom is a structural arrangement consisting of two horizontal plates — the **outer bottom plating** (shell plating forming the ship's underside in contact with seawater) and the **inner bottom plating** (a watertight deck above it, also called the **tank top**) — separated by a void space of typically **0.6 m to 1.2 m** height and reinforced by a grillage of vertical and horizontal structural members. The members (floors, center girder, side girders, brackets, stiffeners) may be arranged in two principal patterns: **transverse framing** (primary stiffeners run athwartships) or **longitudinal framing** (primary stiffeners run fore-and-aft).

The **transversely framed double bottom** carries the load athwartships using **floors** (transverse plates) spaced typically at frame spacings of **600 mm – 800 mm**, while the **longitudinally framed double bottom** uses longitudinal girders and floor plates arranged to give maximum longitudinal bending strength — the modern standard for ocean-going vessels of length **L > 90 m**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** Students must be able to sketch the structural arrangement, identify all primary and secondary members, and apply rule-based scantling calculations for both framing systems. The longitudinal system is **mandatory** for tankers and bulk carriers per IACS UR-S21.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **two-storey wooden bookshelf** where the bottom shelf rests on the floor (like the outer bottom on the seabed/water) and a second shelf is built above it. Between the two shelves, instead of empty space, you insert:
- A **central thick wooden plank running the full length** → this is the **center girder (keel plate)**
- **Thinner planks running across the width** at regular intervals → these are the **floors** (transverse)
- **Long thin battens running length-wise** to support the upper shelf → these are the **longitudinal stiffeners**

If the bookshelf is to carry heavy books placed sideways, you reinforce the **cross-planks (transverse system)**. If the books are to be carried along the length of the shelf (tall stack leaning), you reinforce the **lengthwise battens (longitudinal system)**. A ship experiences **hogging and sagging** — its belly bends up and down like a long beam — so the **longitudinal system** is structurally superior because the stiffeners align with the principal bending stress.

The double bottom also acts as a **safety hull** — if the outer plate is breached (e.g., by grounding), the inner bottom contains the water and prevents flooding of cargo/engineroom, providing **redundancy and survivability**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Structural Members of a Double Bottom

The double bottom is a **box-like grillage** made of the following members (refer to Section 4 diagrams):

| Sl. | Member | Orientation | Primary Function |
|-----|--------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Plate Keel** | Longitudinal, centreline | Longitudinal strength, grounding protection |
| 2 | **Center Girder** | Vertical, on centreline | Divides double bottom into port & starboard tanks; longitudinal strength |
| 3 | **Side Girders** | Vertical, longitudinal, off-centre | Reduces unsupported span of inner bottom |
| 4 | **Floors** | Vertical, transverse (web plate) | Supports outer & inner bottom; transfers load to shell |
| 5 | **Bottom Longitudinals / Floors (LL)** | Longitudinal stiffeners | Carry longitudinal hull bending stress |
| 6 | **Inner Bottom Longitudinals** | Longitudinal stiffeners on tank top | Reduce tank top span |
| 7 | **Brackets** | Triangular plates at intersections | Stiffen joints, reduce stress concentration |
| 8 | **Margin Plates** | Strips at the side / forward & aft ends | Local reinforcement at stress transitions |
| 9 | **Inner Bottom Plating** | Horizontal, top | Forms tank top, water-tight boundary |
| 10 | **Outer Bottom Plating** | Horizontal, bottom | Shell plating in contact with water |

### 2.2 Transversely Framed Double Bottom — Logic of Design

In a **transversely framed system**:
- Primary stiffeners (floors) run **athwartships (transverse)**
- The floors are **plate webs** with possible face plates (flanges)
- The inner and outer bottom plating act as flanges contributing to floor section modulus
- The **side girders** (longitudinal) act as **secondary supports** reducing the floor span

**Why transverse framing?**
- Simpler fabrication
- Easy to weld — fewer longitudinal intersections
- Suitable for **short ships** (L < 90 m) where longitudinal bending is low
- Used in **tugs, small coasters, naval auxiliaries**

**Why not in large ships?**
- Local transverse frames cannot resist the **global hull girder bending moment** (M = σ·I/y) efficiently
- Material is wasted where stress is highest (top/bottom of hull)

### 2.3 Longitudinally Framed Double Bottom — Logic of Design

In a **longitudinally framed system**:
- Primary stiffeners are **bottom longitudinals** running fore-and-aft, spaced at **300–600 mm**
- They are continuous and pass through cut-outs in the floors
- Floors are now **secondary** transverse plates, spaced more widely (**1.8 m to 3.0 m** apart)
- The longitudinals carry the **hull girder bending stress** efficiently
- Section modulus of the hull is greatly increased because the stiffeners are placed far from the neutral axis

**Why longitudinal framing?**
- Maximum efficiency in resisting **hogging and sagging**
- Reduced plate thickness → weight saving (≈ 10–15% lighter)
- Mandatory for **tankers, bulk carriers, container ships** (IACS requirements)
- Better fatigue life under cyclic wave loading

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are standard naval-architecture design formulas used by classification societies (Lloyd's Register, DNV, IRS). Units: **mm** for scantlings, **N/mm²** for stress, **m** for spans, **t/m³** for density.

#### (A) Design Sea Pressure (IACS UR-S21 style)

| Formula | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| $P_{0} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth h (Pa) |
| $P_{d} = 10 \cdot (T - h) + P_{0}$ | Design pressure at depth (T = draught, kN/m²) |
| $P_{inner} = 10 \cdot h_{tank}$ | Pressure on inner bottom from ballast/cargo |
| $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \; t/m^{3}$ | Density of seawater |
| $\rho_{fw} = 1.000 \; t/m^{3}$ | Density of freshwater |

#### (B) Plate Thickness (Stiffened Plate, simply supported)

$$t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{\sigma_{allow}}} \cdot \gamma \quad [mm]$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $s$ | Stiffener spacing (mm) |
| $k$ | Plate factor (typically **0.0185** for mild steel) |
| $p$ | Design pressure (N/mm²) |
| $\sigma_{allow}$ | Allowable stress (**175 N/mm²** for mild steel, **245 N/mm²** for HTS) |
| $\gamma$ | Correction factor (1.0 for plates ≤ 4 m, increasing for larger areas) |

#### (C) Rule-Based Scantlings (Lloyd's style)

| Member | Thickness Rule (mm) |
|--------|---------------------|
| Plate keel | $t_{pk} = L^{1/3} + 2.5$ (for L < 200 m) |
| Center girder | $t_{cg} = 0.6 \, L^{1/3} + 2.0$ |
| Side girder | $t_{sg} = 0.5 \, L^{1/3} + 2.0$ |
| Floor (web) | $t_{fl} = 0.4 \, L^{1/3} + 2.0$ |
| Inner bottom plate | $t_{ib} = 0.04 \cdot L + 4.0$ (approx.) |
| Outer bottom plate | $t_{ob} = 0.05 \cdot L + 5.0$ (approx.) |

#### (D) Section Modulus & Bending Stress

$$M = \dfrac{\sigma \cdot I}{y} \qquad \sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I} \qquad SM = \dfrac{I}{y}$$

For a hull girder under wave bending moment $M_w$:

$$SM_{req} = \dfrac{M_w}{\sigma_{perm}}$$

#### (E) Effective Breadth of Plating (Shear Lag, IACS UR-S11)

$$b_{eff} = b \cdot \beta \quad \text{where} \quad \beta = \dfrac{1.1}{1 + \dfrac{2.2}{b/L} - \dfrac{1.1}{1 + b/L}}$$

#### (F) Local Pressure on Double Bottom Panels

| Location | Design Pressure $p$ (kN/m²) |
|----------|------------------------------|
| Bottom shell (sea) | $p = 9.81 \cdot d$ where $d$ = depth from WL |
| Inner bottom (ballast tank) | $p = 9.81 \cdot h_{fill}$ |
| Empty DB (slam region) | $p = 25 \cdot T$ to $50 \cdot T$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipyards (Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock)** use longitudinal framing for all large commercial vessels.
- **Naval architecture software** (NAPA, MAXSURF) implements these exact formulas for scantling compliance.
- **Classification societies** certify ships only when the rule formulas are satisfied; longitudinal framing is the global norm for ships > 90 m.
- The **double bottom also serves as ballast tank**, oil bunker, or fresh water tank — multi-functional design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Section Modulus of a Transversely Framed Floor

A floor in a transversely framed double bottom acts as a **simply supported beam** spanning between the two side shell plates, loaded by the sea pressure from below. Let us derive the required section modulus.

**Given:**
- Span (between side shells) $L_f$ in mm
- Design pressure $p$ in N/mm²
- Stiffener spacing along the floor $s$ in mm
- Allowable bending stress $\sigma_{allow}$ in N/mm²

**Step 1: Compute the load per unit length on the floor**

The floor receives load from the bottom plating acting as a flange. The tributary width is the stiffener spacing $s$:

$$
w = p \cdot s \quad [N/mm]
$$

**Step 2: Maximum bending moment at mid-span (simply supported case)**

$$
M_{max} = \dfrac{w \cdot L_f^{\,2}}{8} = \dfrac{p \cdot s \cdot L_f^{\,2}}{8} \quad [N \cdot mm]
$$

**Step 3: Required elastic section modulus**

By the flexure formula $\sigma = M / Z$, the required section modulus is:

$$
Z_{req} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \dfrac{p \cdot s \cdot L_f^{\,2}}{8 \cdot \sigma_{allow}} \quad [mm^{3}]
$$

**Step 4: Actual section modulus provided by floor + effective plating**

The floor (web + face plate) plus effective breadth of bottom plating forms a **T-section**. The effective breadth $b_{eff}$ is given by the smaller of the actual panel breadth or:

$$
b_{eff} = 0.4 \cdot L_f \quad \text{(for bottom plating as upper flange)}
$$

The provided section modulus is:

$$
Z_{prov} = \dfrac{I_{NA}}{y_{max}}
$$

where $I_{NA}$ is the second moment of area about the **neutral axis** of the T-section.

**Step 5: Compliance check**

$$
Z_{prov} \;\geq\; Z_{req} \quad \text{✓ (Floor is adequate)}
$$

Otherwise, increase web thickness, add face plate, or reduce stiffener spacing $s$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

> **Problem:** A transversely framed double bottom of a small cargo vessel has the following data:
> - Span of floor $L_f = 8000 \; mm$
> - Stiffener spacing $s = 700 \; mm$
> - Draught $T = 6.0 \; m$, depth from waterline to bottom $d = 6.5 \; m$
> - Mild steel, $\sigma_{allow} = 165 \; N/mm^{2}$
> - Sea density $\rho = 1.025 \; t/m^{3}$
>
> Calculate: (a) Sea pressure at the bottom; (b) Required section modulus of the floor; (c) The required web thickness of the floor if depth is 600 mm and no face plate is provided.

**Step (a): Sea pressure at the bottom**

$$
P_0 = \rho \cdot g \cdot d = 1.025 \times 9.81 \times 6.5
$$

$$
P_0 = 65.37 \; kN/m^{2} = 0.0654 \; N/mm^{2}
$$

**Step (b): Required section modulus**

Load per unit length on floor:

$$
w = p \cdot s = 0.0654 \times 700 = 45.78 \; N/mm
$$

Maximum bending moment:

$$
M_{max} = \dfrac{w \cdot L_f^{\,2}}{8} = \dfrac{45.78 \times 8000^{2}}{8}
$$

$$
M_{max} = \dfrac{45.78 \times 64{,}000{,}000}{8} = 366{,}240{,}000 \; N \cdot mm
$$

Required section modulus:

$$
Z_{req} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \dfrac{366{,}240{,}000}{165}
$$

$$
Z_{req} \approx 2{,}219{,}636 \; mm^{3} \approx 2.22 \times 10^{6} \; mm^{3}
$$

**Step (c): Web thickness for rectangular floor (no face plate)**

For a rectangular plate floor of depth $h$ and thickness $t$:

$$
Z_{rect} = \dfrac{t \cdot h^{2}}{6}
$$

Setting $Z_{rect} = Z_{req}$:

$$
t = \dfrac{6 \cdot Z_{req}}{h^{2}} = \dfrac{6 \times 2{,}219{,}636}{600^{2}}
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{13{,}317{,}816}{360{,}000} \approx 36.99 \; mm
$$

Add corrosion allowance of **2.0 mm** and round up to rule minimum:

$$
t_{required} \approx 37 + 2 = 39 \; mm \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Use } 40 \; mm
$$

**Verification of shear stress:**

Shear force at support:

$$
V = \dfrac{w \cdot L_f}{2} = \dfrac{45.78 \times 8000}{2} = 183{,}120 \; N
$$

Shear stress:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{V}{h \cdot t} = \dfrac{183{,}120}{600 \times 40} = 7.63 \; N/mm^{2}
$$

Since $\tau < \tau_{allow} = 110 \; N/mm^{2}$ for mild steel, **floor is safe in shear** ✓.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Transverse Framing | Longitudinal Framing |
|-----------|--------------------|----------------------|
| Primary stiffener direction | Athwartships (⊥) | Fore-and-aft (∥) |
| Stiffener spacing | 600 – 800 mm | 300 – 600 mm |
| Floor spacing | 600 – 800 mm | 1.8 – 3.0 m |
| Hull girder efficiency | Low (poor $SM$) | **High** (good $SM$) |
| Plate thickness | Greater (thicker) | **Less (thinner)** |
| Weight | Heavier by ~12 % | **Lighter** |
| Fabrication complexity | Simpler, cheaper | Complex (cut-outs in floors) |
| Inspection ease | Easier | Harder (tight spaces) |
| Suitable for | L < 90 m, tugs, naval auxiliaries | L > 90 m, tankers, bulkers, containers |
| Cost of construction | Lower | **Higher initial**, but **lower lifecycle** |
| Required by IACS for tankers | No | **Mandatory (UR-S21)** |
| Fatigue performance | Average | **Superior** |
| Use of DB space | Compartments easily defined | More openings → careful sealing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Transversely Framed Double Bottom — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef plate fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef member fill:#ffe082,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef text fill:#ffffff,stroke:#37474f,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    sea["SEA PRESSURE p = rho g h"]:::load
    outerP["OUTER BOTTOM PLATING (Shell Plating)"]:::plate
    floorA["FLOOR T1 - 600mm spacing"]:::member
    floorB["FLOOR T2 - 600mm spacing"]:::member
    floorC["FLOOR T3 - 600mm spacing"]:::member
    ctrG["CENTRE GIRDER (Vertical Plate on Centreline)"]:::member
    sdGL["SIDE GIRDER PORT"]:::member
    sdGR["SIDE GIRDER STARBOARD"]:::member
    pkP["PLATE KEEL (Bottom Centreline Plate)"]:::member
    innerP["INNER BOTTOM PLATING (TANK TOP)"]:::plate
    cargo["CARGO HOLD / BALLAST TANK ABOVE"]:::text
    sea --> outerP
    outerP --> floorA
    outerP --> floorB
    outerP --> floorC
    outerP --> pkP
    floorA --> ctrG
    floorB --> ctrG
    floorC --> ctrG
    floorA --> sdGL
    floorA --> sdGR
    innerP --> cargo
    ctrG --- sdGL
    ctrG --- sdGR
```

### 4.2 Longitudinally Framed Double Bottom — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef plate fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef member fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef floorSec fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    sea2["WAVE / SEA PRESSURE"]:::load
    outerP2["OUTER BOTTOM PLATING"]:::plate
    longA["BOTTOM LONGITUDINAL L1 - 400mm spacing"]:::member
    longB["BOTTOM LONGITUDINAL L2"]:::member
    longC["BOTTOM LONGITUDINAL L3"]:::member
    longD["BOTTOM LONGITUDINAL L4"]:::member
    floorP["FLOOR PLATE at every 2.5m SECONDARY"]:::floorSec
    ctrG2["CENTRE GIRDER"]:::member
    sg2["SIDE GIRDER PORT"]:::member
    sg2R["SIDE GIRDER STARBOARD"]:::member
    pk2["PLATE KEEL"]:::member
    innerP2["INNER BOTTOM PLATING"]:::plate
    innerLong["INNER BOTTOM LONGITUDINALS - 600mm spacing"]:::member
    cargo2["BALLAST TANK / CARGO SPACE"]:::load
    bracket["TRIANGULAR BRACKETS at Floor-Girder junctions"]:::member

    sea2 --> outerP2
    outerP2 --> longA
    outerP2 --> longB
    outerP2 --> longC
    outerP2 --> longD
    longA -.passes through cut-out.-> floorP
    longB -.passes through cut-out.-> floorP
    longC -.passes through cut-out.-> floorP
    longD -.passes through cut-out.-> floorP
    floorP --> ctrG2
    floorP --> sg2
    floorP --> sg2R
    longA --> innerP2
    longD --> innerP2
    innerP2 --> innerLong
    innerP2 --> cargo2
    floorP --- bracket
    ctrG2 --- sg2
    ctrG2 --- sg2R
```

### 4.3 Sectional View — Equivalent ASCII Schematic

```
        TRANSVERSE FRAMING                    LONGITUDINAL FRAMING
        ====================                  =====================
        |   inner bottom    |                 |   inner bottom    |
        |====================|                 |====================|
        |       ||||        |                 |  ||||  ||||  ||||  |
        |   floor floor floor|                |  LL  floor LL  LL  |
        |       ||||        |                 |  ||||  ||||  ||||  |
        |====================|                 |====================|
        |   outer bottom    |                 |   outer bottom    |

   floors every 600-800mm             longitudinals every 300-600mm
   longitudinals only at side         floors every 1.8-3.0m
   girders + center girder
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the **transverse** view, the **vertical lines** (floors) are dominant. In the **longitudinal** view, the **vertical lines** (longitudinals) are dominant and the **horizontal lines** (floors) are widely spaced. The **center girder** and **side girders** are common to both systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define a **double bottom** in a ship. List any four structural members of a transversely framed double bottom.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
