- $t_f$ in **mm**, $s$ in **mm**, $P$ in **kN/m²**, $\sigma$ in **N/mm²**
- The constant **0.0315** absorbs unit conversions and the edge fixity coefficient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{t_f = 0.0315 \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{P}{\sigma}}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Stiffener Section Modulus Formula

**Objective:** Derive $Z = P \cdot s \cdot L^{2} / (m \cdot \sigma)$.

#### Step 1 — Identify the Load Path

A foundation stiffener is treated as a beam supported between two primary structural members (frames, transverse beams, or bulkheads).

#### Step 2 — Tributary Load per Stiffener

Each stiffener carries the load from the foundation plate over a **tributary width** equal to the stiffener spacing $s$:

$$w = P \cdot s \quad \text{[kN/m]}$$

Where $P$ is the design pressure in kN/m².

#### Step 3 — Maximum Bending Moment

For a uniformly loaded beam with end fixity coefficient $m$:

$$M_{max} = \frac{w \cdot L^{2}}{m} = \frac{P \cdot s \cdot L^{2}}{m}$$

| End Condition | Coefficient $m$ |
|----------------------|-----------------|
| Both ends simply supported | 8 |
| Both ends clamped | 12 |
| One clamped, one simply supported | $\approx 10.67$ |
| Partial fixity (typical ship) | $\approx 11.43$ |

#### Step 4 — Apply Bending Stress Equation

The required section modulus is:

$$Z = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma} = \frac{P \cdot s \cdot L^{2}}{m \cdot \sigma}$$

Units check:
- $P$: kN/m²
- $s$: m
- $L^{2}$: m²
- $m$: dimensionless
- $\sigma$: N/mm² = MN/m²

So $\frac{P \cdot s \cdot L^{2}}{m \cdot \sigma}$ has units $\frac{kN \cdot m^{2}}{m^{2} \cdot N/mm^{2}} = \frac{kN \cdot m \cdot mm^{2}}{N} = \frac{10^{3} \cdot 10^{3}\ N \cdot mm^{2} \cdot mm^{2}}{N \cdot mm^{2}}$

This needs care — the standard practice is to express **Z in cm³** when using **P in kN/m², L in m, σ in N/mm²**.

#### Step 5 — Practical Numerical Form

Converting to common KTU exam units (P in kN/m², L in m, s in m, σ in N/mm², Z in cm³):

$$Z \text{[cm³]} = \frac{P \text{[kN/m²]} \cdot s \text{[m]} \cdot L^{2} \text{[m²]}}{m \cdot \sigma \text{[N/mm²]}} \times 10^{3}$$

This numerical conversion gives **Z in cm³** — the standard unit used in ship structural design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{Z = \frac{P \cdot s \cdot L^{2}}{m \cdot \sigma} \times 10^{3} \quad \text{[cm³]}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> A marine boiler weighing 200 kN (excluding foundation) is to be supported on a foundation in the boiler room. The foundation plate is stiffened by **6 longitudinal stiffeners** of effective span **3.0 m**, simply supported at the ends. The stiffeners are spaced at **0.5 m**. Take permissible bending stress = **165 N/mm²** and dynamic load factor = **2.0**. Calculate:
> (i) Required section modulus of each stiffener
> (ii) Required foundation plate thickness

**Solution:**

#### Step (i) — Calculate Section Modulus

Total static weight of equipment = 200 kN

Assume foundation weight = 20% of equipment weight = $200 \times 0.2 = 40$ kN

Total static weight: $W_s = 200 + 40 = 240$ kN

Design dynamic load: $W = 240 \times 2.0 = 480$ kN

Foundation plan area: $A = (\text{number of spaces} \times \text{spacing}) \times \text{span} = (6 \times 0.5) \times 3.0 = 9.0$ m²

Design pressure:
$$P = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{480}{9.0} = 53.33 \text{ kN/m²}$$

For simply supported stiffeners: $m = 8$

Stiffener spacing $s = 0.5$ m, span $L = 3.0$ m

Required section modulus:
$$Z = \frac{P \cdot s \cdot L^{2}}{m \cdot \sigma} \times 10^{3}$$

$$Z = \frac{53.33 \times 0.5 \times (3.0)^{2}}{8 \times 165} \times 10^{3}$$

$$Z = \frac{53.33 \times 0.5 \times 9.0}{1320} \times 10^{3}$$

$$Z = \frac{240}{1320} \times 10^{3} = 181.82 \text{ cm³}$$

$$\boxed{Z \approx 182 \text{ cm³}}$$

> **Select standard stiffener:** Bulb flat 120 × 6 mm (Z ≈ 33.6 cm³) is insufficient — use **T-bar 200 × 9 + 100 × 10** (Z ≈ 185 cm³) ✓

#### Step (ii) — Calculate Foundation Plate Thickness

Using simply supported coefficient:

$$t_f = 0.0315 \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{P}{\sigma}}$$

Convert $s$ from m to mm: $s = 0.5 \text{ m} = 500 \text{ mm}$

$$t_f = 0.0315 \times 500 \times \sqrt{\frac{53.33}{165}}$$

$$t_f = 15.75 \times \sqrt{0.3232}$$

$$t_f = 15.75 \times 0.5685$$

$$t_f = 8.95 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{t_f \approx 9 \text{ mm}}$$

> **Sanity check:** If the underlying hull plating is 7 mm, foundation plate of 9 mm is adequate (9 ≥ 7 + 2 = 9 mm). ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Ship Foundation Load Path

The diagram below illustrates the **hierarchical load transfer** from a shipboard machinery item through the foundation into the hull girder.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Equipment: Engine / Boiler / Pump] --> B[Machinery Feet and Bolts]
    B --> C[Foundation Plate Doubling Plate]
    C --> D[Topping Stiffeners T Bars Angles]
    D --> E[Primary Hull Structure Frames Bulkheads Tank Top]
    E --> F[Secondary Hull Structure Stringers Girders]
    F --> G[Hull Girder Shell Deck Plate]
    G --> H[Sea Water Buoyancy Support]
    
    I[Design Load W] --> J[Dynamic Factor Kd 1.5 to 3.0]
    J --> A
    
    K[Material Steel Grade A] --> C
    K --> D
    K --> E
    
    L[Quality Checks] --> L1[Vibration Analysis]
    L --> L2[Alignment Tolerance]
    L --> L3[Stress Distribution]
    L --> L4[Fatigue Life]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Foundation Component Hierarchy

This diagram shows how the foundation system is **decomposed** into its structural components and quality control subsystems.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph F1[Foundation Assembly]
        nodeA[Foundation Plate]
        nodeB[Topping Stiffeners]
        nodeC[Bracket Connections]
        nodeD[Seating Chocks]
        nodeE[Foundation Bolts]
        nodeF[Anti Vibration Pads]
    end
    
    subgraph F2[Load Transfer Path]
        nodeG[Concentrated Bolt Loads]
        nodeH[Distributed Plate Pressure]
        nodeI[Bending in Stiffeners]
        nodeJ[Reaction at Hull Frames]
        nodeK[Shear Lag in Hull Girder]
    end
    
    subgraph F3[Design Verification]
        nodeL[Static Strength Check]
        nodeM[Dynamic Strength Check]
        nodeN[Deflection Limit Check]
        nodeO[Natural Frequency Check]
        nodeP[Fatigue Life Check]
    end
    
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeP
```

---

### 4.3 Conceptual Cross-Section of a Typical Engine Foundation

A simple text schematic showing how a main engine sits on a tank-top foundation:

```
                Topping Stiffener (T-bar)
                       │
                       │
        ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐ ← Foundation Plate (Doubling)
        │                              │
        │    ┌─────────────────┐       │
        │    │                 │       │ ← Engine Bedplate
        │    │   MAIN ENGINE   │       │
        │    │                 │       │
        │    └─────────────────┘       │
        │   ●  ●  ●  ●  ●  ●          │ ← Foundation Bolts (chocks)
        │                              │
        └──────────────┬──────────────┘
                       │
        ═══════════════╪═══════════════ ← Tank Top / Inner Bottom Plate
                       │
                  Primary Frame
```

**Load Path:** Engine weight → Bedplate → Bolts → Foundation plate → Stiffeners → Tank top / Frames → Hull girder.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

#### **Q1. Define a ship foundation. List any four general requirements of a well-designed foundation.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **ship foundation** is a locally reinforced structural assembly comprising a foundation plate, stiffeners, brackets, and seating chocks, designed to transmit the static and dynamic loads of shipboard machinery and equipment safely into the hull girder.

Four general requirements:

1. **Rigidity** — deflection should not exceed span / 500 to preserve shaft alignment.
2. **Strength** — combined loads must keep stresses below permissible limits.
3. **Vibration Resistance** — natural frequency must differ from exciting frequency by >20%.
4. **Accessibility** — adequate clearance for maintenance on all sides of the equipment.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "foundations hold equipment" without mentioning **load transmission to the hull girder**. This single missing phrase costs 1 mark. Always mention the **load path**.

---

#### **Q2. What are the different types of design loads acting on a ship foundation?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The main design loads are:

1. **Static Load** — dead weight of the equipment, foundation, and any liquids it supports.
2. **Dynamic Load** — inertial forces from ship motions (rolling, pitching, heaving) governed by accelerations up to **2–4 g** depending on location.
3. **Vibration Load** — periodic forces from unbalanced rotating machinery.
4. **Thermal Load** — expansion forces from hot equipment like boilers and exhaust manifolds.
5. **Torque Reaction** — rotational reaction from engines and gearboxes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** list only "static and dynamic" — examiners expect **at least 4 categories** with specific examples like torque or vibration. Vague answers lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model Question]**

---

#### **QUESTION A**

**(a) Explain with neat sketches the various types of ship foundations based on the equipment they support.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Ship foundations are classified into the following categories:

**1. Main Machinery Foundations**
These support the **prime mover** of the ship — main diesel engine, steam turbine, or gas turbine. Located on the **tank top** of the engine room, they are the most heavily loaded foundations. They consist of a thick foundation plate (often **12–25 mm**), welded to the tank top, and stiffened by **T-bars or bulb flats** spaced at 400–600 mm. Side brackets transfer loads to the **double bottom girders**.

**2. Auxiliary Machinery Foundations**
These support pumps, generators, compressors, and motors. They are lighter, typically using **flat bars or angles** as stiffeners, and the foundation plate is thinner (8–12 mm).

**3. Boiler Foundations**
Located in the boiler room, these support **oil-fired boilers** or **exhaust gas boilers**. They must accommodate thermal expansion through **slotted bolt holes** and **sliding chocks** on one side.

**4. Deck Machinery Foundations**
Foundations on **weather decks** for winches, windlasses, capstans, and cranes. They are subject to the highest dynamic accelerations (up to **4 g vertical**) due to forward location.

**5. Special Foundations**
Masts, king posts, funnels, and A-frames are examples. They are designed as **tripod or box structures** to resist large overturning moments from wind loads and ship motions.

**Sketch (ASCII representation):**

```
   Main Machinery       Boiler           Deck Machinery
   ┌─────────┐         ┌─────────┐        ┌─────────┐
   │ Engine  │         │ Boiler  │        │ Winch   │
   ├─────────┤         ├─────────┤        ├─────────┤
   │ Fdn Pl. │         │ Fdn Pl. │        │ Fdn Pl. │
   ├─────────┤         ├─────────┤        ├─────────┤
   │Tank Top │         │Tank Top │        │ Deck     │
   └─────────┘         └─────────┘        └─────────┘
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Avoid just listing types — you must mention **specific equipment names**, **locations**, and **structural components** (foundation plate, stiffener, bracket). Missing these details loses 2 marks.

---

**(b) Derive the formula for the required section modulus of a foundation stiffener.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Idealization**
A foundation stiffener is treated as a **beam** of span $L$ (m), simply supported or fixed at both ends. The foundation plate delivers a **uniform pressure** $P$ (kN/m²) on the stiffener.

**Step 2 — Tributary Width**
The stiffener carries the load from a strip of plate of width equal to the stiffener spacing $s$. Hence the **load per unit length** on the stiffener:

$$w = P \cdot s \quad \text{[kN/m]}$$

**Step 3 — Bending Moment**
For a beam with end-fixity coefficient $m$:

$$M = \frac{w \cdot L^{2}}{m} = \frac{P \cdot s \cdot L^{2}}{m} \quad \text{[kN·m]}$$

**Step 4 — Section Modulus**
Using the bending equation $\sigma = M / Z$, solving for $Z$:

$$Z = \frac{M}{\sigma} = \frac{P \cdot s \cdot L^{2}}{m \cdot \sigma}$$

Converting to KTU exam units (Z in cm³):

$$\boxed{Z \text{ [cm³]} = \frac{P \cdot s \cdot L^{2}}{m \cdot \sigma} \times 10^{3}}$$

Where:
- $P$ in kN/m², $s$ in m, $L$ in m, $\sigma$ in N/mm², $m$ = 8 to 12.

**Values of m:**
- Simply supported: **m = 8**
- Clamped-clamped: **m = 12**
- Clamped-simply supported: **m = 10.67**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget the **× 10³** conversion factor for cm³. Also, **state the units clearly** in each step — units are 1 mark.

---

#### **QUESTION B**

**(a) Discuss the general design considerations for ship foundations and explain how dynamic loads are calculated.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Design Considerations:**

1. **Strength and Stiffness** — The foundation must support all anticipated loads without exceeding permissible stresses, with deflection limited to span/500 for machinery.

2. **Vibration** — The natural frequency of the foundation must be **≥ 1.2× the operating frequency** of the equipment to avoid resonance.

3. **Stress Distribution** — Loads from bolts and seating feet must be **gradually distributed** into the hull structure to avoid hard spots.

4. **Accessibility** — Space must be left for **maintenance access** (typically 600 mm minimum).

5. **Alignment** — Shaft alignment tolerance is typically **0.05 mm/m**; foundations must not deflect beyond this.

6. **Corrosion Protection** — Especially in engine rooms, foundations are coated and may use **stainless steel shims** under bolts.

7. **Thermal Expansion** — Boiler and exhaust foundations must allow **sliding** in one direction.

**Dynamic Load Calculation:**

The dynamic load is calculated by multiplying the static weight by a **dynamic load factor $K_d$**:

$$F_{dynamic} = m \cdot a = \frac{W}{g} \cdot a$$

So:

$$K_d = \frac{a}{g}$$

Total design load:

$$F_{design} = W_{static} \cdot K_d$$

**Typical values of $K_d$ (IACS):**
- Midships, mild sea: **1.0–1.5**
- Forward quarter, heavy sea: **2.5–3.0**
- Deck machinery: **3.0–4.0**

Acceleration components are applied in **three orthogonal directions** (vertical, transverse, longitudinal) and combined using the **square root of the sum of squares (SRSS)** method:

$$a_{total} = \sqrt{a_v^{2} + a_t^{2} + a_l^{2}}$$

---

**(b) A centrifugal pump of weight 40 kN is to be mounted on a foundation in the engine room. The foundation plate is stiffened by 4 transverse stiffeners of effective span 1.8 m, spaced at 0.45 m. Calculate the required section modulus of each stiffener. Take permissible bending stress = 165 N/mm², dynamic load factor = 1.75, and assume the stiffeners are partially fixed. Foundation weight = 8 kN.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Total Static Weight**

$$W_s = W_{pump} + W_{foundation} = 40 + 8 = 48 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Design (Dynamic) Load**

$$W = W_s \cdot K_d = 48 \times 1.75 = 84 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Foundation Area**

$$A = (\text{number of spaces} \times s) \times L = (4 \times 0.45) \times 1.8 = 3.24 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Design Pressure**

$$P = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{84}{3.24} = 25.93 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Load per Stiffener**

$$w = P \cdot s = 25.93 \times 0.45 = 11.67 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 6 — Bending Moment (partial fixity, m = 11.43)**

$$M = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{m} = \frac{11.67 \times (1.8)^2}{11.43} = \frac{11.67 \times 3.24}{11.43} = 3.31 \text{ kN·m}$$

Converting to N·mm:

$$M = 3.31 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 7 — Required Section Modulus**

$$Z = \frac{M}{\sigma} = \frac{3.31 \times 10^6}{165} = 20{,}061 \text{ mm}^3 = 20.06 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$\boxed{Z \approx 20.1 \text{ cm}^3}$$

**Step 8 — Select Standard Section**

From ship-structural tables, a **bulb flat 80 × 5 mm** has $Z \approx 14$ cm³ (insufficient).
Use **bulb flat 100 × 6 mm** with $Z \approx 22$ cm³ — **Adequate** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is forgetting to **add foundation weight** to the equipment weight before applying the dynamic factor. This single omission costs 1 full mark. Also, students often confuse **partial fixity (m = 11.43)** with **simply supported (m = 8)**, leading to a stiffer (smaller) section modulus than required — a **dangerous undersizing** in real design.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on Foundation Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency** — mixing mm and m without conversion (loses 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Forgetting Dynamic Factor** — applying static load directly to the formula (loses 1 mark).
> 3. **Wrong m Coefficient** — using m = 8 (simply supported) when the question specifies clamped ends (loses 1 mark).
> 4. **No Sketch in 7-mark Questions** — KTU examiners reward "neat sketches" explicitly. Always include a labelled cross-section.
> 5. **Missing Load Path Discussion** — in design questions, always trace: Equipment → Bolts → Plate → Stiffener → Hull.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Foundations (Module 2)**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ Foundation = load-distribution system, not just a plate.
- ☐ Six general requirements: rigidity, strength, vibration, accessibility, alignment, no hard spots.
- ☐ Six types: main, auxiliary, boiler, deck, special, cargo gear.

**Loads:**
- ☐ $F_{design} = W_{static} \times K_d$ where $K_d = 1.5$ to $3.0$.
- ☐ Acceleration components: vertical (1.0–4.0 g), transverse (0.5–1.5 g), longitudinal (0.25–0.5 g).
- ☐ Combine using SRSS for omnidirectional loading.

**Foundation Plate:**
- ☐ Formula: $t_f = 0.0315 \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{P/\sigma}$ (simply supported).
- ☐ Minimum: $t_f \geq t_h + 2$ mm, or bolt diameter + 1.5 mm, or 6 mm.

**Stiffeners:**
- ☐ Formula: $Z = P \cdot s \cdot L^2 / (m \cdot \sigma) \times 10^3$ cm³.
- ☐ m = 8 (simply supported), 12 (clamped), 10.67 (mixed), 11.43 (partial fixity).
- ☐ Effective breadth: $b_{eff} = \min(s, L/4)$.

**Units Reminder:**
- ☐ Use **N, mm, MPa, kg** consistently. Convert m → mm and kN → N at one step only.

**Design Sequence:**
- ☐ Static weight → Add foundation weight (≈ 15–25%) → Apply $K_d$ → Compute $P$ → Size stiffeners → Size plate → Verify deflection and vibration.

**Common Mistake to Avoid:**
- ☐ Never assume simply supported unless explicitly stated — partial fixity is the most realistic ship condition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shell Plating - Bottom Shell Plating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shell Plating - Bottom Shell Plating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Shell Plating: Bottom Shell Plating

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Bottom Shell Plating** is the horizontal or near-horizontal plated skin of the ship's hull that encloses the lower portion of the vessel, extending from the port side bilge to the starboard side bilge, bounded longitudinally by the **keel plate** at the centerline and the **bilge strake** at the upper turn of the hull. It is the principal structural member that resists **hydrostatic water pressure**, **grounding/slamming loads**, **squat loads**, and a substantial portion of the **hull girder longitudinal bending stresses** (both hogging and sagging).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bottom Shell Plating Definition (KTU / IACS-S6 / DNV Standard):**
> *"Bottom plating comprises the shell plates fitted below the bilge, extending from the garboard strake on each side up to and including the keel plate at the centreline. The bottom plating, in conjunction with the inner bottom, forms a box-girder that is the principal contributor to the ship's overall longitudinal strength."*

**Structural Sub-divisions of Bottom Plating** (from keel outwards):
1. **Keel Plate** — Centreline strake, heaviest thickness.
2. **Garboard Strakes** — Plates immediately adjacent to the keel plate, lapping onto the keel.
3. **Bottom Plates / Bottom Strakes** — All intermediate plates between the garboard and bilge.
4. **Bilge Strake (Lower)** — The transitional strake curving upwards into the bilge radius.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine the **ship's hull as a hollow rectangular tube (a girder)** floating on water. The **top deck is the upper flange**, the **bottom shell plating is the lower flange**, and the **side shell plating and internal bulkheads are the web**.

Now, picture a **large rectangular metal raft** floating in a swimming pool. The raft sags slightly in the middle because gravity pulls it down while the water pushes up only at the ends. The bottom skin of the raft (just like the bottom shell plating of a ship) experiences massive **tensile stretching** when supported at two ends with weight in the middle (this is **sagging** condition on a ship at sea). When the ship is supported by a wave crest amidships (hogging), the bottom plating goes into **compression** and is liable to buckle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bottom plating = Lower Flange of the Hull Girder**
> It is the most highly stressed plate in the hull because it must simultaneously fight:
> - External sea water pressure (compression on outside)
> - Hull bending stresses (tension in sagging, compression in hogging)
> - Local slamming loads (dynamic impact from waves)
> - Grounding forces (if ship touches seabed)

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Material Standards

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Steel Grades for Shipbuilding (KTU Module 2 highlights):**
> - **Grade A** — Yield ≥ **235 MPa**, used in secondary areas.
> - **Grade D** — Yield ≥ **235 MPa**, better notch toughness for bottom plates.
> - **Grade E** — Yield ≥ **235 MPa**, for transverse bulkheads and critical decks.
> - **AH32 / DH32 / EH32** — High Tensile (HT), Yield ≥ **315 MPa**.
> - **AH36 / DH36 / EH36** — High Tensile (HT), Yield ≥ **355 MPa**.
> - **AH40 / DH40 / EH40** — Yield ≥ **390 MPa** (for large vessels).
>
> **Seawater Density:** ρ ≈ **1.025 t/m³** (1025 kg/m³).
> **Acceleration due to gravity:** g = **9.81 m/s²**.
> **Minimum Bottom Plate Thickness (Class Rule of Thumb):** t_min ≈ **4.5 mm + (L/1000) × k**, where k = plate length factor.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Function of Bottom Shell Plating

The bottom plating performs **four distinct structural functions**, each demanding different design considerations:

1. **Hull Girder Action (Longitudinal Strength):**
   - Acts as the lower flange of the hull girder.
   - Carries **σ_L = M·y / I** where M = hull bending moment, y = distance from neutral axis, I = second moment of area.
   - For a vessel sagging in still water, σ_b ≈ **175/L × 10⁴ kPa** (RHS formula) and for hogging, similar magnitude with opposite sign.

2. **Local Plate Bending:**
   - Each plate acts as a simply supported or fixed panel between stiffeners.
   - Hydrostatic pressure p = ρ·g·d, where d = draft at the point under consideration.

3. **Shear Stress Transmission:**
   - Bottom plating transmits **shear flow** q = V·Q/I from the shell into the side and deck.
   - The garboard-keel connection must transfer huge shear.

4. **Grounding & Slamming Resistance:**
   - Keel plate is the first to touch the seabed — must withstand **slamming pressure** p_slam ≈ **ρ·g·H_slam** where H_slam is the impact head.
   - IACS assigns **slamming pressure** up to **2.5 × ρ·g·T** at the forward bottom (T = draft).

## 2.2 Design Pressure for Bottom Plating

The bottom plating is designed using **two pressure regimes** simultaneously:

### (a) Still Water Pressure (Hydrostatic)
At a point at depth d below the waterline:
$$ p_{sw} = \rho \cdot g \cdot d $$

### (b) Dynamic (Slamming) Pressure for Forward Bottom
$$ p_{dyn} = C_s \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot (T_f + h_{s}) $$

where:
- C_s = **slamming coefficient (1.5 to 2.5)** depending on ship type and speed,
- T_f = forward draft,
- h_s = **slamming head allowance** ≈ 1.5 × (B·L) / (something) per IACS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Pressure is the GOVERNING pressure — whichever is higher between:**
> - **p_sw = ρgd** (hydrostatic)
> - **p_slam = 2.0 · ρ · g · T_f** (slamming for forward 0.25L)
> is taken for thickness calculation.

## 2.3 Plate Thickness — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet is essential for the End Semester Examination. All formulas use SI units.**

| Formula No. | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **F-1** | $t = s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{1000 \cdot \sigma_{ref}}}$ | IACS plate thickness for local pressure, s = spacing of stiffeners, σ_ref = reference stress (MPa) |
| **F-2** | $p_{sw} = \rho \cdot g \cdot d$ | Hydrostatic still water pressure |
| **F-3** | $p_{slam} = k_s \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot T$ | Dynamic slamming pressure, k_s = 2.0 to 2.5 |
| **F-4** | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Hull girder bending stress at distance y from NA |
| **F-5** | $M_{sw} = 0.175 \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot C_B$ | Still water bending moment (RHS empirical) |
| **F-6** | $M_{wv} = 0.11 \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot C_B \cdot C_w$ | Wave-induced bending moment (RHS) |
| **F-7** | $M_T = M_{sw} + M_{wv}$ | Total design bending moment |
| **F-8** | $t_{keel} = (1.4 \text{ to } 1.5) \cdot t_{side}$ | Empirical keel plate thickness (Class rule of thumb) |
| **F-9** | $\sigma_{ref} = \dfrac{235}{k}$ | Reference stress for grade A steel |
| **F-10** | $f = \dfrac{l^2}{t \cdot s}$ | Plate slenderness factor (must be < 1.0) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do NOT confuse σ_ref with yield stress directly.** For **Grade A** steel under still water (sagging), σ_ref ≈ **150 MPa**. For wave-induced hogging at the bottom, σ_ref is reduced further by combined stress factor.

## 2.4 Critical Structural Zones of the Bottom

### (A) Keel Plate
- Centred along the centreline, runs full length of the ship.
- **Tapered thickness** — heaviest amidships (0.4L to 0.6L), reducing towards ends.
- Two pieces of keel plating:
  - **Flat Plate Keel** — single thickness up to ~25 mm.
  - **Bar Keel** — additional flat bar welded externally for grounding protection.
- Functions: longitudinal strength, grounding protection, ship-launching load path.

### (B) Garboard Strakes
- The plates immediately adjacent to the keel plate.
- **Lapped onto the keel plate** in older ships (lap-strake connection); modern ships use **butt-welded with collar plate**.
- The **garboard seam** (garboard-bilge strake joint) is a critical shear-transfer line.

### (C) Bottom Plates
- Standard strakes outside the garboard.
- Stiffened by **bottom longitudinals** (longitudinal system) or **transverse bottom frames** (transverse system).
- Plating is typically **staggered butt arrangement** to avoid aligned butts.

### (D) Bilge Strake
- Transition from bottom to side shell.
- Carries maximum shear flow due to hull girder shear.
- Heavily reinforced in way of bilge brackets.

## 2.5 Why Bottom Plating is Heavier than Side Plating

| Parameter | Bottom Plating | Side Plating |
|---|---|---|
| Static Pressure | Maximum at keel | Minimum at waterline |
| Slamming Load | Severe (forward ¼ L) | Moderate |
| Hull Girder Stress | High (lower flange) | Lower (near NA) |
| Class Required Thickness | **~30–40 mm amidships** | ~20–25 mm amidships |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Treating bottom and side plating with the same design pressure. Bottom plating ALWAYS governs the higher thickness calculation due to combined hydrostatic + slamming + hull-girder tension.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Plate Thickness from Local Pressure (IACS Common Structural Rules)

### Statement
For a rectangular plate of width *s* (spacing between supporting stiffeners), length *l*, under uniform pressure *p*, find the required thickness *t*.

### Step 1 — Plate Bending Stress
A plate simply supported along four edges with uniform load is analysed using **plate theory (von Kármán / Timoshenko)**. For a long plate ($l/s > 2$), it bends predominantly in the short-span direction with effective bending moment:

$$ M_{eff} = \frac{p \cdot s^2}{8} $$

### Step 2 — Section Modulus
For unit width of plate, the section modulus is:

$$ Z = \frac{t^2}{6} $$

### Step 3 — Equate Bending Stress to Allowable
$$ \sigma_{allow} = \frac{M_{eff}}{Z} $$

Substituting:
$$ \sigma_{allow} = \frac{p \cdot s^2 / 8}{t^2 / 6} $$

Re-arranging:
$$ \sigma_{allow} = \frac{6 \cdot p \cdot s^2}{8 \cdot t^2} = \frac{3 \cdot p \cdot s^2}{4 \cdot t^2} $$

### Step 4 — Solve for t
$$ t^2 = \frac{3 \cdot p \cdot s^2}{4 \cdot \sigma_{allow}} $$

$$ t = s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{3 \cdot p}{4 \cdot \sigma_{allow}}} $$

### Step 5 — Final KTU Working Form
By absorbing the constant **3/4 ≈ 0.75** into the safety factor and using σ_ref as the **reference stress** (MPa) with *p* in **kPa**:

$$ t = s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{1000 \cdot \sigma_{ref}}} \quad \text{(mm)} $$

where:
- *t* in **mm**,
- *s* in **mm**,
- *p* in **kPa**,
- σ_ref in **MPa** ≈ **0.7 to 0.9 × σ_yield** depending on loading condition.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem)

### Problem Statement
A bulk carrier has:
- Length L = **150 m**,
- Breadth B = **25 m**,
- Draft T = **9.5 m**,
- Block coefficient C_B = **0.78**,
- Bottom longitudinal spacing s = **700 mm**,
- Steel grade: **AH36** (σ_y = 355 MPa).

**Calculate the required bottom shell plate thickness amidships** using the still water bending stress approach.

### Step 1 — Still Water Bending Moment
Using **RHS empirical formula**:
$$ M_{sw} = 0.175 \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot C_B $$
$$ M_{sw} = 0.175 \times (150)^2 \times 25 \times 0.78 $$
$$ M_{sw} = 0.175 \times 22500 \times 25 \times 0.78 $$

Intermediate multiplication:
$$ M_{sw} = 0.175 \times 438750 $$
$$ M_{sw} = 76{,}781.25 \text{ kN·m} $$

### Step 2 — Convert to kN·mm for Plate Stress
$$ M_{sw} = 76{,}781.25 \times 10^6 \text{ kN·mm} $$

### Step 3 — Section Modulus of Hull Girder (Approximation)
For a typical bulk carrier: $I/c$ (at bottom) ≈ **0.65 × L·B·T** in m³ × ρ_equiv correction. Using the simplified:
$$ Z_{bottom} = 0.65 \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T $$
$$ Z_{bottom} = 0.65 \times 150 \times 25 \times 9.5 $$
$$ Z_{bottom} = 23{,}156.25 \text{ m}^3 = 23{,}156.25 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^3 $$

### Step 4 — Hull Girder Stress at Bottom
$$ \sigma_L = \frac{M_{sw}}{Z_{bottom}} $$
$$ \sigma_L = \frac{76{,}781.25 \times 10^6}{23{,}156.25 \times 10^9} \text{ kN/mm}^2 = \frac{76{,}781.25}{23{,}156.25} \text{ kN/mm}^2 $$

Computing the ratio:
$$ \sigma_L = 3.316 \text{ kN/mm}^2 = 3316 \text{ MPa} $$

> [!WARNING]
> This value is **physically absurd** — it tells us the simplified section modulus formula is too crude. The KTU problem expects you to compute σ_L using **class rules** where allowable is around **150–190 MPa**. Use the **IACS combined stress** approach instead.

### Step 5 — Use IACS Combined Stress
For still water sagging on a bulk carrier amidships:
$$ \sigma_L = \frac{175}{L} \cdot 10^3 \text{ (in kPa using L in m)} $$
$$ \sigma_L = \frac{175}{150} \cdot 1000 = 1166.67 \text{ kPa} $$

For AH36 steel, σ_ref under combined SW + hull girder action ≈ **150 MPa**.

### Step 6 — Hydrostatic Pressure
At the bottom (z = T):
$$ p_{sw} = \rho \cdot g \cdot T = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 9.5 $$
$$ p_{sw} = 95{,}500.4 \text{ Pa} = 95.5 \text{ kPa} $$

### Step 7 — Plate Thickness
$$ t = s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p_{sw}}{1000 \cdot \sigma_{ref}}} $$
$$ t = 700 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{95.5}{1000 \times 150}} $$
$$ t = 700 \cdot \sqrt{6.367 \times 10^{-4}} $$

Computing the square root:
$$ \sqrt{6.367 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.02523 $$

Therefore:
$$ t = 700 \times 0.02523 = 17.66 \text{ mm} $$

### Step 8 — Apply Class Rule Add-on
For bottom plate amidships of a bulk carrier with slamming consideration, add **+25%**:
$$ t_{final} = 17.66 \times 1.25 = 22.07 \text{ mm} $$

Adopt **t = 22 mm** as the bottom plate thickness.

### Step 9 — Keel Plate
$$ t_{keel} = 1.5 \times 22 = 33 \text{ mm} $$

Adopt **t_keel = 33 mm** (or 35 mm per standard plate increments).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bottom Plating vs Side Plating vs Deck Plating

| Criterion | Bottom Plating | Side Plating | Deck Plating |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Load | Hydrostatic + Slamming + Tension | Hydrostatic + Hull-girder | Axial Compression + Local |
| Max Thickness | **35 mm (keel)** | 22–25 mm | 25–30 mm |
| Stiffener System | **Longitudinal** (modern) | Longitudinal | Longitudinal |
| Stiffener Spacing s | 600–800 mm | 700–900 mm | 700–900 mm |
| σ_ref (HT steel) | 150 MPa | 165 MPa | 140 MPa |
| Critical Zone | Keel, Garboard, Forward ¼L | Forward impact zone | Hatch corners |
| Stress Type | Tension (sagging) / Compression (hogging) | Tension/Compression gradient | Compression (hogging) |

## 3.4 Length & Width of Plates — KTU Rules

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Class Plate Geometry Rules:**
> - Standard plate width (girth-wise): typically **0.9 to 1.8 m** per strake.
> - Plate length (longitudinal): **2 to 3 standard frame spaces**, typically **3.0 to 6.0 m**.
> - **Butts staggered** so that adjacent strakes do not align (typically staggered by **1 frame space**).
> - **Keel plate length** = full length of ship in one piece (often 2 pieces joined at midship).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bottom Plating Load Path

This Mermaid diagram maps the **flow of forces** from sea → bottom plating → supporting structure → hull girder. It is used instead of a 2D drawing for clarity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sea Water Pressure rho g d] --> B[Bottom Shell Plate]
    A1[Slamming Force Forward Quarter L] --> B
    B --> C[Plate Bending Stress sigma local]
    B --> D[Hull Girder Stress sigma L]
    C --> E[Reaction Transferred to Stiffeners]
    D --> F[Reaction Transferred to Longitudinals]
    E --> G[Bottom Longitudinals]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Transverse Web Frame or Transverse Frame]
    H --> I[Double Bottom Floors Inner Bottom Plate]
    I --> J[Side Shell via Bilge Bracket]
    J --> K[Deck Plate Upper Flange]
    K --> L[Complete Hull Girder Load Path]
    B --> M[Keel Plate Centreline]
    M --> L
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Bottom Plating Layout (Cross-Section View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["PORT SIDE"]
        S1P[Side Shell Plate]
    end
    subgraph BB["BILGE REGION"]
        BBS[Bilge Strake]
    end
    subgraph BS["BOTTOM PLATING"]
        BS1[Bottom Plate Strake 1]
        BS2[Bottom Plate Strake 2]
        BS3[Bottom Plate Strake 3]
    end
    subgraph KP["CENTRELINE"]
        KP1[Garboard Strake Port]
        KP2[Keel Plate]
        KP3[Garboard Strake Starboard]
    end
    subgraph S2["STBD SIDE"]
        S2P[Side Shell Plate]
    end
    S1P --> BBS
    BBS --> BS1
    BS1 --> BS2
    BS2 --> BS3
    BS3 --> KP1
    KP1 --> KP2
    KP2 --> KP3
    KP3 --> BS3
    BS3 --> BS2
    BS2 --> BS1
    BS1 --> BBS
    BBS --> S2P
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Load Distribution on Bottom Plate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pressure p on Plate] --> M1[Moment Mx across short span s]
    P --> M2[Moment My across long span l]
    M1 --> T1[Required Thickness tx]
    M2 --> T2[Required Thickness ty]
    T1 --> T3[Governing Thickness t equals max tx ty]
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> CHECK{Plate Slenderness less than 1.0?}
    CHECK --> YES[Accept Plate]
    CHECK --> NO[Increase Thickness or Reduce Spacing]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Plate Thickness Determination Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start Plate Thickness Calculation] --> A1[Identify Position Keel Garboard Bottom]
    A1 --> A2[Determine Governing Pressure p hydrostatic or slamming]
    A2 --> A3[Identify Spacing s of stiffeners]
    A3 --> A4[Determine Reference Stress sigma ref from class rules]
    A4 --> A5[Compute t using Formula F-1]
    A5 --> A6[Check Hull Girder Combined Stress]
    A6 --> A7{Stress exceeds Allowable?}
    A7 --> YES[Increase t or use higher grade steel]
    A7 --> NO[Adopt Calculated t]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define bottom shell plating. List its main components. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Bottom shell plating is the external plating that forms the underside of the ship's hull, extending from the keel plate at the centreline up to the bilge strake on each side. It is the most heavily loaded portion of the shell plating.

**Main components (any 4):**
1. **Keel plate** — centreline strake.
2. **Garboard strakes** — adjacent to keel plate.
3. **Bottom plates** — intermediate strakes.
4. **Bilge strake** — transitional curving plate at the bilge.
5. **Stern bottom plates** — around the stern tube.
6. **Fore bottom plates** — at the forward slamming region.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse *inner bottom plating* (which is the top of the double bottom tank) with the *bottom shell plating* (the external hull). They are two different plates separated by the depth of the double bottom.

### Q2. Why is the keel plate made thicker than the side shell plating? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The keel plate is the centreline strake and is made thicker than side shell plating for **four key reasons**:

1. **Hull girder longitudinal strength:** It carries maximum tensile stress during sagging (still water condition).
2. **Grounding and drydocking loads:** It bears directly on the dock blocks and seabed.
3. **Launching loads:** During ship launching, the keel takes initial impact.
4. **Shear transfer:** The garboard-keel joint transfers shear flow between bottom and side.

**Typical rule:** $t_{keel} = 1.4 \text{ to } 1.5 \times t_{side}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Define bottom shell plating. With a neat cross-section sketch, describe the various strakes of bottom shell plating and explain their functions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Bottom shell plating is the external plating forming the hull's underside, extending from the keel plate at the centreline to the bilge strakes on each side.

**Strakes (from centreline outward):**

1. **Keel Plate (1 mark):**
   - Centreline strake running full ship length.
   - Carries maximum hull-girder bending stress.
   - Thicker than all other bottom plates (1.4 to 1.5 times side plate).
   - Acts as grounding/launching structural member.

2. **Garboard Strakes (1.5 marks):**
   - Two strakes flanking the keel plate (port and starboard).
   - Their inboard edges lap onto the keel plate (lap connection).
   - Transmit hull-girder shear into the keel.
   - Subjected to combined local + global stresses.

3. **Bottom Plates / Strakes (1.5 marks):**
   - Plates between garboard and bilge strakes.
   - Subjected to hydrostatic pressure (max at keel, reducing outward).
   - Width typically 0.9 to 1.8 m.
   - Staggered butt arrangement between adjacent strakes.

4. **Bilge Strakes (1 mark):**
   - Transitional plate curving from horizontal bottom to vertical side.
   - Maximum shear flow concentration.
   - Reinforced with double brackets.

5. **Fore Bottom Plates / Stern Bottom Plates (1 mark):**
   - Special strengthening for slamming (forward) and propeller/wave loads (aft).

**Functions (1 mark):**
- Provides watertight envelope.
- Contributes lower flange of hull girder.
- Resists hydrostatic + slamming loads.

---

**Q. (b)** A bulk carrier has the following particulars: L = 180 m, B = 28 m, T = 12 m, C_B = 0.80. The bottom longitudinal spacing is 750 mm. Steel grade is AH36 (σ_y = 355 MPa). Calculate the bottom shell plate thickness amidships using the IACS plate formula. Take σ_ref = 150 MPa. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure at Bottom:**
$$ p_{sw} = \rho \cdot g \cdot T $$
$$ p_{sw} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 12 $$
$$ p_{sw} = 120{,}651 \text{ Pa} = 120.65 \text{ kPa} $$

**Step 2 — Slamming Pressure (Forward ¼ L, may govern):**
$$ p_{slam} = 2.0 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 12 = 241{,}302 \text{ Pa} = 241.3 \text{ kPa} $$

(For amidships, slamming is typically not considered; **p_sw = 120.65 kPa** governs amidships.)

**Step 3 — Plate Thickness:**
$$ t = s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{1000 \cdot \sigma_{ref}}} $$
$$ t = 750 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{120.65}{1000 \times 150}} $$

Inside the square root:
$$ \frac{120.65}{150{,}000} = 8.043 \times 10^{-4} $$
$$ \sqrt{8.043 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.02836 $$

Therefore:
$$ t = 750 \times 0.02836 = 21.27 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 4 — Apply Class Minimum & Adopt:**
For AH36 hull-girder combined stress, adopt next standard plate thickness:
$$ t_{bottom} = 22 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 5 — Keel Plate:**
$$ t_{keel} = 1.5 \times 22 = 33 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{Adopt } t_{keel} = 35 \text{ mm} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall / Examiner's Note:** Students often forget to:
> 1. Convert *p* in Pa → kPa before plugging into formula F-1.
> 2. Apply the **slamming pressure check** for forward bottom (¼ L from bow).
> 3. Round up to the next **standard plate thickness** (class requirement).
> 4. Compute the keel plate thickness separately using the 1.5 rule.
> Marks are commonly lost on each of these four items.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Explain the term **'slamming pressure'** on the bottom shell plating of a ship. How does it affect plate thickness in the forward region? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Slamming pressure is the **dynamic impact pressure** experienced by the forward bottom plating when the ship heaves out of a wave and re-enters the water surface with high relative velocity. It produces a **local, transient, high-magnitude pressure pulse** that is much greater than the static hydrostatic head.

**Formula (2 marks):**
$$ p_{slam} = C_s \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot (T_f + h_s) $$

where:
- C_s = **slamming coefficient (≈ 1.5 to 2.5)**,
- T_f = forward draft,
- h_s = **slamming head allowance** (typically **1.5 to 2.5 × B** per IACS, but simplified).

**Effect on Plate Thickness (3 marks):**

1. In the **forward ¼ L region**, the bottom plating is **designed for p_slam** rather than p_sw.
2. Plate thickness **increases by 10–25%** compared to amidships (typically).
3. **Forward bottom plating is typically 5 to 8 mm thicker** than amidships.
4. **Bottom longitudinals** in the slamming zone are also sized for increased load (Z increased).
5. **Stiffener spacing s is reduced** (closer longitudinals) to maintain allowable slenderness.
6. **Ductility requirements:** The forward bottom plating requires **Grade D** or **DH36** with better notch toughness to resist brittle fracture under impact.

**Why this matters:** Ships with fine forward sections (slamming-prone) like **frigates, container ships, naval vessels** suffer heavy plate damage if not designed for slamming.

---

**Q. (b)** Compare the **transverse framing system** and the **longitudinal framing system** for bottom shell plating. Which is preferred for modern ships and why? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Transverse System (3 marks):**
| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| Stiffener Direction | Transverse (frame direction) |
| Plate Panel Size | Large (frame spacing × bottom plate length) |
| Plate Thickness Required | **Higher (because of larger unsupported s)** |
| Longitudinal Strength | Relies entirely on shell plating + inner bottom + deck |
| Application | Older ships, ships with length < 100 m, certain bulk carriers |

**Longitudinal System (3 marks):**
| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| Stiffener Direction | Longitudinal (fore-aft) |
| Plate Panel Size | **Small** (longitudinal spacing × transverse web spacing) |
| Plate Thickness Required | **Lower (smaller s)** |
| Longitudinal Strength | **Plate PLUS longitudinals together** contribute to hull-girder section modulus → **15 to 25% increase** |
| Application | **All modern ships**, oil tankers, bulk carriers, container ships |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The **longitudinal framing system is universally preferred for modern ships** because:
1. Reduces plate thickness (material saving ~15%).
2. Increases hull-girder moment of inertia.
3. Better fatigue life (smaller plate panels, lower stress amplitude).
4. Allows lighter scantlings overall → increased deadweight.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often draw **incomplete comparison tables** missing the **combined hull-girder strength contribution** of longitudinals. Marks are awarded specifically for stating that longitudinals add to the **effective section modulus** of the hull girder — a key KTU marking point.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bottom Shell Plating — Rapid Revision Points:**
> - **Definition:** External plating from keel plate to bilge strakes; lower flange of hull girder.
> - **Subdivisions:** Keel → Garboard → Bottom plates → Bilge strake.
> - **Key formula:** $t = s\sqrt{p/(1000 \cdot \sigma_{ref})}$.
> - **Hydrostatic pressure:** $p_{sw} = \rho g d = 1025 \times 9.81 \times d \text{ Pa}$.
> - **Slamming pressure (forward ¼ L):** $p_{slam} = 2.0 \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot T_f$.
> - **Keel thickness rule:** $t_{keel} = 1.4 \text{ to } 1.5 \times t_{side}$.
> - **Stresses:** Tension (sagging) / Compression (hogging) at hull girder level.
> - **Combined stress check:** σ_local + σ_hull_girder ≤ σ_allowable.
> - **Modern preference:** Longitudinal framing — adds to hull section modulus.
> - **Steel grades for bottom:** Grade D, DH36, EH36 — high notch toughness.
> - **Butt joints staggered** between adjacent strakes.
> - **Plate width:** 0.9 to 1.8 m typical; **plate length:** 3 to 6 m typical.
> - **Critical zones:** Keel plate, garboard seam, forward ¼ L (slamming), bilge strake.
> - **Garboard-keel lap joint:** primary shear transfer zone.
> - **Class rule:** For bottom plate amidships of bulk carrier — t ≈ 22 mm with keel 33–35 mm.
> - **Minimum plate thickness** rule of thumb: $t_{min} \approx 4.5 + (L/1000) \text{ mm}$.
> - **Ductility:** Forward bottom requires **Grade D** or **higher** for fracture toughness.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Side Shell Plating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Side Shell Plating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 – Side Shell Plating

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Side Shell Plating** is the outer skin plating forming the sides (port and starboard) of a ship's hull girder, bounded vertically by the **Sheer Strake** at the upper edge (deck sheer line) and the **Bilge Strake** at the lower edge (curved transition region between the bottom and side shell). It is a primary load-bearing component of the hull envelope, contributing directly to the **longitudinal hull girder strength** and resisting **lateral hydrostatic/wave-induced pressure** as well as **slamming and impact loads** in the bow and stern regions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The side shell plating is treated as a series of **strake-wise rectangular orthogonally-stiffened plate panels** supported on four edges by longitudinal stiffeners (frames) and transverse web frames/girders. Design is governed by **Classification Society Rules** (IACS members such as Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, ABS, IRS).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multi-storey steel building** with concrete walls. The vertical outer walls carry both vertical loads (self-weight, floors) and horizontal loads (wind). Now replace the concrete with steel plates, the floor joists with ship frames, and the wind with seawater pressure — that is exactly what the **side shell plating** does. Each steel "wall" is subdivided into horizontal bands called **strakes** (like courses of bricks), and each band is reinforced by vertical "studs" (frames) and horizontal "beams" (girders) so that the plate doesn't buckle under water pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard Metric:** Plating thickness in merchant ships ranges from **10 mm (small craft)** to **50+ mm (VLCC side shell)**.
> - **Material Grade:** Mild Steel (Grade A, B, D, E per IACS UR S6) or **High Tensile Steel (HTS)** of grades AH32, DH36, EH36, FH36 with yield strength up to **355 MPa**.

### 1.3 Position in Hull Structure

The side shell plating is structurally continuous with:
- **Sheer Strake (Top):** Uppermost strake, often thicker (typically 1.0–1.5 mm extra) as it acts as the upper flange of the hull girder.
- **Bilge Strake (Bottom):** Curved strake joining the bottom shell, again thicker due to high stress concentration.
- **Bottom Shell Plating:** Connected at the bilge.
- **Deck Plating:** Connected at the sheer line.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Strake-wise Subdivision of Side Shell

The side shell is divided horizontally into multiple **strakes** (each ~1.0–2.5 m vertical height) to:
1. Facilitate plate rolling in available mill sizes.
2. Permit staggered **butt joints** (offset seams improve fatigue life).
3. Allow the plating near neutral axis to be thinner and far ends thicker — economising steel.

**Staggering Rule (KTU/IACS):** Butt joints in adjacent strakes must be offset by at least **one frame spacing** to avoid creating a continuous transverse weak line.

### 2.2 Design Loads on Side Shell Plating

| Load Type | Description | Magnitude Reference |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Static Hydrostatic Pressure** | Calm sea, head-to-head wave at L/20 | $p = \rho g h$ |
| **Dynamic Wave Pressure** | SLOSH / Green-water / Bow flare impact | $p_{dyn} = C_d \cdot \rho V^2 / 2$ |
| **Slamming Pressure** | Bow emergence region (forward 0.25L) | Up to **350 kPa** (IACS S21) |
| **Cargo / Internal Pressure** | Tank boundaries, ballast | $p_{tank} = \rho_i g h_i$ |
| **Sloshing Pressure** | Partially filled tanks | IACS S21 / S22 |

### 2.3 Plate Panel Behaviour — The Core Engineering Concept

A side shell **plate panel** is bounded by:
- **Long edges** → Longitudinal frames (e.g., side frames, longitudinals)
- **Short edges** → Transverse web frames / bulkheads

The panel is loaded by transverse pressure $p$, producing:
- **Plate bending** (out-of-plane deflection $w$)
- **In-plane hull-girder stresses** ($\sigma_x$ from bending moment, $\sigma_y$ from shear)
- **Buckling** of the compressed panel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plate Aspect Ratio ($\alpha$):** $\alpha = a/b$ where $a$ = long edge, $b$ = short edge. Critical for determining **boundary condition factor** in yield check.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulae are the **core exam-relevant** equations for side shell plating design in KTU ESE 2024.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $p = \rho g h$ | Static hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | kPa |
| 2 | $t = s \sqrt{p / (\sigma_{perm} \cdot k)}$ | Net plate thickness (simplified CSR) | mm |
| 3 | $t_{gross} = t_{net} + t_{corrosion}$ | Net + corrosion allowance ($t_{cor} = $ 0.5–2.0 mm) | mm |
| 4 | $b_{eff} = \chi \cdot s$ | Effective breadth of plating acting with stiffener | mm |
| 5 | $\chi = \frac{2.4}{\alpha^2} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1.4}{\alpha + 1.8}\right)$ for $\alpha \ge 1$ | Effective breadth factor (IACS CSR) | – |
| 6 | $\sigma_{perm} = \sigma_y / 1.0$ (yield) or $\sigma_y / 1.6$ (buckling) | Permissible stress (CSR) | MPa |
| 7 | $SM_{req} = M / \sigma_{perm}$ | Required section modulus of stiffener + effective plating | cm³ |
| 8 | $\sigma_x = \dfrac{M_{sw}}{Z_{eff}} \cdot 10^3$ | Hull girder bending stress in side shell | MPa |
| 9 | $M_{sw} = C_w \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot C_B$ | Still-water bending moment (CSR) | kN·m |
| 10 | $\lambda = \dfrac{b}{t} \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}}$ | Slenderness ratio of plate (column buckling check) | – |

> **Reserved-symbol note:** All pipe symbols within expressions above are rendered using `$\vert$` to preserve markdown safety.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hull-girder longitudinal strength:** Side shell acts as the **web/upper-flange** of the hull girder.
- **Crashworthiness:** Side shell is the **first barrier** in collision/grounding; its thickness governs the **Pmin** (minimum energy dissipation) per IMO MSC.1/Circ.1455.
- **Fatigue hot-spots:** Side shell near hatch corners, side openings, and bilge region are **class-critical** for fatigue.
- **Corrosion strategy:** Side shell may be **coated + cathodic-protected** (sacrificial Zn/Al anodes).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Breadth Factor $\chi$ (IACS CSR Basis)

The **effective breadth** concept accounts for **shear-lag** in plating attached to a stiffener — the stress is maximum at the stiffener and decays toward mid-panel.

Starting from the elastic shear-lag solution for a simply-supported plate strip:

$$
\sigma_x(y) = \sigma_{max} \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{(y/s)^{2.4}}{\text{const}}\right]
$$

Integrating over the spacing $s$ and equating the mean stress to the effective uniform stress:

$$
\sigma_{eff} \cdot b_{eff} = \int_{-s/2}^{s/2} \sigma_x(y) \, dy
$$

After non-dimensionalising with aspect ratio $\alpha = a/b$, the **IACS CSR closed-form** effective breadth factor for the side shell (a ≥ b) is:

$$
\chi = \dfrac{b_{eff}}{b} = \dfrac{2.4}{\alpha^2}\left(1 - \dfrac{1.4}{\alpha + 1.8}\right)
$$

For $\alpha < 1$ (a < b), interchange the roles of $a$ and $b$ in the formula.

**Boundary condition:** $\chi \le 1.0$ (effective breadth never exceeds the actual spacing).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU ESE Style)

**Problem:** A side shell plate panel in a bulk carrier has frame spacing $s = 0.7$ m and is supported on web frames at $l = 2.1$ m. The panel lies at a depth of $h = 6$ m below the design waterline. Steel grade: AH36 ($\sigma_y = 355$ MPa). Determine the **net required plate thickness** using the simplified CSR formula, with a corrosion allowance $t_{cor} = 1.5$ mm. Sea-water density $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1: Calculate hydrostatic pressure $p$**

$$
p = \rho g h = 1.025 \times 9.81 \times 6
$$

$$
p = 60.33 \, \text{kPa} = 0.0603 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2: Identify permissible stress for plate bending (yield check)**

For plate bending, the permissible bending stress is the yield stress:

$$
\sigma_{perm} = \sigma_y = 355 \, \text{MPa}
$$

(For buckling check, the permissible stress would be reduced; we apply yield criterion for the net plate first.)

**Step 3: Apply the simplified CSR plate thickness formula**

$$
t_{net} = s \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{\sigma_{perm}}}
$$

Substitute numerical values (use $s$ in mm and $p$, $\sigma_{perm}$ in MPa, with the factor $k=1$):

$$
t_{net} = 700 \, \text{mm} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0603}{355}}
$$

First compute the ratio:

$$
\dfrac{0.0603}{355} = 1.699 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Take the square root:

$$
\sqrt{1.699 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01304
$$

Therefore:

$$
t_{net} = 700 \times 0.01304 = 9.13 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 4: Add corrosion allowance to obtain gross thickness**

$$
t_{gross} = t_{net} + t_{cor} = 9.13 + 1.5 = 10.63 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 5: Adopt next standard plate thickness (IACS mill series)**

Standard ship plate thicknesses (mm): …, 10, 11, 12, 12.5, 14, …

Adopted thickness:

$$
\boxed{t_{adopted} = 11 \, \text{mm}}
$$

**Step 6: Verify slenderness for column-type buckling**

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{b}{t} \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}}
$$

With $b = s = 700$ mm, $t = 11$ mm, $E = 2.06 \times 10^5$ MPa:

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{700}{11} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{355}{206000}}
$$

Compute inside the root:

$$
\dfrac{355}{206000} = 1.723 \times 10^{-3} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sqrt{1.723 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.04151
$$

$$
\lambda = 63.64 \times 0.04151 = 2.64
$$

Since $\lambda = 2.64 < 1.0 \cdot \text{limit (CSR limit for plating) } \approx 3.0$, the panel is acceptable. (If $\lambda$ were too high, the thickness would need to be increased.)

**Step 7: Section modulus check on the panel as a beam strip**

For a unit width plate strip (1.0 m wide, 11 mm thick):

$$
SM_{plate} = \dfrac{1000 \times 11^2}{6} = \dfrac{1000 \times 121}{6} = 20{,}166.67 \, \text{mm}^3 / \text{m} = 20.17 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{m}
$$

Bending moment per metre width (simply supported):

$$
M = \dfrac{p \cdot s^2}{8} = \dfrac{0.0603 \times 0.7^2}{8} = \dfrac{0.0295}{8} = 0.003694 \, \text{MPa·m} = 3.694 \, \text{kN·m/m}
$$

Bending stress in plate:

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{3.694 \times 10^6 \, \text{N·mm/m}}{20{,}166.67 \, \text{mm}^3/\text{m}} = 183.2 \, \text{MPa}
$$

Since $183.2 < 355$ MPa → **Plate is safe in bending** by a margin of 1.94.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above is the **net** plate check. **Hull-girder bending stress** $\sigma_x$ must be superimposed before final sizing. KTU ESE problems often combine transverse plate bending with longitudinal hull-girder stress (see CSR Pt 1, Ch 4).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Side Shell Plating in Hull Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SL["SHEER STRAKE (Top - Thicker)"]
        SS["Top strake of side shell\nActs as upper hull girder flange\nTypically 1.0-1.5 mm thicker"]
    end

    subgraph M1["UPPER SIDE STRATE (A)"]
        US["Plating between sheer and 2nd tier\nConnected to side longitudinals"]
    end

    subgraph M2["MID SIDE STRATE (B)"]
        MS["Plating near neutral axis\nThinnest zone in many designs"]
    end

    subgraph M3["LOWER SIDE STRATE (C)"]
        LS["Plating near bilge\nIncreasing thickness toward bilge"]
    end

    subgraph BL["BILGE STRAKE (Curved Transition)"]
        BS["Curved plate at hull knuckle\nHighest thickness - stress hotspot"]
    end

    subgraph BSPL["BOTTOM SHELL PLATING"]
        BT["Plates forming bottom of hull\nConnected at keel"]
    end

    SL --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> BL
    BL --> BSPL

    SF["SIDE FRAMES / LONGITUDINALS\n(Vertical stiffeners between strakes)"] -.-> M1
    SF -.-> M2
    SF -.-> M3

    WF["WEB FRAMES / GIRDERS\n(Horizontal transverse supports)"] ===> SL
    WF ===> M1
    WF ===> M2
    WF ===> M3
    WF ===> BL

    LP["HYDROSTATIC + WAVE PRESSURE\n(Acts on outer face)"] --> SS
    LP --> US
    LP --> MS
    LP --> LS
    LP --> BS

    classDef strate fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1a4d80,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stiff fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class SS,US,MS,LS,BS,BT strate
    class SF,WF stiff
    class LP load
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Design Logic for Side Shell Plate Thickness

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Start: Define Load Case"] --> B["Compute Design Pressure p\n(hydrostatic + dynamic + slamming)"]
    B --> C["Determine Frame Spacing s\nand Web Frame Spacing l"]
    C --> D["Choose Steel Grade\n(Yield Stress sigma_y)"]
    D --> E["Compute Net Thickness\nt_net = s * sqrt(p / sigma_perm)"]
    E --> F{"t_net + Corrosion\nwithin mill series?"}
    F -- No --> G["Step up to next\nstandard thickness"]
    F -- Yes --> H["Adopt t_gross"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Effective Breadth Check\nb_eff = chi * s"]
    I --> J["Slenderness / Buckling Check\nlambda less than CSR limit"]
    J --> K{"Acceptable?"}
    K -- No --> G
    K -- Yes --> L["Finalize: Plate + Stiffener\nSection Modulus Check"]
    L --> M["End: Plating Schedule"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Plate Panel Bending

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Panel["Plate Panel: a x b (long x short)"]
        P1["Top long edge: Longitudinal frame"]
        P2["Bottom long edge: Longitudinal frame"]
        P3["Left short edge: Web frame / Bulkhead"]
        P4["Right short edge: Web frame / Bulkhead"]
    end
    P1 -.Simply Supported.-> P3
    P3 -.Simply Supported.-> P2
    P2 -.Simply Supported.-> P4
    P4 -.Simply Supported.-> P1

    PR["Uniform Lateral Pressure p (kPa)"] ==> P1
    PR ==> P2
    PR ==> P3
    PR ==> P4

    DM["Deflection w(x,y)\nMax at panel centre"] -.-> Panel
    BM["Bending Moment M = p*b^2/8\nper unit width"] -.-> Panel
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model question pattern]**

**Q1. Define side shell plating. Name the strakes that bound it vertically.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Side shell plating is the outer plating on the port and starboard sides of a ship's hull that forms part of the watertight skin and contributes to hull girder strength. Vertically, it is bounded by the **sheer strake** at the top (at the deck sheer line) and the **bilge strake** at the bottom (at the curvature connecting the side shell to the bottom shell). Together with adjacent plating, it transmits hydrostatic and hydrodynamic loads to the framing system. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. What is the "effective breadth" concept in side shell plating design?**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The effective breadth concept accounts for **shear-lag** in plating attached to a longitudinal stiffener. Due to shear deformation, the in-plane longitudinal stress in the plate is maximum at the stiffener and reduces toward the middle of the panel. The **effective breadth** $b_{eff} = \chi \cdot s$ (where $\chi$ is a factor depending on panel aspect ratio $\alpha$ and boundary conditions) represents an equivalent uniform width of plate that, when stressed uniformly, produces the same axial force as the actual non-uniform stress distribution. This value is then combined with the stiffener to compute the section modulus and hull girder stress. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 model question pattern: Choice-based, 14 marks with sub-parts]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q(a). With a neat schematic, explain the side shell plating arrangement of a typical bulk carrier. Identify the strakes and explain why the sheer and bilge strakes are made thicker. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Arrangement:* The side shell plating of a bulk carrier is divided horizontally into a series of strakes between the sheer strake (top) and the bilge strake (bottom). From top to bottom these are typically the **sheer strake**, **upper side strakes**, **middle side strakes**, **lower side strakes**, and the **bilge strake**. Each strake is connected to the next by horizontal **seams** and individual plates within a strake are joined by vertical **butts**. (3 marks)

*Staggering:* Butts in adjacent strakes are offset by at least one frame spacing to avoid a continuous transverse weak joint. (1 mark)

*Sheer strake thickness:* The sheer strake is the uppermost strake, located at the deck sheer line. It acts as the **upper flange of the hull girder** and therefore carries very high longitudinal hull-girder stress (sagging and hogging). Additionally, it is exposed to **green-sea loading and deck-edge impact**, and provides the upper termination for longitudinal framing. Hence it is made 1.0–1.5 mm thicker than adjacent strakes. (1.5 marks)

*Bilge strake thickness:* The bilge strake is the curved plate at the knuckle between the side shell and the bottom shell. It is a region of **high stress concentration** due to (i) sharp curvature causing stress raise, (ii) high combined hull-girder stresses, and (iii) **slamming and impact loading** in the forward region. Hence a higher thickness is adopted. (1.5 marks)

**Q(b). A side shell plate in a single-skin vessel has frame spacing 750 mm. The design pressure at the plate's location is 0.08 MPa. Steel grade is AH32 with $\sigma_y = 315$ MPa. Determine the net and gross plate thickness using the simplified CSR formula, with a corrosion allowance of 2.0 mm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- Frame spacing: $s = 750$ mm
- Design pressure: $p = 0.08$ MPa
- Yield stress: $\sigma_y = 315$ MPa
- Corrosion allowance: $t_{cor} = 2.0$ mm

**Step 1:** Apply the simplified CSR plate thickness formula:

$$
t_{net} = s \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{\sigma_{perm}}}
$$

For yield check, $\sigma_{perm} = \sigma_y = 315$ MPa:

$$
t_{net} = 750 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{0.08}{315}}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the ratio:

$$
\dfrac{0.08}{315} = 2.540 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3:** Square root:

$$
\sqrt{2.540 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01594
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply:

$$
t_{net} = 750 \times 0.01594 = 11.95 \, \text{mm} \approx 12.0 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 5:** Gross thickness:

$$
t_{gross} = t_{net} + t_{cor} = 12.0 + 2.0 = 14.0 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 6:** Adopt the next standard mill size (the mill series includes 12, 14, 16 mm…):

$$
\boxed{t_{adopted} = 14 \, \text{mm}}
$$

*(Full marks: 1 mark for the formula selection, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for arithmetic, 1 mark for adding corrosion allowance, 1 mark for standardisation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative**

**Q(a). Explain the factors influencing the thickness of side shell plating in a merchant ship. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Design Pressure (3 marks):** The thickness is directly proportional to $\sqrt{p}$ where $p$ is the maximum combined hydrostatic + dynamic + slamming pressure at the plate's location. Plates deeper in the hull, in the slamming region, or in ballast/cargo tank boundaries require higher thickness.
2. **Frame Spacing (1 mark):** A larger frame spacing $s$ increases the unsupported plate edge length, requiring higher thickness. The CSR formula $t = s\sqrt{p/\sigma}$ shows the direct proportionality.
3. **Material Grade (1 mark):** Higher-tensile steels (AH32, DH36, EH36) allow higher permissible stress, which reduces required thickness. Mild steel requires more thickness for the same load.
4. **Corrosion Allowance (1 mark):** A 0.5–2.0 mm corrosion margin (depending on service: ballast tanks, cargo oil, sea-side) is added to the net thickness.
5. **Hull Girder Stress (1 mark):** Side shell participates as the upper flange of the hull girder; the longitudinal stress $\sigma_x$ is superimposed on transverse bending, and the combined stress must remain below the permissible. This can govern plate thickness in midship region.

**Q(b). A side shell plate panel has long-edge spacing $a = 2.4$ m (between web frames) and short-edge spacing $b = 0.8$ m (between longitudinal frames). Calculate the effective breadth factor $\chi$ using the IACS CSR formula. If the longitudinal stiffener section modulus is 80 cm³ and the permissible stress is 220 MPa, compute the section modulus required to be provided by the plate (effective width basis) for a hull girder stress of 120 MPa. The plate thickness is 12 mm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute aspect ratio:

$$
\alpha = \dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{2.4}{0.8} = 3.0
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the IACS CSR effective breadth factor formula (for $\alpha \ge 1$):

$$
\chi = \dfrac{2.4}{\alpha^2}\left(1 - \dfrac{1.4}{\alpha + 1.8}\right)
$$

Substitute $\alpha = 3.0$:

$$
\chi = \dfrac{2.4}{9.0}\left(1 - \dfrac{1.4}{3.0 + 1.8}\right)
$$

$$
\chi = 0.2667 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{1.4}{4.8}\right)
$$

$$
\chi = 0.2667 \times (1 - 0.2917) = 0.2667 \times 0.7083 = 0.1889
$$

Since $\chi$ must be capped at 1.0, $\chi = 0.1889$ is acceptable (and well below 1.0).

**Step 3:** Effective breadth:

$$
b_{eff} = \chi \cdot s = 0.1889 \times 800 = 151.1 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Section modulus contributed by the plate:

$$
SM_{plate} = \dfrac{b_{eff} \cdot t^2}{6} = \dfrac{151.1 \times 12^2}{6} = \dfrac{151.1 \times 144}{6} = 3626.4 \, \text{mm}^3 = 3.63 \, \text{cm}^3
$$

**Step 5:** Total force-equivalent required SM for the axial hull girder stress check.

**Required additional SM from plate to share stress with stiffener:**

The stiffener alone carries $120 \times 80 = 9600 \, \text{N·cm} = 96 \, \text{kN·cm}$ in axial force. Adding the plate in parallel:

Total force the combination must carry (per IACS CSR, total force = $\sigma \times A$ approach, not SM approach). Alternatively, for the SM check (bending):

The plate's contribution of $3.63 \, \text{cm}^3$ is **insufficient** to significantly reduce the required stiffener section modulus; in practice, the stiffener's effective SM including effective plating is checked against the bending moment demand.

For a bending moment demand, suppose the moment per spacing is $M = 9.6$ kN·m at the section. Required SM:

$$
SM_{req} = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_{perm}} = \dfrac{9.6 \times 10^6 \, \text{N·mm}}{220 \, \text{N/mm}^2} = 43{,}636 \, \text{mm}^3 = 43.64 \, \text{cm}^3
$$

The stiffener contributes 80 cm³, which is sufficient by itself, so the effective plate provides additional reserve.

**Step 6:** Final answer — the **effective breadth factor is 0.189**, the effective breadth is 151 mm, and the plate's SM contribution is 3.63 cm³, which supplements the stiffener.

*(1 mark for aspect ratio, 2 marks for χ formula and substitution, 1 mark for $b_{eff}$, 1 mark for plate SM, 2 marks for interpretation/required SM.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Mixing units:** $s$ in mm, $p$ in MPa, $\sigma$ in MPa → forgetting the dimensionless ratio. Always compute the ratio $p/\sigma$ as a pure number before taking the root.
> 2. **Forgetting corrosion allowance:** KTU answer key specifically deducts **2 marks** if $t_{cor}$ is omitted in a numerical.
> 3. **Not stating the standard mill series adopted:** Examiners expect a sentence like "Adopting 12 mm from IACS mill series" — losing **1 mark** if skipped.
> 4. **Confusing net vs gross thickness:** "Required thickness" in the problem usually means **gross** in KTU papers. Read carefully!
> 5. **Aspect ratio direction:** $\alpha = a/b$ where $a$ is the longer side. Reversing this gives a wrong $\chi$ — losing **2 marks**.
> 6. **Missing diagram labels:** In 7-mark descriptive questions, an unlabelled sketch attracts only **partial credit** (typically half marks for content + half for diagram).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Side shell plating = outer skin on port/starboard, between **sheer strake** (top) and **bilge strake** (bottom).
- **Key strakes:** Sheer (thicker), Upper/Middle/Lower side strakes, Bilge (thicker).
- **Primary loads:** Lateral hydrostatic pressure, dynamic wave pressure, slamming, hull-girder longitudinal stress.
- **Core formula (memorise):** $t = s\sqrt{p/\sigma_{perm}}$ and $b_{eff} = \chi \cdot s$ with $\chi = \dfrac{2.4}{\alpha^2}\left(1 - \dfrac{1.4}{\alpha+1.8}\right)$.
- **Corrosion allowance:** Always add $t_{cor}$ (0.5–2.0 mm) → **net + corrosion = gross**; adopt next standard mill thickness.
- **Sheer & bilge strakes:** Made 1.0–1.5 mm thicker than adjacent strakes due to high hull-girder stress and slamming/curvature effects.
- **Butt joints:** Must be staggered across strakes by at least one frame spacing.
- **Material grades:** Mild steel (Grade A–E) and HTS (AH32, DH36, EH36, FH36) with $\sigma_y$ up to **355 MPa**.
- **Design sequence (must learn):** Loads → Pressure → Net thickness → + Corrosion → Standardise → Effective breadth → Slenderness → Section modulus check.
- **Effective breadth concept:** Shear-lag → stress concentration at stiffener → use $\chi$ ≤ 1.0.
- **Hull girder participation:** Side shell acts as the upper flange — longitudinal stress $\sigma_x$ must be combined with plate bending.
- **Classification Society:** All thickness rules trace back to IACS CSR Pt 1, Ch 4 / Pt 1, Ch 7 (for buckling).
- **Sketch must include:** Strakes, frames (longitudinals), web frames/girders, pressure arrows, label sheer & bilge strakes.
- **Unit discipline:** $s$ in mm; $p$ in MPa (or convert from kPa by /1000); $\sigma$ in MPa; $t$ in mm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Additional Stiffening for Panting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Additional Stiffening for Panting # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Panting** is a localized dynamic phenomenon observed in the shell plating of a ship, particularly in the **forward and after regions**, wherein the plating between transverse supporting members (floors, frames, bulkheads) alternately deflects **inward and outward** in a cyclic manner due to the rapid, pulsating hydrostatic and hydrodynamic pressure variations caused by wave action, bow emergence, and slamming loads. **Additional Stiffening for Panting** refers to the supplementary structural reinforcement members — distinct from the primary hull girder scantlings — that are introduced specifically to arrest panting-induced plate vibrations and buckling. These include **panting stringers, panting knees, gusset plates, brackets, and thickened plates** in critical regions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Panting is a high-frequency, **plate-localized** vibration phenomenon, *not* a global hull-girder bending problem. It occurs because the unsupported shell plate acts as a thin diaphragm that responds dynamically to fluctuating sea pressure at frequencies approaching its natural flexural frequency. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine holding a **thin steel tray** (like a baking sheet) with both hands and gently pushing the middle of the tray **in and out repeatedly** with your fingers. Even though the tray is rigid, the thin central region of the metal starts to **bow inward and outward** with each push — making a slight "pant" sound. This is exactly what happens to a ship's shell plate. Now imagine placing **a stiff steel rib down the middle of the tray** — the bowing almost vanishes. That rib is analogous to a **panting stringer**. **Real-World Ship Analogy:** A warship or large vessel ploughing through stormy seas has its **bow region alternately submerged and emerged** from water. Each submersion creates a sudden pressure spike on the forward bottom plating, and each emergence creates a partial vacuum. This rapid pressure oscillation is the "finger" pushing the tray — the ship is the tray, the sea is the finger, and the panting stringers are the ribs we add to stiffen it. ## 1.3 Why Panting is a Concern | Aspect | Impact on Ship | |---|---| | **Material fatigue** | Cyclic deflection → crack initiation in shell plating | | **Plate buckling** | Loss of local stiffness → progressive collapse | | **Water ingress** | Joint loosening at welds between floors and shell | | **Strength loss** | Reduction in effective section modulus of hull girder | | **Location** | Most severe at **forward 1/4 length** and **aft region** | > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants** (used in panting analysis): > - Modulus of Elasticity of ship steel: **E = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² = 200 GPa** > - Poisson's ratio: **ν = 0.3** > - Density of sea water: **ρ = 1.025 t/m³** > - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physics of Panting — Why Does it Occur? Panting arises from the **dynamic interaction** between the fluctuating wave/sea pressure and the structural response of the shell plate panel. The mechanism is as follows: 1. **Hydrodynamic Pressure Pulse** — As the ship moves forward, the bow alternately clears and submerges into the wave surface. The forward bottom plating experiences alternating overpressure (submersion) and under-pressure (emergence). 2. **Plate Panel as a Spring-Mass System** — The shell plate panel (bounded by floors and frames) behaves like a thin plate with mass and stiffness. It has a **natural flexural frequency** that can be calculated using plate vibration theory. 3. **Resonance Danger** — If the wave encounter frequency matches the plate's natural frequency, large-amplitude panting oscillations occur. This is the most critical scenario. 4. **Static Buckling Component** — Even without resonance, the combined action of **in-plane hull-girder compression** (sagging/hogging) and **lateral sea pressure** can cause static elastic buckling of the plate. ## 2.2 Structural Response — Step-by-Step Logic - **Step 1:** Identify the shell plate panel — defined between two adjacent floors (longitudinally) and two adjacent frames/girders (transversely). - **Step 2:** Determine the **slenderness ratio** b/t (breadth to thickness). - **Step 3:** Compute the **lateral pressure** p acting on the plate (hydrostatic + hydrodynamic + slamming component). - **Step 4:** Compute the **edge compressive stress** σₓ arising from the hull girder bending action. - **Step 5:** Check the panel against **combined loading buckling criteria** (using classification society rules or theoretical plate buckling). - **Step 6:** If the panel fails, add **panting stringers** to reduce the effective breadth b, thereby raising the critical buckling stress. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Description / Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Lateral Sea Pressure** (hydrostatic at depth h) | $p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Used as static component of panting load | | 2 | **Slenderness Ratio of Plate** | $\lambda = \dfrac{b}{t}$ | Ratio of supported breadth to thickness | | 3 | **Elastic Critical Buckling Stress** (simply supported plate) | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$ | k = buckling coefficient; for uniaxial compression, k ≈ 4.0 | | 4 | **Non-dimensional Slenderness** | $\overline{\lambda} = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{\sigma_{cr}}}$ | Used to classify plate as stocky, intermediate, or slender | | 5 | **Plate Natural Frequency (m,n mode)** | $f_{mn} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \left[\dfrac{m^4}{a^4} + \dfrac{n^4}{b^4} + \dfrac{2 m^2 n^2}{a^2 b^2}\right] \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\rho_s h}}$ | Plate vibration;
