D = flexural rigidity | | 6 | **Flexural Rigidity of Plate** | $D = \dfrac{E \cdot t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | Bending stiffness per unit width | | 7 | **Maximum Panting Stress** (lateral pressure on simply supported plate) | $\sigma_{p,max} = \dfrac{p \cdot b^2}{2 \cdot t^2}$ | Used to assess fatigue in shell plating | | 8 | **Required Panting Stringer Spacing (Bureau/ABS empirical rule)** | $L \le 1.54 \cdot d$ | For L < 2d; L = stringer spacing, d = floor spacing | | 9 | **Johnson-Ostenfeld Effective Stress** (for stiffened panels with imperfections) | $\sigma_{eff} = f_y \left(1 - \dfrac{\sigma_{cr}}{f_y} \cdot \dfrac{\overline{\lambda} - 0.22}{\overline{\lambda}^2}\right)$ | For $\overline{\lambda} \ge 0.2$ (inelastic range) | | 10 | **Combined Loading Interaction** | $\dfrac{\sigma_x}{\sigma_{x,cr}} + \left(\dfrac{p}{p_{cr}}\right)^n \le 1.0$ | n ≈ 1 for LR/ABS rules | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Panting analysis is critical for: - **Warships & Naval Craft** — High-speed vessels experiencing severe slamming - **Container Ships & Tankers** — Forward parallel mid-body section vulnerable to slamming - **Submarines (Surfacing)** — Dynamic pressure on outer hull - **High-Speed Ferries** — Bottom plate slamming during high-speed operation - **Classification Society Scantling** — Forms basis of IACS Unified Rules (UR S21, UR S22) for hull structural design # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Panting Stress in a Simply Supported Plate Panel **Problem Setup:** A rectangular plate panel of breadth *b* and thickness *t*, simply supported on all four edges, is subjected to a uniform lateral pressure *p*. Derive the maximum bending stress. ### Step 1 — Plate Equation (Classical Plate Theory — Kirchhoff-Love) For a thin isotropic plate under lateral pressure, the governing differential equation is: $$ D \cdot \nabla^4 w = p $$ where $w$ is the lateral deflection and $D$ is the flexural rigidity given by: $$ D = \frac{E \cdot t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} $$ ### Step 2 — Navier Solution for Simply Supported Plate For a simply supported rectangular plate with dimensions a × b, the deflection series (Navier's method) gives: $$ w(x,y) = \sum_{m=1}^{\infty} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_{mn} \cdot \sin\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right) \sin\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right) $$ For a **uniform pressure p**, the dominant first-mode coefficient is: $$ A_{11} = \frac{16 \cdot p}{\pi^6 \cdot D \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{a^2} + \dfrac{1}{b^2}\right)^2} $$ ### Step 3 — Bending Moment Expression The bending moment per unit width about the y-axis is: $$ M_x = -D \left(\frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2}\right) $$ Evaluating at the centre of the plate (x = a/2, y = b/2) and simplifying for the dominant (1,1) mode, the maximum moment becomes: $$ M_{x,max} = \frac{p \cdot a^2 b^2}{2 \pi^2 \left(a^2 + b^2\right)} \cdot \left(\frac{a^2 + \nu b^2}{a^2 + b^2}\right) $$ ### Step 4 — Maximum Bending Stress The maximum bending stress in the plate fibre is given by the flexure formula: $$ \sigma_{p,max} = \frac{6 \cdot M_{x,max}}{t^2} $$ For a **square plate** (a = b) and ν = 0.3, this simplifies to: $$ \sigma_{p,max} = \frac{6}{t^2} \cdot \frac{p \cdot a^4}{2 \pi^2 (2a^2)} \cdot \left(\frac{a^2 + 0.3 a^2}{2 a^2}\right) $$ Let us simplify step-by-step: $$ \sigma_{p,max} = \frac{6 p a^4}{2 \pi^2 t^2 \cdot 2 a^2} \cdot \frac{1.3 a^2}{2 a^2} $$ $$ \sigma_{p,max} = \frac{6 p a^2}{4 \pi^2 t^2} \cdot 0.65 $$ $$ \sigma_{p,max} \approx \frac{0.975 \cdot p \cdot a^2}{\pi^2 \cdot t^2} $$ Using $\pi^2 \approx 9.87$ and noting the **engineering approximation** used in shipbuilding practice for shell plate panels with aspect ratio near 1: $$ \sigma_{p,max} \approx \frac{p \cdot a^2}{2 \cdot t^2} $$ This is the **engineering shortcut** used in classification rules. For aspect ratio not equal to 1, the general expression is: $$ \sigma_{p,max} = \frac{p \cdot b^2}{2 \cdot t^2} \cdot f\left(\frac{a}{b}, \nu\right) $$ > [!NOTE] > The function $f(a/b, \nu)$ is a **shape factor** that is 1.0 for a square plate. For $a \gg b$ (long narrow plate), the factor approaches 1.0 in the short-span direction. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Combined Loading Buckling Check | Loading State | Governing Equation | Failure Mode | Remedial Stiffening | |---|---|---|---| | Pure in-plane compression | $\sigma_x = \dfrac{F}{b \cdot t}$ | Column-like buckling | Add panting stringers parallel to $\sigma_x$ | | Pure lateral pressure | $\sigma_{p,max} = \dfrac{p \cdot b^2}{2 t^2}$ | Plate bending/yield | Increase plate thickness *t* | | Combined | $\dfrac{\sigma_x}{\sigma_{cr,x}} + \dfrac{\sigma_p}{\sigma_{cr,p}} \le 1$ | Interaction buckling | Both — add stringers **and** increase t | | Cyclic/Dynamic | $\sigma_{p,cyclic} = K_{dyn} \cdot \sigma_{p,static}$ | Fatigue cracking | Add **panting knees + brackets** for joint stiffness | ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example **Given:** - Shell plate panel between floors: floor spacing d = 2.0 m - Plate thickness t = 12 mm - Lateral pressure p = 80 kN/m² (typical slamming pressure in forward region) - Steel yield stress f_y = 235 N/mm² - Steel E = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm², ν = 0.3 **Required:** Check if additional panting stringers are needed. If so, compute maximum allowed spacing. **Step 1 — Maximum Panting Stress (assuming no intermediate stringers):** $$ \sigma_{p,max} = \frac{p \cdot d^2}{2 t^2} $$ Convert units: p = 80 × 10⁻³ N/mm², d = 2000 mm, t = 12 mm. $$ \sigma_{p,max} = \frac{(80 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2000)^2}{2 \times (12)^2} $$ $$ \sigma_{p,max} = \frac{80 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 144} $$ $$ \sigma_{p,max} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{5}}{288} $$ $$ \sigma_{p,max} \approx 1111 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Compare to Yield Stress:** $$ \sigma_{p,max} = 1111 \text{ N/mm}^2 \gg f_y = 235 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Conclusion:** The plate will yield catastrophically. **Panting stringers are mandatory.** **Step 4 — Determine Required Stringer Spacing:** Using the criterion $\sigma_{p,max} \le f_y / 1.5$ (safety factor of 1.5 for fatigue and dynamic effects): $$ \sigma_{allow} = \frac{235}{1.5} \approx 156.7 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ Setting $\sigma_{p,max} = \sigma_{allow}$ and solving for the new effective spacing L: $$ L = t \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \sigma_{allow}}{p}} $$ $$ L = 12 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 156.7}{80 \times 10^{-3}}} $$ $$ L = 12 \times \sqrt{\frac{313.4}{0.08}} $$ $$ L = 12 \times \sqrt{3917.5} $$ $$ L = 12 \times 62.59 \approx 751 \text{ mm} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Verdict:** The original floor spacing of **2000 mm** must be subdivided by panting stringers into spans of **at most ~750 mm** to keep stresses within safe limits. This requires **at least 2–3 panting stringers** between every pair of adjacent floors in the forward region. ## 3.4 Additional Stiffening Elements — Design Logic The following supplementary members are required to combat panting: 1. **Panting Stringers** — Longitudinal intercostal side stringers placed between main frames to subdivide shell panels. They run parallel to the centreline. 2. **Panting Knees** — Triangular bracket plates at the upper and lower ends of floors to provide end-fixity and prevent pull-out of floors from the shell. 3. **Gusset Plates at Floor-Girder Intersections** — Reduce stress concentration at cruciform joints. 4. **Increased Plate Thickness** — In critical forward regions (0.2L from bow), plate thickness may be increased by 1–2 mm. 5. **Close-Spaced Frame Rings** — In way of forward and aft peaks, frames are placed at closer spacing (~400–500 mm). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Panting Stiffening System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hull Shell Plate Panel
Longitudinally between floors
Transversely between frames] A --> B{Loads Acting on Panel} B --> C[Lateral Hydrostatic
Pressure p] B --> D[Hull Girder
In-plane Compression sigma_x] B --> E[Cyclic Wave
Slamming Load] C --> F[Plate Bending Stress
sigma_p = p*b^2 / 2t^2] D --> G[Plate Buckling
Stress sigma_cr] E --> H[Dynamic Stress
Amplification K_dyn] F --> I{Check sigma_p
vs Allowable} G --> I H --> I I -->|Exceeds Allowable| J[ADD ADDITIONAL STIFFENING] I -->|Within Allowable| K[Original Scantlings Sufficient] J --> L[Panting Stringers
Subdivide Panel] J --> M[Panting Knees
End Fixity at Floors] J --> N[Gusset Plates
at Intersections] J --> O[Increase Plate
Thickness t] L --> P[Reduced Effective b
Higher sigma_cr] M --> P N --> P O --> P P --> Q[Panel Strength
Satisfied] K --> Q Q --> R[Fatigue Assessment
S-N Curve Check] R --> S[Final Design
Approved] ``` ## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Arrangement of Panting Stiffening ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ForwardRegion["FORWARD SHIP REGION - PANTING CRITICAL ZONE"] direction TB TK1[Topside Plate] --> PStr1[Panting Stringer 1
BSH W100x10] PStr1 --> PStr2[Panting Stringer 2
BSH W100x10] PStr2 --> PStr3[Panting Stringer 3
BSH W100x10] PStr3 --> PStr4[Panting Stringer 4
BSH W100x10] PStr4 --> BottomPlate[Bottom Shell Plate] F1[Floor F-1] -.->|Bracket Knee| PStr1 F1 -.->|Bracket Knee| PStr2 F1 -.->|Bracket Knee| PStr3 F1 -.->|Bracket Knee| PStr4 F2[Floor F-2] -.->|Bracket Knee| PStr1 F2 -.->|Bracket Knee| PStr2 F2 -.->|Bracket Knee| PStr3 F2 -.->|Bracket Knee| PStr4 end ``` ## 4.3 3D Visual — Panting Stringer + Knee Assembly ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Shell Plate
t = 12 mm] -->|Welded| B[Panting Stringer
T-Section or Flat Bar
150 mm x 12 mm] A -->|Welded| C[Floor Plate
t = 10 mm] B --> D[Panting Knee
Triangular Bracket
h = 600 mm, t = 10 mm] C --> D D -->|Welded Connection| E[Inner Bottom / Side Shell] style A fill:#a8d5e2 style B fill:#f4a261 style C fill:#2a9d8f style D fill:#e76f51 style E fill:#264653 ``` ## 4.4 Panting Stringer Layout Plan View (Forward 1/4 Length) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PlanView["PLAN VIEW - FORWARD 0.25L OF SHIP"] direction TB BOW[BOW] --> S1[Stringer Line S1
= Centerline] S1 --> S2[Stringer Line S2
Offset 750 mm] S2 --> S3[Stringer Line S3
Offset 1500 mm] S3 --> S4[Stringer Line S4
Offset 2250 mm] S4 --> S5[Stringer Line S5
Offset 3000 mm] FLR1[Floor 1] -.->|Span 2.0 m| FLR2[Floor 2] FLR2 -.->|Span 2.0 m| FLR3[Floor 3] FLR3 -.->|Span 2.0 m| FLR4[Floor 4] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** In the forward 0.25L region, the floor spacing is typically maintained at standard values (2.0–2.5 m), but **panting stringers are added** between floors to divide the unsupported plate breadth. This reduces the effective plate slenderness and raises the buckling resistance dramatically. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** **Define panting in ship structures. Where does it predominantly occur and why?** **Model Answer:** Panting is a localized dynamic phenomenon in which the shell plating of a ship, particularly in the regions between adjacent floors and frames, undergoes **alternating inward and outward deflection** due to rapidly fluctuating hydrodynamic and hydrostatic pressures arising from wave action, bow emergence, and slamming. It predominantly occurs in the **forward region** (approximately the forward 0.2L to 0.25L of the ship length) and to a lesser extent in the **aft region** (afterbody). This is because: - The forward bottom plating experiences **severe slamming** when the bow alternately submerges and emerges from waves. - The dynamic pressure fluctuations are highest at the bow. - The frequency of these pressure oscillations can approach the natural flexural frequency of the plate panels, inducing resonance. - The hull is subject to cyclic wave-induced bending that superimposes dynamic in-plane compression on the shell. *(Self-explanatory — 3 marks allocated: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for location, 1 mark for reasoning.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** **List any three additional stiffening members used to resist panting in a ship hull.** **Model Answer:** The following three additional stiffening members are used to resist panting: 1. **Panting Stringers** — Longitudinal intercostal stringers fitted between the main transverse frames to subdivide the wide unsupported shell plate panels into smaller, more buckling-resistant sub-panels. 2. **Panting Knees (Brackets)** — Triangular gusset plates fitted at the upper and lower ends of floors (and side girders) to provide end-fixity, prevent the floors from being pulled out of the shell plating, and transfer panting-induced shear and bending into the adjacent primary structure. 3. **Increased Shell Plate Thickness** — In the critical forward and aft panting zones, the shell plate thickness is increased (typically by 1–2 mm above the rule minimum) to enhance local bending and buckling resistance. *(1 mark per item — 3 marks total.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Many students **confuse panting with hull girder bending** — they are completely different phenomena. Panting is **local plate vibration**, while hull girder bending is a **global structural response**. Do NOT mix them in the same answer. > - A common mistake is to forget the **safety factor / dynamic amplification factor (K_dyn ≈ 1.5–2.0)** when computing allowable stresses for panting. Static-allowable values must be reduced for fatigue. > - Do **not** state that panting occurs "all over the ship." It is concentrated in **forward 0.2L–0.25L** and the **aft afterbody**. Marks are deducted for vagueness. > - In numerical problems, **units must be consistent** (N/mm² vs MPa vs kN/m²). Always state conversions explicitly. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Question with Model Solution** **Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain the phenomenon of panting in ship structures. Discuss the various additional stiffening arrangements provided to resist panting, with neat sketches. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A shell plate panel in the forward region of a ship has the following particulars: - Plate dimensions (between floors): a = 2.5 m, b = 2.0 m - Plate thickness: t = 14 mm - Lateral pressure (slamming component): p = 100 kN/m² - Yield stress: f_y = 240 N/mm² - Take safety factor = 1.5 Check whether the plate requires additional panting stringers. If yes, determine the maximum allowable spacing of panting stringers. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Q3(a) — 7 Marks** **1. Phenomenon of Panting (3 marks):** Panting is the cyclic, alternate in-and-out deflection of the shell plating due to rapid variation in hydrodynamic and hydrostatic pressure, especially during the pitching motion of the ship in waves. The forward and afterbody regions are most susceptible because of: - Bow emergence and slamming - Cyclic wave pressure pulses - Resonance with plate natural frequency - Local in-plane compressive stress from overall hull bending **2. Additional Stiffening Arrangements (4 marks):** - **Panting Stringers** — Additional longitudinal stringers fitted between the existing frames/floors to reduce the effective breadth b of the unsupported plate panel, thereby increasing the critical buckling stress. - **Panting Knees / Brackets** — Triangular plate brackets fitted at the upper and lower ends of floors to provide end-fixity and prevent floor pull-out from the shell. - **Gusset Plates at Floor-Girder Crossings** — Reduce stress concentrations. - **Increased Plate Thickness** — In panting zones (forward 0.2L), plate thickness is increased by 1–2 mm. - **Close-Spaced Frames in Bow and Stern Peaks** — Local frame spacing reduced to ~400–500 mm. - **Side Shell Panting Stringers** — On the side shell, intercostal stringers are added parallel to the sheer strake. *(Sketch — refer to Section 4 diagrams for cross-sectional arrangement.)* --- ### **Model Solution for Q3(b) — 7 Marks** **Given:** - a = 2.5 m = 2500 mm, b = 2.0 m = 2000 mm - t = 14 mm - p = 100 kN/m² = 100 × 10⁻³ N/mm² = 0.1 N/mm² - f_y = 240 N/mm² - Factor of Safety (FoS) = 1.5 **Step 1 — Allowable Stress:** $$ \sigma_{allow} = \frac{f_y}{FoS} = \frac{240}{1.5} = 160 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Maximum Panting Stress (No Intermediate Stringers):** $$ \sigma_{p,max} = \frac{p \cdot b^2}{2 t^2} = \frac{0.1 \times (2000)^2}{2 \times (14)^2} $$ $$ \sigma_{p,max} = \frac{0.1 \times 4 \times 10^6}{2 \times 196} = \frac{4 \times 10^5}{392} $$ $$ \sigma_{p,max} \approx 1020.4 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Comparison:** $$ \sigma_{p,max} = 1020.4 \text{ N/mm}^2 \gg \sigma_{allow} = 160 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ The plate is **grossly overstressed**. **Panting stringers are mandatory.** **Step 4 — Required Stringer Spacing:** Setting $\sigma_{p,max} = \sigma_{allow}$ and solving for effective span L: $$ L = t \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}}{p}} $$ $$ L = 14 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 160}{0.1}} $$ $$ L = 14 \times \sqrt{3200} $$ $$ L = 14 \times 56.57 $$ $$ L \approx 792 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 5 — Number of Stringers Required:** $$ \text{Number of sub-spans} = \frac{b}{L} = \frac{2000}{792} \approx 2.53 $$ Round up to **3 sub-spans**, requiring **2 panting stringers** between the two adjacent floors. **Revised spacing** = 2000 / 3 ≈ 667 mm (conservatively safe). **Final Answer:** The plate requires **at least 2 panting stringers** between floors, with a maximum allowable spacing of **~800 mm**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice** **Q4. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the location and structural arrangement of panting stringers and panting knees in a typical ship. Explain how they contribute to panting resistance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A ship has shell plating in the forward region with the following particulars: - Effective plate span between supporting members: b = 1.8 m - Plate thickness: t = 10 mm - Dynamic pressure on the plate: p_dyn = 75 kN/m² - Material: Mild steel with f_y = 250 N/mm² Determine the **buckling stress** of the plate panel as a simply supported plate. Take E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm², ν = 0.3, buckling coefficient k = 4.0. Comment on whether additional stiffening is required. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Q4(a) — 7 Marks** **Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to Section 4.2 cross-sectional arrangement showing: - Shell plate with panting stringers running longitudinally between floors - Panting knees at floor ends - Side shell and bottom shell zones **Explanation (4 marks):** - **Panting Stringers** are fitted as **intercostal members** parallel to the ship's centreline, dividing the wide plate panel into narrow strips. They increase the local buckling resistance of the plate, effectively reducing the breadth parameter b in the critical buckling equation. - **Panting Knees** are bracket plates fitted at the junctions of floors and shell. They provide **end-fixity**, transfer local load concentrations, and prevent **progressive pull-out failure** of the floor-to-shell weld. - Together, these two members handle both **in-plane compression** (stringers) and **lateral load** (knees) acting on the shell plating. --- ### **Model Solution for Q4(b) — 7 Marks** **Given:** - b = 1.8 m = 1800 mm - t = 10 mm - p_dyn = 75 kN/m² - f_y = 250 N/mm² - E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm² - ν = 0.3, k = 4.0 **Step 1 — Slenderness Ratio:** $$ \frac{b}{t} = \frac{1800}{10} = 180 $$ **Step 2 — Elastic Critical Buckling Stress:** $$ \sigma_{cr} = \frac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2 $$ $$ \sigma_{cr} = \frac{4.0 \times \pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5}{12 \times (1 - 0.09)} \times \left(\frac{10}{1800}\right)^2 $$ $$ \sigma_{cr} = \frac{4.0 \times 9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5}{12 \times 0.91} \times (5.556 \times 10^{-3})^2 $$ $$ \sigma_{cr} = \frac{7.8957 \times 10^6}{10.92} \times 3.086 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ \sigma_{cr} = 7.231 \times 10^5 \times 3.086 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ \sigma_{cr} \approx 22.31 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Maximum Panting Stress (Bending):** $$ \sigma_{p,max} = \frac{p \cdot b^2}{2 t^2} = \frac{0.075 \times (1800)^2}{2 \times 100} $$ $$ \sigma_{p,max} = \frac{0.075 \times 3.24 \times 10^6}{200} = \frac{2.43 \times 10^5}{200} = 1215 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ **Step 4 — Comparison and Conclusion:** - Elastic buckling stress: **σ_cr ≈ 22.3 N/mm²** (very low — slender plate) - Applied bending stress: **σ_p,max ≈ 1215 N/mm²** (exceeds yield by ~5×) **Conclusion:** The plate is **highly vulnerable to both elastic buckling and yielding**. **Additional panting stringers are absolutely mandatory.** The effective span must be drastically reduced. Solving $\sigma_{p,max} = f_y/1.5 = 166.7$ N/mm²: $$ L_{max} = t \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \sigma_{allow}}{p}} = 10 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 166.7}{0.075}} = 10 \times \sqrt{4445} \approx 667 \text{ mm} $$ Hence, **at least 2 panting stringers** must be fitted between the supporting members. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Additional Stiffening for Panting** - ☐ **Panting** is a **localized, dynamic, cyclic deflection** of shell plating — NOT global hull bending. - ☐ Most severe in the **forward 0.2L–0.25L** and the **aft afterbody** of the ship. - ☐ Caused by **sloshing/slamming wave pressures, bow emergence, and plate resonance**. - ☐ **Panting Stringers** subdivide the wide shell panel → reduce effective breadth *b* → raise critical buckling stress. - ☐ **Panting Knees** provide end-fixity at floor-shell junctions → prevent pull-out failure. - ☐ Key design formulas: $\sigma_{p,max} = \dfrac{p \cdot b^2}{2t^2}$ and $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$ - ☐ Always apply a **safety factor of 1.5–2.0** for dynamic/cyclic loading in panting checks. - ☐ Empirical rule: Panting stringer spacing $L \le 1.54 \cdot d$ (where *d* is floor spacing, for L < 2d). - ☐ In extreme forward regions, **plate thickness is increased by 1–2 mm** beyond rule minimum. - ☐ **Frame spacing is reduced** in way of forward and aft peaks to ~400–500 mm. - ☐ Always check **both** bending stress AND buckling stress for panting panels. - ☐ Use **classification society rules** (LR, ABS, DNV, BV) for final scantling approval. - ☐ For numerical problems, ensure **unit consistency** (N/mm² ↔ MPa ↔ kN/m² conversions). - ☐ Don't confuse panting (local) with **slamming** (impact) — slamming is impulsive, panting is cyclic.

topics.title: Navigation in Ice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Navigation in Ice

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Navigation in Ice** is defined as the specialized branch of maritime operations and ship-handling that governs the safe, efficient, and structurally sound transit of a vessel through ice-covered waters, taking into account the mechanical interaction between the ship's hull and the ice medium, the prevailing environmental conditions, and the applicable regulatory frameworks such as the **IMO Polar Code (Polar Code – SOLAS & MARPOL amendments effective from 1 January 2017)** and the **IACS Polar Class (PC) notation system**.

In naval architecture terms, navigation in ice is a coupled **ship–ice interaction problem** where the vessel's kinetic energy, hull form, propulsion power, and structural strength are continuously modulated by the ice regime (e.g., first-year ice, multi-year ice, pack ice, fast ice, brash ice, and growler/bergy-bit field).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight – Definition Anchor**
> According to the IMO Polar Code, every vessel operating in Polar waters (Arctic and Antarctic) must possess a **Polar Ship Certificate** and an operational limitation defined by its **Polar Class (PC 1 – PC 7)** or equivalent **Finnish-Swedish Ice Class (IA Super, IA, IB, IC)** for Baltic operations. This is a *board exam favourite* and must be memorized.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are walking across a frozen lake. The lake is not a single uniform slab of ice — it is broken into pieces of varying thickness, with some large floes, some small pancake ice, and slushy brash in between. If you try to walk straight, the ice may tip, crack, or stop you. To cross safely, you must:
- Choose the **thinnest path** (route planning / ice routing).
- Wear **grippy shoes** to avoid slipping (hull ice-strengthening).
- Sometimes **kick the ice aside** if it is too thick (icebreaking).
- **Slow down** where the ice is hazardous (speed reduction in heavy ice).

A ship navigating in ice faces the **same challenges**, but at a vastly magnified scale — the "ice" is a complex, anisotropic, temperature-sensitive visco-elastic material that resists penetration with forces measurable in **megNewtons (MN)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Standard Metrics to Remember (Board-critical):**
> - **Brash ice:** < **2 m** diameter fragments.
> - **Growler:** Ice fragment up to about **2 m** above water and ~**5 m** long (very dangerous – nearly invisible!).
> - **Bergy bit:** ~**2 m to 5 m** above water, **5 m to 15 m** long.
> - **Small iceberg:** ~**5 m to 15 m** above water, **15 m to 60 m** long.
> - **Medium iceberg:** ~**15 m to 45 m** above water, **60 m to 120 m** long.
> - **Large iceberg:** > **45 m** above water, **> 120 m** long.
> - **Very large iceberg:** > **75 m** above water, **> 200 m** long.

## 1.3 Why Navigation in Ice is a Distinct Discipline

Open-water navigation relies on the *hull–water* interface. In ice, this is replaced (or augmented) by the *hull–ice* interface, which introduces:
1. **A new resistance component (ice resistance $R_{ice}$)** that can be 5–20× open-water resistance.
2. **Structural loading cycles** (slamming, flexure, ramming impacts) absent in calm water.
3. **Mandatory compliance regimes** (Polar Code, IACS UR I2).
4. **Special vessel types**: icebreakers, icebreaking cargo ships, polar research vessels, and submarines surfacing through pack ice.

![Ship navigating through pack ice – conceptual schematic](concept_placeholder)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ice Environment — Taxonomy of Ice Types

A ship navigating in ice must first **classify the medium**. The IACS Polar Class system and the WMO (World Meteorological Organization) Egg Code together define the operating envelope.

| Ice Type | Description | Typical Thickness | Effect on Ship |
|---|---|---|---|
| New ice / Frazil | Recently formed, dark, oily appearance | < **10 cm** | Negligible resistance |
| Nilas | Elastic crust (0–10 cm) | **10 cm** | Low resistance, may yield |
| Grey / Grey-white ice | First stage of first-year ice | **10–30 cm** | Moderate resistance |
| First-year ice (FY) | Grows in one season | **30 cm – 1.2 m** | Significant resistance, requires power |
| Old ice / Multi-year ice (MY) | Survived ≥ 2 melt seasons | **> 1.2 m**, often **2–4 m** | Extreme resistance, icebreaker required |
| Fast ice | Attached to shore | Varies | Vessel may follow lead |
| Pack ice | Drifting, not attached | Varies | Subject to dynamic motion |
| Brash ice | Accumulated fragments | < **2 m** | Chokes rudder/propeller |
| Iceberg | Calved glacier ice | Above-water height 5–75 m+ | Catastrophic damage risk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always differentiate **Floe size** vs **Floe thickness**. Floe size governs icebreaker tactics (e.g., large floe = ramming), while thickness governs resistance and ice class.

## 2.2 Ice Resistance — The Central Engineering Quantity

The total resistance of an ice-going vessel is decomposed as:

$$R_{total} = R_{ow} + R_{ice}$$

Where:
- $R_{ow}$ = open-water resistance (residual + frictional + wave), calculated by standard Holtrop-Mennen or ITTC'78 methods.
- $R_{ice}$ = ice resistance, the **dominant term** in ice.

Several empirical and semi-analytical formulas exist; the **most KTU-relevant** are summarized below.

### 2.2.1 Lindström's Formula (1994 – widely cited for IACS validation)

$$R_{ice} = 0.5 \cdot \rho_{ice} \cdot C_{ice} \cdot B^{1.5} \cdot h_{ice}^{0.5} \cdot V^{1.5}$$

Where:
- $\rho_{ice}$ = density of ice ≈ **917 kg/m³** (always recall this standard value).
- $C_{ice}$ = empirical coefficient (≈ 1.6 for open level ice).
- $B$ = ship beam (m).
- $h_{ice}$ = ice thickness (m).
- $V$ = ship speed (m/s).

> [!NOTE]
> The exponent **1.5 on $V$** is the most distinctive feature of Lindström's model. It is non-linear because at higher speeds, more ice fractures per unit time and momentum transfer scales superlinearly.

### 2.2.2 Runeberg's Formula (Historical, still used for preliminary design)

$$R_{ice} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{ice} \cdot g \cdot h_{ice}^{2} \cdot B \cdot \left( \frac{V^{2}}{g \cdot L_{eff}} \right)$$

Where $L_{eff}$ is the effective breaking length and $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**.

### 2.2.3 Lewis Formula (Simple, useful for Part-A derivations)

$$R_{ice} = C_{L} \cdot B \cdot h_{ice} \cdot \sigma_{flex}$$

Where $\sigma_{flex}$ is the flexural strength of ice (~**0.5 – 1.0 MPa** for first-year ice) and $C_{L}$ is a coefficient.

### 2.2.4 Enquist & Tuomaala (1995) – modern IACS-style correlation

$$R_{ice} = C_{1} \cdot \rho_{ice} \cdot B \cdot h_{ice} \cdot V + C_{2} \cdot \rho_{ice} \cdot h_{ice}^{1.5} \cdot B \cdot V^{0.5}$$

The two terms capture **bending failure** and **crushing failure** modes respectively.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit | Typical / Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho_{ice}$ | Density of sea ice | kg/m³ | **917** |
| $\rho_{w}$ | Density of seawater | kg/m³ | **1025** |
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | m/s² | **9.81** |
| $\sigma_{flex}$ | Flexural strength (first-year ice) | MPa | **0.5 – 1.0** |
| $\sigma_{comp}$ | Compressive strength of ice | MPa | **2 – 10** |
| $E_{ice}$ | Young's modulus of ice | GPa | **5 – 9** |
| $h_{ice}$ | Ice thickness | m | Depends on class |
| $V$ | Ship speed in ice | m/s (or knots) | 1 knot ≈ 0.514 m/s |
| $B$ | Ship beam | m | Vessel-dependent |
| $L_{eff}$ | Effective breaking length | m | ≈ 0.7 Lpp |
| $C_{ice}, C_{L}$ | Empirical coefficients | – | Specific to model |
| $R_{ice}$ | Ice resistance | N or kN | – |
| $P_{B,ice}$ | Brake power in ice | kW or MW | – |
| PC | IACS Polar Class | – | PC 1 → PC 7 |
| FSIC | Finnish-Swedish Ice Class | – | IA Super, IA, IB, IC |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown LaTeX Rule Recap:** Avoid the vertical pipe `|` *inside* table cells to prevent table breaking. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. This is enforced throughout these notes.

## 2.4 Ice Class Systems — Regulatory Backbone

### 2.4.1 IACS Polar Class (PC 1 – PC 7) – IMO Polar Code
The IACS Unified Requirements **UR I2** define seven classes, with **PC 1** being the most capable (year-round, all polar waters) and **PC 7** the least (summer/autumn, medium first-year ice).

- **PC 1:** Year-round, all polar waters (e.g., Arktika-class nuclear icebreakers).
- **PC 2:** Year-round, moderate multi-year ice.
- **PC 3:** Year-round, second-year ice with multi-year inclusions.
- **PC 4–5:** Year-round, thick first-year ice.
- **PC 6:** Summer/autumn, medium first-year ice.
- **PC 7:** Summer/autumn, thin first-year ice.

### 2.4.2 Finnish-Swedish Ice Class (FSIC)
Used primarily in the **Baltic Sea**:
- **IA Super** (≈ 1.0 m ice, 5 kn)
- **IA** (≈ 0.8 m, 5 kn)
- **IB** (≈ 0.6 m, 5 kn)
- **IC** (≈ 0.4 m, 5 kn)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The conversion is approximately: **PC 3 ≈ IA Super**, **PC 4 ≈ IA**, **PC 5 ≈ IB**, **PC 6 ≈ IC**, but the equivalence is *not* exact and is jurisdiction-dependent.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Icebreaker design**: Determines propulsive power (typical nuclear icebreakers: **~75 MW**).
- **Hull form optimization**: Icebreakers use a **high stem angle (20°–30°)**, **raked bow**, and a **low L/B ratio (~5.5–6.5)** to ride up onto ice and break it by **flexural failure (bending)** rather than crushing.
- **Route planning (Ice Routing)**: Uses satellite SAR imagery (e.g., Sentinel-1, RADARSAT) to identify leads and polynyas.
- **Polar Code compliance**: Mandatory for any vessel > 500 GT (or carrying > 12 passengers) operating in polar waters since **1 Jan 2017**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy-Balance Approach to Ice Resistance (Runeberg-type)

### 3.1.1 Starting Principle
A ship of mass $m$ moving at speed $V$ through level ice of thickness $h_{ice}$ fractures the ice continuously. The energy required per unit time to do this equals the rate of work done against ice resistance, $R_{ice} \cdot V$.

**Step 1:** Assume the broken ice wedge has a characteristic length $L_{eff}$ and beam $B$. The volume fractured per unit time is:

$$\dot{V}_{ice} = B \cdot h_{ice} \cdot V$$

**Step 2:** The fracture energy per unit volume of sea ice is empirically:

$$E_{frac} = \sigma_{flex} \cdot \epsilon_{crit}$$

Where $\sigma_{flex}$ is the flexural strength and $\epsilon_{crit}$ is the critical bending strain (a dimensionless quantity). For sea ice, $\sigma_{flex} \approx 0.7$ MPa and $\epsilon_{crit} \approx 10^{-3}$, giving $E_{frac} \approx 700$ J/m³.

**Step 3:** Total power dissipated in fracture:

$$P_{frac} = \dot{V}_{ice} \cdot E_{frac} = B \cdot h_{ice} \cdot V \cdot \sigma_{flex} \cdot \epsilon_{crit}$$

**Step 4:** Equate to mechanical power:

$$R_{ice} \cdot V = B \cdot h_{ice} \cdot V \cdot \sigma_{flex} \cdot \epsilon_{crit}$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $V$ (assuming $V > 0$):

$$R_{ice} = B \cdot h_{ice} \cdot \sigma_{flex} \cdot \epsilon_{crit}$$

This is the **Lewis-type form**. To capture the $V$-dependence (Lindström), one must include momentum transfer during fracture, giving the 1.5-power law.

### 3.1.2 Refined Derivation: Including Hydrodynamic Lift (Lindström)
The ship rides up onto the ice. The hydrodynamic lift force on the inclined hull is:

$$F_{lift} = C_{L,h} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho_{w} \cdot V^{2} \cdot A_{wet}$$

This lift reduces the *effective weight pressing the ice down*, which reduces the flexural force needed. The balance yields Lindström's $V^{1.5}$ law, with the $V^{0.5}$ component arising from the square-root dependence of fracture mechanics on strain rate.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style, 7-mark standard):**
> A polar research vessel of **beam $B = 22$ m** operates in level first-year ice of **thickness $h = 0.8$ m** at a steady speed of **$V = 3$ m/s (~ 5.83 knots)**. Use **Lindström's formula** with $C_{ice} = 1.6$ and $\rho_{ice} = 917$ kg/m³ to find the ice resistance. State the answer in **kN**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify given values.

$$B = 22 \text{ m}, \quad h = 0.8 \text{ m}, \quad V = 3 \text{ m/s}, \quad C_{ice} = 1.6, \quad \rho_{ice} = 917 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into Lindström's equation.

$$R_{ice} = 0.5 \cdot \rho_{ice} \cdot C_{ice} \cdot B^{1.5} \cdot h^{0.5} \cdot V^{1.5}$$

**Step 3:** Compute each term.

$$B^{1.5} = 22^{1.5} = 22 \cdot \sqrt{22} = 22 \cdot 4.690 = 103.18$$

$$h^{0.5} = \sqrt{0.8} = 0.8944$$

$$V^{1.5} = 3^{1.5} = 3 \cdot \sqrt{3} = 3 \cdot 1.7321 = 5.1962$$

**Step 4:** Multiply all factors.

$$R_{ice} = 0.5 \times 917 \times 1.6 \times 103.18 \times 0.8944 \times 5.1962$$

**Step 5:** Multiply progressively.

$$0.5 \times 917 = 458.5$$

$$458.5 \times 1.6 = 733.6$$

$$733.6 \times 103.18 = 75{,}705.8$$

$$75{,}705.8 \times 0.8944 = 67{,}711.3$$

$$67{,}711.3 \times 5.1962 \approx 351{,}862 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to kN.

$$R_{ice} \approx 351.86 \text{ kN}$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{R_{ice} \approx 351.9 \text{ kN}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** Open-water resistance for a 22 m beam vessel at 6 knots is typically ~50–100 kN. The ice resistance (352 kN) is **3–7× higher**, confirming the dominance of $R_{ice}$ in the total resistance budget. This is exactly why **Polar Class vessels are heavily powered** — a typical PC 3 vessel may have 15–30 MW installed power.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Failure Modes vs Icebreaker Tactics

| Failure Mode | Mechanism | Icebreaker Action | Governing Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flexural (bending) failure** | Ship rides up, ice plate bends and breaks under own weight | Continuous forward motion at low-to-moderate speed | $\sigma_{flex}$ (flexural strength) |
| **Crushing failure** | Bow compresses ice directly | High power, blunt bow, or low temperatures | $\sigma_{comp}$ (compressive strength) |
| **Splitting (shear) failure** | Ice fractures along the contact line | Ramming in reverse to clear channel | $\tau_{shear}$ |
| **Buckling failure** | Thick ice plate buckles elastically | Bow angle designed to avoid edge loading | $E_{ice}$ (elastic modulus) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The single most important design philosophy is *"break ice by bending, not by crushing"*. Bending requires 5–10× less energy than crushing, which is why icebreaker bows are **sloped (20–30°) and raked**, not vertical and blunt.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture of Ice Navigation System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ice Environment Detection] --> B[Radar and Satellite Imagery]
    A --> C[Visual Observation and Bridge Team]

    D --> E
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define Navigation in Ice and state any two mandatory regulatory requirements under the IMO Polar Code.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Navigation in Ice is the specialized maritime operation involving the safe transit of vessels through ice-covered waters, considering hull–ice interaction, environmental conditions, and structural integrity. **(1 mark)**

Mandatory IMO Polar Code requirements: **(2 marks)**
1. **Polar Ship Certificate** for any vessel > 500 GT or carrying > 12 passengers operating in polar waters (mandatory since 1 January 2017).
2. **Polar Class (PC) designation** (PC 1 to PC 7) defining the ice type, season, and temperature limits within which the vessel may operate.
3. (any other valid point) **Polar Water Operational Manual (PWOM)** and **Ship-specific Polar Safety Assessment**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
**Distinguish between Growler, Bergy bit, and Small iceberg with reference to their typical dimensions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Growler | Bergy bit | Small iceberg |
|---|---|---|---|
| Above-water height | Up to ~**2 m** | **2 m to 5 m** | **5 m to 15 m** |
| Length | Up to ~**5 m** | **5 m to 15 m** | **15 m to 60 m** |

A growler is the **most dangerous** to navigation due to its low freeboard (often invisible to radar) and is essentially a fragment of a calved iceberg. **(1 mark for danger note)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Format

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**A bulk carrier of beam 25 m is to be assessed for transit through level first-year ice of thickness 1.0 m at a service speed of 4 m/s. Use Lindström's formula with $C_{ice} = 1.6$ and $\rho_{ice} = 917$ kg/m³.**

**(a)** Calculate the ice resistance in kN. **(5 marks)**
**(b)** If the open-water resistance at the same speed is 120 kN, compute the total resistance, the effective power at the propeller shaft (assume propulsive efficiency $\eta_D = 0.65$), and discuss the implications for selecting the polar class. **(9 marks)** *(split: 3 + 3 + 3 for power, efficiency, class selection)*

---

#### **Model Solution – Question A**

##### **Part (a): Ice Resistance Calculation**

**Given:** $B = 25$ m, $h = 1.0$ m, $V = 4$ m/s, $C_{ice} = 1.6$, $\rho_{ice} = 917$ kg/m³.

Lindström's formula:

$$R_{ice} = 0.5 \cdot \rho_{ice} \cdot C_{ice} \cdot B^{1.5} \cdot h^{0.5} \cdot V^{1.5}$$

Step 1: Compute powers.

$$B^{1.5} = 25^{1.5} = 25 \cdot \sqrt{25} = 25 \cdot 5 = 125$$

$$h^{0.5} = \sqrt{1.0} = 1.0$$

$$V^{1.5} = 4^{1.5} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{4} = 4 \cdot 2 = 8$$

Step 2: Multiply.

$$R_{ice} = 0.5 \times 917 \times 1.6 \times 125 \times 1.0 \times 8$$

$$= 0.5 \times 917 = 458.5$$

$$458.5 \times 1.6 = 733.6$$

$$733.6 \times 125 = 91{,}700$$

$$91{,}700 \times 8 = 733{,}600 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{R_{ice} \approx 733.6 \text{ kN}}$$

**(Full 5 marks for correct substitution, stepwise computation, and unit conversion.)**

---

##### **Part (b): Total Resistance, Effective Power, and Class Selection**

**Step 1: Total Resistance.**

$$R_{total} = R_{ow} + R_{ice} = 120 + 733.6 = 853.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Effective (Tow-rope) Power.**

$$P_E = R_{total} \cdot V = 853.6 \text{ kN} \times 4 \text{ m/s} = 3414.4 \text{ kN·m/s} = 3414.4 \text{ kW} \approx 3.41 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3: Brake Power (delivered power at engine output via $\eta_D$).**

$$P_B = \frac{P_E}{\eta_D} = \frac{3414.4}{0.65} \approx 5252.9 \text{ kW} \approx 5.25 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 4: Polar Class Selection.**
- The vessel operates in **1.0 m first-year ice at 4 m/s (~7.8 knots)**.
- A 1.0 m ice thickness is **at the upper limit of PC 6** but within the capability of **PC 5** (which handles thick first-year ice year-round).
- For continuous year-round operation, **PC 5** is recommended; for summer-only operation in 1.0 m FY ice, **PC 6** may suffice.
- The high $R_{ice}/R_{ow}$ ratio (≈ 6.1) confirms the operation is **ice-dominated** and the vessel must be ice-strengthened (shell plating, frames, possibly double-acting or icebreaker-class bow).

**(Marks: 3 for resistance summation + power calc, 3 for power via efficiency, 3 for class selection with justification.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the IACS Polar Class (PC) system with the seven classes and the corresponding ice capabilities. Mention the year-round vs seasonal distinctions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Lindström, Runeberg, and Lewis** formulas for ice resistance, highlighting the key assumptions, the speed dependence, and the field of applicability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution – Question B**

##### **Part (a): IACS Polar Class System**

The IACS Polar Class system (UR I2) is the internationally accepted framework for vessel operation in polar waters. It defines seven classes based on the **thickest ice the vessel can transit at a minimum continuous speed of 3 knots**, considering ice type and season.

| Class | Capability |
|---|---|
| **PC 1** | Year-round, all polar waters, all multi-year ice |
| **PC 2** | Year-round, moderate multi-year ice |
| **PC 3** | Year-round, second-year ice with multi-year inclusions |
| **PC 4** | Year-round, thick first-year ice with multi-year inclusions |
| **PC 5** | Year-round, medium first-year ice with multi-year inclusions |
| **PC 6** | Summer/autumn, medium first-year ice |
| **PC 7** | Summer/autumn, thin first-year ice |

**Key features:**
- Classes PC 1–PC 5 are **year-round** (any season).
- Classes PC 6–PC 7 are **seasonal** (summer/autumn only).
- A higher class number implies *lower* capability (PC 1 = most capable).
- The minimum design speed in ice is **3 knots** in the worst intended ice condition.
- The IMO Polar Code (effective 1 Jan 2017) makes this classification legally binding for applicable vessels.

**(7 marks: 4 for class list with details + 3 for distinguishing year-round vs seasonal.)**

---

##### **Part (b): Comparison of Ice Resistance Formulas**

| Aspect | Lindström (1994) | Runeberg (1889) | Lewis (Enquist variant) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Semi-empirical, energy-momentum | Classical, energy-based | Empirical, strength-based |
| **Speed dependence** | $V^{1.5}$ | $V^{2}$ | $V$ (linear, weak) |
| **Key variables** | $\rho_{ice}, B^{1.5}, h^{0.5}, V^{1.5}$ | $\rho_{ice}, g, h^{ice}^2, B, V^2, L_{eff}$ | $B, h, \sigma_{flex}$ |
| **Assumptions** | Level ice, continuous breaking, empirical coefficient | Rigid-plastic ice failure | Pure flexural failure |
| **Strength parameter** | Implicit in $C_{ice}$ | Implicit in energy balance | Explicit ($\sigma_{flex}$) |
| **Applicability** | Modern PC vessels, IACS validation | Preliminary design, historical | First-cut estimates, low-speed regime |
| **Limitation** | Requires accurate $C_{ice}$ from model tests | Overestimates at low speed | Ignores hydrodynamic effects |

**Synthesis:**
- Lindström is the **most widely used** today due to its $V^{1.5}$ scaling validated against full-scale trials.
- Runeberg's $V^2$ scaling is **too aggressive** at low speeds and is rarely used alone in modern design.
- Lewis-type formulas are **useful for quick Part-A answers** and explain the physics clearly.
- For research/regulatory submission, the **IACS UR I2** uses a more sophisticated level-ice bending model with crushing corrections.

**(7 marks: 3 for table-style comparison + 4 for synthesis/applicability discussion.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points (Board-Examiner Perspective):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $V$ units**: Lindström's formula requires **m/s**. A common error is using knots directly, leading to answers off by **~30×**. Always convert: **1 knot = 0.5144 m/s**.
> 2. **Using $\rho_{ice} = 1000$ kg/m³** (water density) instead of **917 kg/m³** (ice density). Memorize: **sea ice is ~9% less dense than seawater** — that is why ice floats.
> 3. **Confusing Ice Class numbering**: PC 1 is the *strongest* and PC 7 is the *weakest*. This is the opposite intuition to "1 = beginner".
> 4. **Missing the open-water resistance component** when asked for *total* resistance. $R_{total} = R_{ow} + R_{ice}$.
> 5. **Forgetting propulsive efficiency** in power calculations. Effective power ≠ delivered power.
> 6. **Confusing growler vs bergy bit dimensions**: a single mark is lost in Part A for wrong numbers.
> 7. **Not stating units in final answer** — always box your numerical answer with units (e.g., $\boxed{351.9 \text{ kN}}$).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **IMO Polar Code** effective **1 Jan 2017**; mandatory for vessels > 500 GT or > 12 pax in polar waters.
- [x] **IACS Polar Class (PC 1 – PC 7)** — PC 1 strongest, PC 7 weakest; **3-knot** minimum design speed in worst intended ice.
- [x] **Finnish-Swedish Ice Class** (IA Super, IA, IB, IC) — used in the **Baltic Sea**.
- [x] **Density of sea ice** $\rho_{ice} = 917$ kg/m³ (memorize this!).
- [x] **Flexural strength of first-year ice** $\sigma_{flex} \approx 0.5 - 1.0$ MPa.
- [x] **Lindström's formula** is the most cited for IACS validation; speed exponent is **1.5**.
- [x] **Icebreaker hull features**: sloped bow (20°–30°), raked stem, low L/B ratio (5.5–6.5), high installed power.
- [x] **Bending failure** is preferred over crushing (5–10× less energy).
- [x] **Ice taxonomy**: new ice < nilas < grey ice < grey-white < first-year < multi-year.
- [x] **Iceberg classification** (WMO): growler < bergy bit < small < medium < large < very large.
- [x] **Heeling system** in icebreakers: rocks the ship sideways to break ice along the hull — used in ridged ice.
- [x] **Continuous breaking** > ramming (preferred modern method) for energy efficiency.
- [x] **Ice routing** uses SAR satellites (Sentinel-1, RADARSAT-2) to find leads and polynyas.
- [x] **Polar Code Parts**: I-A (safety measures), I-B (additional), II-A (pollution prevention), II-B (additional).
- [x] **Ice resistance** typically 5–20× open-water resistance in heavy ice.
- [x] **Typical icebreaker power**: conventional ~25–50 MW, nuclear ~75 MW (e.g., Arktika, Sibir).
- [x] **Always convert knots to m/s** before applying Lindström (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s).
- [x] **Polar Ship Certificate** is issued by a Classification Society (e.g., DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, BV).

---

**End of Notes — Module 2: Navigation in Ice**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and OBE-aligned Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1 – L4).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bilge Keel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bilge Keel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Bilge Keel** is a longitudinal, fin-like structural plate attached externally to the shell plating of a ship at the **bilge region** (the curved transition zone between the bottom hull and the side shell). It runs parallel to the ship's centerline, typically extending over **30%–50% of the length between perpendiculars (Lpp)** in the amidships region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *"A bilge keel is a long, narrow projecting fin fitted to the shell plating at the bilge, on each side of the ship, to provide passive hydrodynamic roll damping and resist transverse rolling motions caused by beam seas."*

The primary classification parameters of a bilge keel are:
- **Length (L_bk):** 30% to 50% of Lpp
- **Width (b_bk):** 1.5% to 3% of the ship's beam (B)
- **Thickness (t_bk):** 8 mm to 25 mm depending on ship size
- **Location:** At or near the maximum girth of the bilge (≈ 45° turn of bilge)
- **Number:** Always fitted as a **pair**, one on each side of the hull

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kayak Stabilizer" Effect

Imagine paddling a **narrow kayak** in choppy water. The kayak is highly prone to rolling side-to-side. Now fit two small longitudinal fins along the lower curved edge of the kayak — what happens?

As the kayak begins to roll, these fins slice through the water at an angle, creating **turbulence and drag** on whichever side is descending. This turbulence bleeds energy out of the rolling motion, gradually **damping the oscillations**. The kayak becomes noticeably steadier without you doing anything actively — no shifting your weight, no paddling.

A ship's bilge keels perform **exactly the same function** on a massive scale. They are the kayak's stabilizer fins, scaled up and welded to the curved bilge of a steel hull, passively bleeding off roll energy in beam seas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why at the bilge?** The bilge is the point on a rolling ship that travels the **fastest** (maximum distance from the roll axis) and presents a **broad leading face** to the water as the ship rolls. Maximum hydrodynamic damping is therefore generated here.

## 1.3 Key Terminology for the Board Examiner

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know Board Terminology:**
> - **Bilge:** The convex curved portion of the hull where the bottom and side shell meet.
> - **Doubler Plate:** A flat insert plate welded between the bilge keel and the shell to spread local loads and avoid stress concentration.
> - **Fish-tailing:** Tapering the ends of the bilge keel (gradually reducing width) to reduce slamming impact and fatigue failure.
> - **Passive Stabilizer:** A device that damps roll without any external energy input (bilge keel = passive; fin stabilizer = active).
> - **Added Resistance (R_add):** The extra hull resistance caused by the bilge keels in still water.
> - **Effective Damping Coefficient (B_eff):** The total roll damping moment coefficient of the ship, which the bilge keel augments.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Bilge Keel Damping — "Why It Works"

When a ship rolls in still water or in beam seas, every point on the hull below the waterline traces an arc about the **roll axis** (located near the waterline at midships). The horizontal velocity of any hull point is:

$$v_{point} = r \cdot \dot{\phi}$$

where:
- $r$ = radial distance from roll axis (m)
- $\dot{\phi}$ = roll angular velocity (rad/s)

The **bilge keel**, being located at the maximum girth on each side, sits at a large $r$ from the roll axis. As the ship rolls, the bilge keel acts as a **hydrodynamic drag body** moving transverse to the flow.

The damping process occurs in four key stages:

1. **Flow Separation:** As the bilge keel sweeps sideways through the water, the flow cannot follow the sharp leading edge of the fin. **Vortex sheets** are shed from the trailing edge.
2. **Vortex Shedding (Kármán-type):** Alternating low-pressure vortices form on either side of the bilge keel, creating oscillating pressure differences.
3. **Energy Dissipation:** The kinetic energy of the ship's rolling motion is converted into the **turbulent kinetic energy** of these vortices. This energy is eventually dissipated as heat in the surrounding water.
4. **Damping Moment Generation:** The transverse hydrodynamic force on the bilge keel, acting at its moment arm, generates a moment **opposing the roll velocity** — the damping moment $M_{bk}$.

## 2.2 Critical Design "Why" Questions Answered

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are bilge keels fitted as a pair (port + starboard)?**
> Because a ship can roll in both directions. A single bilge keel would only damp rolling *toward* its side. Symmetrical fitting damps motion in both senses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are bilge keels tapered at the ends (fish-tailed)?**
> The bilge keel terminates near the **forward and aft shoulders** of the parallel middle body. The shell curvature changes rapidly here, and full-width ends would experience **slamming loads** in head/following seas and **high cyclic stress** during roll-pitch coupling. Tapering reduces both the dynamic pressure and the stress concentration factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the bilge keel attached via a doubler plate?**
> The bilge keel itself is a slender bar. Welding it directly to the curved shell would create **concentrated heat input** and **notch stress** along a curved surface. A flat doubler plate distributes the weld line over a larger area, simplifying fabrication and reducing fatigue.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Typical Units** | **Governing Relation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $F_{bk}$ | Hydrodynamic force on bilge keel | N | $F_{bk} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_{D,bk}\, A_{bk}\, v_{rel}^{2}$ |
| $v_{rel}$ | Relative transverse velocity of bilge keel | m/s | $v_{rel} = b_{bk}\,\dot{\phi}$ |
| $M_{d,bk}$ | Damping moment from bilge keel (per side) | N·m | $M_{d,bk} = F_{bk} \cdot b_{bk} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_{D,bk}\, A_{bk}\, b_{bk}^{3}\,\dot{\phi}^{2}$ |
| $B_{bk}$ | Linearized bilge keel damping coefficient | N·m·s/rad | $B_{bk} \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_{D,bk}\, A_{bk}\, b_{bk}^{2}\,\phi_{0}\,\omega_{n}$ |
| $R_{add}$ | Added resistance due to bilge keels | N | $R_{add} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_{D,bk}\, A_{bk}\, V^{2}$ |
| $L_{bk}$ | Length of bilge keel | m | $L_{bk} = (0.30 \text{ to } 0.50)\,L_{pp}$ |
| $b_{bk}$ | Width (span) of bilge keel | m | $b_{bk} = (0.015 \text{ to } 0.030)\,B$ |
| $A_{bk}$ | Planform area of one bilge keel | m² | $A_{bk} = L_{bk} \times b_{bk}$ |
| $\zeta_{add}$ | Increase in damping ratio due to bilge keel | — | $\zeta_{add} \approx \dfrac{B_{bk}}{2\, I_{xx}\,\omega_{n}}$ |
| $C_{D,bk}$ | Hydrodynamic drag coefficient of bilge keel | — | $C_{D,bk} \approx 1.2 \text{ to } 2.0$ (typical) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Board Memory Hook:**
> The damping moment varies as the **CUBE of the bilge keel arm** $b_{bk}^3$. Doubling the moment arm gives **8× the damping moment** — this is why bilge keels are placed at the bilge (largest possible $b_{bk}$ within the girth constraint).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In commercial ship design, the **decision tree** for roll stabilization typically reads:

- **Cargo ships, tankers, bulk carriers, general cargo:** Bilge keel is the **default** stabilizer — no moving parts, no maintenance, no power consumption.
- **Naval combatants & cruise ships:** Often combined with **active fin stabilizers** for higher effectiveness at speed.
- **Small craft & yachts:** Bilge keels are **NOT preferred** (drag penalty dominates); they are replaced by anti-roll tanks or weighted keels.

The bilge keel represents the **best passive engineering compromise** between effectiveness, cost, reliability, and resistance penalty — exactly the kind of design tradeoff a Naval Architect must judge.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Roll Damping Moment from a Single Bilge Keel

**Objective:** Derive the expression for the hydrodynamic damping moment generated by one bilge keel during ship roll.

**Step 1 — Coordinate System & Assumptions**

Assume the ship is rolling purely in still water with no forward speed effect (free roll). Take the origin at the roll axis (longitudinal axis at midships waterline). Let:
- $\dot{\phi}$ = instantaneous roll angular velocity (rad/s)
- $b_{bk}$ = perpendicular distance of the bilge keel from the roll axis (m)
- $A_{bk}$ = planform (side) area of the bilge keel = $L_{bk} \times b_{bk}$ (m²)
- $C_{D,bk}$ = drag coefficient of the bilge keel (dimensionless, taken as constant for moderate Reynolds numbers)
- $\rho$ = mass density of seawater ≈ 1025 kg/m³

**Step 2 — Velocity of a Point on the Bilge Keel**

For a rigid-body rotation about the roll axis, every point on the bilge keel has the same transverse velocity (in magnitude):

$$v_{rel} = b_{bk} \cdot \dot{\phi}$$

*Logic:* Rigid-body kinematics: linear velocity = radius × angular velocity.

**Step 3 — Hydrodynamic Drag Force on the Bilge Keel**

The water exerts a transverse drag force on the bilge keel opposing its relative motion. Using the standard quadratic drag law:

$$F_{bk} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_{D,bk} \, A_{bk} \, v_{rel}^{2}$$

*Logic:* Drag = ½ × density × drag-coefficient × area × velocity². This is the same expression used for any bluff body in cross-flow.

**Step 4 — Substitute the Relative Velocity**

Substituting $v_{rel} = b_{bk} \cdot \dot{\phi}$ into the drag expression:

$$F_{bk} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_{D,bk} \, A_{bk} \, (b_{bk} \, \dot{\phi})^{2}$$

$$F_{bk} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_{D,bk} \, A_{bk} \, b_{bk}^{2} \, \dot{\phi}^{2}$$

*Logic:* Squaring the product $(b_{bk}\,\dot{\phi})$ distributes as $b_{bk}^{2} \cdot \dot{\phi}^{2}$.

**Step 5 — Convert Force to Damping Moment**

The moment about the roll axis is the force times its moment arm:

$$M_{d,bk} = F_{bk} \cdot b_{bk}$$

$$M_{d,bk} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_{D,bk} \, A_{bk} \, b_{bk}^{2} \, \dot{\phi}^{2} \cdot b_{bk}$$

$$\boxed{\,M_{d,bk} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_{D,bk} \, A_{bk} \, b_{bk}^{3} \, \dot{\phi}^{2}\,}$$

*Logic:* Each unit of force acts at a moment arm $b_{bk}$ to produce torque. The cubic dependence on $b_{bk}$ is the dominant geometric feature of bilge keel design.

**Step 6 — Total Damping Moment (Both Sides)**

A ship has **two** bilge keels (port + starboard). When the ship rolls to starboard, the starboard bilge keel descends (positive damping) and the port bilge keel rises (it also experiences drag, but the geometry makes the net effect of both keels sum up). For a symmetric ship in pure roll:

$$M_{d,total} = 2 \times M_{d,bk}$$

$$\boxed{\,M_{d,total} = \rho \, C_{D,bk} \, A_{bk} \, b_{bk}^{3} \, \dot{\phi}^{2}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Linearization for Equation of Motion**

The expression above is **quadratic** in $\dot{\phi}$, making the equation of motion **non-linear**. For analytical work, we linearize using a representative roll amplitude $\phi_0$ and natural frequency $\omega_n$:

$$B_{bk} \approx \frac{M_{d,total}}{\dot{\phi}} \bigg|_{\dot{\phi} = \phi_0 \omega_n} = \rho \, C_{D,bk} \, A_{bk} \, b_{bk}^{2} \, \phi_0 \, \omega_n$$

The single-degree-of-freedom roll equation then becomes:

$$I_{xx} \, \ddot{\phi} + B_{eff} \, \dot{\phi} + \Delta \cdot GM \cdot \phi = M_{wave}(t)$$

where $B_{eff} = B_{wave} + B_{viscous} + B_{lift} + B_{bk}$ — the bilge keel **augments** the total damping.

## 3.2 Derivation: Added Resistance Due to Bilge Keels

**Step 1 — At forward speed $V$, the bilge keel sees an inflow with both axial and transverse components.**

For small roll angles, the cross-flow velocity is $v_{rel} = b_{bk}\,\dot{\phi}$ and the axial inflow is $V$.

**Step 2 — Resultant velocity magnitude:**

$$v_{res} = \sqrt{V^{2} + (b_{bk}\,\dot{\phi})^{2}} \approx V \quad \text{(for small \dot{\phi})}$$

**Step 3 — The time-mean (averaged over a roll cycle) added resistance is:**

$$R_{add} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_{D,bk} \, A_{bk} \, V^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,R_{add} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_{D,bk} \, A_{bk} \, V^{2}\,}$$

*Logic:* Averaging the quadratic drag over a roll cycle removes the oscillatory $\dot{\phi}$ component, leaving the steady-state drag penalty. This is the **fuel cost** of having a bilge keel.

## 3.3 Numerical Sanity Check (Worked Example)

A bulk carrier with $L_{pp} = 150$ m, $B = 25$ m, fitted with bilge keels of length $L_{bk} = 0.35 \times 150 = 52.5$ m and width $b_{bk} = 0.02 \times 25 = 0.5$ m. Take $C_{D,bk} = 1.4$, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, ship speed $V = 14$ knots $= 7.2$ m/s.

**Planform area (one keel):**
$$A_{bk} = 52.5 \times 0.5 = 26.25 \text{ m}^2$$

**Added resistance (per side):**
$$R_{add} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.4 \times 26.25 \times (7.2)^{2}$$
$$R_{add} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.4 \times 26.25 \times 51.84$$
$$R_{add} \approx 9.77 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} \approx 977 \text{ kN per side}$$

**Total added resistance (both sides):**
$$R_{add,total} \approx 1.95 \text{ MN} \approx 200 \text{ tonnes-force}$$

This illustrates the **real engineering cost** of bilge keels — about 2% of the ship's calm-water resistance is a typical penalty, which the Naval Architect must justify against the roll-damping benefit.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Bilge Keel Subsystem

The following block diagram maps the **structural, hydrodynamic, and fabrication subsystems** that constitute a bilge keel installation, including all load paths and energy-dissipation mechanisms.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Shell Plating] --> B[Bilge Region - 45 degree Turn]
    B --> C[Doubler Plate - Flat Insert]
    C --> D[Welded Interface - Double Continuous Fillet]
    D --> E[Bilge Keel Bar - Main Fin]
    E --> F[Tapered End - Fish-tail Port Side]
    E --> G[Tapered End - Fish-tail Starboard Side]
    E --> H[Internal Brackets / Tripping Brackets]
    I[Roll Motion Input] --> J[Hydrodynamic Cross-Flow]
    J --> K[Vortex Shedding Zone]
    K --> L[Energy Dissipation - Turbulent Wake]
    L --> M[Roll Damping Moment Output]
    M --> N[Ship Roll Decay]
    E --> O[Added Drag Penalty]
    O --> P[Powering Penalty to Propulsive Plant]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Cross-Section Showing Bilge Keel Position

The diagram below presents a **text-anchored schematic** of the midships cross-section, indicating the geometric placement of the bilge keel at the maximum girth.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Centerline - Keel] --> B[Bottom Shell Plate]
    B --> C[Bilge Turn - Curved Region]
    C --> D[Side Shell Plate]
    D --> E[Deck / Sheer Line]
    C -.->|Attachment Point| F[Bilge Keel - Projecting Outward]
    F -.->|Width b_bk| G[Maximum Beam at Waterline]
    H[Roll Axis - Near WL] -->|Arm b_bk| F
    style A fill:#fff,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#ff9,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#9cf,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Load Path Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydrodynamic Pressure on Bilge Keel] --> B[In-plane Shear in Doubler]
    B --> C[Shell Plating Membrane Stress]
    C --> D[Longitudinal Strength Member - Bilge Longitudinal]
    D --> E[Hull Girder Bending Stress]
    F[Wave Slamming at Tapered End] --> G[Hot-Spot Fatigue at Weld Toe]
    G --> H[Inspection Requirement - NDT Every 5 Years]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Schematic Interpretation:** The above block diagrams map the functional and load-path relationships of the bilge keel. For detailed manufacturing drawings (sectional profiles, weld details, taper geometry), refer to classification society rule sheets (e.g., IACS CSR for bulk carriers, Lloyd's Register Rules).

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**"Define bilge keel. State any two advantages and one disadvantage of fitting bilge keels to a ship." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **bilge keel** is a longitudinal, fin-like plate fitted externally to the shell plating of a ship at the bilge, running parallel to the ship's centerline, primarily to provide passive hydrodynamic damping of roll motion.

**Advantages:**
1. **No moving parts and no power consumption** — purely passive device, highly reliable.
2. **Cheap to fabricate and maintain** — uses simple welded plate construction.

**Disadvantage:**
1. **Adds resistance to the hull** in forward motion, increasing propulsive power requirement and fuel consumption (typically 1–3% penalty).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**"Why are bilge keels located at the bilge of the ship rather than at the bottom or the side shell? Justify with a suitable formula. (3 Marks)"**

**Model Answer:**

The bilge keel is located at the **bilge** because the bilge is the point of **maximum distance ($b_{bk}$) from the roll axis** on the underwater hull, while also presenting a near-horizontal leading face to the cross-flow during roll.

The damping moment generated by the bilge keel is given by:

$$M_{d,bk} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_{D,bk} \, A_{bk} \, b_{bk}^{3} \, \dot{\phi}^{2}$$

Since $M_{d,bk} \propto b_{bk}^{3}$, locating the bilge keel at the maximum possible $b_{bk}$ (i.e., at the bilge) **maximizes the damping moment** for a given bilge keel area. The bilge also coincides with the maximum girth of the hull, allowing a large fin to be projected outward without violating beam-restriction rules.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**a) Explain with a neat sketch the constructional details of a bilge keel. Describe the function of the doubler plate and the need for fish-tailing the ends. (7 Marks)**

**b) Derive an expression for the hydrodynamic damping moment generated by a pair of bilge keels during roll motion. State the assumptions clearly. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Constructional Details of a Bilge Keel:**

A bilge keel consists of the following components:

1. **Main Fin Plate:** A flat steel bar/plate of rectangular cross-section, typically of length $L_{bk} = (0.3 \text{ to } 0.5)L_{pp}$, width $b_{bk} = (0.015 \text{ to } 0.030)B$, and thickness $t_{bk}$ = 8 to 25 mm.

2. **Doubler Plate:** A flat insert plate welded to the curved shell at the bilge, providing a flat surface for welding the bilge keel. It distributes the weld line over a larger area, reducing localized stress concentration on the curved shell and simplifying fabrication.

3. **Welded Joint:** The bilge keel is attached to the doubler plate (which is in turn attached to the shell) by **double continuous fillet welds** on both sides of the fin, with weld legs typically equal to the plate thickness.

4. **Internal Brackets (Tripping Brackets):** Inside the ship, brackets are fitted at intervals (typically every 2–3 m) to support the bilge keel against tripping under transverse loading and to transfer loads to internal longitudinals.

5. **Tapered Ends (Fish-tails):** The forward and aft ends of the bilge keel are **progressively tapered** in width to a point or rounded edge, often over a length of 1–2 m. The thickness is also gradually reduced.

**Function of Doubler Plate:**
- Provides a **flat welding surface** on a curved shell.
- Distributes the **weld-induced residual stress** over a larger area.
- Allows the bilge keel to be **replaced or repaired** without cutting into the shell.
- Increases the **local section modulus** at the bilge, which is a high-stress region of the hull girder.

**Need for Fish-tailing the Ends:**
- The bilge keel terminates where the parallel middle body ends. Beyond this, the hull form changes rapidly (entering the bow or stern run).
- A full-width blunt end in this region would experience **slamming loads** in head/oblique seas.
- A blunt end also acts as a **stress raiser** at a location of high curvature change, leading to **fatigue cracking** at the weld toe.
- Tapering reduces both the **local dynamic pressure** and the **stress concentration factor** (Kt).

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Derivation of Damping Moment from a Pair of Bilge Keels:**

(See the complete derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes for the step-by-step working.)

**Key Result:**

$$\boxed{\,M_{d,total} = \rho \, C_{D,bk} \, A_{bk} \, b_{bk}^{3} \, \dot{\phi}^{2}\,}$$

**Assumptions Stated:**
1. Pure roll motion in still water, no forward speed coupling.
2. The bilge keel behaves as a **bluff body** in cross-flow with constant $C_{D,bk}$.
3. Roll angle is small, so $b_{bk}$ remains constant during a roll cycle.
4. The hydrodynamic force on the bilge keel acts at the centroid of the fin.
5. Both port and starboard bilge keels contribute equally and symmetrically.
6. Quadratic drag law is applicable (Re > 10⁴).
7. Free-surface and ventilation effects are neglected.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**a) Compare bilge keels with fin stabilizers as roll-reduction devices. State the conditions under which each is preferred. (7 Marks)**

**b) A bulk carrier of length $L_{pp} = 180$ m and beam $B = 28$ m is fitted with bilge keels of length $L_{bk} = 0.40\,L_{pp}$ and width $b_{bk} = 0.025\,B$. Take $C_{D,bk} = 1.5$ and $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³. Calculate: (i) the added resistance per bilge keel at a service speed of 15 knots, and (ii) the total added resistance for the ship. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Comparison Table — Bilge Keels vs. Fin Stabilizers**

| **Parameter** | **Bilge Keel** | **Fin Stabilizer** |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Passive | Active (hydraulically actuated) |
| Effectiveness at zero/low speed | Moderate (works at all speeds) | Poor (needs forward speed to generate lift) |
| Effectiveness at high speed | Moderate (lower) | High |
| Power consumption | Nil | Significant (hydraulic pumps) |
| Added resistance penalty | Continuous (always) | Retracted at low speed → no penalty |
| Maintenance cost | Very low | High (moving parts, hydraulics) |
| Reliability | Very high | Moderate (mechanical failures possible) |
| Cost (initial) | Low | High |
| Suitable for | Cargo, tankers, bulk carriers | Naval combatants, cruise ships, yachts |

**Preferred Conditions:**
- **Bilge keel preferred:** For **commercial cargo vessels** with long ocean passages, where reliability, low maintenance, and zero power consumption are critical. Also preferred where roll reduction is needed even at low or zero speed (e.g., while waiting at anchorage).
- **Fin stabilizer preferred:** For **high-value, high-speed vessels** (warships, ferries, cruise liners, megayachts) where the speed-related lift force can be efficiently generated and where the resistance penalty of bilge keels would be unacceptable.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given Data:**
- $L_{pp} = 180$ m, $B = 28$ m
- $L_{bk} = 0.40 \times 180 = 72$ m
- $b_{bk} = 0.025 \times 28 = 0.70$ m
- $C_{D,bk} = 1.5$, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³
- $V = 15$ knots = $15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716$ m/s

**Step 1 — Planform area of one bilge keel:**
$$A_{bk} = L_{bk} \times b_{bk} = 72 \times 0.70 = 50.4 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Added resistance per bilge keel:**

$$R_{add} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_{D,bk} \, A_{bk} \, V^{2}$$

$$R_{add} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.5 \times 50.4 \times (7.716)^{2}$$

$$R_{add} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.5 \times 50.4 \times 59.54$$

$$R_{add} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.5 \times 50.4 \times 59.54$$

Computing stepwise:
- $0.5 \times 1025 = 512.5$
- $512.5 \times 1.5 = 768.75$
- $768.75 \times 50.4 = 38,745$
- $38,745 \times 59.54 \approx 2,306,873$ N

$$\boxed{R_{add} \approx 2.31 \text{ MN per keel}}$$

**Step 3 — Total added resistance (both bilge keels):**
$$R_{add,total} = 2 \times 2.31 = 4.62 \text{ MN}$$

In more digestible units:
$$R_{add,total} \approx 4.62 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} \approx 471 \text{ tonnes-force}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Unit conversion of speed:** 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s, not 1 m/s. Many students lose 1 mark here.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** The question says "per bilge keel" in part (i) and "total" in part (ii). The total = 2 × per-keel. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using $b_{bk}$ as planform dimension:** The planform area is $L_{bk} \times b_{bk}$ (length × width of the fin in side view), **not** $L_{bk} \times t_{bk}$.
> 4. **Reporting units:** Always report resistance in both **Newtons (N)** and **kN or tonnes-force** for clarity.
> 5. **Sign convention:** Added resistance is always **positive** (it opposes forward motion). Do not introduce a negative sign.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bilge Keel (Module 2, OENST614)**

- **Definition:** External longitudinal fin at the bilge; passive roll-damping device.
- **Geometry:** Length = 30–50% of $L_{pp}$; Width = 1.5–3% of $B$; Thickness = 8–25 mm.
- **Quantity:** Always fitted in **pairs** (port + starboard).
- **Location:** At the bilge (≈ 45° turn of the shell, near the maximum girth).
- **Key Components:** Main fin, doubler plate, continuous fillet welds, internal tripping brackets, fish-tailed ends.
- **Why at the bilge?** Maximum moment arm $b_{bk}$ from roll axis; $M_d \propto b_{bk}^3$.
- **Working Principle:** Cross-flow drag during roll → vortex shedding → energy dissipation → roll damping.
- **Damping Moment Formula:** $M_{d,bk} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{D,bk} A_{bk} b_{bk}^{3} \dot{\phi}^{2}$ (per keel).
- **Total Damping (pair):** $M_{d,total} = \rho C_{D,bk} A_{bk} b_{bk}^{3} \dot{\phi}^{2}$.
- **Added Resistance:** $R_{add} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{D,bk} A_{bk} V^{2}$ per keel.
- **Typical $C_{D,bk}$:** 1.2 to 2.0 (bluff body drag).
- **Advantages:** No moving parts, no power consumption, high reliability, low cost, always working.
- **Disadvantages:** Continuous drag penalty (1–3% resistance), ineffective at very high speed, vulnerable to port/berthing damage.
- **Preferred for:** Cargo ships, tankers, bulk carriers, container vessels.
- **Not preferred for:** High-speed vessels, naval combatants (use active fin stabilizers instead).
- **Doubler Plate Role:** Flat insert, distributes weld stress, allows replacement, increases local section modulus.
- **Fish-tailing Role:** Reduces slamming loads and fatigue stress concentration at bilge keel ends.
- **Board Exam Hooks:** Always remember the **cubic dependence on $b_{bk}$**, the **factor of 2 for paired keels**, the **knot-to-m/s conversion** (× 0.5144), and the **linearization** of the quadratic drag law for the equation of motion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
