topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Typical Ship Structural Drawings - Midship Section

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: TYPICAL SHIP STRUCTURAL DRAWINGS — MIDSHIP SECTION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **Midship Section** (also called the **Midship Sectional Plan** or **Typical Transverse Section**) is defined as a transverse cross-sectional drawing of a vessel taken at the **midships point** — i.e., at the amidships perpendicular located exactly halfway between the **Forward Perpendicular (FP)** and the **Aft Perpendicular (AP)** along the ship's design length (LBP).

In KTU 2024 Naval Architecture terminology, the midship section constitutes the **principal structural reference drawing** of the entire vessel because it represents:
- The **maximum cross-sectional area** of the hull girder.
- The location of the **highest longitudinal bending stresses** (sagging and hogging).
- The basis for computing the **section modulus (Z)** of the hull girder.
- The reference plane for **all scantling determinations** under IACS / Classification Society rules.

Formally, under the **Common Structural Rules (CSR)** and **IACS Unified Requirements**, the midship section is the benchmark section used to evaluate the **minimum hull girder section modulus** required to resist vertical wave bending moments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The midship section is NOT just a drawing — it is the **structural design seed** from which all longitudinal strength calculations, plate/frame scantling tables, and hull-girder ultimate strength assessments originate.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **cylindrical drinking straw** that you press gently from above and below. The middle portion of the straw is the **weakest point** for bending — it bends the most. Now imagine a much larger hollow tube (a ship's hull) floating on water; the wave-induced bending moments are largest at the **middle of its length**, not at the ends. The midship section is literally the **structural blueprint of that weakest, most heavily loaded cross-section** in the entire ship.

Think of the ship as a **horizontal beam** (the "hull girder") supported by a continuous water column (the buoyancy) and loaded by gravity (weight). The maximum bending moment of a simply-supported beam under uniform loading occurs at midspan — exactly where the midship section is located. This is why naval architects pour their most concentrated design effort into this single drawing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world context:** If you walk through any shipyard's design office, you will find that the midship section drawing on a drafting board is often the most patched, erased, and revised document — it is the heart of the structural design process.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Key Symbols

The following standard metrics, **mandatory** for KTU 2024 examinations, must be memorized:

- **LBP** (Length Between Perpendiculars) — in **metres (m)**
- **B** (Moulded Breadth) — in **metres (m)**
- **D** (Moulded Depth) — in **metres (m)**
- **T** (Design Draught) — in **metres (m)**
- **CB** (Block Coefficient) — typically **0.60 to 0.85**
- **Section Modulus (Z)** — in **m³**
- **Frame Spacing (s)** — typically **0.6 m to 1.0 m** for transverse framing
- **Longitudinal Spacing** — typically **0.4 m to 0.8 m** for longitudinal framing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold constant to remember:** The ship rule-of-thumb for midship area coefficient is **Cm ≈ 0.93 to 0.99**, meaning the midship is nearly a rectangle. This is why we treat it as a "box" for first-pass section modulus calculations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Components of the Midship Section

A complete midship section drawing contains the following **hierarchically arranged** structural elements:

### A. Shell Plating (Outer Skin)
- **Bottom Shell Plating** — between keel and bilge.
- **Bilge Plating** — curved transition region; subjected to slamming.
- **Side Shell Plating** — between bilge and sheer strake.
- **Sheer Strake** — topmost side strake; thicker than adjacent strakes.
- **Keel Plate** — flat or raised (bar) keel at the centerline.

### B. Deck Plating
- **Strength Deck** (typically uppermost continuous deck) — main contributor to hull-girder bending.
- **Lower Decks** (second deck, third deck, platform deck).
- **Margin Plates** — boundary between inner bottom and side shell at the turn of bilge.
- **Stringer Plates** — at the connection of strength deck to side shell.

### C. Longitudinal Members
- **Longitudinals** (deck, side, bottom, inner-bottom).
- **Hatch side and end coamings**.
- **Centre Keelson / Centreline Girder** — runs along bottom.
- **Side Girders** — transverse girders within the double bottom.
- **Deck Girders** — transverse members below decks.

### D. Transverse Members
- **Transverse Frames** — closely spaced vertical members.
- **Web Frames** — large transverse stiffening plates.
- **Floors** — transverse bottom plates (solid or bracket).
- **Transverse Bulkheads** — watertight divisions.
- **Side Stringers** — horizontal struts on web frames.

### E. Stiffeners
- **Angle bars, T-bars, bulb plates, flat bars** attached to plating.

## 2.2 Framing Systems

The midship section reveals the **framing system** employed. KTU 2024 requires knowledge of all three:

| Framing System | Arrangement | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Transverse Framing** | Frames closely spaced (≈ 0.6 m); longitudinals widely spaced (≈ 2.5 m) | Bulk carriers, tankers, conventional cargo ships |
| **Longitudinal Framing** | Longitudinals closely spaced (≈ 0.4–0.8 m); web frames widely spaced (≈ 3–4 m) | Large tankers, container ships, naval vessels |
| **Combined / Mixed Framing** | Transverse in some regions, longitudinal in others | Specialized vessels, e.g., bulk carriers with longitudinal bottom |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why longitudinal framing in modern ships?** Longitudinals carry the **hull-girder bending stresses** more efficiently by being continuous and aligned with the principal stress direction. This yields a much higher **section modulus for the same weight of steel**, making it mandatory for large vessels.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favorite:** The following formulas appear in nearly every Part B (14-mark) question on Module 3. Master them.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y}$ | Section modulus (general) | $\mathrm{m^3}$ |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Bending stress at extreme fibre | $\mathrm{N/mm^2}$ or $\mathrm{MPa}$ |
| 3 | $I = \dfrac{b h^{3}}{12}$ | Second moment of area of a rectangle about its own centroidal axis | $\mathrm{m^4}$ |
| 4 | $I_{\text{built}} = \sum \left( I_{i} + A_{i} d_{i}^{2} \right)$ | Parallel-axis theorem for built-up sections | $\mathrm{m^4}$ |
| 5 | $M_{\text{perm}} = \dfrac{W L}{8}$ | Permissible still-water bending moment (uniform load approximation) | $\mathrm{N \cdot m}$ |
| 6 | $M_{\text{wave}} = k \cdot L^{2} \cdot B \cdot C_{B}$ | Rule-of-thumb wave bending moment | $\mathrm{N \cdot m}$ |
| 7 | $p = \dfrac{M}{Z \cdot s}$ | Plating thickness from bending (per CSR) | $\mathrm{mm}$ |
| 8 | $s = \dfrac{p \cdot l^{2}}{f \cdot 12}$ | Stiffener spacing from plate bending | $\mathrm{m}$ |
| 9 | $Z_{\min} = \dfrac{M_{\text{total}}}{f_{\text{perm}}}$ | Required minimum section modulus (per IRS / IACS) | $\mathrm{m^3}$ |
| 10 | $f_{\text{perm}} = \dfrac{175}{k}$ | Permissible bending stress in steel (in MPa, $k$ = material factor) | $\mathrm{MPa}$ |

> **Critical formatting note:** In the formulas above, the symbol $\vert$ is rendered using `\vert` style notation to avoid markdown table breaks. All variables are pure scalars.

## 2.4 Physical Constants and Material Properties (Recall)

- **Density of steel:** $\rho_{\text{steel}} = 7.85 \;\mathrm{t/m^3}$ = **7850 kg/m³**
- **Young's Modulus of steel:** $E = 210 \;\mathrm{GPa}$ = **210 × 10⁹ N/m²**
- **Yield stress of mild shipbuilding steel (Grade A):** $\sigma_{y} = 235 \;\mathrm{MPa}$
- **Yield stress of high-tensile steel (Grade AH32):** $\sigma_{y} = 315 \;\mathrm{MPa}$
- **Yield stress of Grade DH36:** $\sigma_{y} = 355 \;\mathrm{MPa}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the midship section use higher-grade steel?** Because the **bending stresses are maximum at amidships**, the plates in this region (especially sheer strake, strength deck, and keel) are required to be of **higher tensile grade** per Classification Society rules (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Naval Architecture

The midship section drawing is used by:
1. **Hull structural designers** — to specify plate thicknesses and stiffener sizes.
2. **Yard production engineers** — to plan cutting, bending, and welding sequences.
3. **Classification society surveyors** — to verify scantlings against rules.
4. **Stability and strength analysts** — to compute the section modulus.
5. **FEM (Finite Element Method) modellers** — to mesh the midship for fine-grain stress analysis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Section Modulus of a Simplified Box-Shaped Midship Section

For a first-order KTU-style calculation, the midship section is treated as a **closed box** consisting of:
- A top flange (the strength deck) of width $B$ and thickness $t_{d}$.
- A bottom flange (the keel plate) of width $B$ and thickness $t_{b}$.
- Two webs (the side shells) of height $D$ and thickness $t_{s}$.

We assume **horizontal neutral axis at mid-depth** (symmetric box).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the contributing flange areas.
$$
A_{d} = B \cdot t_{d} \qquad \text{(deck flange area)}
$$
$$
A_{b} = B \cdot t_{b} \qquad \text{(bottom flange area)}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the side web areas.
$$
A_{s,\text{left}} = A_{s,\text{right}} = D \cdot t_{s}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the lever arm of each flange from the neutral axis (at mid-depth, $y = D/2$).
$$
y_{d} = \frac{D}{2} - \frac{t_{d}}{2} \quad ; \quad y_{b} = \frac{D}{2} - \frac{t_{b}}{2}
$$
For thin plates, we approximate $y_{d} \approx y_{b} \approx D/2$.

**Step 4:** Apply the parallel-axis theorem for the **second moment of area** of the flanges about the horizontal neutral axis.
$$
I_{\text{flange}} = 2 \left( \frac{B \, t^{3}}{12} + B \cdot t \cdot \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^{2} \right)
$$
where $t$ is the average flange thickness.

**Step 5:** Compute the second moment of area of the two side webs.
$$
I_{\text{webs}} = 2 \left( \frac{t_{s} \cdot D^{3}}{12} \right)
$$

**Step 6:** Total second moment of area:
$$
I_{\text{total}} = I_{\text{flange}} + I_{\text{webs}}
$$

$$
I_{\text{total}} = 2 B \, t \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^{2} + \frac{t_{s} \, D^{3}}{6}
$$

**Step 7:** Compute the section modulus. Since $y_{\max} = D/2$:
$$
Z = \frac{I_{\text{total}}}{y_{\max}} = \frac{I_{\text{total}}}{D/2}
$$

$$
Z = \frac{2}{D} \left[ 2 B \, t \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^{2} + \frac{t_{s} \, D^{3}}{6} \right]
$$

**Step 8:** Simplify.
$$
Z = B \, t \, D + \frac{t_{s} \, D^{2}}{3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** For a **box-shaped** midship section, the section modulus is dominated by the product of flange area and depth: $Z \approx B \cdot t \cdot D$. The webs contribute only one-third of the squared-depth term.

### 3.1.1 Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

Given: A bulk carrier has $B = 32 \;\mathrm{m}$, $D = 18 \;\mathrm{m}$, $t_{d} = t_{b} = 30 \;\mathrm{mm} = 0.030 \;\mathrm{m}$, $t_{s} = 25 \;\mathrm{mm} = 0.025 \;\mathrm{m}$.

**Compute deck flange contribution:**
$$
Z_{\text{flange}} = B \cdot t \cdot D = 32 \times 0.030 \times 18 = 17.28 \;\mathrm{m^{3}}
$$

**Compute web contribution:**
$$
Z_{\text{web}} = \frac{t_{s} \cdot D^{2}}{3} = \frac{0.025 \times 18^{2}}{3} = \frac{8.10}{3} = 2.70 \;\mathrm{m^{3}}
$$

**Total section modulus:**
$$
Z_{\text{total}} = 17.28 + 2.70 = 19.98 \;\mathrm{m^{3}} \approx 20.0 \;\mathrm{m^{3}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Section Modulus from Permissible Bending Stress

The total bending moment at midships, combining still-water and wave components, must be resisted by a section modulus such that the bending stress does not exceed the permissible value.

**Step 1:** Total bending moment:
$$
M_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{sw}} + M_{\text{wave}}
$$
where $M_{\text{sw}}$ = still-water bending moment, $M_{\text{wave}}$ = wave-induced bending moment.

**Step 2:** Permissible bending stress for mild steel (Grade A) per IRS rules:
$$
f_{\text{perm}} = \frac{175}{k} \;\mathrm{MPa}
$$
For a single-skin ship ($k = 1.0$):
$$
f_{\text{perm}} = 175 \;\mathrm{MPa} = 175 \times 10^{6} \;\mathrm{N/m^{2}}
$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange the bending stress equation to obtain the **required section modulus**:
$$
\sigma_{\text{bending}} = \frac{M_{\text{total}}}{Z} \leq f_{\text{perm}}
$$

$$
Z_{\text{required}} \geq \frac{M_{\text{total}}}{f_{\text{perm}}}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute units. If $M_{\text{total}}$ is in $\mathrm{N \cdot m}$ and $f_{\text{perm}}$ in $\mathrm{N/m^{2}}$, then $Z$ is in $\mathrm{m^{3}}$.
$$
Z_{\text{required}} = \frac{M_{\text{total}}}{175 \times 10^{6}} \;\mathrm{m^{3}}
$$

**Step 5:** Numerical check. Let $M_{\text{total}} = 3.5 \times 10^{9} \;\mathrm{N \cdot m}$.
$$
Z_{\text{required}} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^{9}}{175 \times 10^{6}} = 20.0 \;\mathrm{m^{3}}
$$

This matches our Derivation 1 result, confirming structural adequacy.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frame Spacing for Transverse Framing

For a plate between two adjacent transverse frames, treated as a simply-supported rectangular panel of dimensions $s \times l$ (where $s$ is the frame spacing and $l$ is the unsupported span of the plate), the maximum bending moment per unit width under a uniformly distributed pressure $p$ is:

$$
m = \frac{p \cdot l^{2}}{8} \quad \text{(per unit width)}
$$

**Step 1:** For the plate of thickness $t_{p}$, the section modulus per unit width is:
$$
z_{\text{plate}} = \frac{t_{p}^{2}}{6} \quad \text{(per unit width)}
$$

**Step 2:** Allowable bending stress in the plate $f$:
$$
\sigma = \frac{m}{z_{\text{plate}}} \leq f
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute and solve for spacing $s$ (which appears implicitly through $l$ in stiffened plate design):
$$
\frac{p \cdot s^{2}}{8} \cdot \frac{6}{t_{p}^{2}} \leq f
$$

$$
s \leq t_{p} \sqrt{\frac{4 \, f}{3 \, p}}
$$

**Step 4:** Numerical example. Let $p = 50 \;\mathrm{kN/m^{2}}$, $t_{p} = 15 \;\mathrm{mm} = 0.015 \;\mathrm{m}$, $f = 175 \times 10^{3} \;\mathrm{kN/m^{2}}$.
$$
s \leq 0.015 \times \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 175 \times 10^{3}}{3 \times 50}}
$$

$$
s \leq 0.015 \times \sqrt{4666.67} \leq 0.015 \times 68.30 \leq 1.02 \;\mathrm{m}
$$

Hence, frame spacing of approximately **1.0 m** is structurally acceptable for this plating.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (Choosing Framing System)

| Decision Criterion | Transverse Framing | Longitudinal Framing | KTU Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ship length $L$ | $L < 100 \;\mathrm{m}$ | $L > 150 \;\mathrm{m}$ | Use longitudinal for large vessels |
| Plate thickness required | Higher | Lower (saves steel) | Longitudinal saves 10–15% steel |
| Construction complexity | Simpler | More complex welding | Transverse is yard-friendly |
| Fatigue performance | Lower (concentrated welds) | Higher (distributed) | Longitudinal preferred |
| Use case | Small cargo, tugs | Tankers, bulkers, container ships | Based on ship size |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Midship Section Components

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **hierarchical structure** of a typical midship section, showing how each component interacts with the central hull girder. The diagram is organized by **spatial region** (Bottom → Side → Deck) and **structural function** (Longitudinal vs. Transverse).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootNode["MIDSHIP SECTION OF A SHIP HULL GIRDER"]
    
    rootNode --> grpShell["OUTER SHELL PLATING GROUP"]
    rootNode --> grpDeck["DECK PLATING GROUP"]
    rootNode --> grpDoubleBottom["DOUBLE BOTTOM GROUP"]
    rootNode --> grpTransverse["TRANSVERSE FRAMING GROUP"]
    rootNode --> grpLongitudinal["LONGITUDINAL FRAMING GROUP"]
    rootNode --> grpBulkhead["BULKHEAD GROUP"]
    
    grpShell --> keel["KEEL PLATE"]
    grpShell --> bottomShell["BOTTOM SHELL PLATING"]
    grpShell --> bilge["BILGE PLATING"]
    grpShell --> sideShell["SIDE SHELL PLATING"]
    grpShell --> sheerStrake["SHEER STRAKE TOP STRENGTH STRIP"]
    
    grpDeck --> strDeck["STRENGTH DECK PLATING"]
    grpDeck --> strPlates["STRINGER PLATE AT SHEER"]
    grpDeck --> marginPlates["MARGIN PLATE AT BILGE"]
    grpDeck --> lowerDeck["LOWER DECK PLATING"]
    
    grpDoubleBottom --> innerBottom["INNER BOTTOM PLATING"]
    grpDoubleBottom --> centerGirder["CENTRELINE GIRDER KEELSON"]
    grpDoubleBottom --> sideGirder["SIDE GIRDERS"]
    grpDoubleBottom --> floorsPlate["FLOOR PLATES"]
    
    grpTransverse --> tFrames["TRANSVERSE FRAMES EVERY 0.6 TO 1.0 METRE"]
    grpTransverse --> webFrames["WEB FRAMES EVERY 3 TO 4 METRES"]
    grpTransverse --> sideStrut["SIDE STRINGERS HORIZONTAL"]
    
    grpLongitudinal --> deckLong["DECK LONGITUDINALS"]
    grpLongitudinal --> sideLong["SIDE LONGITUDINALS"]
    grpLongitudinal --> bottomLong["BOTTOM LONGITUDINALS"]
    grpLongitudinal --> innerLong["INNER BOTTOM LONGITUDINALS"]
    
    grpBulkhead --> tBulkhead["TRANSVERSE BULKHEADS WATERTIGHT"]
    grpBulkhead --> lBulkhead["LONGITUDINAL BULKHEADS"]
    
    grpShell -.-> stressFlow["CARRIES HULL GIRDER STRESS"]
    grpDeck -.-> stressFlow
    grpDoubleBottom -.-> stressFlow
    stressFlow --> neutralAxis["HORIZONTAL NEUTRAL AXIS AT MID DEPTH"]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Stress Flow Path in the Hull Girder

The following block diagram illustrates the **load path** through the midship section when the ship is in **hogging condition** (sagging is mirror image).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    loadA["WAVE LOAD WATER BELOW MIDSHIPS"]
    loadA --> deckComp["DECK PLATING IN TENSION TOP FLANGE"]
    loadA --> keelComp["KEEL PLATING IN COMPRESSION BOTTOM FLANGE"]
    
    deckComp --> deckLong["DECK LONGITUDINALS CARRY TENSION"]
    keelComp --> botLong["BOTTOM LONGITUDINALS CARRY COMPRESSION"]
    
    deckLong --> sideShellA["SIDE SHELL WEBS TRANSFER SHEAR"]
    botLong --> sideShellA
    
    sideShellA --> neutral["NEUTRAL AXIS ZERO STRESS"]
    sideShellA --> stiffA["STIFFENERS PREVENT BUCKLING"]
    stiffA --> margin["MARGIN PLATE AND STRINGER PLATE JUNCTIONS"]
    
    neutral --> final["RESULTANT SECTION MODULUS Z DETERMINES STRESS"]
```

## 4.3 Diagram Interpretation Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each box represents a **physical structural member** of the midship section. The arrows denote **load transfer** and **structural interaction**. The **horizontal neutral axis** is the locus of zero longitudinal stress and lies approximately at **mid-depth** for a symmetric box-shaped hull.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key observation from the diagrams:** The **strength deck** and the **keel plate** are the **flanges** of the hull girder — they carry the maximum longitudinal stresses. The **side shells** act as **webs** transferring shear between top and bottom flanges. This is the **I-beam analogy** of the ship hull.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the term "Midship Section" of a ship. Why is it considered the most important structural drawing?**

**Model Answer:**

The midship section is a transverse cross-sectional drawing of a vessel taken at the **amidships perpendicular** — the location exactly halfway between the Forward Perpendicular (FP) and Aft Perpendicular (AP).

It is considered the most important structural drawing because:

1. It represents the **maximum cross-sectional area** of the hull girder.
2. It experiences the **highest longitudinal bending stresses** (sagging and hogging).
3. It serves as the **basis for computing the hull-girder section modulus (Z)**.
4. It dictates the **scantlings of all primary structural members** at amidships.
5. All **Classification Society rule scantling tables** are anchored to the midship section.
6. It is the **reference plane for FEM (Finite Element) stress analysis** of the hull girder.

**Marking Distribution (KTU 2024 pattern):**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Any four importance points: 2 marks (0.5 each)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between transverse framing and longitudinal framing systems in a ship.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Transverse Framing | Longitudinal Framing |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transverse frames closely spaced (≈ 0.6 m) | Transverse frames widely spaced (≈ 3–4 m) |
| 2 | Longitudinals widely spaced (≈ 2.5 m) | Longitudinals closely spaced (≈ 0.4–0.8 m) |
| 3 | Used in small to medium ships | Used in large tankers, bulkers, container ships |
| 4 | Higher plate thickness required | Lower plate thickness sufficient |
| 5 | Simpler to fabricate in shipyards | More complex welding sequence |
| 6 | Lower fatigue resistance | Superior fatigue performance |

**Marking Distribution (KTU 2024 pattern):**
- Tabular differentiation: 3 marks (1 for definition + 2 for contrasts)
