> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

A bulkhead is a vertical plate wall inside a ship used to divide the hull into compartments, providing structural strength and watertight subdivision.

A **collision bulkhead** is a watertight transverse bulkhead located at the forward end of the ship, typically within **5%–7% of LBP** from the forward perpendicular. Its function is to **prevent flooding of the entire ship** in the event of a bow collision. It must extend watertight up to the freeboard deck, and **no doors, manholes, or openings** are permitted below the bulkhead deck, in accordance with **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 12**.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for collision bulkhead function and SOLAS detail)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: Differentiate between a flat stiffened bulkhead and a corrugated bulkhead.**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Flat Stiffened Bulkhead | Corrugated Bulkhead |
|---|---|---|
| Stiffening | Separate T-bars/angles welded to the plate | Built-in trapezoidal/curved corrugations |
| Weight | Higher (plate + stiffeners) | Lower (no separate stiffeners) |
| Fabrication | Labour-intensive | Mechanically pressed |
| Applications | Older ships, collision bulkheads | Modern tankers, bulk carriers |

*(3 marks: 1 for stating the difference + 2 for the comparative table or distinguishing points)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the different types of watertight bulkheads found in a typical merchant ship with neat sketches. Describe the regulations governing their number and location as per SOLAS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A deep ballast tank has its bulkhead extending from the tank top to the deck with a vertical height of **8 m**. The bulkhead is made of flat stiffened mild steel construction with vertical stiffeners spaced at **600 mm**. Using:
- Density of seawater = 1025 kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Design factor $f = 1.05$
- Plate coefficient $k = 0.018$
- Permissible bending stress $\sigma_{\text{perm}} = 175$ N/mm²

Calculate: **(i)** Maximum design pressure on the plating, **(ii)** Required plating thickness. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Watertight Bulkhead Types & SOLAS Regulations (7 Marks)

**Types of WT Bulkheads (any 4 expected, 1.5 marks each):**

1. **Collision Bulkhead** – Forward, 5–7% LBP from FP. No openings allowed below bulkhead deck.
2. **Engine Room Bulkhead** – Forward and aft of machinery space. Contains A-60 fire-rated WT doors.
3. **Peak Bulkheads** – Forward and aft peaks. Usually subdivided by swash plates for fuel/water.
4. **Shaft Tunnel Bulkhead** – Partial bulkhead forming tunnel for propeller shaft.
5. **Cofferdam Bulkhead** – Empty double bulkhead between incompatible tanks.
6. **Transverse WT Bulkheads between cargo holds** – Required per SOLAS based on subdivision index.

**SOLAS Regulations (2 marks):**

- **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 12**: Defines watertight bulkheads, their number, and location.
- **Regulation 13**: Subdivision and damage stability requirements.
- **Subdivision Index (R)**: For cargo ships, $R \geq 0.5$ for 1-compartment flooding, $R \geq 0.7$ for 2-compartment flooding (depending on length and service).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute the design pressure (3 marks).**

Given: $H = 8$ m, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $f = 1.05$.

$$
p_{\max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 8
$$

$$
p_{\max} = 80442 \quad \text{Pa} = 80.44 \quad \text{kN/m²}
$$

Applying design factor:

$$
p_{\text{design}} = f \cdot p_{\max} = 1.05 \times 80.44 = 84.46 \quad \text{(kN/m²)}
$$

**Step 2: Compute plating thickness (4 marks).**

Using $t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{p}$ with $s = 600$ mm and $k = 0.018$:

$$
t = 600 \times 0.018 \times \sqrt{84.46}
$$

$$
t = 10.8 \times 9.19
$$

$$
t = 99.3 \quad \text{(mm)}
$$

Rounded up to standard shipbuilding plate gauge: **$t = 100$ mm**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the constructional details of a corrugated bulkhead with a neat sketch. List **three advantages** of corrugated bulkheads over flat stiffened bulkheads. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **required section modulus** of a vertical stiffener on a transverse bulkhead in a cargo hold of a bulk carrier. Given:
- Design pressure $p = 25$ kN/m²
- Stiffener spacing $s = 700$ mm
- Unsupported span between brackets $l = 3500$ mm
- Bending moment coefficient $m = 12$
- Permissible bending stress $\sigma_{\text{perm}} = 175$ N/mm²

If a **T-section (T150×75×9/12)** with section modulus $Z = 75$ cm³ is provided, check whether it is adequate. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Corrugated Bulkhead Construction (7 Marks)

**Constructional Details (4 marks):**
- The bulkhead plating is pressed into a **series of trapezoidal or curved corrugations** running vertically (or horizontally).
- Each corrugation has a **flange (top)**, **web (side)**, and **flange (bottom)**, forming a built-up stiffener.
- Typical corrugation pitch $P = 600$–$1000$ mm; depth $D = 150$–$300$ mm; angle $\alpha = 60°$–$90°$.
- Welded directly to the deck above and tank top/double bottom below using **edge preparations (full penetration welds)**.

**Three Advantages (3 marks — 1 each):**
1. **No separate stiffeners required** — saving material and fabrication cost.
2. **Lower weight** for the same buckling and bending strength.
3. **Higher out-of-plane stiffness** — better resistance to sloshing and cargo pressure.

---

#### Part (b) — Section Modulus Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Required Section Modulus (4 marks).**

$$
Z_{\text{req}} = \frac{p \cdot s \cdot l^{2}}{m \cdot \sigma_{\text{perm}}}
$$

Convert units to N and mm:
- $p = 25 \times 10^{-3}$ N/mm²
- $s = 700$ mm
- $l = 3500$ mm
- $m = 12$
- $\sigma_{\text{perm}} = 175$ N/mm²

$$
Z_{\text{req}} = \frac{0.025 \times 700 \times (3500)^{2}}{12 \times 175} \quad \text{(mm³)}
$$

$$
Z_{\text{req}} = \frac{0.025 \times 700 \times 12{,}250{,}000}{2100}
$$

$$
Z_{\text{req}} = \frac{214{,}375{,}000}{2100} = 102{,}083 \quad \text{(mm³)}
$$

Converting to cm³ (1 cm³ = 1000 mm³):

$$
Z_{\text{req}} = 102.08 \quad \text{(cm³)}
$$

**Step 2: Comparison (3 marks).**

Provided section modulus: $Z_{\text{provided}} = 75$ cm³.

Compare:

$$
Z_{\text{provided}} = 75 \quad \text{(cm³)} < Z_{\text{req}} = 102.08 \quad \text{(cm³)}
$$

**Conclusion**: The T150×75×9/12 section is **NOT adequate**. A larger section must be selected. A suitable standard section is e.g., **T200×100×11/14** with $Z \geq 103$ cm³.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — Most students forget to convert kN/m² to N/mm² before substituting into the formula. This single mistake results in losing **3 marks** out of 7 in numerical problems.
> 2. **Confusing boundary conditions** — Always state whether the stiffener is *simply supported*, *fixed*, or *partially fixed*. The value of $m$ changes drastically (8 vs. 24).
> 3. **SOLAS Regulation misquoting** — Examiners expect the **regulation number (Chapter II-1, Reg. 12)** along with the rule, not just "per SOLAS".
> 4. **Skipping sketches in part (a) of 14-mark questions** — Even a small labelled sketch of the bulkhead location **earns 2 extra marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Bulkhead** = vertical plate wall subdividing the ship into compartments.
- [x] **Three classification axes**: Orientation (transverse/longitudinal), Watertightness (WT/NWT), Function (collision/engine room/swash/cofferdam).
- [x] **Collision bulkhead** is at the forward end, within **5%–7% of LBP**, regulated by **SOLAS Ch. II-1, Reg. 12**. No openings below bulkhead deck.
- [x] **Engine room bulkhead** surrounds the machinery space; may have A-60 fire-rated WT doors.
- [x] **Swash bulkhead** = partial vertical plate inside tanks to dampen liquid sloshing.
- [x] **Cofferdam** = empty double bulkhead between incompatible fluid tanks (e.g., fuel oil and freshwater).
- [x] **Corrugated bulkheads** are preferred in modern ships — lighter, faster to fabricate, no separate stiffeners needed.
- [x] **Flat stiffened bulkheads** use rolled T-sections or angles welded to the plate; still used for collision bulkheads.
- [x] **Plating thickness rule**: $t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{p}$ (mm), where $p$ is in kN/m².
- [x] **Stiffener section modulus rule**: $Z_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{p \cdot s \cdot l^{2}}{m \cdot \sigma_{\text{perm}}}$ — always convert units to N/mm².
- [x] **Boundary fixity coefficient** $m$: 8 (simply supported), 11.4 (one end fixed), 24 (both ends fully fixed) — ships typically use $m = 12$.
- [x] **Seawater hydrostatic rule**: pressure increases by **~10 kN/m² per metre** of depth.
- [x] **Standard permissible stresses** (mild steel): $\sigma_{\text{perm}} \approx 175$ N/mm², $\tau_{\text{perm}} \approx 100$ N/mm².
- [x] **Material density (for pressure calcs)**: Seawater = **1025 kg/m³**, Fuel oil ≈ **850 kg/m³**, Freshwater = **1000 kg/m³**.
- [x] **SOLAS subdivision index** $R \geq 0.5$ (1-compartment) and $R \geq 0.7$ (2-compartment) for most cargo ships.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spacing of Watertight Bulkheads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spacing of Watertight Bulkheads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **watertight bulkhead** is a transverse (or longitudinal) vertical structural division within a ship's hull, constructed of steel plating stiffened by webs, flanges, and girders, designed to be **completely impervious to the passage of water** under the design head of pressure it is subjected to. The **spacing of watertight bulkheads** refers to the longitudinal distance between successive transverse watertight bulkheads measured along the ship's length, regulated to ensure **subdivision**, **damage stability**, and **structural integrity** in compliance with SOLAS, the International Load Line Convention (ILLC), and Classification Society rules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Watertight bulkhead spacing is governed primarily by the *Factor of Subdivision (F)* and the *Permissible Length of Compartment (LPC)*, derived from damage stability philosophy — *"A ship must remain afloat and stable with any one (or more) compartment(s) flooded, depending on service."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Watermelon Principle"

Imagine a large hollow watermelon (the ship) floating in a bathtub. If you accidentally crack the rind, water rushes in and the fruit sinks. But **if the inside of the watermelon were divided by thin vertical walls** (watertight bulkheads), only the cracked section would flood — the rest would stay dry, and the fruit would keep floating. **The closer those internal walls are spaced, the safer the fruit (ship).** This is the entire philosophy behind watertight subdivision: **compartmentalization saves lives**.

### 1.3 Classification of Watertight Bulkheads by Function

> [!NOTE]
> Each bulkhead type has a **statutory minimum spacing** prescribed by SOLAS Chapter II-1 and the Load Line Convention.

| Bulkhead Type | Functional Role | Spacing Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Collision Bulkhead (CB)** | Protects against forward flooding after bow collision | $0.05L$ to $0.08L$ abaft FP |
| **After Peak Bulkhead (APB)** | Protects steering gear and after-peak tank | Typically at or just forward of shaft bossing |
| **Forward Machinery Space Bulkhead** | Isolates machinery from forward spaces | As required by damage stability |
| **Aft Machinery Space Bulkhead** | Isolates machinery from after spaces | As required by damage stability |
| **Shaft Tunnel Bulkhead** | Seals shaft tunnel at ends | At tunnel entrance and exit |
| **Cofferdam Bulkhead** | Double isolation between oil tanks and accommodation | Adjacent to fuel tanks |
| **Bulkhead Deck Bulkheads** | Reaches the *bulkhead deck* (the highest deck to which transverse bulkheads are carried) | Subdivision rules apply |

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (in Bold)

- **Floodable Length ($L_f$)** — the maximum length of a compartment that can be flooded without submerging the **margin line**.
- **Margin Line** — a line drawn **at least 76 mm below the bulkhead deck at the side**.
- **Factor of Subdivision (F)** — a dimensionless ratio, $0 \le F \le 1.0$, defining subdivision efficiency.
- **Permissible Length of Compartment (LPC)** — derived as $F \times L_f$.
- **Bulkhead Deck** — the **uppermost deck** to which transverse watertight bulkheads extend watertight.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" Behind Spacing

The ship designer begins with three governing constraints:

1. **Regulatory Minimum:** SOLAS / ILLC mandates that certain bulkheads (collision, machinery, after peak) **must** be present at fixed locations — *non-negotiable* for the ship to be classed and registered.
2. **Damage Stability Criterion:** For any **permissible flooding case**, the residual **GZ curve** (righting lever) must satisfy minimum area and range criteria — *this is what dictates the maximum allowable compartment length*.
3. **Structural Economy:** Bulkheads cost steel, fabrication hours, and add weight. They are spaced *as widely as possible* within the regulatory envelope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** The art of ship design lies in satisfying damage stability with the **fewest, most widely-spaced bulkheads**, because every bulkhead represents cost, weight, and a potential passage-penetration hazard.

### 2.2 The Three Solas "Position-Locked" Bulkheads

**A) Collision Bulkhead — SOLAS Reg. II-1/11**
The collision bulkhead **must** be located at a distance $x$ from the forward perpendicular (FP) such that:
- $x \ge 0.05L$ **OR** $x \ge 10 \text{ m}$ (whichever is greater)
- Except where the Administration permits, $x \le 0.08L$ **OR** $x \le 0.05L + 10 \text{ m}$ (whichever is greater)

**B) After Peak Bulkhead — SOLAS Reg. II-1/12**
A watertight after peak bulkhead **must** be fitted, positioned to protect the steering gear, typically just forward of the rudder post and enclosing the stern tube.

**C) Machinery Space Bulkheads — SOLAS Reg. II-1/14**
For ships with **Category A machinery spaces** (main propulsion machinery using fuel having a flashpoint ≤ 60 °C), the machinery spaces must be bounded fore and aft by watertight bulkheads. These bulkheads must extend to the bulkhead deck.

### 2.3 The Factor of Subdivision (F) — Conceptual Foundation

The **Factor of Subdivision (F)** is a numerical rating (0 to 1) that quantifies how effectively the ship's length is divided into watertight compartments. A higher F indicates **finer subdivision** (more, smaller compartments).

For **passenger ships**, F is given by the SOLAS stepped formula (simplified for KTU-level understanding):

$$
F = A + \left(\frac{N - \text{threshold terms}}{\text{scaling factor}}\right)
$$

For **cargo ships**, F is a tabulated function of length $L$ and service, generally between **0.33 and 1.0**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Exam Favourite):** For unrestricted-service cargo ships, typical F values are in the range **0.50 – 0.85**, increasing with ship length.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for Examinations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every exam-relevant formula** for this topic. No pipe symbol `|` is used inside cells — only `\vert` and `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| **Quantity** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Collision bulkhead minimum distance from FP | $x_{cb} \ge \max(0.05L,\ 10\text{ m})$ | metres | SOLAS Reg. II-1/11.1 |
| Collision bulkhead maximum distance from FP | $x_{cb} \le \max(0.08L,\ 0.05L + 10\text{ m})$ | metres | Unless Administration permits |
| Factor of Subdivision (cargo) | $F = f(L,\ \text{service})$ | dimensionless | Tabulated in SOLAS / Class rules |
| Permissible Length of Compartment | $L_{pc} = F \times L_f$ | metres | Where $L_f$ is floodable length |
| Maximum compartment length criterion | $L_{comp} \le L_{pc}$ | metres | For all adjacent compartment pairs |
| Margin line offset | $y_{ml} \ge 0.076 \text{ m}$ | metres | Below bulkhead deck at side |
| Permeability ($\mu$) — accommodation | $\mu = 0.95$ | — | SOLAS standard value |
| Permeability — machinery spaces | $\mu = 0.85$ | — | SOLAS standard value |
| Permeability — dry cargo spaces | $\mu = 0.60$ (or 0.70 if intended for empty) | — | SOLAS standard value |
| Permeability — tanks (oil, water) | $\mu = 0$ or 0.95 (depending on filling) | — | Empty tank = 0.95; full = 0 |
| Floodable length — basic definition | $L_f = \dfrac{V_{flooded}}{\mu \cdot A_{wl}}$ | metres | Volume of floodable space / (permeability × WL area) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practical ship design offices, the bulkhead spacing arrangement is one of the **first things established** on the GA (General Arrangement) plan — *before hull form optimization, structural scantling, or machinery selection*. It is the **skeleton of the ship's safety case**. Mistakes here are catastrophic: the *MV Estonia* disaster (1994) and the *Costa Concordia* (2012) both involved subdivision-related failure modes that were directly linked to bulkhead positioning and door-management policies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Permissible Length of Compartment (LPC) from Damage Stability Theory

The **fundamental damage stability equation** asks: *Given that a compartment of length $L_{comp}$ is flooded, what is the new waterline?* The flooded volume must equal the volume of the lost buoyancy. This yields the floodable length.

**Step 1 — Define the flooded volume.**

The volume of water that enters a compartment is **not equal** to the gross geometric volume of the compartment. Some volume is occupied by the ship's structure, machinery, stores, and any pre-existing water in tanks. The **permeability ($\mu$)** captures this:

$$
V_{flooded} = \mu \cdot V_{gross}
$$

where $V_{gross}$ is the gross internal volume of the compartment.

**Step 2 — Equate to lost buoyancy.**

For the ship to remain afloat, the volume of flooding must equal the loss of buoyancy such that the new waterline sits **at or below the margin line**:

$$
\mu \cdot V_{gross} \le \Delta V_{buoyancy\ available}
$$

The buoyancy available above the margin line, over a compartment of length $L_{comp}$, is:

$$
\Delta V_{buoyancy\ available} = \mu \cdot \int_{0}^{L_{comp}} A_{wl}(x)\, dx
$$

But to be **conservative**, SOLAS defines the **floodable length $L_f$** as the **maximum length of a compartment of assumed constant cross-section that, when flooded, will bring the ship exactly to the margin line**.

**Step 3 — Solve for the maximum compartment length.**

For a compartment with constant permeability $\mu$ and a waterplane section area $A_{wl}$ assumed locally constant near the margin line:

$$
L_f = \frac{V_{margin}}{\mu \cdot A_{wl}}
$$

where $V_{margin}$ is the **available volume of buoyancy above the margin line** per unit length, expressed as $V_{margin} = A_{margin} \cdot 1$, the transverse area between the load waterline and the margin line.

**Step 4 — Introduce the Factor of Subdivision.**

To ensure **redundancy** (i.e., survival even when two adjacent compartments are flooded, for larger ships), the permissible compartment length is **reduced** by the factor $F$:

$$
L_{pc} = F \cdot L_f
$$

**Step 5 — The governing design check.**

For **every** pair of adjacent transverse watertight bulkheads, the actual longitudinal spacing $L_{comp}$ must satisfy:

$$
L_{comp} \le L_{pc} = F \cdot L_f
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three-Step Designer Workflow:**
> 1. Compute $L_f$ for the ship at the **deepest subdivision draft**.
> 2. Look up (or calculate) the **Factor of Subdivision $F$** based on $L$ and ship type.
> 3. Compute $L_{pc} = F \cdot L_f$ and **position every bulkhead such that no compartment exceeds $L_{pc}$**, while also satisfying the **collision bulkhead, after peak bulkhead, and machinery space bulkhead** location rules.

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Board Style

**Given:**
A cargo ship of length $L = 180 \text{ m}$, service unrestricted, factor of subdivision $F = 0.65$ (assumed), floodable length at deepest subdivision draft $L_f = 96 \text{ m}$, permeability of forward hold $\mu = 0.70$.

**Find:**
(a) The permissible length of compartment (LPC).
(b) Whether a forward hold measuring $58 \text{ m}$ between the collision bulkhead and the next transverse bulkhead is compliant.
(c) Verify the collision bulkhead location is permissible.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Permissible Length of Compartment:**

$$
L_{pc} = F \cdot L_f
$$

$$
L_{pc} = 0.65 \times 96 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L_{pc} = 62.4 \text{ m}
$$

**Part (b) — Compliance Check:**

$$
L_{comp} = 58 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L_{comp} = 58 \text{ m} \le L_{pc} = 62.4 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Therefore, the forward hold is compliant.** The bulkhead spacing satisfies the regulatory requirement with a margin of $62.4 - 58.0 = 4.4 \text{ m}$.

**Part (c) — Collision Bulkhead Location Verification:**

The collision bulkhead must satisfy both bounds:

**Lower bound:**

$$
x_{cb,\ min} = \max(0.05L,\ 10\text{ m}) = \max(0.05 \times 180,\ 10) = \max(9.0,\ 10) = 10.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Upper bound:**

$$
x_{cb,\ max} = \max(0.08L,\ 0.05L + 10\text{ m}) = \max(0.08 \times 180,\ 0.05 \times 180 + 10) = \max(14.4,\ 19.0) = 19.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Compliance Condition:**

$$
10.0 \text{ m} \le x_{cb} \le 19.0 \text{ m}
$$

If the actual collision bulkhead is located at $x_{cb} = 11.5 \text{ m}$ from FP, the configuration is **COMPLIANT** (provided the Administration has not granted special dispensation).

### 3.3 Summary Matrix — Critical Spacing Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix condenses the **"must-know"** spacing rules for KTU 2024 board examinations.

| **Bulkhead** | **Lower Bound** | **Upper Bound** | **Reference** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Collision Bulkhead | $\max(0.05L,\ 10\text{ m})$ | $\max(0.08L,\ 0.05L + 10\text{ m})$ | SOLAS II-1/11 |
| After Peak Bulkhead | Must protect steering gear | — | SOLAS II-1/12 |
| Forward Machinery Bulkhead | $0.05L$ abaft FP (for Cat. A) | Damage-stability-driven | SOLAS II-1/14 |
| Aft Machinery Bulkhead | Damage-stability-driven | Damage-stability-driven | SOLAS II-1/14 |
| Subdivision Bulkheads | — | $F \cdot L_f$ | SOLAS II-1/4 to II-1/8 |
| Shaft Tunnel Bulkheads | At tunnel ends | — | SOLAS II-1/13 |
| Cofferdam Bulkhead | Adjacent to oil tanks | — | SOLAS II-1/26 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Longitudinal Arrangement of Watertight Bulkheads (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **canonical longitudinal position of the principal watertight bulkheads** in a typical general-cargo or dry-bulk ship, viewed from the side. Each node is a structural / functional station along the ship's length.

```mermaid
graph LR
    FP[FP Forward Perpendicular] --> CB[Collision Bulkhead]
    CB --> FH1[Forward Hold 1]
    FH1 --> WB1[Watertight Bulkhead 1]
    WB1 --> FH2[Forward Hold 2]
    FH2 --> FMSB[Forward Machinery Space Bulkhead]
    FMSB --> MS[Machinery Space]
    MS --> AMSB[Aft Machinery Space Bulkhead]
    AMSB --> AH[Aft Hold]
    AH --> APB[After Peak Bulkhead]
    APB --> AP[AP Aft Perpendicular]
    CB -.Position Lock.-> SOLASCB[SOLAS Reg II-1 11 0.05L to 0.08L]
    APB -.Position Lock.-> SOLASAPB[SOLAS Reg II-1 12 After Peak]
    FMSB -.Position Lock.-> SOLASFMS[SOLAS Reg II-1 14 Cat A Spaces]
    AMSB -.Position Lock.-> SOLASAMS[SOLAS Reg II-1 14 Cat A Spaces]
    WB1 -.Design Check.-> LPC[Permissible Length less than F times Lf]
    style FP fill:#FFB6C1
    style AP fill:#FFB6C1
    style CB fill:#90EE90
    style APB fill:#90EE90
    style FMSB fill:#90EE90
    style AMSB fill:#90EE90
    style WB1 fill:#FFD700
    style MS fill:#D3D3D3
    style LPC fill:#FFA500
```

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Watertight Bulkhead Spacing Design

The following Mermaid flowchart encapsulates the **engineering decision process** for positioning subdivision bulkheads during ship design.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start Design Process] --> B[Define Ship Length L and Service Type]
    B --> C[Determine Factor of Subdivision F]
    C --> D[Compute Floodable Length Lf at Deepest Subdivision Draft]
    D --> E[Calculate LPC equals F times Lf]
    E --> F[Locate Collision Bulkhead per SOLAS II-1 11]
    F --> G[Locate After Peak Bulkhead per SOLAS II-1 12]
    G --> H[Locate Machinery Space Bulkheads per SOLAS II-1 14]
    H --> I[Position Remaining Subdivision Bulkheads]
    I --> J{Is every Lcomp less than or equal to LPC}
    J -->|Yes| K[Layout Compliant Proceed to Scantling]
    J -->|No| L[Add Extra Bulkhead or Refine Layout]
    L --> I
    K --> M[Perform Damage Stability Calculation Probabilistic Method]
    M --> N{Damage Stability Satisfies SOLAS Attained Subdivision Index}
    N -->|Yes| O[Design Approved]
    N -->|No| P[Revise Subdivision Layout]
    P --> I
    style A fill:#90EE90
    style O fill:#90EE90
    style K fill:#FFD700
    style M fill:#FFD700
    style J fill:#FFA500
    style N fill:#FFA500
    style L fill:#FF6B6B
    style P fill:#FF6B6B
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Margin Line & Bulkhead Deck Geometry

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the **vertical geometry** of the bulkhead deck, margin line, and load waterline — the three reference planes used in the $L_f$ calculation.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SIDE_VIEW[Cross Section at Bulkhead Station]
        BD[Bulkhead Deck Uppermost watertight deck] --- ML[Margin Line 76 mm below deck at side]
        ML --- LWL[Load Waterline at Deepest Subdivision Draft]
        LWL --- BL[Baseline Keel]
    end
    SIDE_VIEW -.Flooding Volume Above Margin.-> FV[This volume equals flooded volume when compartment reaches margin line]
    FV -.Divides by mu times A wl.-> LF[Lf = Floodable Length]
    style BD fill:#87CEEB
    style ML fill:#FFB6C1
    style LWL fill:#4682B4
    style BL fill:#696969
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Define the term "permissible length of compartment" and state its physical significance in ship subdivision design.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Permissible Length of Compartment (LPC)** is the maximum longitudinal length of any watertight compartment on a ship, determined as:

$$
L_{pc} = F \cdot L_f
$$

where $F$ is the **Factor of Subdivision** and $L_f$ is the **Floodable Length** at the deepest subdivision draft.

**Physical Significance:**
1. It represents the **upper bound** on compartment length to ensure the ship **does not sink below the margin line** when the compartment is fully flooded.
2. It enforces **damage stability** — by limiting the largest floodable volume, residual freeboard and righting levers remain within survivable limits.
3. It governs the **placement of every transverse watertight bulkhead** not otherwise locked by SOLAS positional rules (collision, after peak, machinery).

> **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1.5 marks for formula, 1 mark for floodable-length definition, 0.5 mark for damage stability significance]**

---

#### **Question 2** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**State SOLAS regulations for the position of the collision bulkhead with respect to the forward perpendicular (FP).**

**Model Answer:**

According to **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 11**, the collision bulkhead must be located at a distance $x$ from FP satisfying both:

1. **Lower Bound:** $x \ge 0.05L$ **OR** $x \ge 10 \text{ m}$, whichever is **greater**.
2. **Upper Bound:** $x \le 0.08L$ **OR** $x \le 0.05L + 10 \text{ m}$, whichever is **greater**, unless the Administration permits otherwise.

$$
\max(0.05L,\ 10 \text{ m}) \le x_{cb} \le \max(0.08L,\ 0.05L + 10 \text{ m})
$$

> **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark lower bound, 1 mark upper bound, 1 mark reference to SOLAS II-1/11]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Modified]`

A bulk carrier has the following particulars:
- **Length between perpendiculars:** $L = 220 \text{ m}$
- **Floodable length at deepest subdivision draft:** $L_f = 110 \text{ m}$
- **Factor of Subdivision (from SOLAS tables):** $F = 0.70$
- **Permeability of cargo holds:** $\mu = 0.70$

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:**
Calculate the Permissible Length of Compartment (LPC) and determine the **maximum number of equally-spaced forward cargo holds** the designer may use if each hold (after deducting bulkhead thicknesses) must be ≤ LPC. Assume the ship is required to have **three** forward holds, and compute whether this layout is **compliant**. Show all steps.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:**
Verify the **collision bulkhead location** for this ship. State the lower and upper bound positions per SOLAS, and confirm whether a collision bulkhead at **13.5 m from FP** is compliant. Also explain the **consequence of non-compliance**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute LPC:**

$$
L_{pc} = F \cdot L_f
$$

$$
L_{pc} = 0.70 \times 110 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L_{pc} = 77.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with three equally-spaced holds:**

If three forward cargo holds are arranged between the collision bulkhead and the forward machinery space bulkhead, the total available length is, say, $L_{avail} = 200 \text{ m}$ (illustrative). Each hold's mean length is:

$$
L_{hold} = \frac{L_{avail}}{3} = \frac{200}{3} = 66.67 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compliance check:**

$$
L_{hold} = 66.67 \text{ m} \le L_{pc} = 77.0 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Result:** The layout is **COMPLIANT**, with a margin of $77.0 - 66.67 = 10.33 \text{ m}$.

**[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks LPC formula, 2 marks substitution, 2 marks comparison & conclusion, 1 mark for noting the margin]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Lower bound (SOLAS Reg. II-1/11):**

$$
x_{cb,\ min} = \max(0.05L,\ 10 \text{ m})
$$

$$
x_{cb,\ min} = \max(0.05 \times 220,\ 10) = \max(11.0,\ 10) = 11.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Upper bound (SOLAS Reg. II-1/11):**

$$
x_{cb,\ max} = \max(0.08L,\ 0.05L + 10 \text{ m})
$$

$$
x_{cb,\ max} = \max(0.08 \times 220,\ 0.05 \times 220 + 10) = \max(17.6,\ 21.0) = 21.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compliance check for $x_{cb} = 13.5$ m:**

$$
11.0 \text{ m} \le 13.5 \text{ m} \le 21.0 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Result:** The collision bulkhead at 13.5 m abaft FP is **COMPLIANT** with SOLAS.

**Step 4 — Consequence of non-compliance:**

If the collision bulkhead were placed **closer to FP than 11.0 m**, the forward-most compartment (between FP and CB) would be too small to be a useful store/liquid-compartment, and the structural integrity in bow-collision scenarios could be compromised (the bow would breach the CB before energy absorption in the forepeak). If placed **farther than 21.0 m from FP**, the forepeak becomes excessively large — increasing floodable volume, and in collision scenarios, the forward compartment could flood, causing the ship to sink bows-first (progressive flooding). Both extremes **violate SOLAS** and invalidate the ship's Load Line and Passenger Ship Safety Certificates.

**[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks lower bound, 2 marks upper bound, 1 mark compliance check, 2 marks consequence discussion]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Modified]`

A general-cargo ship has:
- **Length:** $L = 150 \text{ m}$
- **Permeability of accommodation:** $\mu_{acc} = 0.95$
- **Permeability of machinery space:** $\mu_{mach} = 0.85$
- **Factor of Subdivision:** $F = 0.60$

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:**
Explain the **concept and significance of permeability ($\mu$)** in the floodable length calculation. Tabulate **standard SOLAS permeability values** for at least four distinct space types.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:**
A midship accommodation block of length $L_{acc} = 24 \text{ m}$ is bounded by watertight bulkheads. The local floodable length at the relevant draft is $L_f = 50 \text{ m}$. Check whether the accommodation block spacing is compliant with the factor of subdivision criterion.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Concept of Permeability:**

The **permeability $\mu$** of a space is the **ratio of the volume that can actually be occupied by water** to the **gross internal volume** of that space, when the space is flooded. It accounts for the volume taken up by machinery, stores, furniture, insulation, structural members, piping, and any pre-existing liquid in tanks.

$$
\mu = \frac{V_{floodable}}{V_{gross}}
$$

**Significance:**

1. **Realistic flooding volume:** $\mu$ ensures the calculation uses the *actual* volume of water ingress, not the geometric volume — preventing underestimation of flooding.
2. **Differentiates space types:** A dry cargo hold (loose grain, machinery) accepts far more water than a fully-flooded empty space; an oil tank is "tight" only if full.
3. **Critical input to $L_f$:** $L_f \propto 1/\mu$, so high-$\mu$ spaces (accommodation) require *shorter* permissible compartments.

**SOLAS Standard Permeability Values:**

| **Space Type** | **Permeability $\mu$** |
|---|---|
| Accommodation spaces | 0.95 |
| Machinery spaces | 0.85 |
| Dry cargo holds (general) | 0.70 |
| Dry cargo holds (empty, intended empty) | 0.60 |
| Tanks intended to be empty (e.g., cofferdams) | 0.95 |
| Tanks intended to be full (e.g., loaded oil/water) | 0.00 |

**[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks concept, 2 marks significance, 3 marks tabulation]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute the Permissible Length of Compartment:**

$$
L_{pc} = F \cdot L_f
$$

$$
L_{pc} = 0.60 \times 50 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L_{pc} = 30.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the actual accommodation block length:**

$$
L_{acc} = 24 \text{ m} \le L_{pc} = 30.0 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Result:** The accommodation block spacing is **COMPLIANT** with the factor of subdivision criterion. The bulkhead-to-bulkhead spacing of 24 m is within the 30 m permissible limit, with a margin of 6 m.

**Step 3 — Sensitivity Comment:**

Note that although $\mu$ does not appear directly in the *LPC* formula, it does appear in the **$L_f$ calculation** upstream. For accommodation ($\mu = 0.95$), the actual $L_f$ would have been computed using this high permeability, so a "30 m LPC" is already conservative for accommodation spaces. A 24 m accommodation block is therefore highly safe from a damage-stability standpoint.

**[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks LPC formula, 2 marks substitution, 2 marks compliance check, 1 mark engineering comment]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the inequality direction:** Students often write $L_{pc} \le L_{comp}$ instead of $L_{comp} \le L_{pc}$. **The actual compartment length must be ≤ the permissible length.**
> 2. **Forgetting to take the maximum of the two bounds** in collision bulkhead position. Both the $0.05L$ *and* the $10$ m terms must be computed, and the **maximum** selected — not the sum, not the average.
> 3. **Using $L_{oa}$ (length overall) instead of $L$ (length between perpendiculars).** SOLAS regulations are based on $L$ as defined in the Load Line Convention, **not** the designer's overall hull length.
> 4. **Ignoring permeability in $L_f$ derivation:** A student who writes $L_f$ without referencing $\mu$ will lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Failing to verify the rule of both bounds:** Even if the lower bound is satisfied, the upper bound of the collision bulkhead must also be checked. Marks are awarded for both checks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Spacing of Watertight Bulkheads**

- [x] **Watertight Bulkhead** = vertical structural partition, fully impervious to water at design pressure head.
- [x] **SOLAS-mandated position-locked bulkheads:** Collision, After Peak, Forward & Aft Machinery Space (Category A), Shaft Tunnel.
- [x] **Collision bulkhead location** must satisfy both lower AND upper bound: $\max(0.05L,\ 10\text{ m}) \le x_{cb} \le \max(0.08L,\ 0.05L + 10\text{ m})$.
- [x] **Factor of Subdivision (F)** is dimensionless, $0 \le F \le 1.0$, tabulated vs. $L$ and ship service.
- [x] **Floodable length $L_f$** = max compartment length that, when flooded, brings waterline exactly to **margin line** (76 mm below bulkhead deck at side).
- [x] **Permissible Length of Compartment (LPC):** $L_{pc} = F \cdot L_f$.
- [x] **Design Check:** $L_{comp} \le L_{pc}$ for every pair of adjacent transverse bulkheads.
- [x] **Permeability $\mu$** is critical: accommodation = 0.95, machinery = 0.85, dry cargo = 0.60–0.70, full tank = 0.
- [x] **Bulkhead Deck** = the uppermost deck to which transverse watertight bulkheads are carried watertight.
- [x] **Margin line** = the 76 mm clearance line — the **survival boundary** for damage stability.
- [x] **Three-step designer workflow:** Compute $L_f$ → Find $F$ → Compute $L_{pc}$ → Position bulkheads.
- [x] **Real ships use probabilistic damage stability** (SOLAS 2009 Annex) for final verification, but the LPC method remains the *initial design sizing tool*.
- [x] **Cost vs. safety trade-off:** bulkhead spacing is widened *only* up to the LPC limit; never beyond.
- [x] **"L" in all SOLAS formulas = Length as per the International Load Line Convention (1966), i.e., 96% of $L_{oa}$ for ships with $L_{oa} \le 150$ m, else the length from FP to the rudder post centerline for waterline lengths ≥ 97% of $L_{oa}$.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction of Watertight Bulkheads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Construction of Watertight Bulkheads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Watertight Bulkhead (WT Bulkhead):** A vertical structural partition made of plating and stiffeners, designed and constructed to prevent the passage of water under pressure head between adjacent compartments of a ship. According to SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) and Classification Society rules (IRS, LR, DNV, ABS), a watertight bulkhead must withstand a calculated pressure head on one side without leakage, ensuring subdivision of the hull for damage stability, structural continuity, and operational safety.

A watertight bulkhead is fundamentally a **transverse or longitudinal plate structure** running from the **inner bottom (or tank top) to the deck** (or between specific decks), welded continuously to the surrounding shell plating, decks, and adjacent bulkheads. The bulkhead plating is reinforced by **stiffeners and girders** to resist the hydrostatic and hydrodynamic pressure loads imposed by flooding or by adjacent compartment contents.

### 1.2 Types of Watertight Bulkheads (KTU Board Classification)

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU examinations, students must know the **location-based classification** of watertight bulkheads since questions are frequently asked on this.

| Type | Location | Primary Function |
|------|----------|------------------|
| **Collision Bulkhead** | Forward-most, typically 5–10% of Lpp from FP | Limits flooding after forward collision |
| **Engine Room Bulkhead** | Aft of machinery space | Protects engine room from flooding |
| **Boiler Room Bulkhead** | Around boiler spaces | Heat & watertight isolation |
| **Shaft Tunnel Bulkhead** | Around propeller shaft | Maintains tunnel integrity |
| **Cofferdam Bulkhead** | Between fuel oil tanks & other spaces | Prevents oil ingress |
| **Peak Bulkheads** | Forward peak & after peak | Limits fore/aft flooding |
| **Hold Bulkheads** | Between cargo holds | Cargo separation, stability |
| **Aft Peak Bulkhead** | Just forward of rudder trunk | Final subdivision barrier |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a multi-storey apartment building where each floor is divided into multiple rooms by brick walls. If a water pipe bursts in the kitchen, only that one room (compartment) gets flooded because the walls (bulkheads) prevent water from spreading. Now imagine this building is floating on water and could crack open — *you absolutely need these walls to be perfectly sealed and structurally strong*, otherwise the entire building sinks. That is exactly what a **watertight bulkhead** does for a ship. It acts as a **vertical "dam wall"** that keeps water in its own compartment, protecting the rest of the ship.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Maximum subdivision loadline (Load Line Convention)** = Freeboard line defining permissible flooding
- **Permeability (μ)** = Ratio of volume of water a space can hold to total volume (typically **0.60 for general cargo, 0.95 for machinery, 0.30 for stores/empty spaces**)
- **Permissible head of water** = Determined by **bulkhead location and loadline**
- **Steel density for design** = **7.85 t/m³** (mild steel)
- **Yield stress** = **235 N/mm²** for mild steel, **315 N/mm²** for HTS
- **Factor of Safety** = **1.0 to 1.5** as per Classification Society rules (already factored in design head)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Philosophy — The "Why" of Bulkhead Design

A watertight bulkhead must perform **four simultaneous roles**:

1. **Flooding barrier (Functional role):** Prevent water passage even at full design head.
2. **Structural diaphragm (Strength role):** Transfer shear and bending loads between fore and aft hull sections (acts like a deep girder).
3. **Boundary of compartments (Operational role):** Carry local loads from tank contents, cargo, machinery vibration.
4. **Damage stability element (Regulatory role):** Maintain the ship's survivability envelope per SOLAS Chapter II-1.

### 2.2 Components of a Watertight Bulkhead

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A complete watertight bulkhead consists of **Plating, Stiffeners, Girders, Edging Stiffeners, Sleeper Bars (where applicable), Welding Connections, and Penetrations (manholes/cables/pipes)**.

#### 2.2.1 Plating
- The plate steel skin which forms the watertight membrane.
- Thickness depends on **design pressure head, unsupported span, and material grade**.
- Two basic plate types: **Flat (single/double) plate** or **Corrugated plate**.

#### 2.2.2 Stiffeners
- **Vertical stiffeners (Main stiffeners):** Run between top and bottom girders.
- **Horizontal stiffeners:** Used in deep tanks or where vertical span is large.
- Section types: **Flat bar, bulb plate, L-angle, T-section**.

#### 2.2.3 Girders
- **Vertical girders (Main girders):** Support stiffeners laterally; transmit load to boundary structure.
- **Horizontal girders (Deck/bottom girders):** Placed at deck and bottom levels.

#### 2.2.4 Sleeper Bars / Collars / Doublers
- Doubling plates added at high-stress zones (around openings, connections to shell).

#### 2.2.5 Edge Connections
- The **critical junction**: bulkhead is connected by **continuous fillet weld** to the deck plating, bottom plating, and side shell plating.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **essential formula bank** for KTU Part-B numerical problems on watertight bulkheads.

| Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|-----------|---------|---------------|
| **Design head, h** | $h = d + s \times \text{(permeability factor)}$ | metres; $d$ = bulkhead depth, $s$ = super-stage factor |
| **Pressure on bulkhead** | $p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | $\rho$ = 1025 kg/m³ (seawater), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $p$ in **kN/m²** |
| **Allowable pressure on plate** | $p_{allow} = \dfrac{2.2 \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot t^2}{s \cdot \ell \cdot 10^6}$ | $t$ = plate thickness (mm), $s, \ell$ = short/long span (mm) |
| **Required plating thickness** | $t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{k_2 \cdot \sigma_y}}$ | mm; $k$ depends on aspect ratio |
| **Modular ratio (slenderness)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{s}{t} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}}$ | Dimensionless |
| **Stiffener section modulus** | $Z = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_{perm}}$ where $M = \dfrac{p \cdot s \cdot \ell^2}{8 \cdot 10^6}$ | $Z$ in cm³, $M$ in N·mm |
| **Stiffener spacing** | $s$ | mm (typically 600–900 mm) |
| **Corrugated bulkhead thickness** | $t_{corr} \approx 0.85 \times t_{flat}$ | Reduction factor (saves ~15% steel) |
| **Permeability (μ)** | $\mu = \dfrac{\text{Volume of water space can hold}}{\text{Total volume}}$ | Dimensionless, ranges **0.30–0.95** |
| **Bulkhead material density** | $\rho_{steel} = 7.85 \text{ t/m}^3$ | Mild steel reference |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions (must be remembered for KTU derivation questions):**
> - Edge of bulkhead is **simply supported** at deck, bottom, and side shell.
> - **Free edges** of partial bulkheads (e.g., swash bulkhead) are **unsupported**.
> - Plate is treated as a **thin rectangular plate** under uniform pressure.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Watertight bulkheads are an **essential regulatory requirement** under **SOLAS 1974 (Chapter II-1, Reg. 10–20)**, **MARPOL Annex I (oil tanker double-hull)**, and **Classification Society rules (IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, BV, KR)**. Beyond regulation:

- **Damage stability:** Ensures the ship remains afloat after flooding of 1 or 2 compartments.
- **Cargo integrity:** Prevents shifting liquids (oil/water) from destabilising the ship.
- **Fire protection:** Acts as a fire boundary between high-risk zones.
- **Cost of steel:** In a large tanker, **bulkhead steel represents 3–5% of total hull steel weight**, making efficiency (corrugated designs) important.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Plate Thickness for a Watertight Bulkhead (Plate Analysis)

Consider a rectangular plate panel of a watertight bulkhead, simply supported on all four edges, subjected to a uniformly distributed pressure $p$. Let the **shorter span** be $s$ and the **longer span** be $\ell$ (where $\ell \geq s$).

#### Step 1 — Define the aspect ratio

The aspect ratio is defined as:

$$
\alpha = \dfrac{s}{\ell} \quad \text{where} \quad 0 < \alpha \leq 1
$$

This ratio is essential because it determines whether the plate bends primarily in the **short-span direction** ($\alpha$ small) or is approximately **isotropic** ($\alpha \approx 1$).

#### Step 2 — Identify the design pressure head

The hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of the bulkhead is the maximum. If the bulkhead extends from the **bottom of the inner hull to the load waterline**, with a super-stage factor (often applied to collision bulkhead) $f$ (typically **1.0 to 1.2**):

$$
h = f \cdot d
$$

where $d$ = vertical height of the bulkhead (m).

Then the **pressure at the deepest point**:

$$
p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h
$$

For $p$ in kN/m² with $h$ in metres and $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³:

$$
p = 1.025 \times 9.81 \times h \approx 10.05 \cdot h \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

#### Step 3 — Maximum bending moment in a simply supported plate

The maximum bending moment per unit width (in N·mm) is given by the elastic plate theory:

$$
M = \dfrac{k \cdot p \cdot s^2}{10^6} \text{ N·mm/mm}
$$

where $k$ is a coefficient depending on $\alpha$ (from the Bureau of Ships / CSR-B rules). For $\alpha = 1.0$ (square panel), $k \approx 0.308$; for $\alpha = 0.5$, $k \approx 0.450$. The value of $k$ **increases** as the panel becomes more elongated (one-way bending).

#### Step 4 — Relate moment to required thickness

For a plate of unit width, the section modulus is:

$$
Z = \dfrac{t^2}{6} \text{ (per mm width, in mm}^3\text{)}
$$

The required moment of resistance must satisfy:

$$
\sigma_{allow} \cdot Z \geq M
$$

Substituting $Z = t^2/6$:

$$
\sigma_{allow} \cdot \dfrac{t^2}{6} \geq \dfrac{k \cdot p \cdot s^2}{10^6}
$$

#### Step 5 — Solve for plate thickness $t$

Rearranging for $t$:

$$
t \geq s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \cdot k \cdot p}{10^6 \cdot \sigma_{allow}}}
$$

Or equivalently (in KTU board-friendly form):

$$
\boxed{\,t = s \cdot k_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{k_2 \cdot \sigma_y}} \text{ mm}\,}
$$

where:
- $k_1$ = aspect-ratio dependent coefficient (often consolidated),
- $k_2$ = slenderness correction factor (typically 1.0 for thick plates, decreasing for thin plates),
- $p$ = design pressure in N/mm²,
- $\sigma_y$ = yield stress of the plate material in N/mm².

#### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Given:** A watertight bulkhead is **8 m high**, fitted on a vessel with a **0.6 m super-stage factor**. Bulkhead plating is **mild steel** with $\sigma_y = 235$ N/mm². The plate panel is simply supported with **shorter span s = 700 mm** and **aspect ratio α = 0.7**. Assume $k_1 = 0.65$ and $k_2 = 1.0$. Find the required plate thickness.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Calculate design head:**
$$
h = f \cdot d = 0.6 \times 8 = 4.8 \text{ m}
$$

**Step B — Calculate design pressure:**
$$
p = 10.05 \times 4.8 = 48.24 \text{ kN/m}^2 = 0.04824 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step C — Apply the thickness formula:**
$$
t = s \cdot k_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{k_2 \cdot \sigma_y}}
$$

$$
t = 700 \times 0.65 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{0.04824}{1.0 \times 235}}
$$

**Step D — Evaluate the square root:**
$$
\dfrac{0.04824}{235} = 0.0002053
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.0002053} = 0.01433
$$

**Step E — Compute the thickness:**
$$
t = 700 \times 0.65 \times 0.01433 = 455 \times 0.01433 = 6.52 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step F — Round up to standard plate thickness:**
$$
\boxed{t = 7 \text{ mm (standard size)}}
$$

This is a typical KTU numerical where the **final answer is rounded to the next higher standard plate thickness** (6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 14, 16 mm etc.).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Types of Bulkhead Construction

| Feature | Flat Plate (Single Skin) | Flat Plate (Double Skin) | Corrugated Plate |
|---------|--------------------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| **Steel weight** | High (lots of stiffeners) | Medium-High | **Lowest** |
| **Fabrication cost** | High | High (more welding) | Medium |
| **Inspection ease** | Easy on one side | Difficult (both sides) | Easy both sides |
| **Corrosion trap** | Some | **High (between plates)** | None |
| **Stiffening needed** | Extensive (both sides) | Required | **Minimal/None** |
| **Used in** | Small ships, peak bulkheads | **Tanker engine rooms** | **Modern tankers, bulk carriers** |
| **Watertightness** | Excellent (if welded) | Excellent | Excellent (slightly tricky at corners) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams illustrate the **construction architecture, load path, and component layout** of a typical watertight bulkhead. These are block-level functional flow diagrams adapted for clarity in lieu of physical drawings.

### 4.1 Bulkhead Construction — Component Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SH1[Ship Hull Outer Shell]
    SH2[Deck Plating Top Boundary]
    SH3[Inner Bottom / Tank Top]
    SH4[Side Shell Plating]

    WT1[Watertight Bulkhead Assembly]
    PL1[Plate Plating - Main Membrane]
    CR1[Corrugation Profile if used]
    VS1[Vertical Stiffeners]
    HS1[Horizontal Stiffeners]
    VG1[Vertical Girders]
    HG1[Horizontal Girders Main Deck Line]
    HG2[Horizontal Girders Bottom Line]
    ED1[Edging Stiffener at Connections]
    DB1[Doubling Plate at Openings]
    SL1[Sleeve Pipes for Cable Pipe Penetration]
    MH1[Manhole Cover WT Type]

    CON1[Continuous Fillet Welds]
    CON2[Boundary Connections]

    TST1[Hose Test Watertightness]
    TST2[Head Test]
    TST3[Ultrasonic Weld Test]

    SH1 --> CON2
    SH2 --> CON2
    SH3 --> CON2
    SH4 --> CON2
    CON2 --> WT1

    WT1 --> PL1
    PL1 --> CR1
    PL1 --> VS1
    PL1 --> HS1
    VS1 --> VG1
    HS1 --> HG1
    HS1 --> HG2
    PL1 --> ED1
    PL1 --> DB1
    PL1 --> SL1
    PL1 --> MH1

    WT1 --> CON1
    WT1 --> TST1
    WT1 --> TST2
    WT1 --> TST3
```

### 4.2 Load Path Flow Diagram — How Pressure Travels Through Bulkhead

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Flooding Water Hydrostatic Pressure]
    P2[Sloshing Impact Load]
    P3[Cargo or Oil Static Pressure]

    A1[Step 1 Pressure Acts on Plating]
    A2[Step 2 Plating Bends Spans Between Stiffeners]
    A3[Step 3 Stiffeners Act as Beams Transfer Load to Girders]
    A4[Step 4 Girders Transfer Load to Boundary Structure]
    A5[Step 5 Boundary Girders Distribute Load to Deck Bottom Side Shell]
    A6[Step 6 Load Dissipates into Global Hull Girder]

    P1 --> A1
    P2 --> A1
    P3 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
```

### 4.3 Watertight Bulkhead Manufacturing & Testing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Stage 1 Material Receipt MS or HTS Plate]
    S2[Stage 2 Cutting and Shaping Plates to Size]
    S3[Stage 3 Welding Stiffeners to Plate Sub Assembly]
    S4[Stage 4 Erection into Hull Position]
    S5[Stage 5 Connection Welding to Deck Bottom Side Shell]
    S6[Stage 6 NDT of Welds Ultrasonic Radiographic]
    S7[Stage 7 Hose Test Watertightness Verification]
    S8[Stage 8 Final Inspection and Sign Off by Class Surveyor]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
```

### 4.4 Classification of Watertight Bulkheads by Location

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[Watertight Bulkheads]

    ROOT --> A1[Collision Bulkhead]
    ROOT --> A2[Engine Room Bulkheads]
    ROOT --> A3[Boiler Room Bulkheads]
    ROOT --> A4[Cofferdam Bulkheads]
    ROOT --> A5[Peak Bulkheads Forward and Aft]
    ROOT --> A6[Hold Bulkheads]
    ROOT --> A7[Shaft Tunnel Bulkhead]

    A1 --> A1A[Forward most 5 to 10 percent Lpp]
    A1 --> A1B[Highest structural integrity required]

    A4 --> A4A[Separates fuel oil tanks from other spaces]
    A4 --> A4B[Empty space or filled with inert material]

    A7 --> A7A[Encloses shaft tunnel above floor]
    A7 --> A7B[Watertight connection to machinery spaces]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define a watertight bulkhead. List any four types of watertight bulkheads found in a typical merchant ship.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **watertight bulkhead** is a vertical plate-and-stiffener structural partition in a ship designed to prevent the passage of water between adjacent compartments, ensuring damage stability and operational safety as required by SOLAS.

**Four Types:**
1. **Collision Bulkhead** — located at the forward end of the ship.
2. **Engine Room Bulkhead** — encloses the machinery space.
3. **Boiler Room Bulkhead** — encloses boiler spaces (for fire and water isolation).
4. **Cofferdam Bulkhead** — separates fuel oil tanks from accommodation/machinery spaces.

*(Any 4 of: Peak, Hold, Shaft Tunnel, After Peak, Wash Bulkhead.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State the standard permeability values for (a) machinery spaces, (b) general cargo holds, and (c) empty/void spaces.**

**Model Answer:**

| Compartment | Permeability (μ) |
|-------------|------------------|
| Machinery spaces | **0.85 – 0.95** |
| General cargo holds | **0.60 – 0.70** |
| Empty/void/stores | **0.30 – 0.50** |

Permeability is the ratio of the volume of water a space can hold to its total geometric volume. It is used in the **damage stability calculations** of SOLAS to determine the **flooded volume** and hence the **heeling and trimming moments** after hull breach.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **construction of a corrugated watertight bulkhead**. Discuss its advantages over a flat plate bulkhead. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **corrugated watertight bulkhead** is built from plates that are **cold-formed into a wave or trapezoidal profile** before being erected. The corrugations act as **integrated stiffeners**, eliminating the need for separate stiffener members on either side.

**Construction Steps:**
1. **Plate selection** — Mild steel (MS) or high tensile steel (HTS), typically 6–12 mm thick.
2. **Cold forming** — The flat plate is passed through rolling machines to obtain the corrugated shape (most common: **trapezoidal** profile of 150 mm pitch × 100 mm depth, or 200 × 150).
3. **Cutting to size** — The corrugated sheet is cut to the exact bulkhead dimensions.
4. **Edge preparation** — Edges of the corrugated plate are bevelled for welding.
5. **Erection** — The corrugated panel is hoisted into position between the deck and inner bottom.
6. **Boundary welding** — Continuous double fillet welds are applied along the deck, bottom, and side shell connections.
7. **Stiffener attachment at openings** — Small flat-bar stiffeners are added around manholes and penetrations.
8. **Testing** — Hose test for watertightness; UT/RT for welds.

**Advantages over Flat Plate Bulkhead:**

| Aspect | Corrugated | Flat Plate |
|--------|-----------|------------|
| **Steel weight** | Lighter (15–25% saving) | Heavier |
| **Stiffeners required** | None / minimal | Extensive |
| **Welding cost** | Less | More |
| **Corrosion traps** | None (open profile) | Hidden behind stiffeners |
| **Inspection** | Both sides accessible | Difficult if double-skinned |
| **Load-bearing capacity** | Excellent in both directions | Anisotropic |
| **Aesthetic finish** | Better | Needs more finishing |

---

**Q. (b)** A watertight bulkhead is **9 m high**, fitted on a vessel with a **super-stage factor of 0.55**. The bulkhead panel is **simply supported on all four sides**, with shorter span **s = 750 mm** and aspect ratio α = 0.7. The material is **mild steel with σ_y = 235 N/mm²**. Use $k_1 = 0.65$, $k_2 = 1.0$. Find the **required plate thickness** and round up to standard size. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Design head:**
$$
h = f \cdot d = 0.55 \times 9 = 4.95 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Design pressure:**
$$
p = 10.05 \times 4.95 = 49.75 \text{ kN/m}^2 = 0.04975 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Thickness formula:**
$$
t = s \cdot k_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{k_2 \cdot \sigma_y}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**
$$
t = 750 \times 0.65 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{0.04975}{1.0 \times 235}}
$$

**Step 5 — Inner calculation:**
$$
\dfrac{0.04975}{235} = 0.0002117
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.0002117} = 0.01455
$$

**Step 6 — Final result:**
$$
t = 487.5 \times 0.01455 = 7.09 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Round up to standard plate size:**
$$
\boxed{t = 8 \text{ mm (standard)}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Plate Thickness Problems):**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** — Pressure must be in **N/mm²** (not kN/m²) before applying the formula. This alone causes 2–3 mark deductions.
> 2. **Not rounding UP to standard thickness** — Students often give 7.09 mm and stop. Always round **up** to next standard (6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 12, 14, 16 mm).
> 3. **Forgetting the super-stage factor** in design head calculation.
> 4. **Confusing s (shorter span) and ℓ (longer span)** — $s$ is always the **shorter** one.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **method of construction of a flat plate watertight bulkhead with stiffeners**. List its main components with a sketch. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **flat plate watertight bulkhead** is the conventional and most widely used bulkhead type, especially in older vessels and small ships. The plate remains **flat** (uncorrugated), and stiffness is achieved by adding **longitudinal and transverse stiffeners** on one or both sides.

**Construction Sequence:**

1. **Fabrication of sub-assemblies** — Plate panels are welded with stiffeners and girders in the workshop.
2. **Stiffener attachment** — Vertical stiffeners (flat bars, bulb plates, or T-bars) are welded at intervals (typically **600–900 mm**) running full height.
3. **Girder attachment** — Horizontal girders (main girders) are fitted at deck and bottom levels to act as end supports for the stiffeners.
4. **Doubling plates** — Added at the boundaries (top, bottom, side) for high-stress transfer.
5. **Erection** — The complete panel is hoisted into position.
6. **Boundary welding** — Continuous fillet welds to deck, inner bottom, and side shell.
7. **Penetration treatment** — Manholes, cable sleeves, pipe penetrations are sealed with collars and gaskets.

**Main Components:**
- Plate plating (5–10 mm)
- Vertical stiffeners (FB 80×10, BP 100×7, etc.)
- Horizontal girders (T-sections 200×100 + plate 100×10)
- Doubling plates at boundaries
- Sleeve pipes and manhole covers (WT type)

---

**Q. (b)** Describe the **testing procedures for watertight bulkheads** during and after construction. Why is each test important? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> All tests are conducted per **Classification Society rules** and **SOLAS Reg. 11-1/11-2**. Tests must be passed before the vessel is permitted to sail.

**1. Hose Test**
- A high-pressure water jet (typically **100 kPa** pressure) is sprayed on all welded joints from a distance of **1.5 m**.
- **Purpose:** Detects gross leakage and weld porosity.
- **Conducted on:** All boundary welds and connections.

**2. Head Test (Hydrostatic Test)**
- The compartment on one side is filled with water to the **deepest load waterline** (or to the bulkhead head height).
- Bulkhead is observed on the **dry side for 24 hours** for seepage or sweating.
- **Purpose:** Confirms integrity under full design head — the **most stringent test**.

**3. Chalk Test (Sounding Test)**
- Performed on compartments that cannot be flooded (e.g., adjacent to machinery).
- Chalk paste is applied on one side; compressed air at **20 kPa** is applied on the other.
- Bubbles indicate leakage points.
- **Purpose:** Localised leak detection without flooding the space.

**4. Ultrasonic Testing (UT) and Radiographic Testing (RT)**
- Non-destructive testing of **all critical welds** (boundary welds, around penetrations).
- **Purpose:** Detects internal flaws, lack of fusion, porosity, cracks.

**5. Vacuum Test**
- Used on **flat plate bulkheads** where flooding the other side is impractical.
- A vacuum box is placed over the weld; if vacuum drops, there is a leak.
- **Purpose:** Quick local-area test on long weld seams.

**6. Final Visual and Survey**
- Class surveyor verifies all penetrations, manholes, doors, and connections.
- Confirms alignment of stiffeners, correctness of stiffener section, no visible damage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Mistakes on Testing Questions:**
> 1. Students often mention only **"hose test"** and forget other tests — list **at least 3** for full marks.
> 2. **Pressures and durations** must be mentioned (e.g., 100 kPa for hose test, 24 hours for head test).
> 3. **Head test is the gold standard** — always state it as the ultimate confirmation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must be memorised before the KTU exam):**

- ✅ Watertight bulkhead = vertical **plate + stiffener + girder** structural partition welded continuously to deck, bottom, and side shell.
- ✅ **Collision bulkhead** is the most critical; cannot be opened and has no doors below the bulkhead deck.
- ✅ **Cofferdam** is an empty narrow space separating fuel oil tanks from other compartments (regulatory safety requirement).
- ✅ **Corrugated bulkheads** save **15–25% steel weight** and eliminate most stiffeners — preferred in modern tankers.
- ✅ **Design head** = Super-stage factor × bulkhead depth; maximum at the **bottom of the bulkhead**.
- ✅ Plate thickness formula: $t = s \cdot k_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{k_2 \cdot \sigma_y}}$ — remember unit conversion **N/mm²**.
- ✅ **Permeability** = ratio of water-space-volume to total volume. Machinery ≈ 0.95, cargo ≈ 0.60, voids ≈ 0.30.
- ✅ **Testing sequence:** Visual → UT/RT → Hose → Head → Class surveyor sign-off.
- ✅ **Boundary welds** are **continuous double fillet** — the most critical structural feature.
- ✅ Watertight bulkheads contribute to **damage stability** — ship must survive flooding of 1 or more compartments per SOLAS.
- ✅ **Penetrations** must be **minimal**; every pipe/cable passes through a sleeve with gasket; manholes have WT covers dogged down.
- ✅ **Material:** MS (σ_y = 235 N/mm²) for most; HTS (σ_y = 315 N/mm²) for high-stress zones.
- ✅ Bulkhead **acts as a deep girder** transferring longitudinal shear between hull sections.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Tip for KTU Exam:** Always draw a **labelled sketch** of the bulkhead cross-section showing plate, stiffener, girder, and boundary welds. Even a rough sketch earns 2–3 easy marks in Part-B answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing of Watertight Bulkheads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Testing of Watertight Bulkheads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Testing of Watertight Bulkheads

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **watertight bulkhead** is a vertical plate-type transverse or longitudinal structural division in a ship's hull, designed to prevent the passage of water between adjacent compartments under operational and flooding conditions. Once fabricated and erected, every watertight bulkhead **must be subjected to a non-destructive integrity test** — historically and per the current **IACS Unified Requirements (UR Z17 / Z23.1)** and **SOLAS Chapter II-1** — to confirm that it will neither leak nor structurally fail when the ship is exposed to hydrostatic heads it may experience during its service life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — OENST614):**
> Testing of watertight bulkheads is a mandatory Module-3 deliverable. The expected competency is the ability to *prescribe the test head for a given bulkhead position, identify the test medium (water/air), state permissible leakage rates, and interpret the test certificate*.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a row of glass aquariums stacked side by side inside a building. Before declaring the building "flood-proof," the engineer must fill each aquarium with water and verify that the **glass walls do not leak, sweat, or crack**. The *higher* the aquarium sits in the building, the *more pressure* the water exerts on the lower walls — so the engineer must fill each tank to a **height (head) equal to the worst case the wall will ever see in service**.

A watertight bulkhead in a ship behaves exactly the same way:

- The **bulkhead** is the wall.
- The **compartment** on one side is the aquarium.
- The **test head** is the water height the engineer must pour in.
- The **leakage check** is the "look for drips" test.

### 1.3 Governing Standards & Code References

| Code / Standard | Issuing Body | Scope of Application |
|---|---|---|
| SOLAS Reg. II-1/11, 12, 14, 18 | IMO | Statutory minimum test requirements |
| IACS UR Z17 | IACS | Unified procedural rules for testing watertight bulkheads |
| IACS UR Z23.1 | IACS | Tightness testing of hatches, doors, and closing appliances |
| Classification Rules (e.g., DNV, LR, ABS, BV) | Individual Class Societies | Detailed survey procedures and acceptance criteria |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Bulkhead Testing:**
> *The test head applied to a bulkhead must always be equal to, or greater than, the maximum hydrostatic head the bulkhead will experience in the worst credible operational or flooding condition.* The test is therefore a **proof-load**, not a service load.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Bulkheads by Position

Understanding the test head requires us to first **classify** every bulkhead by its location in the hull, because the test head depends entirely on the worst-case water column acting on that bulkhead.

| Bulkhead Type | Definition | Typical Service Head |
|---|---|---|
| **Collision Bulkhead (CB)** | Forward-most WT bulkhead (Reg. II-1/11) | Keel → deepest subdivision load line |
| **Engine-room Aft Peak Bulkhead** | Aft of machinery space | Keel → bulkhead deck |
| **Watertight Floor (Wet) Bulkhead** | Engine-room transverse divisions | Top of floor → load line |
| **Dry-Compartment Bulkhead** | Holds between cargo spaces | Light side → heavy side flooding |
| **Deep-Tank Bulkhead** | Forms boundary of deep tanks, ballast tanks | Top of overflow / filling line |
| **Cofferdam Bulkhead** | Between fuel oil and other spaces | Highest adjacent tank top |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hydrostatic Pressure Principle

A watertight bulkhead resists water pressure exactly as does a vertical retaining wall. The **gauge pressure** at any point on the bulkhead is governed by:

$$p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

where:

- $p$ = hydrostatic pressure in **Pascals (Pa)**,
- $\rho$ = density of water ($\approx \mathbf{1025\ kg/m^3}$ for seawater, $\mathbf{1000\ kg/m^3}$ for fresh water),
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = $\mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$,
- $h$ = vertical height (head) of water in **metres (m)**.

The **equivalent head** expressed in metres of water column is therefore the governing design parameter. In marine practice we use the simpler:

$$H_{test}\ (\text{m of water}) = h_{WL} + 1.0\ \text{m}$$

The extra **1.0 m margin** is the **safety margin** required by IACS UR Z17 and SOLAS to account for:

1. Dynamic ship motions (slamming, rolling) momentarily raising effective water height.
2. Settlement, trim, and squat in real sea conditions.
3. Future permissible loading variations.

> [!NOTE]
> The classical engineering unit **"metres of water column" (mWC)** is numerically equal to the head $h$ (in m) when $\rho = 1000\ kg/m^3$ and $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$. This is the standard unit used in shipyards worldwide and the one KTU expects in Part-B answers.

## 2.2 Test Head Prescriptions per IACS UR Z17

The table below is the **single most important KTU 2024 reference** for this topic. Memorise it.

| # | Bulkhead Location | Test Head $H_{test}$ to be Applied |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Below deepest subdivision load line** (e.g., collision bulkhead) | From the **keel** to a level **1.0 m above the deepest subdivision load line** |
| 2 | **Above deepest subdivision load line, but below bulkhead deck** | From the **lower edge of any opening** in the bulkhead to a level **1.0 m above deepest subdivision load line** |
| 3 | **Above bulkhead deck** (freeing-port / deck regions) | To a level of **1.0 m above bulkhead deck** |
| 4 | **Deep-tank bulkhead** | Top of **overflow / highest filling line**, or to **1.0 m above top of tank** (whichever is higher) |
| 5 | **Cofferdam bulkhead** | **1.0 m above** the top of the highest adjacent tank |
| 6 | **Shaft tunnel bulkhead** | To a head of water equal to the **load line**, or **1.0 m above top of tunnel** |
| 7 | **Hose-testable bulkhead (above waterline)** | **Hose test only** — no hydrostatic fill required |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the lower edge of opening for Rule 2?**
> Because in a damaged condition, water can enter a compartment through any opening (door, manhole, vent) located in the bulkhead itself or in a deck above. The water column above that opening's lower edge is therefore the realistic worst case the bulkhead must resist.

## 2.3 Methods of Testing

Three principal methods are sanctioned by IACS UR Z17:

### 2.3.1 Hydrostatic (Flood) Test

- **Medium:** Sea or fresh water.
- **Procedure:** One side of bulkhead is sealed, then filled progressively in stages.
- **Stage-rise protocol:** Fill in increments, inspect at each stage, then top up to the full prescribed head.
- **Hold time:** Typically **30 minutes** at full head.
- **Acceptance:** No visible leakage on the dry side, no sweating at welded joints, no permanent deformation.
- **Used for:** All bulkheads whose lower edge lies at or below the load line.

### 2.3.2 Hose Test

- **Medium:** Pressurised water jet.
- **Procedure:** A hose of $\geq$ **12.5 mm** bore delivers water at a pressure of **$\geq$ 0.2 MPa** from a distance of **$\leq$ 1.5 m** along the bulkhead surface.
- **Used for:** Bulkheads above the load line where flooding is impossible (Reg. UR Z17 para 3.3).

### 2.3.3 Air (Pneumatic) Test

- **Medium:** Compressed air or inert gas.
- **Procedure:** Seal both sides, pressurise to a low gauge pressure (typically **0.02–0.05 MPa**), then apply soapy water to welded joints.
- **Used for:** Spaces where water is inadmissible (fuel-oil tanks, lubricating-oil tanks, cofferdams on chemical carriers).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** All numerical Part-B problems on this topic reduce to one of the two formulas below. Practise them.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H_{test} = h_{keel\ to\ WL} + 1.0$ | Test head for sub-WL bulkhead | **m of water** |
| 2 | $H_{test} = h_{lower\ edge\ of\ opening} + 1.0$ | Test head for partially submerged bulkhead | **m of water** |
| 3 | $p = \rho g h$ | Pressure at base of test water column | **Pa** |
| 4 | $p_{kPa} = 9.81 \cdot h_{m}$ | Quick conversion (fresh water) | **kPa** |
| 5 | $Q_{allow} = \dfrac{V_{compartment}}{T}$ | Permissible leakage rate (rule of thumb) | **L/h** |
| 6 | $H_{sea} = H_{fresh} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{fresh}}{\rho_{sea}} = 0.976 \cdot H_{fresh}$ | Equivalent sea-water head | **m** |

> **Note on Table:** In every formula above, $h$ denotes the **vertical head in metres**, NOT a numerical pressure. The vertical line notation $\vert x \vert$ is replaced here by writing "head in metres" explicitly to preserve KTU table rendering.

## 2.5 Permissible Leakage Acceptance Criteria

| Leakage Class | Definition | KTU-Expected Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Zero leakage** | No visible droplets or weeping at welded joints | **Pass — full certificate** |
| **Sweating** | Continuous moisture film, no discrete drops | Conditional — to be re-tested after repair |
| **Dripping** | Discrete drops falling from joint | **Fail — repair and re-test** |
| **Streaming** | Continuous water flow from any point | **Fail — structural concern, full re-fabrication** |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivations (Model Solutions)

### 3.1.1 Sample Problem 1 — Collision Bulkhead Test Head

**Problem (KTU Model):**
A bulk carrier has a moulded depth of 18.0 m. The collision bulkhead is located at frame 5, with its lower edge at the **inner bottom (keel level)**. The deepest subdivision load line is at a draft of 12.5 m. Calculate the **prescribed test head** and the **gauge pressure at the keel** during the test.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing rule.**
The collision bulkhead lies entirely below the deepest subdivision load line. Hence IACS UR Z17 Row 1 applies:

$$H_{test} = h_{keel\ to\ WL} + 1.0\ m$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the draft.**
The water column from the keel to the load line equals the ship's **draft** at that load line condition.

$$h_{keel\ to\ WL} = 12.5\ m$$

**Step 3 — Add the safety margin.**

$$H_{test} = 12.5 + 1.0 = 13.5\ m\ of\ water$$

**Step 4 — Compute the pressure at the keel during the test.**

$$p_{keel} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H_{test}$$

For seawater ($\rho = 1025\ kg/m^3$):

$$p_{keel} = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 13.5$$

$$p_{keel} = 1.025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 13.5 \times 10^{3}\ \text{Pa}$$

Performing the multiplication step by step:

$$\begin{aligned}
9.81 \cdot 13.5 &= 132.435\ \text{N/kg·m} \\
1.025 \cdot 132.435 &= 135.746\ \text{kN/m}^{2} \\
p_{keel} &= 135.75\ \text{kPa}\ \ (\text{approx.}) 
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to bar for shipyard use (optional).**

$$p_{keel} = \dfrac{135.75}{100} = 1.3575\ \text{bar}$$

**Final Answer:**
$H_{test} = \mathbf{13.5\ m\ of\ water}$, equivalent to a keel pressure of $\mathbf{135.75\ kPa}\ (\approx \mathbf{1.36\ bar})$.

---

### 3.1.2 Sample Problem 2 — Bulkhead with Openings Above Load Line

**Problem (KTU Model):**
A watertight bulkhead separating a forward cargo hold from the engine room is partly submerged. The lowest edge of an access door cut into the bulkhead is at a height of **8.0 m above the keel**. The deepest subdivision load line is at a draft of **10.0 m**. Determine the test head to be applied.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing rule.**
Since the bulkhead is partly above the load line and the door's lower edge is below the load line, IACS UR Z17 Row 2 applies:

$$H_{test} = h_{lower\ edge\ of\ opening} + 1.0\ m$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the height of the door's lower edge.**

$$H_{test} = 8.0 + 1.0 = 9.0\ m\ of\ water$$

**Step 3 — Pressure at the lower edge of the door during test (informational).**

$$\begin{aligned}
p_{door\ edge} &= \rho \cdot g \cdot H_{test} \\
&= 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 9.0 \\
&= 88.29\ \text{kPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Pressure at the keel of this bulkhead (informational, if its bottom is at keel).**
If the bulkhead's bottom plate sits on the keel:

$$h_{keel\ to\ door\ lower\ edge} = 8.0\ m$$

Pressure at keel = $1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (8.0 + 9.0) = 166.77\ kPa$ (this is the maximum stress point).

**Final Answer:**
$H_{test} = \mathbf{9.0\ m\ of\ water}$.

---

### 3.1.3 Sample Problem 3 — Deep Tank Bulkhead

**Problem (KTU Model):**
A deep tank extends from the inner bottom (keel level) to a height of 12.0 m. Its overflow pipe terminates at 11.5 m. Calculate the test head per IACS UR Z17.

**Step 1 — Apply Rule 4.**
Test head = higher of:
- Top of overflow = **11.5 m**
- Top of tank + 1.0 m = $12.0 + 1.0 = 13.0\ m$

**Step 2 — Select the higher value.**

$$H_{test} = \max(11.5,\ 13.0) = 13.0\ m\ of\ water$$

**Step 3 — Pressure at the inner bottom of the tank during test.**

$$p = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 13.0 = 127.53\ kPa$$

**Final Answer:**
$H_{test} = \mathbf{13.0\ m\ of\ water}$, $p_{bottom} = \mathbf{127.53\ kPa}$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — How to Choose the Test Method

The KTU examiner often asks: *"For this given bulkhead, what type of test will you prescribe, and why?"* The decision matrix below is the correct analytical response.

| Bulkhead Position (relative to WL) | Recommended Test | Reasoning | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Entirely below WL | **Hydrostatic fill** | Bulkhead experiences full hydrostatic head in service | UR Z17 §3.1 |
| Top edge below WL, bottom above WL (rare) | **Hydrostatic fill** | Worst case is full flooding | UR Z17 §3.1 |
