## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023] Define a "swash bulkhead" and explain its purpose in tank design.**

**Model Answer:**

A **swash bulkhead** (also called a **wash bulkhead**) is a perforated or partial-height longitudinal bulkhead fitted within a tank (typically a ballast or cargo tank) to **reduce the effect of liquid surging (sloshing) on ship stability**.

**Purposes:**
1. **Reduces free surface effect** — by compartmentalizing the liquid surface, it limits the moment of inertia of the free liquid.
2. **Damps sloshing** — breaks up the natural frequency resonance of the liquid in the tank.
3. **Improves roll damping** — critical during partially-filled conditions.
4. **Reduces impact pressures** on tank boundaries during heavy rolling.

Typical design: vertically corrugated plate with **lightening holes** (50–60% opening) covering 60–80% of the tank length.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks purposes)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] What are the standard tank testing methods prescribed by IACS?**

**Model Answer:**

The standard IACS / Classification Society tank testing methods are:

1. **Hydraulic Test** — filling the tank with water to a level 0.3 m below the overflow/vent and checking for leaks. Most common and reliable.
2. **Hose Test** — spraying water at 0.2 MPa pressure on welds, seams, and connections.
3. **Air Test** — pressurizing with 0.02 MPa air and checking soap-bubble formation at joints.
4. **Vacuum Test** — applying vacuum to flat surfaces only (not for corrugated or curved).

*(3 marks: 4 methods listed = 0.75 mark each, brief explanation = bonus)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Corrugated Bulkhead Design**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **constructional details of a corrugated tank bulkhead** used in a seawater ballast tank of a bulk carrier. Discuss the advantages of corrugation over flat-stiffened construction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **corrugated bulkhead** in a double-bottom ballast tank has the following data:
- Tank length = 8 m, tank height = 4 m, breadth = 5 m
- Liquid: seawater ($\rho$ = 1025 kg/m³)
- Corrugation pitch $P$ = 350 mm, depth $d$ = 120 mm
- Plate thickness $t$ = 8 mm
- Corrugation is simply supported at top and bottom

Calculate the **maximum bending stress** in the corrugation wall. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Constructional Details of Corrugated Bulkhead:**

A corrugated bulkhead is fabricated from a single plate formed into a series of **alternating ridges (troughs and webs)** running vertically. Typical configuration:

- **Profile:** Trapezoidal (most common) or sinusoidal
- **Pitch (P):** 300–400 mm
- **Depth (d):** 100–150 mm
- **Thickness (t):** 6–12 mm
- **End connections:** Slot-welded to deck/inner bottom (allows for thermal expansion)
- **Manhole:** Reinforced oval opening with doubler plate
- **Stiffeners:** Usually NOT required as corrugation provides stiffness
- **Tripping brackets:** At every 4th corrugation at ends

**Sketch (described):**
> Vertical lines showing alternating trapezoidal corrugations from inner bottom to deck, with a manhole in the centre, edge stiffeners at top and bottom, and slot-welded connections.

**Advantages of Corrugation:**
1. **Higher strength-to-weight ratio** — stiffness built into the geometry
2. **No flat surfaces** — easier to coat, less corrosion
3. **Fewer stiffeners** — reduces fabrication and welding cost
4. **Better sloshing resistance** — corrugations dampen liquid movement
5. **Reduced plate thickness** for same pressure
6. **Easier inspection** — open access on both sides

*(7 marks: 2 marks for sketch description + 3 marks for constructional details + 2 marks for advantages)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $H = 4$ m, $B = 5$ m, $L = 8$ m
- $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $P = 350$ mm, $d = 120$ mm, $t = 8$ mm

**Step 1: Maximum hydrostatic pressure at base**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{max} &= \rho g H = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 4 \\[4pt]
&= 40221 \text{ Pa} = 40.221 \text{ kPa} \\[4pt]
&= 0.040221 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Load per corrugation**

The load on one corrugation is the pressure acting over the **projected breadth** of one pitch:

$$w = p_{max} \cdot P = 0.040221 \times 350 = 14.077 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 3: Maximum moment for a simply-supported corrugation of span H**

The corrugation is simply supported at top and bottom, with **triangular** pressure load (linearly varying from 0 at top to $w$ at base). For a simply-supported beam with triangular load (zero at one end, max at other):

$$M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{9\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.0642 \cdot w L^2$$

Using exact formula $M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{9\sqrt{3}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{max} &= \frac{14.077 \times (4000)^2}{9\sqrt{3}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{14.077 \times 16{,}000{,}000}{15.588} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2.2523 \times 10^8}{15.588} \\[4pt]
&\approx 1.445 \times 10^7 \text{ N·mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Section modulus of corrugation (approx.)**

For a trapezoidal corrugation of pitch $P$, depth $d$, thickness $t$:

$$Z \approx \frac{P \cdot t^2}{6} + \frac{t \cdot d^2}{6}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{350 \times 8^2}{6} + \frac{8 \times 120^2}{6} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{350 \times 64}{6} + \frac{8 \times 14400}{6} \\[4pt]
&= 3733.3 + 19200 \\[4pt]
&= 22933.3 \text{ mm}^3 \approx 22.93 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Maximum bending stress**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} &= \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{1.445 \times 10^7}{22933.3} \\[4pt]
&\approx 630.1 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The high stress value (630 N/mm²) is above yield — this indicates that the actual calculation should use the more accurate **effective flange method** considering only a portion of the plate flange (typically $0.2t\sqrt{E/\sigma_y}$ width as effective flange, per CSR). For a 14-mark KTU answer, present this simplified result but **comment on the conservatism**.

A more realistic **effective breadth** consideration (IACS CSR) would reduce this value. Students should mention this refinement.

**Final Adopted Stress: Re-design with thicker corrugation (t = 10 mm) or additional stiffening.**

*(7 marks: 1 mark setup + 2 marks pressure/load + 2 marks moment + 1 mark section modulus + 1 mark stress calculation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Tank Structural Layout & Free Surface Effect**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a **sketch of a double-bottom tank arrangement**, describe the structural components and explain how the loads are transferred from the tank to the ship's hull girder. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **double-bottom fuel oil tank** is 12 m long, 10 m wide, and 1.5 m deep. The tank is **80% full** with HFO of density **960 kg/m³**. Calculate:
- (i) The **free surface effect moment** (moment of inertia of free liquid surface) for a single rectangular compartment.
- (ii) The **shift of the centre of gravity** of the liquid if the ship rolls through 5° to one side. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Sketch (described):**

> A cross-section showing the **inner bottom plating** at the top of the double-bottom, the **outer bottom plating** at the bottom, with **vertical floors** (solid plate or bracket floors) at regular intervals, and **longitudinal stiffeners** running fore-aft. The tank boundary is the inner bottom on top, the outer bottom and side shell on the sides, and the centreline girder in the middle.

**Structural Components:**

1. **Outer Bottom Plating** — bottom hull plate, thickness typically 12–25 mm
2. **Inner Bottom Plating** — top of double bottom, 8–15 mm, often longitudinally stiffened
3. **Centreline Keel/Girder** — vertical plate at the ship's centreline, may be solid or bracket
4. **Side Girders** — vertical or bracket plates at the side tanks
5. **Bottom Floors** — transverse plate floors every 2–3 m, full or bracket type
6. **Longitudinal Stiffeners** — small T-bars/L-bars at 600–800 mm spacing on inner bottom
7. **Manholes / Lightening Holes** — for access, must be reinforced
8. **Tank Top Stiffeners** (optional) — when tank top is heavily loaded

**Load Transfer Path:**

- **Hydrostatic pressure** on inner bottom (from tank liquid) → carried by **inner bottom plating** as a plate between stiffeners → into **longitudinal stiffeners** → into **transverse floors / centre girder / side girders** → into **bottom shell plating** → into the **ship's hull girder** (global bending).

- **Side pressure** → side shell → longitudinal → bottom shell → hull girder.

- **Sloshing / inertial loads** → directly into the tank boundary plating → stiffening system → floor/girder network → hull girder.

*(7 marks: 2 marks sketch + 2 marks components + 3 marks load path explanation)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $L_t = 12$ m, $B_t = 10$ m, $H_t = 1.5$ m
- Filling ratio: 80% (so free liquid surface exists)
- $\rho = 960$ kg/m³
- Roll angle $\phi = 5°$

**Step 1: Free Surface Effect — Moment of Inertia**

For a rectangular free surface of length $L$ and breadth $B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{FS} &= \frac{L_t \cdot B_t^3}{12} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{12 \times (10)^3}{12} \\[4pt]
&= 12 \times \frac{1000}{12} \\[4pt]
&= 1000 \text{ m}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Virtual Loss in GM due to Free Surface**

$$\Delta GM = \frac{i_{FS}}{V_{disp}} \cdot \rho_{liquid} \cdot \frac{1}{\rho_{sw}}$$

For the question, assume a typical ship displacement $V_{disp}$. If the question does not specify displacement, the **virtual rise in G** is calculated as:

$$\Delta G = \frac{\rho_{liquid} \cdot i_{FS}}{V_{disp}}$$

**Assuming displacement volume $V = 15000$ m³ (typical for the size):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G &= \frac{960 \times 1000}{15000} = 64 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This represents the **virtual rise of the centre of gravity** due to free surface.

**Step 3: Transverse Shift of Liquid Centre of Gravity at 5° Roll**

For a partially filled rectangular tank, the shift of liquid centre of gravity depends on the **free surface area** and **roll angle**.

**Freeboard after 80% fill:** $h_{air} = 0.2 \times 1.5 = 0.3$ m

**Shift in centre of gravity (transverse):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta G &= \frac{i_{FS}}{V_{liquid}} \cdot \tan(\phi) \\[4pt]
V_{liquid} &= L \times B \times 0.8 H = 12 \times 10 \times 0.8 \times 1.5 = 144 \text{ m}^3 \\[4pt]
\delta G &= \frac{1000}{144} \times \tan(5°) \\[4pt]
&= 6.944 \times 0.0875 \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.608 \text{ m} \quad (608 \text{ mm})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note: The **transverse shift** of the liquid's centre of gravity is **not** the ship's roll-induced GZ; it is the **liquid-only moment** that contributes to the free surface effect. The relevant naval architecture term is the **virtual loss in metacentric height** $\Delta GM = i_{FS}/V_{disp}$ — students should distinguish these clearly in KTU answers.

*(7 marks: 2 marks FSE moment of inertia + 2 marks displacement effect + 2 marks shift + 1 mark result and conclusion)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Tank Questions:**
> 1. **Unit confusion** — Mixing Pa, kPa, N/m², and N/mm². Always state the unit in every step.
> 2. **Using wrong density** — Default to **seawater 1025 kg/m³** unless specified; marks lost for using 1000.
> 3. **Forgetting the breadth** in total force — KTU students often write $F = ½\rho g H^2$ (per unit width) but forget to multiply by breadth $B$ when asked for total force.
> 4. **Confusing FSE terms** — Mixing up "moment of inertia of free surface" ($i_{FS}$) with "moment of inertia of waterplane area" ($I_W$). They are different.
> 5. **Incomplete sketches** — In 14-mark questions, a labelled sketch is **mandatory**. Skipping it costs 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting corrosion addition** in plate thickness problems.
> 7. **Not stating assumptions** — Always mention "simply supported," "fresh water," "no sloshing," etc., as applicable.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Tank Types:** Ballast, fuel oil, fresh water, cargo, peak, cofferdam, deep, slop, bilge.
- **Hydrostatic Pressure:** $p = \rho g h$ — linear, triangular distribution on vertical walls.
- **Force per unit width on vertical bulkhead:** $w = ½\rho g H^2$.
- **Base moment per unit width:** $M = \rho g H^3 / 6$.
- **Resultant location:** $H/3$ from the base.
- **Plate thickness (CSR):** $t_0 = s \sqrt{p_d / (C \sigma_{allow})}$ + corrosion addition (0.5–2 mm).
- **Stiffener section modulus:** $Z = M / \sigma_{allow}$.
- **Corrugated bulkhead** advantages: high stiffness-to-weight, no flat pockets, easy to coat, slosh-resistant.
- **Swash bulkhead** reduces free surface effect and sloshing; perforated, ~50–60% open area.
- **Free surface effect moment** for rectangle: $i_{FS} = L B^3 / 12$.
- **Virtual loss in GM:** $\Delta GM = i_{FS}/V_{disp}$.
- **Standard tank tests:** Hydraulic (preferred), hose (0.2 MPa), air (0.02 MPa), vacuum (flat only).
- **Design pressure** = max of static, sloshing, inertial.
- **Material constants:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $\rho_{fw} = 1000$ kg/m³, $\sigma_{allow,mild} = 175$ N/mm².
- **H^3 sensitivity** — bending moment grows as the cube of tank depth; deep tanks need heavy reinforcement.
- **End connections** of corrugations: slot-welded (allows thermal expansion) at deck, inner bottom, and side shell.
- **Corrosion addition** must always be added in shipbuilding (ship plates corrode in service).
- **KTU favourite 14-mark questions:** corrugated bulkhead design, swash bulkhead with FSE, double-bottom tank load path.
- **IACS UR S21/S22** is the controlling rule for tank scantlings in modern ships.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deep Tanks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deep Tanks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Deep Tank** is defined by classification societies (IACS members like IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, BV, ABS) as a tank that has a **depth of 1.8 m (6 ft) or more**, measured from the tank top to the tank bottom, and is intended to carry liquids such as fresh water, sea water (ballast), fuel oil, lubricating oil, or liquid cargo. Deep tanks that span the full breadth or depth of the ship are termed **Full-Depth Deep Tanks** or **Full-Breadth Deep Tanks**, and they play a dual role as both liquid-containment compartments and primary hull-strength members (contributing to longitudinal and transverse strength).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A deep tank is any tank exceeding **1.8 m (6 ft)** in depth, subjected to hydrostatic pressure that varies linearly with depth. They are generally bounded by the shell plating, deck plating, inner bottom, and transverse bulkheads, and require enhanced scantlings compared to dry spaces.

### 1.2 Classification of Deep Tanks

| Sl. No. | Type | Location | Primary Use |
|---------|------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Peak Tanks** | Forward / Aft of collision bulkhead / after peak | Trim ballast, emergency water |
| 2 | **Double Bottom Deep Tanks** | Between inner bottom and shell | Ballast, fresh water, fuel oil |
| 3 | **Wing Deep Tanks** | Between side shell and inner skin | Ballast, cargo oil |
| 4 | **Deep Cargo Tanks** | Within cargo hold region | Liquid cargo (tankers) |
| 5 | **Cofferdams** | Between two adjacent compartments | Isolation / Safety |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Building Water Tank**
> Think of a tall overhead water tank in an apartment building. The bottom of the tank experiences much more pressure than the top because of the weight of water above it. Similarly, in a ship's deep tank, the **lower plating carries much greater hydrostatic pressure** than the upper plating. This is why deep tanks have **thicker plates and stronger stiffeners near the bottom**, just as the base of an overhead water tank is heavily reinforced.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Density of sea water: **ρ_sw = 1.025 t/m³**
- Density of fresh water: **ρ_fw = 1.000 t/m³**
- Density of fuel oil: **ρ_fo ≈ 0.85 t/m³**
- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Minimum deep tank depth (per IRS rules): **1.8 m**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principles

A deep tank in a ship operates under the following engineering principles:

- **Hydrostatic Pressure Principle:** Pressure at any depth *h* below the free surface of liquid is $p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$.
- **Maximum Pressure at Bottom:** Occurs at the lowest point of the tank (near inner bottom), equal to $p_{max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H$, where *H* is the total depth of liquid.
- **Structural Integration:** Deep tanks are framed by the **inner hull (shell plating), deck plating, inner bottom plating, and transverse bulkheads** — all of which must resist both hydrostatic pressure and external wave slamming.
- **Stiffener Orientation:** Stiffeners (T-bars, flat bars, or bulb plates) are arranged vertically on transverse bulkheads to resist bending caused by pressure.
- **Special Bulkheads:** **Wash bulkheads** (perforated or slotted) are fitted in very large tanks to dampen sloshing effects; **swash bulkheads** reduce free-surface effect on stability.

### 2.2 Design Pressure Determination

The design pressure for deep tank scantlings is taken as the **maximum of:**

1. Static head due to filling (1.025 t/m³ for sea water ballast)
2. Pressure due to combined head plus accelerations (sloshing)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Formula | Unit |
|---------|-----------|---------|------|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure at depth *h* | $p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | kN/m² |
| 2 | Maximum pressure at tank bottom | $p_{max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H$ | kN/m² |
| 3 | Plate thickness (IRS rule) | $t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{p / \sigma} + t_k$ | mm |
| 4 | Section modulus of stiffener | $Z = \frac{p \cdot s \cdot l^2}{m \cdot \sigma}$ | cm³ |
| 5 | Web height of stiffener | $h_w = (4.2 + 0.07 \cdot l) \cdot \sqrt[3]{Z}$ | mm |
| 6 | Web thickness | $t_w = 0.015 \cdot h_w + 2.5$ | mm |
| 7 | Flange breadth | $b_f = 6.2 \cdot h_w / 100 + 25$ | mm |
| 8 | Flange thickness | $t_f = 0.6 \cdot t_w$ | mm |
| 9 | Pressure (in m head of water) | $h = p / (\rho \cdot g)$ | m |
| 10 | Specific gravity × head | $p = 10 \cdot s$ (approx) | kN/m² |

Where:
- *s* = stiffener spacing (mm)
- *l* = stiffener span (mm)
- *p* = design pressure (kN/m²)
- *σ* = allowable stress (N/mm²)
- *k* = form factor
- *m* = boundary condition factor
- *t_k* = corrosion addition

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural Safety:** Deep tank scantlings ensure the hull does not rupture under cargo loading or ballast operations.
- **Longitudinal Strength:** Full-depth deep tanks act as box girders contributing to the ship's hull girder bending resistance.
- **Stability & Trim:** Ballast deep tanks allow fine-tuning of ship's trim and transverse stability.
- **Damage Control:** Properly designed deep tanks limit flooding propagation in case of hull breach.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** In KTU exams, you will always be given the design pressure at a specific level. Always draw the **pressure prism** (a triangular diagram showing pressure increasing with depth) before solving any stiffener/plate problem.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure in a Deep Tank

Consider a deep tank filled with sea water of density $\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$ to a total depth $H$ meters.

**Step 1: Pressure at a depth *h* from the free surface**

The weight of the column of liquid above any point per unit area gives the pressure.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= \text{Weight density} \times \text{height of liquid column} \\
p &= \rho \cdot g \cdot h
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the fundamental hydrostatic equation derived from Pascal's principle.

**Step 2: Substitute numerical values**

For sea water, $\rho \cdot g = 1.025 \times 9.81 = 10.055 \text{ kN/m}^3$

Thus,

$$
p = 10.055 \cdot h \;\; \text{(kN/m}^2\text{)}
$$

For fresh water, $\rho \cdot g = 1.000 \times 9.81 = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$

Thus,

$$
p = 9.81 \cdot h \;\; \text{(kN/m}^2\text{)}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum pressure at the bottom of the tank**

Substituting $h = H$ (total depth):

$$
p_{max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H
$$

For example, if $H = 6 \text{ m}$ of sea water:

$$
p_{max} = 10.055 \times 6 = 60.33 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Plate Thickness (Stiffened Panel)

A plate panel between two stiffeners, simply supported on all four edges, with pressure $p$ acting perpendicular to its surface.

**Step 1: Bending stress in plate**

The maximum bending moment per unit width of the plate is:

$$
M = \frac{p \cdot s^2}{8}
$$

where *s* is the shorter span (stiffener spacing).

**Step 2: Section modulus per unit width**

For a plate of unit width and thickness *t*:

$$
Z_{plate} = \frac{1 \cdot t^2}{6}
$$

**Step 3: Equating bending stress to allowable**

$$
\sigma = \frac{M}{Z_{plate}} = \frac{p \cdot s^2 / 8}{t^2 / 6} = \frac{6 \cdot p \cdot s^2}{8 \cdot t^2}
$$

**Step 4: Solving for plate thickness *t***

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{3 \cdot p \cdot s^2}{4 \cdot t^2} \\
t^2 &= \frac{3 \cdot p \cdot s^2}{4 \cdot \sigma} \\
t &= s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{3 \cdot p}{4 \cdot \sigma}} \\
t &= k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{\sigma}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $k = \sqrt{3/4} = 0.866$ (form factor for simply supported plate).

**Step 5: Including corrosion addition $t_k$**

$$
t_{required} = k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{\sigma}} + t_k
$$

For IRS rules, a typical value is $t_k = 1.0 \text{ mm}$ for deep tanks.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A deep tank has a depth of 5 m and is filled with sea water. Calculate (a) the pressure at the bottom, (b) the required plate thickness for the bottom plating if stiffener spacing is 600 mm and allowable stress is 160 N/mm². Use form factor *k* = 0.866 and corrosion addition = 1 mm.

**Solution (a): Pressure at the bottom**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= \rho \cdot g \cdot h \\
p &= 10.055 \times 5 \\
p &= 50.275 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
p &= 50.275 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
p &= 0.05028 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (b): Plate thickness**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{\sigma}} + t_k \\
t &= 0.866 \times 600 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.05028}{160}} + 1 \\
t &= 519.6 \times \sqrt{0.0003142} + 1 \\
t &= 519.6 \times 0.01773 + 1 \\
t &= 9.21 + 1 \\
t &= 10.21 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the standard plate thickness adopted = **11 mm**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Deep Tank

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Ship Hull Structure] --> nodeB[Deep Tank Compartment]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Top Boundary: Deck Plating]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Bottom Boundary: Inner Bottom Plating]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Side Boundaries: Shell Plating]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Transverse Bulkheads]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Ordinary Bulkhead]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Wash Bulkhead]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Swash Bulkhead]
    nodeB --> nodeJ[Stiffener Network]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Vertical Stiffeners on Bulkheads]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Horizontal Girders]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Brackets at Connections]
    nodeB --> nodeN[Liquid Loading]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Hydrostatic Pressure Increases with Depth]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Pressure Prism: Triangular Distribution]
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Maximum Scantling at Bottom]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Plate Thickness t]
    nodeQ --> nodeS[Stiffener Section Modulus Z]
```

### 4.2 Pressure Distribution Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Free Surface of Liquid at top] --> nodeB[Pressure = 0 kN per square m]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Depth h1: Pressure p1]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Depth h2: Pressure p2]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Bottom Depth H: Pressure pmax]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Zero Pressure Line]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Maximum Pressure Line]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Pressure Prism - Triangular Shape]
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

### 4.3 Typical Deep Tank Arrangement (Cross Section View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Deck Plating at Top] --> nodeB[Upper Stiffener Ring at 1.8 m from top]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Intermediate Stiffener Ring]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Lower Stiffener Ring - Heavier Section]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Bottom Corner Bracket]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Inner Bottom Plating - Thickest]
    nodeA --> nodeG[Tank Side Shell]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Bilge Region]
    nodeH --> nodeF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The above diagrams illustrate that the **scantlings (plate thickness and stiffener section modulus) progressively increase from the top of the tank to the bottom**, mirroring the linearly increasing hydrostatic pressure. The bottom corner bracket is a critical structural element resisting shear and bending.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1: Define a deep tank. State the minimum depth criteria.**

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A deep tank is defined as any tank in a ship having a **depth of 1.8 m (6 ft) or more**, measured from the top of the tank to the bottom. Deep tanks are used for carrying liquids such as ballast water, fresh water, fuel oil, lubricating oil, or liquid cargo.

They are subjected to **high hydrostatic pressure** that varies linearly from zero at the free surface to a maximum at the bottom, equal to $p_{max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H$.

Such tanks require **enhanced scantlings** for plating and stiffeners compared to dry spaces of similar dimensions, since the structural integrity must withstand the pressure at the deepest point as well as dynamic loads from ship motion.

---

**Question 2: What is a wash bulkhead? State its purpose.**

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **wash bulkhead** is a perforated or corrugated transverse bulkhead fitted within a deep tank to **reduce the adverse effects of sloshing** of the liquid cargo during ship motion.

**Purposes:**

1. It breaks the free surface of the liquid, thereby reducing the **free-surface effect** on the ship's stability.
2. It dampens the violent oscillation of liquid, preventing structural damage to tank boundaries.
3. It improves the effectiveness of ballast operations by reducing dynamic loads.

Wash bulkheads are typically made with slots, holes, or corrugations to allow free passage of liquid while providing structural continuity.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3]`

**(a)** A double-bottom deep tank is 4 m deep and is filled with sea water. Calculate the hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of the tank. Also determine the pressure at 1 m and 3 m depths from the free surface.

**(b)** For the same tank, determine the required plate thickness for the bottom plating if the stiffener spacing is 500 mm and the allowable bending stress is 150 N/mm². Use a form factor of 0.866 and corrosion addition of 1.5 mm. State the standard plate thickness to be adopted.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Pressure Calculations:**

Given:
- Depth of tank, $H = 4$ m
- Sea water, $\rho \cdot g = 10.055 \text{ kN/m}^3$

Pressure at depth *h*:

$$
p = 10.055 \cdot h
$$

**At bottom (h = 4 m):**

$$
p_{max} = 10.055 \times 4 = 40.22 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**At h = 1 m:**

$$
p_1 = 10.055 \times 1 = 10.055 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**At h = 3 m:**

$$
p_2 = 10.055 \times 3 = 30.165 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

| Depth h (m) | Pressure p (kN/m²) |
|-------------|---------------------|
| 1.0 | 10.055 |
| 3.0 | 30.165 |
| 4.0 | 40.22 |

---

**Part (b) — Plate Thickness Calculation:**

Given:
- Pressure at bottom, $p = 40.22 \text{ kN/m}^2 = 0.04022 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- Stiffener spacing, $s = 500$ mm
- Allowable stress, $\sigma = 150 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- Form factor, $k = 0.866$
- Corrosion addition, $t_k = 1.5$ mm

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{\sigma}} + t_k \\
t &= 0.866 \times 500 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.04022}{150}} + 1.5 \\
t &= 433 \times \sqrt{0.0002681} + 1.5 \\
t &= 433 \times 0.01637 + 1.5 \\
t &= 7.09 + 1.5 \\
t &= 8.59 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Standard plate thickness adopted = 9 mm** (rounded up to the next available standard size).

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the classification of deep tanks based on location in a ship. State the function of each type.

**(b)** A peak deep tank is 6 m deep and is filled with fresh water. Determine (i) the maximum hydrostatic pressure at the bottom, and (ii) the section modulus required for a vertical stiffener on the transverse bulkhead if the stiffener span is 3 m and spacing is 700 mm. Take allowable stress = 165 N/mm².

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Classification of Deep Tanks:**

Deep tanks are classified based on their **location within the hull** as follows:

1. **Peak Deep Tanks:** Located in the fore peak and aft peak, between the collision bulkhead (or after peak bulkhead) and the stem (or stern frame). Used for trim ballast and emergency water.

2. **Double-Bottom Deep Tanks:** Formed between the inner bottom plating and the bottom shell plating. Used primarily for ballast water and fuel oil storage.

3. **Wing Deep Tanks:** Located between the side shell plating and the longitudinal bulkhead. Used for ballast or cargo oil in tankers.

4. **Cargo Deep Tanks:** Located within the cargo region of tankers, designed specifically for liquid cargo (crude oil, chemicals).

5. **Cofferdams:** Small double-walled spaces between two adjacent tanks, used to prevent mixing of incompatible liquids and for safety isolation.

**Function:** All deep tanks serve the dual purpose of liquid storage and contribution to the ship's **longitudinal and transverse structural strength**.

---

**Part (b) — Section Modulus Calculation:**

Given:
- Depth, $H = 6$ m
- Fresh water, $\rho \cdot g = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- Stiffener span, $l = 3$ m = 3000 mm
- Stiffener spacing, $s = 700$ mm
- Allowable stress, $\sigma = 165 \text{ N/mm}^2$

**(i) Maximum hydrostatic pressure:**

$$
p_{max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H = 9.81 \times 6 = 58.86 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

Converting to N/mm²:

$$
p_{max} = 0.05886 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**(ii) Section modulus required:**

For a simply supported stiffener:

$$
Z = \frac{p \cdot s \cdot l^2}{m \cdot \sigma}
$$

Taking $m = 8$ for simply supported beam:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{0.05886 \times 700 \times (3000)^2}{8 \times 165} \\
Z &= \frac{0.05886 \times 700 \times 9 \times 10^6}{1320} \\
Z &= \frac{3.7082 \times 10^8}{1320} \\
Z &= 280,924 \text{ mm}^3 \\
Z &= 280.92 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Required section modulus Z ≈ 281 cm³.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Forgetting to convert pressure from kN/m² to N/mm² before substituting into the formula. (1 kN/m² = 10⁻³ N/mm²). This is the **#1 reason for losing 2-3 marks** in deep tank problems.
>
> 2. **Confusing Form Factor:** Using the wrong form factor (*k* = 0.866 for simply supported, *k* = 1.0 for clamped). Always state the boundary condition assumed.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Corrosion Addition:** Students often forget to add $t_k$, leading to a deficit in plate thickness by 1-1.5 mm.
>
> 4. **Boundary Condition Factor *m*:** Using *m* = 8 for simply supported but the stiffener in a deep tank is usually continuous; partial fixity gives *m* ≈ 12-13.
>
> 5. **Standard Rounding:** Always round UP the calculated plate thickness to the next standard thickness (e.g., 8.59 mm → 9 mm; 10.21 mm → 11 mm). Never round down.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **deep tank** is any tank with depth ≥ **1.8 m (6 ft)**.
- [x] Hydrostatic pressure follows $p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$, increasing **linearly with depth**.
- [x] For **sea water**: $\rho \cdot g \approx 10.055 \text{ kN/m}^3$; for **fresh water**: $9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$.
- [x] Plate thickness formula: $t = k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{p/\sigma} + t_k$.
- [x] Section modulus formula: $Z = p \cdot s \cdot l^2 / (m \cdot \sigma)$.
- [x] Scantlings are **heaviest at the bottom** of the deep tank.
- [x] **Wash bulkheads** reduce sloshing effects; **swash bulkheads** reduce free-surface effect.
- [x] **Full-depth deep tanks** also contribute to hull girder strength.
- [x] Always convert units: **1 kN/m² = 10⁻³ N/mm²**.
- [x] Always round plate thickness **up** to the next standard size.
- [x] Common tank types: **peak, double-bottom, wing, cargo, cofferdam**.
- [x] IRS allowable stresses: typically **150-165 N/mm²** for mild steel in deep tanks.
- [x] Corrosion addition for deep tanks: **1.0 to 1.5 mm** (depending on service).
- [x] Deep tanks must satisfy **damage stability** requirements for ballast operations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing of Deep Tanks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Testing of Deep Tanks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Deep Tank** in shipbuilding is defined by classification societies as any tank that has a **depth (height) greater than 1.5 m (or as per society rules)** and is used for the carriage of water ballast, fuel oil, lubricating oil, or other liquid cargo. A tank is specifically classified as a *deep tank* when its **depth exceeds 1/6th of its breadth** (or 1/6th of its transverse extent) as per IRS (Indian Register of Shipping) and IACS unified requirements. The structural testing of these tanks is mandatory after fabrication and during new construction to verify **leaktightness, structural integrity, and weld soundness** under simulated hydrostatic loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Testing of deep tanks is governed by IACS Unified Requirements **UR S21** (Special Service Ships), **S22** (Testing Procedures), and **Z17** (Procedural Requirements for Service Notations). Classification societies like IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, and BV have near-identical test requirements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Standard (IACS UR S21):** A tank with a length/depth ratio such that the hydrostatic head produces stresses requiring strengthened internal framing is termed a deep tank. Typically, if depth $\geq 1.5 \text{ m}$ or $\geq 1/6$ of breadth, it qualifies.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **large plastic water tank** at home that holds 1000 litres. Before the manufacturer sells it, they fill it completely with water to see if any seams leak or the walls bulge out. If the walls hold the water pressure without leaking, the tank is approved.

A **ship's deep tank** works on exactly the same principle, but scaled up enormously:
- A typical ship's deep tank can hold **hundreds of tonnes of water** (sea water ballast).
- The water exerts **enormous pressure** on the bottom and side plates.
- The tank has dozens of **welded seams** joining plates together — these welds are the weakest points.
- Therefore, after the tank is built (during new construction), it is filled with water and held under pressure to verify it is leak-free and structurally sound.

**Why is testing critical?** Because once the ship sails, the tank may carry fuel or ballast. If it leaks, it can:
1. Cause **flooding** (if ballast) — sinking risk.
2. Cause **oil pollution** (if fuel) — environmental disaster and heavy fines.
3. Weaken surrounding hull structure through **corrosion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of deep tank testing as the **"stress test" for a ship's stomach.** Just like a doctor pumps your stomach with air to check for ulcers, the shipyard fills the tank with water under pressure to check for weld "ulcers" (cracks/leaks).

---

### 1.3 Standard Test Metrics (Highlighted Constants)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Standard test head | **2.45 m above the tank top** (i.e., 2.45 m above the highest point of the tank) |
| Or pressure equivalent | **24 kPa (0.24 bar) minimum** at the top of the tank |
| Test medium | **Sea water or fresh water** (never oil for tightness test) |
| Test duration | **Typically 15–30 minutes** under sustained head |
| Safety factor on yield | **1.0 (elastic)** to **1.5 (ultimate)** for different test types |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Deep Tank Testing:** The test head of **2.45 m of water** (≈ 24 kPa) above the tank top is the most universally accepted pressure per IACS UR Z17.1. Some societies (Lloyd's) also accept **0.5 m head above the design head** for hydrostatic tests.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Are Deep Tanks Tested?

Deep tanks are critical structural elements because they:
1. **Carry high-density liquids** (sea water ballast at ρ ≈ 1025 kg/m³).
2. **Experience large hydrostatic pressures** due to depth.
3. Are subject to **sloshing loads** when partially filled.
4. Form part of the **hull girder** structure (contribute to hull strength).
5. Must satisfy **SOLAS** (Safety of Life at Sea) and **MARPOL** (Marine Pollution) requirements.

A failure in a deep tank can compromise:
- **Watertight integrity** (flooding risk).
- **Structural strength** (plate buckling, weld cracking).
- **Environmental compliance** (oil pollution).

---

### 2.2 Types of Tests Performed on Deep Tanks

Classification societies require a combination of the following tests:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Mandatory Tests:**
> 1. **Tightness Test (Leak Test)** — verifies no leakage through joints.
> 2. **Strength Test (Hydrostatic Test)** — verifies structural adequacy.

#### A. Hydrostatic Test (Strength Test)
- The tank is **completely filled with water** and pressurized to a specified head.
- All penetrations (manholes, sounding pipes) are sealed.
- The tank is **held under pressure for a specified time** (usually 15–30 min).
- Visual inspection is conducted for:
  - Permanent deformation (bulging, sagging).
  - Weld cracks.
  - Leakage at joints.
- **Test Head Formula:** As per IACS UR Z17.1:

$$
h_{test} = h_{design} + 1.0 \text{ m (minimum)}
$$

where:
- $h_{design}$ = maximum design liquid head above the tank top (or 2.45 m above top, whichever is greater).

#### B. Hose Test (Tightness Test)
- Conducted **after the hydrostatic test** or as an alternative for smaller tanks.
- A pressurized water jet is sprayed onto all welded joints.
- The person spraying stands **not more than 1.5 m** from the joint.
- Water pressure: typically **2 bar minimum**.
- Any leak is identified by visible water seepage on the dry side.

#### C. Air Test (Pneumatic Test)
- Used **only when water testing is impractical** (e.g., tank has been coated or insulation installed).
- The tank is pressurized with **air at 0.15–0.2 bar**.
- Soap solution is applied to all joints.
- Bubbles indicate leaks.
- **Safety:** Pneumatic tests are dangerous due to stored energy; only conducted with strict safety protocols.

#### D. Vacuum Test
- Used for **double-bottom tanks** and certain void spaces.
- The tank is partially evacuated (vacuum).
- Atmospheric pressure on the outside verifies integrity.

#### E. Ultrasonic / NDT (Non-Destructive Testing)
- **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — detects internal flaws in welds.
- **Radiographic Testing (RT)** — X-ray or gamma-ray inspection of butt welds.
- **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)** — for surface cracks in welds.
- **Dye Penetrant Testing (PT)** — for surface defects.

---

### 2.3 Structural Background: Why Depth Matters

The hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of a tank filled to height $H$ is:

$$
P = \rho \cdot g \cdot H
$$

For sea water ($\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$):

$$
P \approx 10.05 \cdot H \quad [\text{kPa}]
$$

So a 10 m deep tank experiences ~**100 kPa (1 bar)** at the bottom — this is significant and can:
- Cause **plate bending** between stiffeners.
- Cause **stiffener yielding**.
- Cause **weld cracking** at corners.

The tank must be designed (stiffened) to withstand these loads **with a safety factor**, and the test verifies this.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Deep tank testing is the **last line of defense** in shipbuilding quality control. Even with strict fabrication tolerances (1–2 mm), welding introduces residual stresses and possible defects. Testing catches what NDT might miss and provides a full-scale, real-load verification.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units |
|---|---------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P = \rho g H$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $H$ | Pa |
| 2 | $h_{test} = h_{design} + 1.0 \text{ m}$ | IACS UR Z17.1 test head (minimum 2.45 m above top) | m of water |
| 3 | $P_{test} = 9.81 \cdot h_{test}$ | Test pressure for **fresh water** (ρ = 1000 kg/m³) | kPa |
| 4 | $P_{test} = 10.05 \cdot h_{test}$ | Test pressure for **sea water** (ρ = 1025 kg/m³) | kPa |
| 5 | $\sigma_{bending} = \dfrac{6 M}{t^2}$ | Bending stress in plate per unit width | Pa |
| 6 | $p_{ult} = \dfrac{4 \sigma_y t^2}{L^2}$ | Collapse pressure of simply supported plate (Bryan) | Pa |
| 7 | Test duration | 15–30 min for hydrostatic; NDT before filling | min |
| 8 | Hose test pressure | ≥ 2 bar, nozzle ≤ 1.5 m from joint | bar, m |
| 9 | Air test pressure | 0.15–0.20 bar (gauge) | bar |
| 10 | Safety factor on yield for test | 1.0 (elastic, no permanent set expected) | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Units:** In shipbuilding, **test head** is almost always expressed in **metres of water column** (not in bar or kPa). This is because the head is directly related to the height of water filled in the tank.

---

### 2.5 Acceptance Criteria

After testing, the tank is approved if:
1. **No leakage** is observed at any welded joint, pipe connection, or penetrations.
2. **No permanent deformation** of plates, stiffeners, or bulkheads.
3. **No cracks** in welds or parent metal.
4. **No excessive bulging** (typically deflection limit is $L/250$ or as per society rules).
5. **All penetrations remain watertight.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the **hydrostatic test head** (2.45 m above tank top) with the **design head** (top of fill level). The test head is **always greater** than the design head — it's a "proof load."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure and Test Head

We start with the fundamental relationship for hydrostatic pressure in a liquid column.

**Step 1:** Consider a vertical column of liquid of height $H$ and cross-sectional area $A$.

The weight of the liquid column is:

$$
W = \rho \cdot g \cdot H \cdot A
$$

where $\rho$ is the density of the liquid and $g$ is gravitational acceleration.

**Step 2:** The pressure at the bottom of the column is the force per unit area:

$$
P = \frac{W}{A} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H
$$

This is the standard hydrostatic equation.

**Step 3:** Substituting values for sea water ($\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$):

$$
P = 1025 \times 9.81 \times H \approx 10055 \cdot H \quad [\text{Pa}]
$$

$$
P \approx 10.05 \cdot H \quad [\text{kPa}]
$$

**Step 4:** Converting to **metres of water column** (head):
The "head" $h$ is simply the height of the water column above the point of interest.

For sea water:
$$
P \, [\text{kPa}] \approx 10.05 \cdot h \, [\text{m}]
$$

**Step 5:** For a **deep tank with top at elevation $z_{top}$** and the tank being filled with water up to a head $h_{test}$ above the top, the pressure at any depth $d$ below the water surface is:

$$
P(d) = \rho \cdot g \cdot d
$$

The **maximum pressure** at the **bottom** of the tank (at depth $H_{tank}$ below tank top) plus the additional $h_{test}$ above the top is:

$$
P_{max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot (H_{tank} + h_{test})
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Test Pressure Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem**

**Problem:** A deep tank on a bulk carrier has a **depth of 8 m** (from bottom plate to top of tank). The tank is to be tested hydrostatically as per IACS UR Z17.1. The design filling level is **flush with the tank top**. Calculate:
> (a) The required test head above the tank top.
> (b) The maximum pressure at the bottom of the tank during the test.
> (c) The test pressure at the mid-height of the tank.

Take $\rho_{sea} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

---

**Solution (a): Required test head**

Per IACS UR Z17.1:

$$
h_{test} = \max(h_{design} + 1.0 \text{ m}, \ 2.45 \text{ m})
$$

Here, $h_{design} = 0$ (filled flush with tank top).

$$
h_{test} = \max(0 + 1.0, \ 2.45) = \max(1.0, \ 2.45) = 2.45 \text{ m}
$$

So the water level during testing must be **2.45 m above the tank top**.

---

**Solution (b): Maximum pressure at tank bottom**

Total water column height = tank depth + test head above top:

$$
H_{total} = H_{tank} + h_{test} = 8.0 + 2.45 = 10.45 \text{ m}
$$

Pressure at the bottom:

$$
P_{max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H_{total}
$$

$$
P_{max} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 10.45
$$

$$
P_{max} = 1025 \times 9.81 = 10055.25 \text{ Pa/m} \times 10.45 \text{ m}
$$

$$
P_{max} = 10055.25 \times 10.45 = 105,077.4 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{max} \approx 105.1 \text{ kPa} \approx 1.05 \text{ bar}}
$$

---

**Solution (c): Pressure at mid-height**

Mid-height of tank is at depth $4.0 \text{ m}$ below the tank top.

Total water column above this point:

$$
H_{mid} = 4.0 + 2.45 = 6.45 \text{ m}
$$

Pressure at mid-height:

$$
P_{mid} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H_{mid} = 10055.25 \times 6.45
$$

$$
P_{mid} = 64,856.4 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{mid} \approx 64.9 \text{ kPa} \approx 0.65 \text{ bar}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Test Methods

| Method | Medium | Pressure | Application | Advantages | Limitations |
|--------|--------|----------|-------------|------------|-------------|
| **Hydrostatic** | Water | 2.45 m head + design | All tanks | Safest, full-scale load | Heavy (water weight); cannot test coated tanks |
| **Hose Test** | Water jet | ≥ 2 bar | Welded joints (visual check) | Quick, low cost | Only checks surface; does not test structural strength |
| **Air Test** | Air | 0.15–0.20 bar | When water test impractical | Light, fast | Dangerous (stored energy); small leaks hard to detect |
| **Vacuum Test** | Air (reduced) | -0.3 bar gauge | Double-bottom tanks, void spaces | Detects leaks via inward leak | Limited to enclosed spaces |
| **NDT (UT/RT/MT/PT)** | Various | N/A | Welds (localized) | Detects internal/surface flaws | Does not verify global strength |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Test Head Justification (Why 2.45 m?)

The **2.45 m** figure comes from a **safety factor consideration**:

The IACS requirement ensures that the test pressure is at least:

$$
h_{test} = h_{design} + 1.0 \text{ m} \geq 2.45 \text{ m}
$$

This **1.0 m** additional head provides a **safety margin** of approximately:

$$
\text{Safety factor} \approx \frac{h_{design} + 1.0}{h_{design}}
$$

For typical design heads of 5–10 m (ballast tanks), this is a **factor of 1.1 to 1.2**.

The **2.45 m absolute minimum** ensures that even for tanks with very low design head, the test still applies a meaningful load. The value **2.45 m** corresponds to approximately **24 kPa**, which historically corresponds to the maximum expected **dynamic pressure** during ballast transfer or partial filling, multiplied by a safety factor.

---

### 3.5 Plate Collapse Under Test Pressure (Analytical Check)

For a simply supported plate of length $L$, breadth $b$ (with $b \leq L$), thickness $t$, the **ultimate collapse pressure** (Bryan equation) is:

$$
p_{ult} = \frac{4 \sigma_y t^2}{b^2}
$$

For a deep tank bottom plate (e.g., $b = 0.8 \text{ m}$, $t = 0.012 \text{ m}$, $\sigma_y = 235 \text{ MPa}$):

$$
p_{ult} = \frac{4 \times 235 \times 10^6 \times (0.012)^2}{(0.8)^2}
$$

$$
p_{ult} = \frac{4 \times 235 \times 10^6 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-4}}{0.64}
$$

$$
p_{ult} = \frac{135,360}{0.64} = 211,500 \text{ Pa} \approx 211.5 \text{ kPa}
$$

Test pressure (from our earlier example): **105 kPa**.

Safety factor:

$$
SF = \frac{p_{ult}}{p_{test}} = \frac{211.5}{105.1} \approx 2.01
$$

This is a healthy safety factor, confirming the tank will not yield under test.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Deep Tank Testing Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tank Fabrication Complete] --> B{Visual Inspection of Welds}
    B -->|Pass| C[Non-Destructive Testing NDT]
    B -->|Fail| BX[Repair and Re-weld]
    BX --> B
    C -->|Pass| D[Close All Penetrations]
    C -->|Fail| CX[Repair Weld Defects]
    CX --> C
    D --> E[Fill Tank with Sea Water]
    E --> F[Raise Water Level to Test Head 2.45m above top]
    F --> G[Hold Pressure for 15 to 30 Minutes]
    G --> H{Inspection During Test}
    H -->|No Leak| I{Hose Test on All Joints}
    H -->|Leak Found| HX[Mark and Repair Defect]
    HX --> E
    I -->|Pass| J[Empty Tank Slowly]
    I -->|Fail| JX[Repair and Re-test]
    JX --> I
    J --> K[Dry and Apply Coating]
    K --> L[Surveyor Sign-off]
    L --> M[Tank Approved]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define a **deep tank** as per classification society rules. When is a tank classified as a deep tank?

**Model Answer:**
A deep tank is any tank in a ship used for the carriage of liquids (water ballast, fuel oil, lubricating oil, etc.) where the **depth of the tank exceeds 1.5 m** or where the depth is **greater than 1/6th of its transverse breadth** (as per IRS rules and IACS UR S21). The classification of a tank as a deep tank triggers additional structural strengthening requirements (closer stiffener spacing, thicker plates) and mandatory **hydrostatic testing** during new construction.

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
> **Q2.** What is the standard **test head** for hydrostatic testing of a deep tank as per IACS UR Z17.1?

**Model Answer:**
The standard test head is **2.45 m of water column above the top of the tank** (equivalent to approximately **24 kPa** at the top of the tank), or the design head plus 1.0 m, whichever is greater. This test head is applied by filling the tank with water and maintaining the level for a specified duration (typically 15–30 minutes) to verify structural integrity and leaktightness.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **various methods of testing deep tanks** on ships. Discuss their relative merits and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A deep tank has dimensions **10 m (length) × 6 m (breadth) × 5 m (depth)**. The design filling level is **flush with the tank top**. Calculate:
- (i) The required **test head** as per IACS UR Z17.1.
- (ii) The **maximum pressure** at the bottom of the tank during testing.
- (iii) The **total force** acting on the bottom plate during the test. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a): Methods of Testing Deep Tanks (7 Marks)**

The following methods are used to test deep tanks on ships:

1. **Hydrostatic Test (Strength Test):**
   - The tank is filled completely with water.
   - Water level is raised to **2.45 m above tank top** (or design head + 1.0 m, whichever is greater).
   - Pressure is maintained for **15–30 minutes**.
   - Visual inspection for leaks, deformation, cracks.
   - **Merits:** Most reliable; provides full-scale structural verification; safe.
   - **Limitations:** Heavy (large water weight); cannot test if tank already coated or insulated.

2. **Hose Test (Tightness Test):**
   - Pressurized water jet (≥ 2 bar) sprayed on welded joints.
   - Nozzle held within **1.5 m** of the joint.
   - Inspect dry side for seepage.
   - **Merits:** Quick, low cost, suitable for coated tanks.
   - **Limitations:** Only checks joint tightness; not structural strength.

3. **Air Test (Pneumatic Test):**
   - Tank pressurized with **air at 0.15–0.20 bar**.
   - Soap solution applied to joints; bubbles indicate leaks.
   - **Merits:** Light, fast, suitable when water test not possible.
   - **Limitations:** Dangerous due to stored energy; small leaks hard to detect.

4. **Vacuum Test:**
   - Used for **double-bottom tanks** and **void spaces**.
   - Tank is partially evacuated.
   - Atmospheric pressure verifies integrity.
   - **Merits:** Detects inward leaks.
   - **Limitations:** Limited to enclosed spaces.

5. **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT):**
   - Ultrasonic (UT), Radiographic (RT), Magnetic Particle (MT), Dye Penetrant (PT).
   - Used **before** filling to detect weld flaws.
   - **Merits:** Detects internal and surface flaws.
   - **Limitations:** Does not verify global structural strength.

---

**Part (b): Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Tank dimensions: L = 10 m, B = 6 m, H = 5 m
- Design filling level: flush with tank top (h_design = 0)
- ρ_sea = 1025 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s²

**(i) Required Test Head:**

$$
h_{test} = \max(h_{design} + 1.0, \ 2.45) = \max(0 + 1.0, \ 2.45) = 2.45 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{h_{test} = 2.45 \text{ m above tank top}}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Pressure at Tank Bottom:**

Total water column height:

$$
H_{total} = H_{tank} + h_{test} = 5.0 + 2.45 = 7.45 \text{ m}
$$

Pressure at bottom:

$$
P_{max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H_{total}
$$

$$
P_{max} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 7.45
$$

$$
P_{max} = 10055.25 \times 7.45
$$

$$
P_{max} = 74,911.6 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{max} \approx 74.9 \text{ kPa} \approx 0.749 \text{ bar}}
$$

**(iii) Total Force on Bottom Plate:**

Area of bottom plate:

$$
A = L \times B = 10 \times 6 = 60 \text{ m}^2
$$

Total force (using average pressure if varying, but bottom plate is at constant depth 7.45 m):

$$
F = P_{max} \times A = 74,911.6 \times 60
$$

$$
F = 4,494,696 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F \approx 4.49 \text{ MN} \approx 449.5 \text{ tonnes-force}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Discuss the **IACS Unified Requirements (UR Z17.1)** for testing of deep tanks. What are the **acceptance criteria** after the test? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure** for conducting a hydrostatic test on a deep tank. What safety precautions must be observed? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a): IACS UR Z17.1 and Acceptance Criteria (7 Marks)**

**IACS UR Z17.1 — Testing Requirements:**

The IACS Unified Requirement UR Z17.1 ("Procedural Requirements for Service Notations") specifies the testing requirements for tanks, including deep tanks, ballast tanks, and void spaces. Key requirements include:

1. **All new tanks** must be tested during new construction before being put into service.
2. **Test head:** Water level **2.45 m above the highest point of the tank** OR the **design head + 1.0 m**, whichever is greater.
3. **Test medium:** Sea water or fresh water (sea water preferred for ballast tanks).
4. **Test duration:** Pressure maintained for a **sufficient time** (typically 15–30 minutes) to allow for thorough inspection.
5. **All penetrations** (manholes, sounding pipes, air pipes) must be sealed before testing.
6. **NDT** must be conducted on critical welds before the hydrostatic test.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

After the test, the tank is deemed acceptable if:
1. **No leakage** is observed at any welded joint, pipe connection, or penetration.
2. **No permanent deformation** (bulging, sagging) of plates or stiffeners.
3. **No cracks** in welds or parent metal.
4. **No excessive bulging** beyond permissible deflection limits (typically L/250).
5. **All penetrations** remain watertight.
6. **Gaskets and seals** are intact.
7. The tank is **drained and dried** properly after the test.

If any defects are found, **repairs are carried out** and the tank is **re-tested** until acceptance.

---

**Part (b): Hydrostatic Test Procedure and Safety (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Pre-Test Preparations:**
   - Ensure all structural fabrication is complete.
   - Conduct **NDT (UT/RT/MT/PT)** on all critical welds.
   - **Close and seal** all penetrations (manholes, sounding pipes, etc.) using blanks and gaskets.
   - Install **pressure gauges** at the bottom of the tank.
   - Install **sounding pipes or level indicators** to measure water level.
   - Obtain **surveyor's approval** before filling.

2. **Filling the Tank:**
   - Connect filling hose/pump to the tank inlet.
   - Begin filling the tank **slowly** to allow air to escape through vents.
   - Open all **air vents** to prevent air pockets.
   - Continue filling until water reaches the **test head** (2.45 m above tank top).

3. **Holding Pressure:**
   - Maintain the water level (test head) for **15–30 minutes**.
   - **Monitor pressure gauges** continuously.
   - **Inspect all joints, welds, and penetrations** for leakage.
   - **Inspect structure** for any bulging, sagging, or deformation.
   - Surveyor witnesses the test.

4. **Drainage:**
   - After approval, **drain the tank slowly** through the drain valve.
   - Open all vents to prevent vacuum formation.
   - **Dry the tank** thoroughly to prevent corrosion.
   - Apply protective coating if required.

**Safety Precautions:**

1. **No personnel inside the tank** during filling and pressurization.
2. **All personnel must be trained** in hydrostatic testing procedures.
3. **Emergency drainage plan** must be in place.
4. **Communication system** (radio, intercom) between inspector inside and supervisor outside.
5. **Oxygen level check** if anyone enters the tank after drainage.
6. **Slip/trip hazards** addressed (wet surfaces).
7. **Pressure relief** at the tank top to prevent over-pressurization.
8. **Fire-fighting equipment** available.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write the test head as **"2.45 m depth"** instead of **"2.45 m above the tank top."** This is a critical distinction. The 2.45 m is the water level **above the highest point of the tank**, not the tank depth.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **hydrostatic test** (full tank, structural check) with **hose test** (joint check only). Both are mandatory but serve different purposes.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Using **fresh water density (1000 kg/m³)** instead of sea water (**1025 kg/m³**) for ballast tank calculations. Always check the medium.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to add the **1.0 m margin** in the test head formula when the design head is specified.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Not mentioning **NDT** as a prerequisite to hydrostatic testing — NDT must be completed **before** the tank is filled.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Testing of Deep Tanks**

- [x] **Deep Tank Definition:** Tank with depth **> 1.5 m** or **> 1/6th of breadth** (as per IRS/IACS).
- [x] **Test Head:** **2.45 m of water above tank top** OR **design head + 1.0 m**, whichever is **greater** (IACS UR Z17.1).
- [x] **Hydrostatic Pressure Formula:** $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot H$ (in Pa).
- [x] **Sea Water Pressure Factor:** ~**10.05 kPa per metre** depth.
- [x] **Fresh Water Pressure Factor:** ~**9.81 kPa per metre** depth.
- [x] **Test Methods:** Hydrostatic, Hose Test, Air Test, Vacuum Test, NDT.
- [x] **Hose Test Pressure:** ≥ **2 bar**, nozzle ≤ **1.5 m** from joint.
- [x] **Air Test Pressure:** **0.15–0.20 bar** (gauge).
- [x] **Test Duration:** **15–30 minutes** for hydrostatic.
- [x] **Acceptance Criteria:** No leakage, no permanent deformation, no cracks, no excessive bulging (≤ L/250).
- [x] **NDT Pre-requisite:** Ultrasonic (UT), Radiographic (RT), Magnetic Particle (MT), Dye Penetrant (PT) **before** filling.
- [x] **Test Order:** NDT → Hydrostatic → Hose Test → Drain → Dry → Coat → Sign-off.
- [x] **Safety:** No personnel inside during pressurization; trained personnel only; emergency drainage plan.
- [x] **Regulatory Bodies:** IACS, IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, BV, ABS — all follow similar rules (UR Z17.1).
- [x] **Real-World Impact:** Prevents flooding, oil pollution, structural failure at sea.
- [x] **Unit Convention:** Shipbuilding uses **metres of water column (head)** rather than pressure units — remember this for exam answers.

> **End of Notes — Module 3 — Testing of Deep Tanks**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Topside Tanks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Topside Tanks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Topside Tanks** are liquid storage compartments constructed within the **superstructure** or **deckhouse** of a merchant ship, located entirely **above the strength deck** (the uppermost continuous watertight deck) or within the **poop/forecastle** regions. Unlike double-bottom tanks or deep tanks which are placed in the hull envelope, topside tanks occupy the upper volumes of the vessel and are primarily used for storing liquids of relatively low density such as **fresh water, domestic fuel oil, lubricating oil, drain oil, and boiler feed water**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology, these tanks fall under the broader classification of **secondary structural spaces** because they do not contribute to the longitudinal strength of the hull girder, although they impose significant **local loads** on the supporting deck plating and stiffeners beneath them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Topside tanks are categorised under "Tanks and Deep Tanks" in Module 3. The examiner specifically tests student understanding of (a) their classification, (b) arrangement of internal framing, (c) capacity calculation methods, and (d) their structural interaction with the parent deck plating.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a multi-storey building where the **ground floor** is the ship's hull and the **roof** is the strength deck. Now picture the floor above the roof as the **superstructure** — this upper storey is exactly where **topside tanks** live.

Think of a **water tank on top of a residential apartment building**:
- It stores water for daily use.
- It is supported by a flat roof with reinforced beams.
- It must be strong enough to hold its own weight plus the water inside.
- It needs a vent, an overflow pipe, a filling pipe, and a sounding pipe.

A ship’s topside tank is essentially the **marine cousin** of this rooftop water tank, but with **much stricter requirements** because:
- The ship **rolls and pitches** continuously (sloshing effect).
- The tank is exposed to **sea conditions, vibration, and corrosion**.
- It must comply with **classification society rules** (DNV, Lloyd’s Register, ABS, IRS).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are universally applied in topside tank design under the **2024 KTU Naval Architecture syllabus**:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|-----------|---------------|------|
| Density of fresh water | **1000** | kg/m³ |
| Density of salt water | **1025** | kg/m³ |
| Density of fuel oil (HFO) | **940 – 980** | kg/m³ |
| Density of diesel oil (DO) | **860 – 900** | kg/m³ |
| Density of lubricating oil | **870 – 920** | kg/m³ |
| Design filling ratio (fuel) | **0.95 – 0.98** | — |
| Design filling ratio (water) | **0.97 – 1.00** | — |
| Specific gas constant (R) | **287** | J/(kg·K) |
| Gravitational acceleration (g) | **9.81** | m/s² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these densities matter in KTU exams:** The hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of a topside tank is computed as $p = \rho g h$. If a student mistakenly uses **1025 kg/m³** for fresh water, the examiner will deduct marks for unit/system inconsistency.

### 1.4 Classification of Topside Tanks (KTU Module 3 Mapping)

Topside tanks are broadly classified as follows:

1. **Fresh Water Tanks (F.W.T.)** — Potable and domestic water storage.
2. **Fuel Oil Tanks** — Sub-divided into:
   - **Settling Tanks** — Allow water and sediment to settle out of fuel.
   - **Service Tanks** — Daily-use fuel storage.
   - **Overflow Tanks** — Receive overflow from settling tanks.
3. **Lubricating Oil Tanks (L.O. Tanks)** — Storage for engine lubricating oil.
4. **Drain Oil Tanks** — Collect waste oil from engine room.
5. **Boiler Feed Water Tanks** — Supply de-aerated water to boilers.
6. **Bilge Tanks** (in superstructure regions) — Collect small quantities of bilge water.
7. **Cofferdams** — Empty void spaces separating tanks carrying incompatible liquids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cofferdams are NOT storage tanks.** They are dry, empty spaces designed to prevent cross-contamination between adjacent tanks carrying different liquids (e.g., a fuel oil tank adjacent to a fresh water tank). KTU examiners frequently ask this as a **short-answer question** to test conceptual clarity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Philosophy of Topside Tanks

Topside tanks are **secondary structures** — meaning they do **NOT participate** in the global hull-girder bending moment. However, they transfer **localised loads** to the parent deck plating, which must be reinforced accordingly.

The structural hierarchy from **inside to outside** of a typical topside tank is:

1. **Inner bottom / inner deck plating** — forms the tank boundary.
2. **Internal stiffeners** — brackets, webs, and diaphragms to resist hydrostatic pressure.
3. **Tank boundary bulkhead plating** — vertical walls of the tank.
4. **External stiffeners** — connecting the tank bulkheads to the ship's main structure.
5. **Parent deck plating** — the actual ship's deck that physically supports the tank.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why" Section — Why are topside tanks designed with internal stiffeners?**
> Hydrostatic pressure increases linearly with depth. A flat plate simply supported on all edges will deflect excessively if it is too thin. Adding **T-section stiffeners** reduces the unsupported span, thereby reducing deflection and bending stress. This is governed by **classification society plate-buckling formulae**.

### 2.2 Design Loads Acting on a Topside Tank

The following loads must be considered when designing a topside tank:

| Load Type | Source | Design Magnitude |
|-----------|--------|-----------------|
| **Internal pressure (head)** | Liquid column | $p = \rho g h$ |
| **External pressure** | Atmospheric on empty tank | $p_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa |
| **Sloshing pressure** | Liquid oscillation during rolling | $p_{slosh} = \rho a h_{slosh}$ |
| **Thermal expansion** | Hot fuel/lub oil | $\Delta L = \alpha L \Delta T$ |
| **Vibration** | Machinery | Dynamic amplification factor |
| **Ship motion loads** | Heeling/trimming | Inertial loads $F = m a$ |

### 2.3 The "Free Surface Effect" — Critical Concept for KTU

When a partially-filled tank rolls with the ship, the liquid surface tilts but does NOT remain horizontal relative to the tank. This causes a **shift of the centre of gravity** of the liquid mass sideways, creating a **moment that opposes the righting moment** of the ship. This is the **Free Surface Effect (FSE)**.

The free surface correction to the metacentric height (GM) is given by:

$$GM_{corrected} = GM_{original} - \dfrac{\rho \cdot i \cdot V_{liq}}{W_{ship}}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of liquid (kg/m³)
- $i$ = transverse moment of inertia of the free water surface area (m⁴)
- $V_{liq}$ = volume of liquid in the tank (m³)
- $W_{ship}$ = total displacement of the ship (tonnes or kg)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** Why are topside tanks sometimes called "stability hazards"? Because their **large free surface area** (broad and shallow) creates a **huge moment of inertia $i$**, which can catastrophically reduce the ship's GM during partial filling. Hence, classification societies impose **free surface moment limits**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V = L \times B \times H$ | Volume of rectangular tank | m³ |
| 2 | $V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ | Volume of cylindrical tank | m³ |
| 3 | $V = \dfrac{1}{3} \pi h (R^2 + Rr + r^2)$ | Volume of frustum-shaped tank | m³ |
| 4 | $W = \rho g V$ | Weight of liquid stored | N |
| 5 | $p = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth h | Pa |
| 6 | $P_{total} = \rho g \bar{h} A$ | Total force on a vertical surface | N |
| 7 | $h_{centroid} = \dfrac{\int y dA}{\int dA}$ | Depth of centre of pressure | m |
| 8 | $GM_{corr} = GM - \dfrac{\rho i V}{W}$ | Free surface correction | m |
| 9 | $i = \dfrac{L B^3}{12}$ | Moment of inertia of rectangular FS area | m⁴ |
| 10 | $t_{min} = \dfrac{p s k}{\sigma_{allow} \eta}$ | Minimum plating thickness (rules-based) | mm |
| 11 | $f_{slosh} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g \tanh(\pi h/L)}{r}}$ | Sloshing natural frequency (Lamb) | Hz |
| 12 | $\Delta M = \rho g A \cdot e_{max}$ | Maximum sloshing moment | N·m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the **backbone** of every Part-B (14-mark) question in KTU exams related to topside tanks. Memorise them along with **boundary conditions** (e.g., at $h=0$, $p=0$; at $h=H$, $p=\rho g H$).

### 2.5 Freeboard and Air-Pipe Requirements

A topside tank must never be filled to the brim because:
- Thermal expansion requires expansion space.
- Sloshing in seaway can cause spillage.

**Air pipes** (vent pipes) are fitted to allow escape of air during filling and intake of air during emptying. The **KTU standard** is:

$$H_{airpipe} \geq 760 \text{ mm above the deck or above the load waterline (whichever is higher)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world relevance:** This 760 mm rule is mandated by **SOLAS** (Safety of Life at Sea) convention, which the KTU syllabus references indirectly through classification society rules.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Volume and Weight Capacity of a Rectangular Topside Tank

**Given:** A topside fresh water tank with internal dimensions:
- Length $L = 6.0$ m
- Breadth $B = 3.0$ m
- Depth $H = 2.5$ m

**Step 1: Identify the geometric shape.**
The tank is a rectangular parallelepiped (cuboid). All internal corners are right-angled.

**Step 2: Apply the volume formula.**

$$V = L \times B \times H$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values.**

$$V = 6.0 \times 3.0 \times 2.5$$

$$V = 45.0 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4: Convert to litres (useful in KTU numerical problems).**

$$V = 45.0 \times 1000 = 45000 \text{ litres}$$

**Step 5: Compute the weight of fresh water stored (assuming 95% filling).**

$$W = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_{filled}$$

$$W = 1000 \times 9.81 \times (0.95 \times 45.0)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 42.75 \\
W &= 419377.5 \text{ N} \\
W &\approx 419.38 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Convert to mass tonnes for ship stability analysis.**

$$m = \dfrac{W}{g} = \dfrac{419377.5}{9.81} \approx 42750 \text{ kg} = 42.75 \text{ tonnes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** A common mistake students make is to use **g = 9.8** instead of **9.81**. Both are acceptable in KTU, but consistency is preferred.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure Distribution and Centre of Pressure

**Given:** A vertical rectangular bulkhead of a topside tank:
- Breadth (horizontal width) $b = 4.0$ m
- Depth of liquid $h = 3.0$ m
- Density $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³

**Step 1: Establish the pressure variation.**
Hydrostatic pressure varies linearly with depth:

$$p(y) = \rho g y$$

where $y$ is measured downward from the free surface.

**Step 2: Total force on the bulkhead.**

$$P = \int_0^h p(y) \cdot b \, dy = \int_0^h \rho g y \cdot b \, dy$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \rho g b \int_0^h y \, dy \\
P &= \rho g b \left[\dfrac{y^2}{2}\right]_0^h \\
P &= \rho g b \cdot \dfrac{h^2}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values.**

$$P = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 4.0 \times \dfrac{3.0^2}{2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 4.0 \times 4.5 \\
P &= 176580 \text{ N} \\
P &= 176.58 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Locate the centre of pressure (depth from free surface).**

The centre of pressure is where the resultant force acts. Its depth $\bar{y}$ is given by:

$$\bar{y} = \dfrac{\int_0^h y \cdot p(y) \cdot b \, dy}{\int_0^h p(y) \cdot b \, dy} = \dfrac{\int_0^h y^2 \, dy}{\int_0^h y \, dy}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{y} &= \dfrac{[y^3/3]_0^h}{[y^2/2]_0^h} \\
\bar{y} &= \dfrac{h^3/3}{h^2/2} \\
\bar{y} &= \dfrac{2h}{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Compute numerical centre of pressure depth.**

$$\bar{y} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.0}{3} = 2.0 \text{ m below free surface}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Rule:** For a vertical rectangular surface, the centre of pressure is always at $\dfrac{2h}{3}$ below the free surface (where $h$ is the depth of liquid).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Free Surface Effect on a Partially-Filled Topside Tank

**Given:** A topside tank of internal length $L_t = 8.0$ m, breadth $B_t = 5.0$ m, partially filled with fuel oil (density = 950 kg/m³) to a depth of $h_{fill} = 1.5$ m. Ship's displacement $W = 8000$ tonnes. Original $GM = 0.45$ m.

**Step 1: Calculate the free surface moment of inertia $i$.**
For a rectangular free surface of length $L_t$ and breadth $B_t$:

$$i = \dfrac{L_t \cdot B_t^3}{12}$$

**Step 2: Substitute.**

$$i = \dfrac{8.0 \times 5.0^3}{12} = \dfrac{8.0 \times 125}{12} = \dfrac{1000}{12}$$

$$i \approx 83.33 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3: Calculate the volume of fuel in the tank.**

$$V_{liq} = L_t \times B_t \times h_{fill} = 8.0 \times 5.0 \times 1.5 = 60.0 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4: Apply the free surface correction formula.**

$$\Delta GM = \dfrac{\rho \cdot i \cdot V_{liq}}{W_{ship}}$$

**Step 5: Substitute with consistent units.**
Convert $W_{ship}$ to SI units: $W_{ship} = 8000 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 78.48 \times 10^6$ N

But it is more convenient to use the **mass displacement form**:

$$\Delta GM = \dfrac{\rho \cdot i \cdot V_{liq}}{m_{ship}}$$

where $m_{ship} = 8 \times 10^6$ kg.

$$\Delta GM = \dfrac{950 \times 83.33 \times 60.0}{8 \times 10^6}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta GM &= \dfrac{4750000}{8 \times 10^6} \\
\Delta GM &= 0.5938 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — let me recheck. The formula in **length units** uses **displacement in mass** with density in tonnes/m³:

$$\Delta GM = \dfrac{\rho \cdot i}{MT1}$$

where $MT1 = \dfrac{m_{ship}}{V_{disp}}$ (mass per unit volume of displacement, in tonnes/m³).

Since sea water density is approximately **1.025 t/m³**:

$$MT1 \approx 1.025 \text{ t/m³ for sea water}$$

So:

$$\Delta GM = \dfrac{950/1000 \times 83.33}{1.025} = \dfrac{79.17}{1.025}$$

$$\Delta GM \approx 77.24 \text{ — no, let me redo}$$

**Correct calculation:**

$$\Delta GM = \dfrac{\rho_{liq} \cdot i}{\rho_{sw} \cdot A_W} \times \text{(number of tanks)}$$

where $A_W$ is waterplane area. Without $A_W$, use the direct mass form:

$$\Delta GM (\text{in m}) = \dfrac{\rho_{liq} \times i \times V_{liq}}{W_{ship} (\text{in tonnes}) \times 1000}$$

Actually the **standard KTU formula** is:

$$\Delta GM = \dfrac{\rho \cdot i \cdot v}{W}$$

where $\rho$ in t/m³, $i$ in m⁴, $v$ in m³, $W$ in tonnes.

Let us redo with correct units:

$$\Delta GM = \dfrac{0.950 \times 83.33 \times 60.0}{8000}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta GM &= \dfrac{4750}{8000} \\
\Delta GM &= 0.59375 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Apply correction.**

$$GM_{corrected} = GM_{original} - \Delta GM = 0.45 - 0.59375$$

$$GM_{corrected} = -0.14375 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is a NEGATIVE GM!** The ship has effectively lost all its metacentric stability due to the free surface effect of the topside tank. This is precisely why classification societies impose restrictions on topside tank usage and require them to be **pressed up** (fully filled or completely empty) during navigation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Topside Tank Types

| Parameter | Fresh Water Tank | Fuel Oil Settling Tank | Lub Oil Tank | Cofferdam |
|-----------|------------------|------------------------|--------------|-----------|
| Density (kg/m³) | **1000** | **950** | **900** | **Empty (0)** |
| Filling limit | **0.97 – 1.00** | **0.95 – 0.98** | **0.85 – 0.90** | **0** |
| Free surface risk | **High** | **Medium** | **Low** | **None** |
| Heating required | No | Yes (fuel heaters) | Yes | No |
| Cleaning frequency | Monthly | Quarterly | Half-yearly | Never |
| Classification rule | SOLAS Ch. II-1 | SOLAS Ch. II-2 | Class-specific | Class-specific |
| KT frequency in exam | **Very High** | **High** | **Medium** | **Low** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Topside Tank Architecture and Load Path

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Ship Superstructure] --> B[Topside Tank System]
    B --> C1[Fresh Water Tank FWT]
    B --> C2[Fuel Oil Service Tank FOST]
    B --> C3[Fuel Oil Settling Tank FOST-S]
    B --> C4[Lubricating Oil Tank LOT]
    B --> C5[Drain Oil Tank DOT]
    B --> C6[Cofferdam CD]

    C1 --> D1[Tank Boundary Bulkheads]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D1
    C6 --> D1

    D1 --> E[Internal Stiffeners T Sections]
    E --> F[Parent Deck Plating]
    F --> G[Deck Longitudinals]
    G --> H[Deck Transverse Beams]
    H --> I[Deck Girders]
    I --> J[Superstructure Pillars]
    J --> K[Strength Deck Plating]

    K --> L[Ship Hull Girder]

    C1 --> M1[Filling Pipe]
    C1 --> M2[Suction Pipe]
    C1 --> M3[Overflow Pipe]
    C1 --> M4[Air Vent Pipe]
    C1 --> M5[Sounding Pipe]

    C2 --> M6[Heated Fuel Supply Line]
    C2 --> M7[Settling Line to Service Tank]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Chart: Functional Architecture of Liquid Transfer in Topside Tanks

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Settling Tank Topside] -- Pump --> S2[Service Tank Topside]
    S2 -- Gravity Flow --> S3[Engine Room Daily Service]
    S3 -- Overflow Returns --> S4[Overflow Tank Topside]
    S4 -- Transfer Back --> S1

    FW1[Fresh Water Tank Topside] -- Hydrophore --> FW2[Domestic System]
    FW2 -- Waste Water --> FW3[Drain Collecting Tank]

    LO1[Lub Oil Tank Topside] -- Gravity --> LO2[Engine Lub Oil Sump]
    LO2 -- Used Oil Returns --> LO3[Drain Oil Tank]
    LO3 -- Transfer Ashore --> LO4[Waste Oil Disposal]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description: Internal Layout of a Typical Topside Tank

Since a physical 3D drawing is not feasible in this format, the following **functional block schematic** describes the principal components:

```
+--------------------------------------------------------+
```
